# DROP-code findings — full extract by cluster

> Read-only. Generated 2026-06-11. Every active finding referencing a §3 **DROP** tier code (catalogue restructured v2). Sources: `cluster_finding` (obs_id) + `wa_session_b_findings` via `wa_finding_catalogue_links`. The L2 verse-read (`finding_question_link`) references these codes **0 times** — they are characteristic/synthesis-level, never written at the verse. **Total occurrences: 1955** across 20 clusters. This is what the DROP would retire/relabel.

## Full extract

### C10  (43 findings)

**T1.2.3 · Best working description**  (4)

- `T1.2.3` · session_b · MEANING_OBSERVATION/resolved_qa — Best working description: Goodness is a characterological quality of the inner being — constitutionally located at the level of moral character and disposition — that engages moral evaluation, volition, and relational capacity simultaneously. It originates in God's own character and is received, expressed, and cultivated in the human person through encounter with divine goodness, Spirit-production, and volitional alignment with what is genuinely beneficial. Its impact on the person is threefold: it orients the will toward what is genuinely good (volitional alignment); it shapes the character to be and do good toward others (moral-relational formation); and it produces inner stability when rooted in nearness to God (experiential well-being). When absent, the person's will is misdirected, their moral assessment is inverted, and their inner stability is fragile. The registry description's formulation — "structurally beneficial... not just pleasing or approved but actually doing good in the deep sense" — accurately captures the ontological weight of the term. (OBS-067-OBS-001, OBS-067-OBS-029, OBS-067-OBS-048)  [anchors: Psa 73:28, Mic 6:8, Gal 5:22]
- `T1.2.3` · session_b · MEANING_OBSERVATION/resolved_qa — Working description: Faithfulness in this registry is the inner-being quality by which a person (primarily God, secondarily the human image-bearer) enacts their inner commitments, orientations, and compassions through giving — giving that is always relational, directed, and sourced in an inner-being state rather than in external obligation. It operates at the intersection of moral character (covenant keeping), relational disposition (compassion enacted), cognition (endowing inner capacities), transformation (eschatological reversal of inner states), and divine-human correspondence (glory-ascription in its proper direction). The primary faculty engagement is the whole person acting from within: the heart (leb) as source (Hos 11:8), the soul (nephesh) as the seat of attachment that motivates giving (Jer 12:7), the spirit (ruach) as both the object of endowing-giving (Neh 9:20) and the faculty transformed by eschatological-giving (Isa 61:3). Impact on the person: receiving divine inner-being giving produces trust, praise, transformed inner states, and restored responsiveness; giving from inner orientation produces alignment with God and participation in the divine pattern of faithful action.  [anchors: Hos 11:8, Jer 12:7, Neh 9:20, Isa 61:3, Job 1:21]
- `T1.2.3` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTRA_TIER/pending — T1 as a whole reveals that faithfulness in this registry is structurally composite and directional. The name ("faithfulness") promises a character quality; the OWNER term (na.tan) delivers an action. These are not contradictory — they are complementary aspects of the same inner-being reality: the dispositional quality only becomes faithfulness when it enacts itself (Q&A-016, Q&A-018). The triadic architecture (inner-being source → act → object given) is the definitional shape of this characteristic: it cannot be reduced to disposition alone or act alone (Q&A-017). The five operational modes (Q&A-022) show that this single definitional structure produces radically different expressions depending on what is given and from what inner state — the characteristic is definitionally unified but expressionally plural. The primary dimension (Moral Character) grounds this — it is the character quality that makes the giving faithful rather than merely generous. The definition's most distinctive feature is its disposition-in-action structure: faithfulness is a verb that must be enacted to be real, not a noun that can be possessed without expression.
- `T1.2.3` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER/pending — The working description of the characteristic (T1.2.3, Q&A-018) — faithfulness as an inner-being quality that enacts commitments, orientations, and compassions through giving — is directly illuminated by T5's formation account. The working description names a state of the person; T5 reveals how that state is produced and where it is oriented. Specifically: the sustained inner-being effects (T1.6: trust, praise orientation, responsiveness, cognitive formation) are precisely the formation arc outcomes of T5. Sustained covenantal giving produces identity as recipients of promise (T5.5); endowing giving constitutes cognitive capacity (T5.3); eschatological giving produces the endpoint state of "oaks of righteousness" (T5.6). What this pair reveals: the working definition of the characteristic is not a static description but an arc description — it names the person being formed toward a fullness that is not yet complete. The characteristic is definitionally developmental.

**T1.8 · Dimension Classification**  (10)

- `T1.8.1` · session_b · SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY/resolved_qa — The primary dimension is Moral Character (05) — this is the dominant register of H2896A in its human-subject groups (884-002: human moral character and conduct; 884-006: moral assessment of not-good; 886-002: Spirit-produced kindness as relational character quality). Moral Character covers the largest number of groups and the greatest verse count in the human register. However, given the 12 confirmed modes spanning multiple dimensions, the primary classification requires qualification: Moral Character is primary for human-subject goodness; Divine-Human Correspondence (11) is primary for divine-subject goodness (884-001, 884-007, 886-001). The registry is bipartite by dimension at the group level. (OBS-067-OBS-002, OBS-067-OBS-012, OBS-067-OBS-017, OBS-067-OBS-048)  [anchors: Mic 6:8, Psa 119:68]
- `T1.8.2` · session_b · SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY/resolved_qa — For Moral Character (05) as primary human dimension: Mic 6:8 defines human goodness as a tri-fold moral orientation (justice, kindness, humble walk). 884-002 (39 verses) is the largest human-subject group and explicitly names inner moral quality. 884-006 (22 verses) names the negative moral verdict — both groups confirm moral character as the primary register of human goodness in H2896A. Rom 15:14 (agathōsunē) confirms: "full of goodness" is a moral character assessment of the community. For Divine-Human Correspondence (11): Rom 11:22 (chrēstotēs as God's disposition), Eph 2:7 (God's chrēstotēs in Christ), Gen 1:31 (creative pronouncement), Psa 119:68 (God's goodness as origin). Both poles structurally correspond — God's goodness establishes the pattern and source; human goodness is its derivative expression. (OBS-067-OBS-012, OBS-067-OBS-017, OBS-067-OBS-027)  [anchors: Mic 6:8, Rom 11:22, Rom 15:14]
- `T1.8.3` · session_b · SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY/resolved_qa — Secondary dimensions confirmed: Cognition (03) — 884-004 (comparative wisdom evaluation) and 884-006 (moral verdict assessment) both require the cognitive evaluation faculty. Volition (04) — 884-008 (volitional preference idiom, 40 verses) and 885-001 (Spirit-produced goodness as resolve, 2Th 1:11). The secondary dimensions do not compete with the primary — they represent the multi-modal nature of the characteristic. Moral Character is primary because it characterises the person's inner being as good; Cognition is secondary because the moral evaluation faculty is engaged in assessing what is good; Volition is secondary because goodness both criteria the will and reconstitutes it. The WARN item #3 (no dim_review record) is noted: these assignments are based on Stage 2a observation and are analytically grounded but have not passed a formal dimension review process. (OBS-067-OBS-011, OBS-067-OBS-012, OBS-067-OBS-014, OBS-067-OBS-016)  [anchors: Pro 15:16, Jer 26:14, Gal 5:22]
- `T1.8.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTRA_TIER/pending — T1 as a whole reveals that goodness is constitutively multi-modal but unified in origin. The characteristic's 12 distinct operational modes (Q&A-022) span quality, disposition, condition, and evaluative act — and yet all share a single definitional structure: goodness is what proceeds from and returns to God. The immediate response varies by encounter type (Q&A-025-026), the sustained effect is constitutive rather than reactive (Q&A-030), and the dimension structure is bipartite — Moral Character for human-subject goodness, Divine-Human Correspondence for divine-subject (Q&A-034). What the tier as a whole reveals is that the apparent definitional complexity of goodness (12 modes, multiple dimensions, varied immediate responses) resolves into a coherent characterological portrait: goodness is the quality of alignment with the character of the one who is Good — variable in its expression but unified in its source and standard.
- `T1.8.3` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTRA_TIER/pending — T1 as a whole reveals that goodness is constitutively multi-modal but unified in origin. The characteristic's 12 distinct operational modes (Q&A-022) span quality, disposition, condition, and evaluative act — and yet all share a single definitional structure: goodness is what proceeds from and returns to God. The immediate response varies by encounter type (Q&A-025-026), the sustained effect is constitutive rather than reactive (Q&A-030), and the dimension structure is bipartite — Moral Character for human-subject goodness, Divine-Human Correspondence for divine-subject (Q&A-034). What the tier as a whole reveals is that the apparent definitional complexity of goodness (12 modes, multiple dimensions, varied immediate responses) resolves into a coherent characterological portrait: goodness is the quality of alignment with the character of the one who is Good — variable in its expression but unified in its source and standard.
- `T1.8.1` · session_b · SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY/resolved_qa — The primary dimension is Moral Character — specifically as the enacted expression of covenantal commitment. This is supported by the largest group (872-001, 37 relevant verses, KEYWORD_STRONG confidence) whose description names "divine giving as the expression of covenant faithfulness — God's giving of land, promise, and blessing as the embodiment of his inner covenantal commitment." The registry-level dimension assignment 'Moral/Conscience' is consistent with this. The covenantal dimension of na.tan (commitment enacted over generations, across changing circumstances, regardless of human merit) most directly expresses Moral Character as a dimension — the giving is the character made visible. This determination addresses DIM-60-001 (OBS-060-053): the primary dimension of the registry is Moral Character, but the registry carries four additional significant dimensions (Relational Disposition, Cognition, Transformation, DHC) that the single registry-level assignment does not capture.  [anchors: Gen 12:7, Gen 15:18, Mal 2:5]
- `T1.8.2` · session_b · MEANING_OBSERVATION/resolved_qa — The Moral Character classification is supported by: (a) Group 872-001 (37 verses, KEYWORD_STRONG) — the covenant-faithfulness giving texts centre on God's character expressed in enacted commitment over time: Gen 12:7, Gen 15:18, Neh 9:8 ("You found his heart faithful before you, and made with him the covenant to give..."), Mal 2:5 ("My covenant with him was one of life and peace, and I gave them to him"). The giving is framed as the expression of God's inner character, not merely a transactional provision. (b) Group 872-004 (11 verses) — the judicial giving-over is also classified as Moral Character, evidencing that God's moral character is expressed in both directions of giving. (c) DIM-60-001 (OBS-060-032, OBS-060-053): the existing finding names the theological/divine-human register of the registry, consistent with Moral Character as the primary dimension for a registry dominated by divine inner-being expression.  [anchors: Gen 12:7, Neh 9:8, Mal 2:5]
- `T1.8.3` · session_b · MEANING_OBSERVATION/resolved_qa — Yes — four secondary dimensions are carried, each with substantial group-level evidence: (1) Relational Disposition (groups 872-002 and 872-005) — compassionate responsive giving (GOD) and self-giving as inner orientation (HUMAN). This dimension is the most quantitatively significant after Moral Character (30 + 19 = 49 relevant verses combined). (2) Cognition (group 872-003) — divine giving of inner-being capacities: Spirit, wisdom, understanding. (3) Divine-Human Correspondence (group 872-006) — glory-ascription in its proper directional structure. (4) Transformation (group 872-007) — eschatological reversal of inner states. These secondary dimensions do not compete with Moral Character as primary — they are distinct operational modes of the same characteristic, each revealing a different dimension of how inner-being giving functions in the biblical corpus. The DIM-60-SD001 pointer (OBS-060-033) identifies this dimensional breadth as a programme-level finding: na.tan illustrates that a single action-verb can carry the full breadth of inner-being dimensional engagement depending on context.  [anchors: Gen 29:33, Neh 9:20, Isa 42:8, Isa 61:3]
- `T1.8.3` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTRA_TIER/pending — T1 as a whole reveals that faithfulness in this registry is structurally composite and directional. The name ("faithfulness") promises a character quality; the OWNER term (na.tan) delivers an action. These are not contradictory — they are complementary aspects of the same inner-being reality: the dispositional quality only becomes faithfulness when it enacts itself (Q&A-016, Q&A-018). The triadic architecture (inner-being source → act → object given) is the definitional shape of this characteristic: it cannot be reduced to disposition alone or act alone (Q&A-017). The five operational modes (Q&A-022) show that this single definitional structure produces radically different expressions depending on what is given and from what inner state — the characteristic is definitionally unified but expressionally plural. The primary dimension (Moral Character) grounds this — it is the character quality that makes the giving faithful rather than merely generous. The definition's most distinctive feature is its disposition-in-action structure: faithfulness is a verb that must be enacted to be real, not a noun that can be possessed without expression.
- `T1.8.3` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER/pending — The disposition-in-action structure (T1) and the connecting-hub position (T6) are directly related. The characteristic's actional nature (it is a verb, not a noun) means it produces other characteristics as its outputs (worship, hope, wisdom) and is produced by others as its inputs (love, compassion, trust). This producing/produced-by structure is precisely what makes it a hub: it sits at the intersection of multiple causal chains. The distinction of kind from nearest neighbours (T6.5, Q&A-162 — dispositional vs. actional) confirms that its structural position in the landscape is as the enacted form of what dispositional characteristics name. What this pair reveals: the definition of faithfulness-as-giving (actional) is the structural reason for its hub position. Because it is an act that can express any of the adjacent dispositions, it sits at the centre of the dispositional cluster. It is the verbal form of a field of nouns.

**T2.8 · Body-Deposit (DNA / generational)**  (3)

- `T2.8.1` · session_b · SOMATIC_EVIDENCE/resolved_qa — The data provides one possible instance of generational consequence: the covenant-giving texts in group 872-001 (Gen 12:7, Gen 17:8, Gen 26:3) address Abraham and his offspring — the land-giving is explicitly intergenerational ("to you and to your offspring forever"). The covenantal giving carries across generations as a constitutive element of Israelite identity. Whether this constitutes a constitutional body-deposit in the technical sense the prompt intends (including DNA-level consequence) cannot be determined from this data. The covenantal transmission is relational-identity rather than somatic-constitutional in the evidence available. The Neh 9:20 Spirit-giving to the wilderness generation suggests each generation receives the endowing giving fresh rather than inheriting it somatically. No explicit body-level deposit is evidenced.  [anchors: Gen 17:8, Neh 9:20]
- `T2.8.2` · session_b · SOMATIC_EVIDENCE/resolved_qa — Supporting generational transmission (not body-deposit per se): the covenant-giving texts address Abraham's offspring across multiple generations — Gen 12:7, Gen 17:8, Gen 26:3, Neh 9:35-36. The land-giving is inherited identity, not re-given to each individual. Contradicting constitutional body-deposit: the Spirit-giving in Neh 9:20 is presented as a specific historical act to the wilderness generation, not as a permanent somatic inheritance. The eschatological giving in Isa 61:3 and Hos 2:15 is presented as new initiative, not as retrieval of an inherited deposit. The evidence does not support a constitutional body deposit in the somatic sense; what transmits generationally is covenantal identity and promise, which is relational-narratival rather than biological.  [anchors: Gen 17:8, Neh 9:20, Isa 61:3]
- `T2.8.3` · session_b · SOMATIC_EVIDENCE/resolved_qa — The evidence is not silent but does not confirm a constitutional body deposit. No explicit somatic deposit is evidenced. The data is silent on DNA-level or physiological generational deposit. T5.7 consequence: per the catalogue instruction, since no constitutional body deposit is confirmed at T2.8, T5.7 (Deposit Consequence) closes formally — all three T5.7 prompts receive N with this dependency stated explicitly.  [anchors: N/A]

**T5.7 · Deposit Consequence**  (2)

- `T5.7.3` · session_b · OBSERVATION/resolved_qa — T2.8 found no confirmed constitutional body deposit (Q&A-059 through Q&A-061). T5.7 is now closed formally. No body-deposit consequence is evidenced. The generational transmission of covenantal identity (Gen 17:8) is relational-narratival, not somatic. T5.7 closed.  [anchors: N/A]
- `T5.7.3` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTRA_TIER/pending — T5 as a whole reveals that this characteristic has an eschatological orientation as its formative telos. The formation arc moves from constitutive endowment (Spirit and wisdom given to form inner-being capacity) through sustained covenantal narrative (the memory of received giving as formation environment) through suffering-testing (revealing whether inner-being giving is genuine) toward eschatological fullness ("oaks of righteousness," total communal transformation). The mechanisms are plural and context-specific — encounter (covenantal), gradual formation (endowing), sovereign reversal (eschatological) — revealing that formation is not a single pathway but a multi-modal process. The suffering relation is analytically significant: the characteristic is suffering-tested (revealed in human direction) and suffering-responsive (triggered in divine direction), showing that suffering is not incidental to the formation process but an integral context. T5.7 closes formally — no constitutional body deposit. What T5 reveals as a whole: faithfulness-as-giving is constitutively developmental — its present forms anticipate and move toward a fullness that is not yet fully realised. Every giving is, in some sense, an eschatological act: it participates in the pattern of divine giving that is moving toward total, permanent, communal transformation.

**T6.6 · Shared Verse Anchors**  (14)

- `T6.6.1` · session_b · CROSS_REGISTRY/resolved_qa — Shared anchors are extensively documented (§6 of data package): Gen 1:31 shared with experience/R58; Est 5:9 shared with joy/R97; Psa 34:8 shared with hope/R78 and discernment/R49; Pro 16:32 shared with patience/R116, anger/R4, dominion/R199; Eze 36:31 shared with abomination/R1; Mic 6:8 shared with love/R103, kindness/R99, will/R173, humility/R80, compassion/R23, condemnation/R24 (6 registries); Gal 5:22 shared with love/R103, patience/R116, joy/R97, peace/R117, faith/R59 (5 registries). Total of 7 anchor verses shared across multiple registries. (OBS-067-OBS-021, SBF-067-Q138)  [anchors: Mic 6:8, Gal 5:22, Psa 34:8]
- `T6.6.2` · session_b · CROSS_REGISTRY/resolved_qa — Mic 6:8 (shared with 6 registries) reveals that goodness, justice, kindness, love, humility, compassion, and will are not independently operating characteristics that happen to co-appear — they are co-constitutive elements of a single comprehensive moral orientation. Mic 6:8 functions as the programme's primary cross-registry synthesis node for the moral-character cluster. Gal 5:22 (shared with 5 registries) reveals that goodness, love, joy, peace, patience, and faith are co-produced facets of Spirit-formed character — the Spirit-fruit cluster is a programme-defined synthesis unit. Psa 34:8 (shared with discernment/R49 and hope/R78) reveals that divine goodness, inner discernment, and hope are structurally connected at the moment of encounter — tasting goodness produces both discernment (seeing clearly) and hope (taking refuge). Each shared anchor names a structural relationship between the registries that share it, not merely a coincidence of vocabulary. (OBS-067-OBS-021, OBS-067-OBS-025, SBF-067-Q138, SP-067-004, SP-067-005, SP-067-007)  [anchors: Mic 6:8, Gal 5:22, Psa 34:8]
- `T6.6.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTRA_TIER/pending — T6 as a whole reveals that goodness is structurally one of the most connected characteristics in the programme — both in co-occurrence breadth (top-25 co-occurrence list spanning the full dimensional spectrum), shared anchor density (7 shared anchors including the two highest-density synthesis nodes in the programme: Mic 6:8 and Gal 5:22), and root-level vocabulary sharing across three root families (TOV, AGATHO, CHRESTO) distributed across multiple registries. The structural picture confirms what T1 indicated definitionally: goodness is not a specialist characteristic but one that permeates and connects the whole inner-being landscape. The most significant T6 finding is the dual constitutive relationship (Q&A-152): goodness is simultaneously a constituent of the Spirit-fruit whole (Gal 5:22) and itself constituted by a tri-fold structure (Mic 6:8). This means goodness has both a containing relationship (it is part of something larger — Spirit-formed character) and an internal structure (it contains three orientations — justice, kindness, humility). The boundary distinctions (T6.5) remain the weakest component — all three nearest-neighbour boundaries (love, kindness, generosity) require Session D cross-registry synthesis. The R187 (strength) co-occurrence anomaly at 32 shared verses remains an open question for Session D (SP-067-027).
- `T6.6.2` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER/pending — T1's boundary analysis (Q&A-019-021) identified three structural opposites and named the boundary problem with adjacent characteristics as partially unresolved. T6's structural relationships data confirms and deepens this: the boundary between goodness and love (R103), kindness (R99), and generosity (R65) is structurally unresolved at the root level — the vocabulary sharing (T6.4) and co-occurrence data (T6.1) reveal that these registries are adjacent precisely because they share root families and co-anchor at the same verses. T1's definition of goodness as constituted by the Mic 6:8 tri-fold (justice + kindness + humility — Q&A-152) directly names kindness as a component of goodness — which means the goodness-kindness boundary (T1.3 partially answered, T6.5 partially answered) is not between two separate characteristics but between a whole and one of its constituents. This reading resolves the boundary partially: kindness may be a constituent mode of goodness rather than a peer characteristic. Session D testing required.
- `T6.6.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER/pending — T3 confirmed that goodness is fundamentally integrating — it engages and aligns every faculty (Q&A-095). T6 confirmed that goodness is centrally connected across the programme's characteristic landscape — broad co-occurrence, shared anchors at the highest-density synthesis nodes, root-vocabulary sharing across three families. These two findings are structurally related: a characteristic that is internally integrating (T3) is also externally connecting (T6). The Mic 6:8 anchor (T6.6 — shared with 6 registries) integrates justice, kindness, love, humility, compassion, and will in a single verse — this is the structural expression of goodness's integrating faculty-engagement. Gal 5:22 (shared with 5 registries) integrates Spirit-fruit characteristics in a cluster — the same integrating logic. The characteristic that most fully integrates the inner faculties (T3) is also the characteristic most structurally connected to adjacent characteristics (T6). Integration within produces connection without.
- `T6.6.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER/pending — T4 confirmed that goodness operates across all three relational directions (God-to-human, human-to-God, human-to-human). T6's structural relationships and shared anchors data confirms that the cross-registry connections of goodness are organised along the same relational axes. The Mic 6:8 shared anchor (6 co-registries) is organized along the human-to-God and human-to-human axes: humility with God (vertical) and justice/kindness with others (horizontal). The Gal 5:22 shared anchor (5 co-registries) is organized along the human-community axis: Spirit-fruit characteristics that are all expressed in human relationship. The sequential finding (goodness → repentance, goodness → faith — Q&A-147-148) corresponds to the God-to-human relational direction: divine goodness producing these inner-being responses in the human. T6's cross-registry connections are the structural record of goodness's relational reach — the registries it is most connected to are the registries it most closely interacts with in its relational operation. Relational architecture (T4) and structural position (T6) are co-extensive.
- `T6.6.2` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER/pending — T4 confirmed that goodness operates across all three relational directions (God-to-human, human-to-God, human-to-human). T6's structural relationships and shared anchors data confirms that the cross-registry connections of goodness are organised along the same relational axes. The Mic 6:8 shared anchor (6 co-registries) is organized along the human-to-God and human-to-human axes: humility with God (vertical) and justice/kindness with others (horizontal). The Gal 5:22 shared anchor (5 co-registries) is organized along the human-community axis: Spirit-fruit characteristics that are all expressed in human relationship. The sequential finding (goodness → repentance, goodness → faith — Q&A-147-148) corresponds to the God-to-human relational direction: divine goodness producing these inner-being responses in the human. T6's cross-registry connections are the structural record of goodness's relational reach — the registries it is most connected to are the registries it most closely interacts with in its relational operation. Relational architecture (T4) and structural position (T6) are co-extensive.
- `T6.6.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER/pending — T5 confirmed that goodness plays a dual developmental role — formation product and formation agent (Q&A-136). T6 confirmed that the characteristics most closely connected to goodness in co-occurrence and shared anchors are the same characteristics named in the transformation arcs. The Gal 5:22 shared anchors (love, joy, patience, peace, faith) are the characteristics that co-occur in the context of Spirit-produced formation (the fruit list). The Mic 6:8 shared anchors (justice, kindness, love, humility) are the characteristics that are co-constituted with goodness in the definition of the formed human moral life. The cross-registry connections (T6) are the structural record of what characteristics goodness produces in others when it acts as a formation agent (T5). R103 (love) as the highest co-occurrence connection (T6.1 — 28 shared verses) is also the characteristic most naturally produced by encounter with goodness in the positive transformation arc — goodness → trust and love as natural co-products. T5's formation-agent role has T6's cross-registry connections as its structural trace in the programme.
- `T6.6.1` · session_b · CROSS_REGISTRY/resolved_qa — Yes — 12 shared anchor verses confirmed in G.3 (OBS-060-031, OBS-060-034 through OBS-060-038). Key shared anchors: Isa 61:3 (R006 anointing, R071 grief, R121 praise, R151 sorrow), Hos 11:8 (R023 compassion, R120 perverseness, R151 sorrow), Job 1:21 (R194 blessing, R206 vulnerability), Jer 12:7 (R103 love), Gen 12:7 (R058 experience), Job 38:36 (R183 heart). These shared anchors are the most cross-registry dense verses in the registry. Isa 61:3 anchors five other registries alongside R060 — the highest density.  [anchors: Isa 61:3, Hos 11:8, Job 1:21, Jer 12:7, Gen 12:7]
- `T6.6.2` · session_b · CROSS_REGISTRY/resolved_qa — Each shared anchor reveals a specific cross-registry relationship. (1) Isa 61:3 shared with R006, R071, R121, R151: the transformative-giving text simultaneously engages anointing, grief, praise, and sorrow — this verse is a structural node where multiple characteristics converge. Giving is the act that binds them: the anointing, the release from grief, the gift of praise, the end of sorrow are all forms of divine giving in the same verse. (2) Hos 11:8 shared with R023, R120, R151: compassion, perverseness, and sorrow are all present in the divine restraint-from-giving-over verse — the giving is withheld because of compassion, and is provoked by perverseness, in the context of grief. These three characteristics define the relational situation that determines the giving. (3) Job 1:21 shared with R194, R206: blessing and vulnerability are both products of the same experience of divine giving-and-taking — the verse holds them together. (4) Jer 12:7 shared with R103: love and judicial giving are structurally fused — the giving-over proceeds from love. Each shared anchor reveals a genuine inter-characteristic relationship, not coincidence.  [anchors: Isa 61:3, Hos 11:8, Job 1:21, Jer 12:7]
- `T6.6.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTRA_TIER/pending — T6 as a whole reveals that R060 (faithfulness/na.tan) occupies a connecting-hub structural position in the inner-being landscape. Its five-dimensional breadth (MC, RD, Cognition, Transformation, DHC) means it shares dimensional territory with virtually every adjacent characteristic. Its co-occurrence breadth (14 registries ≥6 shared verses) and shared-anchor density (12 shared anchors across 9 registries) confirms the hub position empirically. The sequential relationships (giving→worship, suffering→giving, faithlessness→giving-over) show it operating as both a causal initiator and a causal consequence in relation to other characteristics. The aman root architecture reveals that R060, R059, and R044 are conceptually entangled at the root level — the programme's separation is analytical, not lexical. The distinction from nearest neighbours (hesed/R099, love/R103) is of kind — dispositional vs. actional — confirming that R060 holds a distinct structural position as the actional realisation of what the dispositional characteristics name. What T6 reveals as a whole: this characteristic is not isolated in the inner-being landscape; it is a connector through which multiple adjacent characteristics are expressed, related, and developed.
- `T6.6.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER/pending — The constitutional pervasiveness of the characteristic (T2 — it reaches every constitutional level through distinct modes) and the hub position it occupies in the structural landscape (T6 — connecting multiple adjacent characteristics across all five dimensions) are causally related. The characteristic can occupy the hub position precisely because it is constitutionally pervasive: it is not anchored to a single constitutional level that would limit its relational scope. A purely spirit-level characteristic would only relate structurally to other spirit-level characteristics; a purely somatic characteristic would only relate somatically. This characteristic, moving across all levels, can relate structurally to characteristics at every level. What this pair reveals: constitutional breadth enables structural breadth. The connecting-hub position is a structural consequence of the constitutional polymorphism of giving as an inner-being act.
- `T6.6.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER/pending — The relational interfaces (T4 — God-to-human primary, human-to-God responsive, covenantal scope) and the structural landscape (T6 — hub position, causal sequences, shared anchors) reveal that R060's hub position is constituted by its position at the centre of the divine-human relational network. The sequential patterns (T6.2 — giving→worship, suffering→giving, faithlessness→giving-over) are relational sequences: each involves God and the human in the roles described by T4. The shared anchors (T6.6) are high-density precisely because they are relational nodes — Hos 11:8 anchors five registries because it holds the divine inner-being and the human relational situation simultaneously. What this pair reveals: the structural hub position of R060 in the inner-being landscape is a direct consequence of its occupation of the divine-human relational centre. It is a hub because it is where the primary divine-human dynamic (God gives, human receives and responds) is most directly expressed and most extensively connected.

**T6.7 · Dimensional Sharing**  (10)

- `T6.7.1` · session_b · CROSS_REGISTRY/resolved_qa — R67's confirmed dimensions: Moral Character (05) — primary for human-subject groups; Divine-Human Correspondence (11) — primary for divine-subject groups; Cognition (03) — secondary; Volition (04) — secondary. Each dimension is almost certainly shared with multiple other characteristics: Moral Character (05) is likely the most widely shared dimension in the moral-character cluster (C10 and adjacent). Volition (04) is engaged by will (R173), desire (R043), and others. Cognition (03) is engaged by knowledge (R100), wisdom (R174), and others. Divine-Human Correspondence (11) is engaged by all characteristics that have both divine and human expressions. However, the formal dimensional sharing data (which registries share which dimensions) requires the cross-registry comparative analysis of Session D. The current data package does not contain a dimensional sharing table across registries. This is a partial answer: the dimensions are confirmed; the sharing pattern requires Session D. (OBS-067-OBS-012, OBS-067-OBS-017, SBF-067-Q126)  [anchors: N/A]

Volition (04) shared with will and desire suggests goodness and volition are structurally connected — what the will chooses reflects its goodness orientation. Cognition (03) shared with knowledge and wisdom suggests goodness operates alongside intellectual characteristics rather than independently of them — consistent with Rom 15:14's pairing. - `T6.7.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTRA_TIER/pending — T6 as a whole reveals that goodness is structurally one of the most connected characteristics in the programme — both in co-occurrence breadth (top-25 co-occurrence list spanning the full dimensional spectrum), shared anchor density (7 shared anchors including the two highest-density synthesis nodes in the programme: Mic 6:8 and Gal 5:22), and root-level vocabulary sharing across three root families (TOV, AGATHO, CHRESTO) distributed across multiple registries. The structural picture confirms what T1 indicated definitionally: goodness is not a specialist characteristic but one that permeates and connects the whole inner-being landscape. The most significant T6 finding is the dual constitutive relationship (Q&A-152): goodness is simultaneously a constituent of the Spirit-fruit whole (Gal 5:22) and itself constituted by a tri-fold structure (Mic 6:8). This means goodness has both a containing relationship (it is part of something larger — Spirit-formed character) and an internal structure (it contains three orientations — justice, kindness, humility). The boundary distinctions (T6.5) remain the weakest component — all three nearest-neighbour boundaries (love, kindness, generosity) require Session D cross-registry synthesis. The R187 (strength) co-occurrence anomaly at 32 shared verses remains an open question for Session D (SP-067-027).
- `T6.7.1` · session_b · CROSS_REGISTRY/resolved_qa — R060 carries five confirmed dimensions (T1.8): Moral Character (primary), Relational Disposition, Cognition, Transformation, Divine-Human Correspondence. All five are programme dimensions shared by multiple other registries. The most significant overlaps by co-occurrence signal: (1) Moral Character — shared with R067 (goodness), R090 (innocence), R015 (boastfulness — its opposite), R034 (covenant). (2) Relational Disposition — shared with R099 (kindness/hesed), R103 (love), R023 (compassion), R111 (mercy). (3) Cognition — shared with R166 (understanding), which co-occurs at 6 shared verses confirming the endowing-giving/understanding connection. (4) Transformation — shared with R071 (grief, via Isa 61:3 anchor), R151 (sorrow), R078 (hope). (5) DHC — shared with R176 (worship), R121 (praise), which co-occur at 6 and 5 shared verses respectively. All five dimensions show confirmed co-occurrence overlap with other registries.  [anchors: Isa 61:3, Hos 11:8, Neh 9:20, Isa 42:8]
- `T6.7.2` · session_b · CROSS_REGISTRY/resolved_qa — The five-dimensional breadth of R060 and its confirmed sharing with registries across all five dimensions reveals that this characteristic operates as a connecting characteristic: it touches the same dimensional space as many adjacent characteristics simultaneously. This is consistent with the DIM-60-SD001 finding (OBS-060-033) — na.tan as a single action-verb carries dimensional breadth because the act of giving is dimensionally neutral in itself; the dimension it engages is determined by what is given, from what inner-being source, and in what relational direction. The dimensional sharing therefore reflects the versatility of giving-as-act, not overlap in the characteristic's specific analytical content. R060 is not simply a variant of R099 (kindness) or R103 (love) — it is a distinct actional characteristic that happens to engage the same dimensional space as multiple dispositional characteristics. This distinction is analytically important for Session D's cross-registry synthesis.  [anchors: Multiple (dimensional breadth evidenced across all groups)]

### C13  (34 findings)

**T1.2.3 · Best working description**  (2)

- `T1.2.3` · session_b · OBSERVATION/pending — Drawing from the registry description, the Q&A answers, and the observations:  **Contrition is the inner-being condition of genuine brokenness before God arising from honest self-knowledge about moral failure or creaturely limitation.** It is located in the spirit and heart, characterised by downward movement (lowliness, self-reduction), and structurally opposed to pride and self-sufficiency. In its penitential-relational mode, it is the inner precondition for genuine repentance — the state from which repentance arises rather than the act of repentance itself. In its experiential-anguish mode, it is the inner state produced by suffering or enmity, without necessarily carrying penitential content. The holy God specifically attends to, dwells with, and revives the contrite inner being — making contrition the inner posture that receives divine response.
- `T1.2.3` · session_b · MEANING_OBSERVATION/resolved_qa — Contrition is a settled inner-being condition of brokenness before God — produced by the encounter between divine holiness and human moral failure — in which the spirit and heart are lowered, opened, and made receptive to divine presence and reviving. It operates in three modes (penitential, anguish, substitutionary) unified by the crushing metaphor. It is located at the spirit-soul interface (spirit and heart as primary loci), is creaturely in origin, and positions the person as the one whom God draws near to, revives, and saves. OBS-030-022, OBS-030-045, OBS-030-048, OBS-030-052.  [anchors: Isa 57:15, Psa 34:18, Psa 51:17]

**T1.8 · Dimension Classification**  (8)

- `T1.8.1` · session_b · OBSERVATION/pending — **Dependence / Creatureliness** — confirmed as the primary dimension by Dimension Review. 5 of 9 groups carry this dimension: 7552-003, 7553-001, 7554-001, 7555-001, 7557-001. DIM-30-SD001 notes the convergence and describes contrition as "the penitential sub-form of Dependence/Creatureliness."
- `T1.8.2` · session_b · OBSERVATION/pending — The classification is supported across all five Dependence/Creatureliness groups. The evidence: all five describe a broken/crushed posture specifically *before God* — contrite before the divine presence, in a relational/covenantal frame where the broken posture is the condition from which divine nearness, revival, dwelling, or attention is given. The verse anchors: Psa 34:18 (crushed in spirit → Lord near), Isa 57:15 (contrite and lowly spirit → God dwells and revives), Psa 51:17 (broken and contrite heart → God will not despise), Isa 66:2 (humble and contrite in spirit → God looks to). All present the human in a condition of creaturely lowliness before the holy God. This is Dependence/Creatureliness precisely expressed.
- `T1.8.3` · session_b · OBSERVATION/pending — Yes — two secondary dimensions are present:  1. **Emotion — Negative** (3 of 9 groups: 7552-001, 7554-002, 7556-001): the experiential-anguish register — suffering, corporate desolation, terror/panic. These are not penitential; they are affective states of pain and dismay. This secondary dimension is complementary rather than competing: the crushing vocabulary operates in both registers because the same physical-to-inner metaphor (being crushed) applies to both penitential and experiential brokenness.  2. **Divine-Human Correspondence** (1 group: 7552-002 — Isa 53:5 substitutionary crushing): This group carries a unique dimension — the Servant's crushing is in a different category from either penitential contrition or experiential anguish. It is complementary to the primary dimension in that it shares the crushing vocabulary, but it operates in a distinct theological register.  The secondary dimensions do not compete with the primary — they expand the semantic range of the vocabulary beyond its penitential core.
- `T1.8.1` · session_b · OBSERVATION/resolved_qa — Dependence / Creatureliness — confirmed by the dimension distribution: 5 of 9 groups assigned Dependence/Creatureliness, representing the dominant dimension. This is the penitential-relational mode as the primary register. The Dimension Review confirmed this (WA-DimensionReview-Instruction-v2.2-2026-04-11). OBS-030-022, OBS-030-045.  [anchors: Isa 57:15, Psa 34:18, Psa 51:17]
- `T1.8.2` · session_b · OBSERVATION/resolved_qa — The primary evidence: (1) The dominant group descriptions all frame contrition as the inner being before God in a posture of brokenness and dependence (Groups 7552-003, 7553-001, 7554-001, 7555-001, 7557-001). (2) The anchor verses in this dimension consistently name God as the one to whom the contrite person is exposed (Isa 57:15, Isa 66:2, Psa 34:18, Psa 51:17). (3) The characteristic produces reception of divine action rather than independent human achievement. OBS-030-022, OBS-030-045, OBS-030-048, OBS-030-052.  [anchors: Isa 57:15, Psa 34:18, Psa 51:17, Isa 66:2]
- `T1.8.3` · session_b · OBSERVATION/resolved_qa — Yes — two secondary dimensions: (1) Emotion — Negative (3 groups: the anguish/dismay register of crushing under adversity). (2) Divine-Human Correspondence (1 group: the substitutionary crushing of Isa 53:5). These do not compete with the primary Dependence/Creatureliness dimension but represent the characteristic operating in distinct modes along different relational axes. OBS-030-025, OBS-030-026, OBS-030-038.  [anchors: Psa 143:3, Jer 46:5, Isa 53:5]
- `T1.8.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTRA_TIER/pending — T1 as a whole reveals that contrition is a definitionally complex inner-being condition whose structure is determined by its origin (passive, produced by encounter), its constitutional location (spirit and heart), and its directional logic (downward into brokenness, then threshold toward divine response). The three-mode structure (penitential, anguish, substitutionary) is unified by the crushing metaphor but differentiated by the agent and relational context of the crushing. The definition tier establishes contrition's primary asymmetry: the person is not the author of their own contrition — they receive it from encounter with reality they cannot deflect — and this passivity is precisely what positions them for divine nearness. The structural opposite (inner self-enclosure) and the blocking condition (merit-claiming) together define contrition negatively as the absence of self-sufficiency before God.
- `T1.8.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER/pending — The definition tier (T1) establishes contrition's primary dimension (Dependence/Creatureliness) and its structural opposite (pride); the structural relationships tier (T6) confirms and enriches this through correlation data. T6's convergence position finding (Q&A-147 — contrition appears where heart, fear, and pride dynamics are simultaneously active) is a structural consequence of T1's definition: a characteristic defined by the collapse of self-sufficiency before God will necessarily appear in the same verses as the characteristics that are most involved in self-sufficiency (pride) and in creaturely orientation toward God (fear, heart). The causal sequence (contrition precedes divine nearness; contrition follows encounter with divine holiness) maps onto the sequential relationships in T6, confirming T1's directional analysis.

**T2.8 · Body-Deposit (DNA / generational)**  (6)

The gap is noted. OBS-030-015, OBS-030-055.  [anchors: N/A]
- `T2.8.2` · session_b · SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY/resolved_qa — No evidence supports a bodily deposit. - `T2.8.3` · session_b · SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY/resolved_qa — The evidence is silent on constitutional body deposit. T5.7 closes formally — all three T5.7 prompts receive N.  [anchors: N/A]
- `T2.8.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER/pending — The constitutional tier (T2) confirms no body deposit (T2.8); the formative tier (T5) closes T5.7 accordingly. Together T2 and T5 reveal that contrition's formative work is entirely at the spirit-soul level — it produces characterological change (lowliness, word-permeability) without leaving a somatic residue. The constitutional movement (T2.10 — downward through crushing, upward through reviving) maps precisely onto the formative sequence (T5.2 — crushing → contrite posture → divine reviving). T2's constitutional account and T5's formative account are describing the same event from two perspectives: T2 maps the space; T5 maps the time. The absence of a body deposit (T2.8/T5.7) means contrition's formation is renewable — the person can undergo the crushing-and-reviving cycle repeatedly without constitutional accumulation, which is consistent with T5's account of contrition as a recurring formative event.

The sequence (crushing → contrite posture → divine reviving → restored inner being) shows that the formation contrition produces is not the settling into permanent brokenness but the emergence of a character shaped by repeated breaking-and-reviving. The mechanism is dissolution-through-encounter — the inner being's self-protective structures dissolved by a reality it cannot absorb without being changed. Contrition functions as the recurring inner reset in the sanctification arc, preventing the accumulation of pride and self-sufficiency. T5.7 closes formally (no body deposit), which confirms that contrition's formative work is entirely inner-constitutional, not somatic-residual. The eschatological trajectory (T5.6) points forward but is underdeveloped in the verse evidence — the data indicates contrition is oriented toward joy and revival but does not name a final eschatological fullness.

OBS-030-032) and fellowship (the relational dynamic of substitutionary peace-making). The three-way anchor (contrition + sorrow + fellowship at Isa 53:5) suggests this verse is a convergence point for the inner-being consequences of substitutionary suffering.  2. **Isa 57:15 shared with pride (R123), name (R204), appetite (R008)**: This verse is structurally rich — the high and holy God (name, authority dimension) dwelling with the contrite (contrition) rather than the proud (pride antithesis) to revive (appetite/desire dimension?). The four-registry convergence at this verse marks it as a key Session D synthesis anchor: a single verse that names multiple inner-being realities in structural relationship.  3. **Isa 66:2 shared with fear (R061)**: The three-fold posture (humble + contrite + trembling-at-word) directly links contrition and fear (trembling) as co-present inner-being states. The shared anchor confirms the textual basis for the conceptual connection.  4. **Jer 46:5 shared with fear (R061)**: The military-terror scene links crushing-vocabulary (inner panic/dismay under defeat) with fear-vocabulary. This is the anguish-mode connection between contrition and fear.

Isa 57:15 is shared with R008 (appetite), R123 (pride), and R204 (name). Psa 34:18 is shared with R183 (heart). These shared anchors are the primary cross-registry signal for contrition. OBS-030-031, OBS-030-032, OBS-030-033, OBS-030-034.  [anchors: Isa 53:5, Isa 57:15, Psa 34:18]
- `T6.6.2` · session_b · CROSS_REGISTRY/resolved_qa — Isa 57:15's triple sharing (R008 appetite, R123 pride, R204 name) reveals it as a convergence verse: divine self-identification (name/character), divine disposition (against pride, toward the lowly), and human creaturely response (contrition as the posture receiving divine dwelling). The triple anchoring reveals that Isa 57:15 operates simultaneously in three registers — divine identity, human moral failure, and human receptive posture. Psa 34:18's sharing with R183 confirms that heart-language and contrition-language are structurally inseparable in the penitential bicolon. OBS-030-031, OBS-030-049.  [anchors: Isa 57:15, Psa 34:18]
- `T6.6.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTRA_TIER/pending — T6 as a whole positions contrition within the inner-being landscape as a convergence characteristic with multiple structural connections. The co-occurrence pattern places contrition at the nexus of heart (R183), fear (R061), pride (R123), sorrow (R151), and fellowship (R062) — all meeting in contrition's primary anchor verses. The causal structure is rich: contrition is causally produced by divine-human encounter; it causally produces divine nearness; it is constitutive of genuine repentance (R135). The vocabulary is sufficiently distinctive (dkh/ktt/nkh root families) that contrition maintains lexical identity even amid these structural connections. The shared anchor analysis confirms Isa 57:15 as the convergence verse — simultaneously registering in divine identity, human moral failure, and creaturely posture registers. T6 synthesis: contrition is a structurally central characteristic that connects multiple inner-being registers (dependence, fear, pride, heart, sorrow, fellowship) while maintaining a distinctive vocabulary and position as the penitential sub-form of creaturely dependence before God.
- `T6.6.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER/pending — The constitutional tier (T2) identifies spirit and heart as contrition's primary loci and bones as somatic expression; the structural relationships tier (T6) shows that the characteristics most closely connected to contrition are heart (R183 — 6 shared verses) and fear (R061, which is also a spirit-level characteristic). The constitutional co-location (contrition and heart-characteristics both operating at the heart/spirit level) directly produces the co-occurrence signal (T6.1). The structural connection between contrition and R183 heart is not merely topical (the same word appears in the same verses) but constitutional — they both operate in the same inner-being space. T2's constitutional map and T6's co-occurrence data are mutually confirming.
- `T6.6.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER/pending — The relational tier (T4) establishes contrition's primary vertical direction; the structural relationships tier (T6) maps the horizontal co-occurrence pattern. Together they reveal contrition's full relational landscape: primarily vertical (T4) but embedded in a horizontal network of related characteristics (T6 — heart, fear, pride, sorrow, fellowship). The co-occurrence at key verses (Isa 53:5 shared with R062 fellowship and R151 sorrow; Isa 66:2 shared with R061 fear) shows that contrition's primary vertical relationship (human before God) is always mediated through a cluster of associated inner-being characteristics. The structural connections (T6) are the horizontal context within which the vertical relational event (T4) occurs.
- `T6.6.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER/pending — The structural relationships tier (T6) identifies contrition's convergence position (co-occurring with heart, fear, pride, sorrow, fellowship) and its shared anchor verses; the evidential tier (T7) confirms that the shared anchor verses (Isa 53:5, Isa 57:15, Psa 34:18) are also the exegetically richest verses in the corpus. The co-occurrence pattern (T6) is not arbitrary — it is grounded in specific verses (T7) that are architecturally complex enough to carry multiple characteristics simultaneously. Isa 57:15 as the convergence verse (T6.6 and T7.2.5) is both the verse with the most shared registries and the most complete single-verse expression of contrition. T6's structural network and T7's verse-level analysis are mutually confirming: the verses that carry the most structural connections are the same verses that are analytically richest.

**T6.7 · Dimensional Sharing**  (6)

- `T6.7.1` · session_b · OBSERVATION/pending — R030 carries three dimensions: Dependence/Creatureliness (primary), Emotion — Negative (secondary), and Divine-Human Correspondence (one group). The programme contains many other registries that carry Emotion — Negative as a dimension — anguish (R005), despair (R044), sorrow (R151), and others. Dependence/Creatureliness is carried by registries including trust (R080) and dependence (R010) — named in DIM-30-SD001. Divine-Human Correspondence is the least commonly assigned dimension and its exact distribution across registries is not confirmed from the available data.  *Partial: confirmed dimensional sharing exists for the first two dimensions; full programme-wide dimensional distribution is not available in the current session data.*
- `T6.7.2` · session_b · OBSERVATION/pending — The Emotion — Negative sharing with anguish, despair, and sorrow places contrition in a cluster of characteristics whose affective character overlaps. The Dependence/Creatureliness sharing with trust and dependence places contrition in a theological posture cluster. The distinctive contribution of contrition is that it occupies both clusters simultaneously — it is the characteristic where penitential Dependence/Creatureliness meets Emotion — Negative grief. This dual-dimension occupancy may be unique or near-unique in the programme.  *Partial: the analytical claim about dual-dimension occupancy requires programme-wide dimensional distribution data to confirm.*
- `T6.7.3` · session_b · OBSERVATION/pending — Full programme-wide dimensional distribution data is not available in the current session.
