- `T1.8.1` · session_b · MEANING_OBSERVATION/resolved_qa — The primary dimension of covenant is Relational Disposition (06) — defined as the enduring orientation of the inner person toward another in committed, faithful relationship. This is supported by the largest group count (9 of 32 groups in RD), the dominant theological framing (God's covenant is an expression of his relational disposition of faithful self-commitment — OBS-034-038, OBS-034-039, OBS-034-052), and the definitional core (covenant as the structure of faithful relationship — Q&A-013). The registry-level label 'Relational/Social' approximates but does not match this programme dimension label. The primary classification is 06 — Relational Disposition. This assignment should be recorded in sb_classification field for Registry 034 at session close. Note: this is the primary dimension at the registry level; individual groups are correctly assigned to other dimensions per their specific covenantal context (Volition, Moral Character, Transformation, etc.).  [anchors: Gen 9:15, Deu 7:9, Heb 8:10]
- `T1.8.2` · session_b · MEANING_OBSERVATION/resolved_qa — Three categories of supporting evidence: (1) Group distribution: RD has 9 of 32 active groups — the largest single dimension cluster. These 9 groups span the primary covenant actors (God and humans) and the primary covenant traditions (Noahic, Abrahamic, Mosaic/bilateral-relational, Davidic, new covenant through Christ's blood, divorce metaphor). (2) Dominant subject pattern: the RD groups all have God as the dominant subject in 7 of the 9 cases — God's covenant is his relational disposition of faithful commitment. The 2 human-held RD groups (3306-001, 3304-004) both name person-to-person relational loyalty as the core. (3) Definitional evidence: the programme's description of covenant as "binding agreement... not a contract between equals but a formal, solemn bond in which God commits himself to his people" (§A) explicitly frames covenant as relational disposition — an orientation of committed self-giving. Deu 7:9's "keeps covenant and steadfast love" pairs covenant with hesed as the relational quality that holds it — both are RD.  [anchors: Gen 9:15, Deu 7:9, Lk 1:72]
- `T1.8.3` · session_b · MEANING_OBSERVATION/resolved_qa — Yes — covenant carries substantial secondary dimensions that do not compete with but complement the primary RD classification: (1) Volition (04) — 7 groups: covenant-making is a volitional act of inner commitment; the Sinai covenant is structured around bilateral volitional obligation. Volition is the human response-mode to God's relational disposition. (2) Moral Character (05) — 6 groups: covenant-keeping as a moral quality; oath-keeping as inner integrity; covenant-breaking as moral defect. Moral Character is the sustained-mode expression of covenantal commitment. (3) Transformation (08) — 5 groups: the new covenant trajectory. Transformation is what the new covenant produces constitutionally in the inner person. (4) Minor dimensions: Emotion-Negative (02 — covenantal exclusion and mourning), Vitality/Existence (07 — eschatological new creation), Dependence/Creatureliness (10 — the ark as sign of reverential awe), Emotion-Positive (01 — adjuration in love). The secondary dimensions do not compete: RD is the structural ground (covenant is the relational bond), Volition is the entry mode (how the human enters), Moral Character is the sustaining mode (how the bond is kept), and Transformation is the new covenant's constitutive mode (what the bond produces). They are analytically complementary.  [anchors: Deu 7:9, Neh 10:29, Jer 31:33]
- `T1.8.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTRA_TIER/pending — T1 as a whole reveals that covenant is constitutively architectural — it is not a characteristic that exists on its own terms but one that structures the conditions within which other inner-being characteristics operate. Its working description (Q&A-018) names it as "the solemn, binding relational bond... the structured architecture of faithful relationship." Its primary dimension is Relational Disposition (Q&A-034), but its four modes of operation (disposition, act, quality, condition — Q&A-016) and its composite structure (five constituent elements — Q&A-017) reveal that covenant is genuinely multi-modal. No other characteristic in the programme is described as simultaneously a disposition, an act, a quality, and a condition — this four-modal nature is unique to covenant and is what makes it architecturally prior to other C17 characteristics. T1 establishes that covenant is not one inner-being characteristic among equals but the structural framework within which the relational-grace vocabulary of C17 operates.
- `T1.8.1` · session_b · OBSERVATION/resolved_qa — Primary dimension: Relational Disposition (06). Kindness is fundamentally an orientation-toward-another — stable inner stance directed outward toward a relational partner. The chesed vocabulary is constitutively relational: it exists within and sustains covenant. Eight of 27 OWNER groups carry Relational Disposition — largest single dimension. H2617A group 536-001 (180 relevant verses, dominant subject GOD [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-083]) confirms at scale.  [anchors: Lam 3:22 · Eph 4:32 · Rut 3:10]
- `T1.8.2` · session_b · OBSERVATION/resolved_qa — Multi-stranded: (a) Chesed appears overwhelmingly in covenant contexts [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-089]. (b) All acts of kindness in the data are directed toward specific others [OBS-099-086, OBS-099-063, OBS-099-071, OBS-099-060]. (c) The structural opposite (pride, self-enclosure) is a relational failure [OBS-099-054]. (d) The counterfeit (chrēstologia) fails precisely in the relational direction [OBS-099-074]. Relational Disposition is the correct primary dimension.  [anchors: Rut 3:10 · 2Sa 10:2 · 1Cor 13:4]
- `T1.8.3` · session_b · OBSERVATION/resolved_qa — Yes — two strong secondary dimensions and one weaker: (a) Moral Character (05): seven OWNER groups carry this; H2617A group 536-002 (human chesed as moral character quality) and H2623 (chasid as moral character identity) confirm it [XREG: H2617A/H2623, OBS-099-083/094]. (b) Divine-Human Correspondence (11): 2Sa 22:26 / Eph 4:32 mirror structures are strong evidence [XREG: H2616A, G5544 group 886-001, OBS-099-096/090]. (c) Formative/Transformation: Gal 5:22 and Rom 2:4 show kindness operating formatively — thinner evidence, may properly belong to T5 rather than as dimension.  [anchors: Psa 4:3 · 2Sa 22:26 · Eph 4:32]
- `T1.8.1` · session_b · THEOLOGICAL_NOTE/resolved_qa — The most important single finding from T1: peace (shalom/eirēnē) is not primarily an emotional state or an absence of conflict, but an integrative wholeness grounded in divine identity and structured through covenant. The evidence establishes four dimensions that are not alternatives but a sequence: (1) Covenantal ground: shalom exists because God covenants it — the covenant of peace (Isa 54:10) is the basis; (2) Relational gift: God gives shalom to those in covenant (Num 6:26), speaks it to his saints (Psa 85:8), plans it for his people (Jer 29:11); (3) Inner condition: the received shalom becomes the experienced condition of the whole person — perfect peace for the trust-directed mind (Isa 26:3), soul at rest (Psa 4:8), welfare of the body and soul (Psa 38:3, 55:18); (4) Eschatological telos: the peace given and experienced is not complete until it is embodied in a Person — the Prince of Peace (Isa 9:6), he who shall be their peace (Mic 5:5), he who himself is our peace (Eph 2:14). DIM-117-002 (the pending finding about the nature of shalom) is hereby resolved: shalom is all four dimensions simultaneously, as a structured and connected reality. The central analytical finding is the sequence: divine identity → covenant gift → inner condition → eschatological embodiment in a Person. [RESOLVING DIM-117-002]  [anchors: Isa 54:10, Num 6:26, Isa 26:3, Psa 4:8, Isa 9:6, Mic 5:5, Eph 2:14]
- `T1.8.2` · session_b · OBSERVATION/resolved_qa — Two genuine gaps: (1) Pre-fall peace: the data does not address what shalom was in the original creation before the fall. The earliest peace references (Gen 15, 26, 28) already presuppose a world needing covenant — the pre-fall condition of shalom is not attested in the vocabulary evidence. This is a structural gap in the lexical approach: the peace vocabulary emerges in a fallen world and is therefore always shaped by covenant-restoration; (2) The relationship between the silence/quietness vocabulary (the "inner face" of peace) and shalom itself: the rest/quiet/silence Hebrew terms (H5117, H4496H, H5183A, H8252, and others) form the large majority of the registry's groups, yet their relationship to shalom is not formally defined in the data — they co-occur and co-occur in the same theological contexts but the data does not explicitly state whether shalom produces silence/quiet or silence/quiet produces shalom (though Isa 30:15 — quietness and trust = strength — suggests the latter). Gap deferred to Session D.
- `T1.8.3` · session_b · THEOLOGICAL_NOTE/resolved_qa — Yes — three major implications: (1) The shalom-as-covenant finding implicates R34 (covenant) directly — the covenant of peace (Isa 54:10, Eze 37:26) is both a peace term and a covenant term. Session D must examine whether the peace-covenant is a distinct covenant or the inner dimension of the Sinai/new covenant; (2) The shalom-as-trust-product finding implicates R163 (trust) — Isa 26:3 (trust → perfect peace) and Psa 4:8 (trust in God → lie down in peace) establish trust as peace's causal precondition. Session D must examine whether every instance of shalom-without-trust is either false peace (2509-002) or eschatological peace (2508-004); (3) The "Person IS peace" finding (Mic 5:5, Eph 2:14) implicates R130 (reconciliation) — Col 1:20 (making peace by the blood of the cross) and R130's apokatallassō vocabulary converge at the same event. The peace the Person IS and the reconciliation the cross achieves are the same event named from different angles.  [anchors: Isa 54:10, Isa 26:3, Eph 2:14, Col 1:20]
- `T1.8.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTRA_TIER/pending — The T1 analysis establishes that shalom/eirēnē names a structured four-dimensional reality — not alternative descriptions of one thing but a connected sequence: (1) Covenantal ground (God's covenant of peace, Isa 54:10); (2) Divine gift received (Num 6:26, Joh 14:27); (3) Inner condition experienced (Isa 26:3, Psa 4:8, Lam 3:17); (4) Eschatological embodiment in a Person (Isa 9:6, Mic 5:5, Eph 2:14). These four dimensions are not parallel alternatives but a causal sequence — each presupposes the preceding: covenant is the ground of the gift; the gift is what produces the inner condition; the inner condition points toward its eschatological fullness. This resolves DIM-117-002.
- `T1.8.3` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTRA_TIER/pending — The T1 analysis establishes that shalom/eirēnē names a structured four-dimensional reality — not alternative descriptions of one thing but a connected sequence: (1) Covenantal ground (God's covenant of peace, Isa 54:10); (2) Divine gift received (Num 6:26, Joh 14:27); (3) Inner condition experienced (Isa 26:3, Psa 4:8, Lam 3:17); (4) Eschatological embodiment in a Person (Isa 9:6, Mic 5:5, Eph 2:14). These four dimensions are not parallel alternatives but a causal sequence — each presupposes the preceding: covenant is the ground of the gift; the gift is what produces the inner condition; the inner condition points toward its eschatological fullness. This resolves DIM-117-002.
- `T1.8.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER/pending — T1 establishes peace as a four-dimensional structured reality (covenant → gift → inner condition → eschatological embodiment); T5 shows how peace moves through that structure developmentally. The sequence structure of T1 maps onto the formative sequences of T5: the covenant ground (T1 dimension 1) is the origin (T2.9); the gift received (T1 dimension 2) is the divine-word mechanism (T5.3); the inner condition (T1 dimension 3) is formed through discipline, suffering, and Spirit (T5.4/5); the eschatological embodiment (T1 dimension 4) is what the present experience anticipates (T5.6). The suffering connection (T5.4) fulfils the T1 paradox finding (Q&A-023): peace comes through its opposite because the Servant's suffering is the mechanism of the gift's purchase.
- `T1.8.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER/pending — T1 establishes peace's central T1 finding (covenant → gift → condition → embodiment in Person); T6 confirms this structure through the causal and constitutive relationships found in the corpus. T6's trust → peace causal sequence (Q&A-148/149) maps onto T1's "covenant → gift → inner condition" — trust is the human act that enables reception of the divine gift. T6's reconciliation-constitutes-peace finding (Q&A-153) maps onto T1's eschatological dimension — the cross-accomplished peace (T1 dimension 4) is what reconciliation produces. The shared anchor network (59 relationships across 43 registries) confirms that T1's four dimensions are each structurally connected to multiple other registries — peace is not self-contained but embedded in the programme's full inner-being ecosystem.
- `T1.8.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER/pending — T1's central finding (peace as structured four-dimensional reality culminating in a Person who IS peace) is lexically grounded in T7's root analysis: SHA.L (completeness-wholeness-settlement) names the essence at the root level; the sub-forms (G/H/I/J/K/L) map onto T1's four dimensions; and the two primary anchor verses (Isa 26:3 and Eph 2:14) name the experiential and ontological dimensions respectively. The lexical arc (root → state → act → person-type → NT coinage) maps precisely onto T1's four-dimensional sequence: the root names the ground (completeness), the person-type terms name the formed character, and eirēnopoieō (G1517) names the unique act that accomplishes the eschatological dimension. Word and meaning are structurally aligned.

**T2.8 · Body-Deposit (DNA / generational)**  (42)

- `T2.8.1` · session_b · OBSERVATION/pending — SD042 (Joh 1:14: grace taking permanent bodily form in the incarnation) is the most radical body-deposit statement but is a unique christological event. The tenderheartedness (eusplanchnos, Eph 4:32) produced by grace may constitute a sustained inner-constitutional deposit — a changed affective responsiveness — but whether this constitutes a bodily deposit in the T2.8 sense is not addressed in the data.  *Gap identified: the constitutional deposit question is not resolved for Grace.*
- `T2.8.3` · session_b · OBSERVATION/pending — Unambiguously: grace originates with God. It is received from outside the human person — not generated from within. This is constitutive of the definition. chinnam (H2600: for nothing, without cause) names the structural logic: grace is given for nothing, without cause in the recipient. SD026 radicalises this: even the capacity to receive grace (the supplication posture) is grace-given. Grace originates entirely outside the human person.  *Consistent with prior analysis. Adds T2 framing: external origin is not merely a theological assertion — it is encoded in the vocabulary.*
- `T2.8.2` · session_b · OBSERVATION/pending — Singular in source (God), multiple in channel. Grace comes from God as the single source but is mediated through: direct divine bestowing (889-001, 888-001), the proclaimed word (Act 20:32), the forgiving act of another person (5470-002), the spirit poured out (Zec 12:10). Source singular; channels multiple.  *Adds precision: the source/channel distinction is not explicit in prior analysis.*
- `T2.8.3` · session_b · OBSERVATION/pending — The source (God) does not change. The channel changes significantly. In human-to-human grace (forgiving, expressing), the immediate origin is the human person who extends it — but even this is derivative, patterned on and grounded in the divine forgiveness received (Eph 4:32). No context in the data shows grace originating independently of the divine source.  *Consistent with prior analysis.*
- `T2.8.1` · session_b · OBSERVATION/routed_cluster — The data does not contain direct evidence of a constitutional body deposit from sustained compassion in the individual. However, two lines of evidence approach this question without closing it:  **The womb as generational threshold:** H7358 re.chem and H7356A ra.cham ground compassion in the womb — the very space of biological transmission. Groups 1613-001 and 1613-002 name the womb as "the threshold of human existence over which God exercises sovereign care" and "the origin of human existence." If compassion is etymologically grounded in the womb, and the womb is the space through which life and formation are transmitted generationally, this creates an implicit but unconfirmed connection between compassion as a constitutive quality and its potential generational transmission through the womb-space.  **The Exod 34:6 formula and generational consequence:** The divine compassion-character formula (rachum ve-channum) is embedded in a passage (Exod 34:6-7) that explicitly names generational consequence — "visiting the iniquity of the fathers on the children and the children's children, to the third and the fourth generation." The positive complement (compassion, steadfast love) is named in the same breath. The structural implication is that God's compassionate character is generationally operative, though the text names the generational dimension for iniquity more explicitly than for compassion.  **What is missing (P):** The data does not provide direct evidence of a body deposit from sustained human compassion, nor any verse that names a generational compassion-transmission in human biological terms. The womb-link is suggestive but not probative. This finding feeds directly into T5.7.
- `T2.8.2` · session_b · OBSERVATION/routed_cluster — **Supporting (indirect):** The womb etymology creates a structural resonance between compassion and biological transmission. The eusplanchnos (tender heart) quality in 1 Pet 3:8 names a pre-dispositional inner softness — a constitutional quality that may be formed over time rather than simply present or absent. If tenderness of inner being can be formed and sustained, it is plausible (though not stated) that it could be constitutionally embedded.  The mirroring principle (2 Sam 22:26 — "with the merciful you show yourself merciful") operates reciprocally: God's compassion is responsive to human compassionate character. If sustained divine compassion shapes the human person's inner being constitutively over time, a long-term constitutional effect is implied — though not named as a body deposit.  **Contradicting / limiting:** No verse explicitly names a body-level deposit from sustained compassion in the human person. The Lam 4:10 evidence (compassionate women under siege violating their compassion) implies that even deeply formed compassionate character can be overwhelmed by extreme circumstance — which may suggest that the constitutional formation has limits under pressure, rather than that it produces an indestructible deposit.
- `T2.8.3` · session_b · OBSERVATION/routed_cluster — The evidence is not fully silent — the womb etymology and the Exod 34:6 generational frame create partial signals — but direct evidence of a constitutional body deposit is absent. T5.7 will be handled as a partial/conditional finding: the womb-related generational dimension will be noted, but no deposit will be affirmed as established from the data. The question is a genuine gap for Session D.
- `T2.8.3` · session_b · OBSERVATION/pending — **The evidence is silent on constitutional bodily deposit for fellowship.** This conclusion feeds into T5.7: the T5.7 Deposit Consequence prompts will be handled as S (silent) or P (partial) given the absence of deposit evidence at T2.8. This is noted now for T5.7 handling.
- `T2.8.1` · session_b · THEOLOGICAL_NOTE/resolved_qa — The data provides extensive somatic expression evidence (Unit 7 — kiss, embrace, tears, womb-movement, inner organs) but the question of a lasting somatic deposit is distinct from somatic expression. Evidence relevant to deposit: Pro 3:3 — chesed written on the 'tablet of the heart' — the heart is a somatic location but this is more metaphorical inscription than physiological deposit. The inner organs (me'eka/splanchna) appear as the location of love's compassion-movement — this is a named somatic location but not clearly a lasting deposit. The womb-etymology of rachamim implies a bodily-constitutional rootedness of love. The evidence implies a deep somatic-constitutional rootedness of love without explicitly naming a body deposit. Gap: the precise question of lasting somatic deposit is not directly addressed. Flagging T5.7 dependency per instruction.  [anchors: Pro 3:3, Isa 63:15, 1Ki 3:26]
- `T2.8.2` · session_b · THEOLOGICAL_NOTE/resolved_qa — The heart-inscription of chesed/faithfulness (Pro 3:3, OBS-103-090) — 'write them on the tablet of your heart' — is the closest evidence to a constitutional body-deposit: the heart-tablet metaphor implies that love's dispositional form is inscribed into the person's constitutional core. If this is treated as a deposit analogy, it reveals: (a) love's dispositional form (chesed) is meant to be a permanent inner-being inscription, not a transient orientation; (b) the heart as the deposit location is the deepest accessible somatic address in the love vocabulary; (c) the 'tablet' language implies durability — written, not merely felt. However, this is metaphorical language whose literal constitutional meaning is uncertain. Partial: the heart-inscription is the best available deposit-candidate in the data.  [anchors: Pro 3:3]
- `T2.8.3` · session_b · THEOLOGICAL_NOTE/resolved_qa — Body deposit in the explicit sense is not directly evidenced. The heart-inscription (Pro 3:3) is the closest candidate. The inner organs (me'eka/splanchna) are love's somatic location of compassion-movement but represent somatic expression rather than lasting deposit. For T5.7: if the body deposit at T2.8 is treated as absent or uncertain, T5.7 (Deposit Consequence) will receive not-applicable responses for all three prompts per the instruction's rule. This is flagged here. Recommendation: treat T2.8 as 'partially evidenced' (heart-inscription as metaphorical deposit candidate) and process T5.7 accordingly — neither full confirmation nor full N.  [anchors: Pro 3:3]
- `T2.8.1` · session_b · SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY/resolved_qa — The evidence does not explicitly name a constitutional deposit in the body from mercy's sustained operation. The atonement blood-mechanism operates on the *nephesh* (soul) rather than leaving a bodily deposit. The womb-etymology of *racham* connects mercy to bodily generativity but this is etymological, not functional. SD009 (blood as dominant somatic element) identifies the somatic mechanism but does not identify a residual deposit. The data is silent on whether sustained mercy-practice or mercy-reception leaves a bodily trace. OBS-111-037, OBS-111-015.  [anchors: N/A]
- `T2.8.2` · session_b · SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY/resolved_qa — No evidence explicitly supports a bodily deposit from mercy. Partial contradiction: the atonement texts are clear that the mechanism operates on the *nephesh*, not on the body as deposit-bearing. The blood mediates atonement to the soul — it is not retained in the body as constitutional change. OBS-111-037.  [anchors: Lev 17:11]
- `T2.8.3` · session_b · SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY/resolved_qa — The evidence is silent on constitutional body deposit from mercy's sustained operation. T5.7 closes formally as a result — all three prompts in T5.7 will receive N.  [anchors: N/A]
- `T2.8.1` · session_b · SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY/resolved_qa — The wound vocabulary (H2250 — bruise/stripe) leaves a mark that persists — a wound is a joining that remains visible. Whether this constitutes a constitutional body deposit from sustained fellowship-through-suffering is not directly evidenced. Phili 3:10's "becoming like him in his death" implies a constitutive bodily transformation but the deposit is not named explicitly. OBS-062-039, OBS-062-055.  [anchors: Phili 3:10]
- `T2.8.2` · session_b · SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY/resolved_qa — The wound as a persisting mark (H2250 — its physical logic) supports a body-deposit reading for the suffering-fellowship mode. Against: the inner-being outcomes (cleansing of innermost parts, healing) are internal, not somatic residue. The evidence is ambiguous. Gap noted.
- `T2.8.3` · session_b · SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY/resolved_qa — The evidence is partially silent — the wound-mark persists physically, but whether this constitutes a programme-relevant constitutional body deposit is not established. Recording as gap. T5.7 to be addressed at the level of the evidence available.
- `T2.8.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTRA_TIER/pending — T2 reveals fellowship's constitutional location is distributed across levels according to its mode: existential mode (soul/nephesh level — Ecc 9:4), companionship mode (heart level — Psa 119:63, Jer 13:23), desire-driven mode (desire-faculty — 2Pe 1:4), suffering mode (somatic — Pro 20:30, Isa 53:5). The deepest mode (ontological — 2Pe 1:4) implies spirit-level but this is inferential rather than explicit. T2's most significant finding is the desire-faculty as the constitutional engine directing fellowship: desire determines the fellowship object, and the fellowship object determines the constitutional formation. The body-deposit question (T2.8) remains open — the wound-mark as a persisting joining-trace is the nearest evidence, but its programme-relevance is uncertain.
- `T2.8.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER/pending — T2's constitutional map and T5's formative account describe the same reality from space and time perspectives. The desire-faculty (T2.5) as constitutional engine maps onto T5's formation sequence beginning point (volitional alignment — desire-directed). The heart-level formation (T2.3 — Psa 119:63, Jer 13:23) is T5's constitutional-formation endpoint. The body-deposit question (T2.8) opens into T5.7's developmental consequence analysis — the wound-mark as a formative trace raises the question of whether suffering-fellowship leaves a permanent constitutional marker, a question that remains open pending Session D.
- `T2.8.3` · session_b · SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY/resolved_qa — Evidence is silent on constitutional body deposit from forgiveness. T5.7 to close formally.
- `T2.8.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTRA_TIER/pending — T2 reveals that forgiveness engages multiple constitutional levels but with a distinctive constitutional pattern. The heart is the one explicitly named locus — Mat 18:35 ("from your heart") names the heart as the required depth of genuine forgiveness-giving. The cognition-faculty (Jer 31:34 — "remember no more"; "they shall know me") is the most clearly evidenced constitutional engagement alongside the heart. The somatic compassion faculty (splagchnizomai — Mat 18:27) is the named somatic-affective origin-point of the forgiving act. Spirit and soul levels are inferred from the guilt-forgiveness structural relationship and the Spirit's mediating role (Mar 3:29, Joh 20:23) rather than explicitly named. The body-deposit question is data-silent (T2.8); T5.7 closes formally. The constitutional movement is explicit (Luk 7:47 — guilt-bearing → forgiveness received → love generated; Jer 31:34 — forgiveness → direct knowledge of God). T2's key finding: the constitutional structure of forgiveness is act-centred, not state-centred — it is a constitutional event (the moment of release) that produces constitutional consequences (love, reverence, direct access).
- `T2.8.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER/pending — T2's constitutional movement (guilt-bearing → forgiveness → love → capacity to extend) is T5's formation sequence described at the constitutional level. T2's finding that the constitutional event produces further inner states (love, direct access to God) is T5's transitional-formative account. The body-deposit gap (T2.8 — data silent; T5.7 closed) means the formation is entirely inner-constitutional — the formative event leaves no somatic residue, which is consistent with T5's finding that forgiveness is transitional rather than permanently marking. The eschatological telos (T5.6 — Jer 31:34) is the permanent constitutional state that present forgiveness-events anticipate.
- `T2.8.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTRA_TIER/routed_cluster — T2 produces the most distinctive constitutional portrait in the current review set. Spirit-level location is entirely absent — three consecutive S ratings — forming a new gap. By contrast, soul-level (chesed as covenantal-soul character), heart-level (Hos 11:8 — "my heart recoils"), and somatic-level (re.chem, splanchnon) are all richly evidenced. The constitutional architecture is bottom-up: the womb and entrails are the originating ground; the soul and heart are the engagement points; the act is the constitutional expression. Four body-part functions are identified: constitutive (womb — the generative image of compassion), expressive (gut — the felt movement), indicative (Synoptic pattern — splanchnizō signals the compassion-event), and mediating (heart in Hos 11:8 — the deliberative locus where compassion and judgment contend). The four-stage constitutional movement sequence (somatic arousal → soul engagement → volitional engagement → outward action) is uniquely evidenced in this registry — consistently inside-out and bottom-up. T2.8 established no body deposit; T5.7 is formally closed. The constitutional portrait confirms the sb_classification (soul-body interface) and extends it: compassion is the most somatically grounded characteristic in the programme, with its originating logic residing in the body's deepest interior.

- `T2.8.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER/routed_cluster — T2's four-stage constitutional movement (somatic arousal → soul → volition → action) and T5's three mechanisms of change (encounter/sudden, covenantal-sustaining/gradual, participatory-ontological) are structurally complementary. T2 describes the movement within a single compassion-event; T5 describes how that event accumulates into character over time. The encounter/sudden mechanism (T5) corresponds to the full four-stage sequence of T2 in a single instance. The covenantal-sustaining mechanism (T5) corresponds to the repeated operation of the somatic-soul pathway (T2.2 — soul-level chesed as the sustaining character) over time. The participatory-ontological mechanism (T5) corresponds to the constitutional direction T2 identifies but leaves at spirit-level gap: if the deepest formation is participatory-ontological, the spirit-level dimension (T2.1 — entirely silent) may be where this operates — a convergent gap from two tiers.
- `T2.8.2` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER/routed_cluster — T2's four-stage constitutional movement (somatic arousal → soul → volition → action) and T5's three mechanisms of change (encounter/sudden, covenantal-sustaining/gradual, participatory-ontological) are structurally complementary. - `T2.8.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTRA_TIER/pending — T2 produces the clearest constitutional portrait of any characteristic in the current review set. The sb_classification (spirit-soul interface) is confirmed with precision: spirit level is the primary point of reception (Zec 12:10 — spirit of grace poured out), not the point of generation (grace originates with God — T2.9). The soul level is the site of standing (Rom 5:2 — "this grace in which we stand") and identity (1Cor 15:10 — "by the grace of God I am what I am"). The constitutional movement follows the SD021 four-faces model: divine disposition received at spirit level → relational standing established at soul level → specific capacity gifted → character expressed outward. Heart-location is partially evidenced via eusplanchnos (Eph 4:32 — the tenderheartedness that grace produces, reaching the affective-somatic interface). T2.7 produces a new finding: grace operates bidirectionally through the body-soul interface — soul-to-body (inner posture enacted somatically in prostration, fasting, sackcloth) and body-to-soul (somatic enactment deepening and confirming the inner posture). T2.8 remains open (body deposit question unresolved); T5.7 is left open accordingly. The constitutional origin analysis (T2.9) is the most analytically significant T2 finding: grace does not originate in the human constitutional system at any level — it arrives from outside. This is confirmed at T6 as the structural uniqueness of grace: the only characteristic that is not produced by any other inner-being characteristic.

- `T2.8.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER/pending — T2's constitutional movement (SD021 sequence: disposition → standing → capacity → character) and T5's five formation mechanisms describe the same formation arc from spatial and temporal perspectives. T2 identifies the constitutional levels through which grace moves; T5 identifies the mechanisms by which grace moves through each level in different contexts. The encounter mechanism (T5 — sudden specific relational event) produces the spirit-level reception (T2.1 — the initial point of arrival). The word mechanism (T5 — Act 20:32 — grace through proclaimed word) produces the soul-standing establishment (T2.2 — the word's sustained effect on the person's constitutive condition). The mediated-transformation mechanism (T5 — tenderheartedness → forgiving — Eph 4:32, SD030) corresponds to T2.7's bidirectional body-soul finding: the inner transformation mediates outward through the body-soul interface. T2.8's open question (body deposit) and T5.7's open status are formally aligned — both pending further investigation.
- `T2.8.2` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER/pending — T2's constitutional movement (SD021 sequence: disposition → standing → capacity → character) and T5's five formation mechanisms describe the same formation arc from spatial and temporal perspectives. - `T2.8.1` · session_b · SOMATIC_EVIDENCE/resolved_qa — The evidence provides one clear case of generational/constitutional deposit and one gap. (1) Circumcision as generational deposit: the covenant sign of circumcision is imposed on male descendants through the generations — "in your offspring shall all the families of the earth be blessed" (Act 3:25). The covenant is carried generationally through physical descent and the bodily sign inscribed on each generation. This is the clearest case of a constitutional deposit with generational consequence: covenant membership is inscribed on the body and transmitted across generations through descent and circumcision. (2) The new covenant's deposit: Isa 59:21 — "my words... shall not depart out of your mouth, or out of the mouth of your offspring, or out of the mouth of your children's offspring." The new covenant's content (God's words) is transmitted generationally through the mouth — a form of constitutional deposit that passes through speech across generations. Whether this constitutes a bodily deposit in the strict biological/DNA sense is not stated. Gap: the verse evidence does not address whether the new covenant's inner constitutional transformation (law on the heart) leaves a biological or genetic deposit. This is beyond what the data can answer.  [anchors: Act 3:25, Act 7:8, Isa 59:21]
- `T2.8.2` · session_b · SOMATIC_EVIDENCE/resolved_qa — Supporting: Act 7:8 (covenant of circumcision as generational bodily sign), Act 3:25 ("in your offspring" — covenant blessing transmitted through descent), Isa 59:21 (covenantal word transmitted through generations of offspring's mouths). All three show generational transmission mechanisms for covenant content. Contradicting/complicating: the NT trajectory (Paul's argument in Gal 3 and Rom 2 context) suggests that the covenant's essential deposit is not biological but spiritual — circumcision of the heart, not of the flesh, is the true mark. The bodily sign (circumcision) is relativised in the new covenant administration. This tension — OT covenant carried through bodily descent and circumcision; NT covenant carried through Spirit and faith — is a structural finding. The deposit changes constitutional mode across the covenantal trajectory.  [anchors: Act 7:8, Gal 3:17, Isa 59:21]
- `T2.8.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER/pending — T2 establishes that the heart is the primary constitutional location of covenant, with body (blood, circumcision) as the somatic deposit. T6 reveals that covenant's deepest structural relationship is with faithfulness (lexically co-generated through the emunah-amanah root — Q&A-157). The T2 × T6 synthesis: the constitutional deposit of covenant (bodily circumcision, generational word-transmission — Q&A-059) is the constitutional mechanism through which covenant's structural relationships are transmitted to new participants. Covenant's bond with faithfulness (T6) is transmitted across generations through the bodily sign (circumcision) and the spoken word (Isa 59:21) — the somatic deposit (T2) is the mechanism of structural relationship preservation (T6). The body carries the covenant's relational structure forward through time.
- `T2.8.2` · session_b · OBSERVATION/resolved_qa — The evidence contradicts a body-deposit claim for kindness at two levels. (a) Positive evidence for non-somatic transmission: the primary formation mechanisms evidenced are Spirit-production (Gal 5:22 — organic, non-somatic), heart-inscription (Pro 3:3 — constitutional but not bodily), and encounter with divine kindness (Psa 63:3, Lam 3:22 — relational, not somatic). None of these transmission pathways involve the body as recipient or transmitter [XREG: G5544, OBS-099-092; XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-087]. (b) The covenantal-generational framing of Exo 20:6 — "to a thousand generations of those who love me" — explicitly conditions the generational transmission on relational orientation (loving God), not on biological descent. The condition is volitional and covenantal, not hereditary. No evidence supports and the covenantal transmission pattern actively contradicts a constitutional body deposit.  [anchors: Gal 5:22 · Pro 3:3 · Exo 20:6]
- `T2.8.3` · session_b · OBSERVATION/resolved_qa — Explicitly noting: the evidence is silent on a constitutional body deposit for kindness, and the covenantal-generational evidence actively contradicts it. No body deposit is evidenced. This finding closes T5.7 — all three T5.7 prompts receive N (T5.7.1) or the treatment below (T5.7.2 and T5.7.3). See Q&A-109 and Q&A-110.  [anchors: —]
- `T2.8.1` · session_b · OBSERVATION/resolved_qa — The evidence provides indirect but not conclusive evidence for a bodily deposit. What is present: (1) Psa 38:3 and 32:3 establish that sustained absence of peace (inner disorder) produces somatic deterioration — bones wasting, no soundness in flesh. This implies that sustained presence of peace should produce the inverse — somatic health and soundness; (2) Pro 3:2 ("length of days and years of life and peace they will add to you") — wisdom-and-peace produces length of life; shalom is associated with longevity as its somatic fruit; (3) Pro 3:17 ("all her paths are peace") — wisdom's characteristic product is peace as a sustained life-condition. The wisdom literature implies that sustained shalom has somatic extension effects (length of life). However: the data does not explicitly address constitutional deposit in the technical sense (DNA, generational transmission). The silence is methodological rather than substantive — the biblical vocabulary does not operate with the category of genetic or physiological deposit. Gap: the question of generational or developmental deposit for sustained peace is not addressable from this data. This finding feeds directly into T5.7.  [anchors: Psa 38:3, Pro 3:2]
- `T2.8.2` · session_b · OBSERVATION/resolved_qa — Supporting: Pro 3:2 (peace associated with longer life — a life-outcome that implies somatic extension); the somatic-deterioration pattern under sustained peacelessness (Psa 38:3, 32:3, Deu 28:65) implies by negation that sustained peace protects somatic integrity. Contradicting: no verse explicitly claims that shalom leaves a genetic or physiological deposit. The closest is Isa 66:12 ("like a river... like an overflowing stream") — the image of peace as overflowing suggests abundance rather than deposit. Neutral: 1Th 5:23 (the God of peace sanctifying the whole person — spirit, soul, body) suggests peace's reach is total but does not specify deposit mechanisms. The evidence supports somatic consequence of peace (in both directions) but not constitutional deposit in the genetic/biological sense. Gap explicitly noted for T5.7.  [anchors: Pro 3:2, Psa 38:3, 1Th 5:23]
- `T2.8.3` · session_b · OBSERVATION/resolved_qa — The evidence is partially silent on constitutional deposit in the technical sense. The data establishes somatic consequence (in both directions) but not deposit. T5.7 consequence: since the evidence for a constitutional body deposit is indirect and inconclusive, T5.7 will not be able to affirm a deposit finding — all three T5.7 prompts will receive N (not applicable) unless T5 analysis surfaces new evidence. This is recorded here for that dependency. The somatic consequence findings (T2.6, T2.7) remain valid independent of the deposit question.

**T5.7 · Deposit Consequence**  (22)

- `T5.7.2` · session_b · OBSERVATION/pending — Although T5.7 is closed on the body-deposit question, the generational dimension warrants a separate note because the data touches it obliquely:  DIM-064-SD015 raises the question of **corporate forgiveness** — whether a community's receipt of divine forgiveness has collective inner-being dimensions. Daniel's intercession (Dan 9:19) is on behalf of the nation; Moses intercedes for the nation (Num 14:19 referenced in DIM-064-SD015). The communal scope of forgiveness in the OT intercessory corpus implies that forgiveness can be received at a corporate level — and what is received corporately may carry generational consequence.  The new covenant promise (Jer 31:34) is addressed to the covenant community — "the house of Israel and the house of Judah" — and its scope ("from the least to the greatest") has a generational or at least corporate breadth.  *Gap noted:* Whether corporate receipt of forgiveness produces a generational deposit in the community (a different question from the body-deposit question of T2.8) is not examined in the data. This is a gap that DIM-064-SD015 identifies as a lower-priority Session D question.
- `T5.7.1` · session_b · OBSERVATION/routed_cluster — T2.8 found no direct evidence of a constitutional body deposit but identified two partial signals: the womb etymology as a generational resonance, and the Exod 34:6 generational frame. This prompt is therefore handled as conditional — addressing what can be said given the partial evidence, without affirming a deposit as established.  **From the womb-etymology signal:** If the RACHAM root's grounding in the womb carries a generational dimension, the developmental consequence implied is that compassion as a constitutive inner quality may be transmitted through the generational threshold of birth — not as a determinate deposit but as a predisposition or constitutional openness formed in the earliest stages of human existence. Groups 1613-001 and 1613-002 name the womb as the space where "divine compassion, calling, and consecration originate before birth" — if compassion is already present as a divine action at this threshold, its developmental consequence may be a prior orientation toward compassion built into the person before conscious formation begins.  **From the Exod 34:6 generational frame:** The divine compassion-character named in this formula operates across generations in Israel's experience — the chesed that holds through exile, return, and renewal is generationally sustained. The developmental consequence in the community is a transgenerational formation in which each generation receives the chesed-character of God and is thereby constituted as a compassion-formed people.  **What is missing (P):** Neither signal constitutes direct evidence of a body deposit producing specific developmental consequences in the individual. The question remains partially open and is a genuine gap for Session D.
- `T5.7.2` · session_b · OBSERVATION/routed_cluster — The most direct evidence of generational consequence in the compassion data is not at the body-deposit level but at the community-covenantal level.  **Chesed as transgenerational covenantal reality:** The chesed vocabulary operates across Israel's entire covenantal history — from the Exod 34:6 declaration through the Psalter, the prophets, and into the NT (Lam 3:22). Each generation inherits the prior generation's experience of divine chesed and lives within the covenantal framework it establishes. In this sense, chesed produces a generational consequence that is not biological but covenantal-formative: the community shaped by divine chesed across generations develops a shared inner orientation toward compassion as the defining quality of its relationship with God.  **The chasid as a generational character type:** The devout/loyal person (chasid) is presented in the wisdom and Psalm literature as a recognisable character type — the person whose inner life embodies chesed. The existence of this as a stable, named type across the biblical corpus suggests that chesed-formation produces recognisable generational patterns.  **What is missing (P):** Biological or body-deposit generational consequence is not established from the data. The generational consequence that is evidenced is covenantal and communal, not individual-genetic. This distinction is important for the programme's constitution framework.
- `T5.7.3` · session_b · OBSERVATION/routed_cluster — T2.8 found no direct body deposit. Partial signals were identified and have been carried through T5.7 as conditional analysis. T5.7 is now formally closed. The generational dimension of compassion is a genuine gap requiring Session D attention, particularly regarding the relationship between the womb-etymology, the RACHAM root's generational resonances, and the covenantal-transgenerational chesed pattern.
- `T5.7.3` · session_b · OBSERVATION/pending — **T2.8 found no constitutional bodily deposit for fellowship. T5.7 is formally closed.**  The evidence is silent on constitutional deposit in the body, including DNA or generational consequence. No verse in the registry addresses this question directly. The Jer 13:23 image (indelible character) approaches this territory but operates at the constitutional-character level rather than the somatic-deposit level. T5.7 formally closes on the basis of T2.8 silence.
- `T5.7.1` · session_b · THEOLOGICAL_NOTE/resolved_qa — T2.8 identified the heart-inscription (Pro 3:3) as a partial deposit-candidate — metaphorical but not fully confirmed as a constitutional body-deposit. If the heart-inscription is treated as a deposit: the consequence is that love becomes a stable constitutional feature of the person rather than a fluctuating state — chesed written on the heart is love that endures through circumstance. The heart-inscription consequence: the person whose heart has love inscribed acts from love habitually, not only when feeling love. This is consistent with the chasid profile (OBS-103-026): the godly person whose inner character is shaped by covenantal love. The inscription makes love a character-quality rather than a feeling-event. Gap: the full body-deposit question remains partially evidenced; this response reflects the heart-inscription's probable consequence if it is a deposit, not a fully confirmed answer.  [anchors: Pro 3:3, Psa 4:3]
- `T5.7.2` · session_b · THEOLOGICAL_NOTE/resolved_qa — Per the heart-inscription evidence (Pro 3:3, OBS-103-090): the inscription of love on the heart implies a lasting constitutional change — the person whose heart has chesed written into it is a different person constitutionally from the one who merely performs loving acts occasionally. The chasid (OBS-103-026) is defined as a person-type, not merely a person-doing-things — the love-character is constitutive of who they are. However, the evidence for permanent constitutional change in the biological-body sense (as distinct from the character-level) is not directly stated. The character-change appears lasting; the somatic-deposit sense remains uncertain.  [anchors: Pro 3:3, Psa 4:3]
- `T5.7.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTRA_TIER/routed_cluster — Love's formation operates through a multi-layered process in the data. The primary pathway is receptive: divine love received (1Jo 4:19) → Spirit infusion (Rom 5:5, Gal 5:22) → heart-reception → soul-response → community expression. Secondary pathways: obedience → soul purification → sincere love (1Pe 1:22); forgiveness received → love generated (Luk 7:47 — via SD-020); training within community (Tit 2:4). Love can grow (1Th 3:12) and increase through the Lord's action. Its reversibility is asymmetric: divine love is irreversible by nature (Jer 31:3 — everlasting; Lam 3:22 — never ceasing); human love is subject to redirection through competing love-allegiances (2Ti 4:10 — Demas). The eschatological trajectory is toward perfection and direct knowledge: partial now (mediated by community, neighbour-teaching), moving toward fullness (perfect love casting out fear — 1Jo 4:18; direct divine knowledge — Jer 31:34; pleasures forevermore — Psa 16:11). Formation requires community — love cannot be formed in isolation; it requires both the divine giver and the human community as the formation context.
- `T5.7.2` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTRA_TIER/routed_cluster — Love's formation operates through a multi-layered process in the data. The primary pathway is receptive: divine love received (1Jo 4:19) → Spirit infusion (Rom 5:5, Gal 5:22) → heart-reception → soul-response → community expression. Secondary pathways: obedience → soul purification → sincere love (1Pe 1:22); forgiveness received → love generated (Luk 7:47 — via SD-020); training within community (Tit 2:4). Love can grow (1Th 3:12) and increase through the Lord's action. Its reversibility is asymmetric: divine love is irreversible by nature (Jer 31:3 — everlasting; Lam 3:22 — never ceasing); human love is subject to redirection through competing love-allegiances (2Ti 4:10 — Demas). The eschatological trajectory is toward perfection and direct knowledge: partial now (mediated by community, neighbour-teaching), moving toward fullness (perfect love casting out fear — 1Jo 4:18; direct divine knowledge — Jer 31:34; pleasures forevermore — Psa 16:11). Formation requires community — love cannot be formed in isolation; it requires both the divine giver and the human community as the formation context.
- `T5.7.1` · session_b · OBSERVATION/resolved_qa — T2.8 found partial evidence of body deposit — the wound mark (H2250) persists as a physical trace. If that constitutes a deposit, the developmental consequence is that the wound-fellowship leaves a visible marker of participation. Phili 3:10's "becoming like him in his death" implies a constitutive bodily transformation whose developmental consequence is conformity to Christ's death-and-resurrection pattern. OBS-062-039, OBS-062-055.  [anchors: Phili 3:10]
- `T5.7.2` · session_b · OBSERVATION/resolved_qa — Generational consequence: not evidenced from this registry's anchor verses. The fellowship formations described are personal and corporate, not explicitly generational. Gap noted. OBS-062-052.
- `T5.7.3` · session_b · OBSERVATION/resolved_qa — T2.8 found ambiguous evidence — the wound mark is a physical persisting trace but whether it constitutes a programme-relevant constitutional deposit is uncertain. T5.7 remains partially open pending Session D cross-registry analysis (SP-062-002, SP-062-004 relate to this question). Gap noted; not formally closed.
- `T5.7.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTRA_TIER/pending — T5 establishes fellowship as the primary relational mechanism of sanctification in this registry — character is formed not through solitary disciplines but through participatory community alignment. The formation sequence (volitional alignment → habitual participation → constitutional fixing) runs from free choice to constrained character, meaning the initial choice of fellowship objects is made at maximum freedom and progressively constrains freedom as character forms. Two distinct formative mechanisms operate: participatory transfer (habituation producing conformity — gradual) and wound-as-cleansing (somatic event producing inner purification — acute). The eschatological trajectory (T5.6) is explicit in 2Pe 1:4 — fellowship is oriented toward divine nature participation as its telos.
- `T5.7.3` · session_b · SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY/resolved_qa — T2.8.1 data was silent on constitutional body deposit. T5.7 is formally closed.
- `T5.7.3` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTRA_TIER/pending — T5 establishes forgiveness as a transitional-formative characteristic: it transforms condition (guilt-bearing → guilt-freed) and orientation (toward love-production and reverence-formation) but does not leave a constitutional body deposit (T5.7 closed). The formation sequence is specific (offence → access-posture → forgiveness received → love generated → capacity to extend → horizontal extension) and the eschatological endpoint is identified (Jer 31:34 — permanent new covenant forgiveness as the telos). Two formation mechanisms (declaratory and atoning) operate at different levels but combine in the NT's atonement-grounded declaratory forgiveness. The suffering cost on the forgiver's side (Eph 1:7 — "through his blood"; absorbed wrong in horizontal forgiveness) is structurally necessary. T5.7 closes: forgiveness leaves no constitutional body deposit, which means the formative work is entirely inner-constitutional. The eschatological dimension is strong: Jer 31:34 provides the telos (permanent sins-remembered-no-more) toward which all present forgiveness-acts point.
- `T5.7.3` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER/pending — T2's constitutional movement (guilt-bearing → forgiveness → love → capacity to extend) is T5's formation sequence described at the constitutional level. T2's finding that the constitutional event produces further inner states (love, direct access to God) is T5's transitional-formative account. The body-deposit gap (T2.8 — data silent; T5.7 closed) means the formation is entirely inner-constitutional — the formative event leaves no somatic residue, which is consistent with T5's finding that forgiveness is transitional rather than permanently marking. The eschatological telos (T5.6 — Jer 31:34) is the permanent constitutional state that present forgiveness-events anticipate.
- `T5.7.1` · session_b · THEOLOGICAL_NOTE/resolved_qa — T2.8 (Q&A-059, Q&A-060) identified two deposit mechanisms — circumcision (bodily sign transmitted generationally) and covenantal word-transmission (Isa 59:21 — through the mouth of offspring). The developmental consequences: (1) Circumcision deposit → covenantal community formation: the bodily sign carried across generations produces a covenantal people shaped by the covenant's content and obligations. Each generation born into the circumcision-covenant inherits a covenantal identity that forms their inner-being self-understanding, obligations, and community belonging. The generational deposit of circumcision produces a formed covenant community. (2) Word-transmission deposit → covenantal knowing across generations: Isa 59:21 — "shall not depart out of your mouth, or out of the mouth of your offspring, or out of the mouth of your children's offspring." The new covenant's word-deposit produces a generational chain of covenantal formation through the transmission of God's words — the covenant is taught, confessed, and proclaimed across generations, and this transmission is itself the mechanism of formation. (3) New covenant trajectory: the NT relativises the bodily circumcision deposit (Gal 3:17 context) while retaining and deepening the word-deposit through the Spirit. The developmental consequence shifts from bodily marking to Spirit-enabling of word-transmission.  [anchors: Act 7:8, Isa 59:21, Gal 3:17]
- `T5.7.2` · session_b · THEOLOGICAL_NOTE/resolved_qa — Yes — confirmed in Q&A-059 through Q&A-061 (T2.8) and Q&A-143. Act 3:25 ("in your offspring shall all the families of the earth be blessed"), Act 7:8 (circumcision as generational deposit), Isa 59:21 (covenant words transmitted through generations of offspring's mouths). Generational consequence is one of the most structurally prominent features of the covenant vocabulary — covenant is inherently transgenerational. The blessing and identity conveyed through covenant extend beyond the individual who enters it to their descendants across multiple generations. This is the mechanism through which covenant shapes and forms communities and peoples over historical time.  [anchors: Act 3:25, Act 7:8, Isa 59:21]
- `T5.7.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTRA_TIER/pending — T5 as a whole reveals that covenant is the most developmentally comprehensive of all characteristics in the programme's scope — it operates across all stages of formation (entry, formation, sanctification, eschatological fullness) and all scales of time (individual, generational, covenantal-historical, eschatological). The defining T5 finding is the two-mechanism contrast (Q&A-131): the Sinai covenant uses the external-demand mechanism (discipline and instruction); the new covenant uses the internal-inscription mechanism (Spirit-writing from within). This mechanism shift is not merely methodological — it represents a change in what the covenant does to the inner person. Under the old covenant, the person is required to bring their will to the covenant; under the new, the covenant brings a transformed will to the person. T5 confirms that covenant is not a static reality but a developing one — it moves through a sequence of inner states (Q&A-128: failure → initiative → inscription → belonging → knowing), and its eschatological trajectory points toward a fullness not yet realised but already grounded in the eternal covenant (Heb 13:20).
- `T5.7.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTRA_TIER/pending — T5 reveals that kindness has a rich and multi-layered formative dimension. Three formation mechanisms are evidenced — Spirit-formation (organic, Gal 5:22), inscription (deliberate, Pro 3:3), and encounter with divine kindness (transformative, chesed corpus) — and these are not alternatives but concurrent and mutually reinforcing pathways. The formative arc produces the chasid as its telos: the person whose character is so thoroughly shaped by chesed that it has become their defining identity before God and others. This formation has an eschatological dimension: the chasid is not only preserved through life but eschatologically — Psa 16:10 ("you will not abandon my soul to Sheol") and Eph 2:7 ("in the coming ages he might show the immeasurable riches of his grace in kindness") establish that the formation kindness produces has a future that exceeds the present. Suffering does not impair kindness formation — it may deepen and reveal it (Lam 3:22 in maximum desolation; Gen 44:29/32 as sacrificial solidarity bearing suffering on behalf of another). T5's most significant intra-tier finding: the body-deposit question closes (T5.7 N — no deposit) but this does not diminish kindness's formative depth — the covenantal-formative transmission across generations (Exo 20:6 — "to a thousand generations") is more extensive than biological transmission would be, reaching all who love God and keep his commandments regardless of biological lineage.
- `T5.7.2` · session_b · OBSERVATION/resolved_qa — The evidence does indicate generational consequence but specifies it as covenantal-formative, not somatic. Exo 20:6 — "showing steadfast love to a thousand generations of those who love me and keep my commandments" [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-089] — names a thousand-generation reach, but the mechanism is covenantal faithfulness (those who love God), not biological descent. The generational deposit is not in the body but in the covenant relationship that each generation enters through its own volitional orientation. The chasidim (H2623 — the plural godly/kind persons) across the Psalms suggest a community of kindness-shaped character that persists across generations through formation and witness, not biological transmission [XREG: H2623, OBS-099-094]. Conclusion: generational consequence is evidenced in the covenantal-formative mode; body deposit as carrier of that consequence is not evidenced (see Q&A-098 through Q&A-100).  [anchors: Exo 20:6 · Psa 149:1]
- `T5.7.3` · session_b · OBSERVATION/resolved_qa — T2.8 found no constitutional body deposit for kindness (Q&A-098 through Q&A-100). Explicitly noting: T5.7 is closed on that basis. The generational consequence evidenced (Q&A-116) operates through covenantal-formative means, not through a body deposit. All three T5.7 prompts are now dispositioned: T5.7.1 (addressed in v1 as Q&A-077, N — formal closure); T5.7.2 (Q&A-116 above — covenantal generational consequence evidenced, body deposit not evidenced); T5.7.3 (this entry — formal close confirmed).  [anchors: —]

**T6.6 · Shared Verse Anchors**  (62)

- `T6.6.1` · session_b · OBSERVATION/pending — Yes — Section 6 of R064 lists the shared anchor verses explicitly:  \| Anchor Verse \| Also anchored in \| \|---\|---\| \| Lev 4:20 \| mercy (R111) \| \| Jer 31:34 \| guilt (R073); thought (R160); love (R103) \| \| Mat 5:24 \| reconciliation (R130) \| \| Mar 3:29 \| sin (R147) \|  Four anchor verses are shared across seven other registries. Jer 31:34 is the most multiply-shared anchor — appearing in four registries (forgiveness, guilt, thought/knowledge, love).
- `T6.6.2` · session_b · OBSERVATION/pending — Each shared anchor reveals a specific structural relationship:  **Lev 4:20 — shared with mercy (R111):** The atonement-forgiveness verse is simultaneously a mercy verse. The shared anchor reveals that the OT covenantal forgiveness act is inseparable from the mercy-expression — the priestly mediation that produces forgiveness is an expression of divine mercy. The two characteristics converge at the point of covenantal atonement.  **Jer 31:34 — shared with guilt (R073), thought/knowledge (R160), love (R103):** OBS-064-012 names this as "a structural cluster: the new covenant promise integrates the forgiveness-act with a cognitive transformation (no more remembering) and connects to love and guilt." The four-registry convergence on a single verse reveals: the new covenant is the structural integration point of forgiveness, guilt (what is resolved), thought/knowledge (the new cognitive access enabled), and love (the covenantal disposition that frames it). This is the most structurally significant shared anchor in the programme for this characteristic.  **Mat 5:24 — shared with reconciliation (R130):** OBS-064-028 establishes the structural relationship: forgiveness (the leaving-act) is the prerequisite and enabling condition for reconciliation. The shared anchor confirms forgiveness and reconciliation as sequentially related — forgiveness enables what reconciliation completes.  **Mar 3:29 — shared with sin (R147):** The outer limit of forgiveness is simultaneously a statement about the most extreme form of sin. The shared anchor reveals that sin and forgiveness are bounded by the same extreme — the condition that forecloses forgiveness is the condition that defines the most severe sin. The two registries share their definitional outer limit.
- `T6.6.1` · session_b · OBSERVATION/pending — Yes. Section 6 of the data: Pro 31:30 (shared with praise/R121); Zec 12:10 (shared with mourning/R113); Rom 5:2 (shared with hope/R78); 2Cor 12:9 (shared with pride/R123); Eph 2:8 (shared with debauchery/R39 and yielding/R180); Eph 4:32 (shared with heart/R183 and kindness/R99). Six shared anchor verses across seven other characteristics.  *Consistent with prior analysis.*
- `T6.6.2` · session_b · OBSERVATION/pending — Each shared anchor encodes a structural relationship: Zec 12:10 + mourning: the grace-mourning sequence (SD038) — deepest grace-response is grief. Rom 5:2 + hope: grace and hope co-constituting the same inner standing (SD005). 2Cor 12:9 + pride: grace-in-weakness as structural antithesis of pride (SD004). Eph 2:8 + yielding: surrender of self-contribution as condition of grace-reception (SD033). Eph 4:32 + kindness: grace → tenderheartedness → kindness as causal sequence (SD030). The shared anchors are the most explicit structural relationship data in the programme.  *Consistent with prior analysis (Section 6). Adds T6 organisation.*
- `T6.6.1` · session_b · OBSERVATION/routed_cluster — Yes — the shared anchor verse table (Section 6 of the data package) is definitive and is used directly:  \| Reference \| Also anchored in \| \|---\|---\| \| Exo 33:19 \| love (R103) \| \| Exo 34:6 \| anger (R4); patience (R116); love (R103); faith (R59); calling (R19) \| \| Num 6:25 \| mercy (R111) \| \| Psa 4:1 \| anguish (R5) \| \| Psa 89:14 \| justice (R98) \| \| Lam 3:22 \| love (R103); longing (R102) \| \| Hos 6:6 \| love (R103); knowledge (R100) \| \| Hos 11:8 \| faithfulness (R60); perverseness (R120); sorrow (R151) \| \| Mic 6:8 \| love (R103); goodness (R67); kindness (R99); will (R173); humility (R80); condemnation (R24) \| \| Mat 9:36 \| distress (R51); listen (R213) \| \| Rom 9:15 \| mercy (R111) \| \| Jam 5:11 \| mercy (R111) \| \| 1Pe 3:8 \| love (R103); mind (R112) \| \| Rev 3:17 \| vulnerability (R206) \|  **Total: 14 shared anchor verses across 16 distinct registries.**
- `T6.6.2` · session_b · OBSERVATION/routed_cluster — Several analytically significant shared-anchor relationships emerge:  **Exo 34:6 (shared with anger R4, patience R116, love R103, faith R59, calling R19):** This is the programme's most anchored single verse. Its five-registry membership confirms SD-017's observation that Exod 34:6 functions as a unified theological statement about God's inner character that multiple registries access from different angles. The shared anchoring reveals that compassion, anger, patience, love, faith, and calling are not independent characteristics but aspects of a single divine inner-being reality disclosed in this formula. Session D must decide whether to treat Exod 34:6 as a cluster-level synthesis anchor rather than as a property of individual registries.  **Hos 11:8 (shared with faithfulness R60, perverseness R120, sorrow R151):** The shared anchoring with faithfulness (R60) reveals that the divine inner deliberation in Hos 11:8 is not only about compassion — it is also a statement about God's faithfulness to covenant despite Israel's perverseness. The triple anchoring shows that compassion, faithfulness, perverseness, and sorrow are simultaneously present in a single divine inner-being moment. This is the richest anchor verse for Session D cluster synthesis.  **Mic 6:8 (shared with love R103, goodness R67, kindness R99, will R173, humility R80, condemnation R24):** Six registries share this anchor — the most clustered single verse after Exod 34:6. The verse integrates justice, chesed (compassion/kindness), and humility as three co-equal requirements. The shared anchoring reveals that compassion (chesed) is constitutively linked to justice and humility in the programme's moral anthropology — it cannot be understood in isolation from either.  **Mat 9:36 (shared with distress R51, listen R213):** The Synoptic splanchnizō anchor is shared with distress and listening. The connection to listening (R213 — a recently added registry) is particularly notable: Jesus sees the crowds and is moved — the compassion-movement follows perceiving-and-hearing. Compassion and listening may be constitutively linked in a way the programme has not yet fully explored.  **Rev 3:17 (shared with vulnerability R206):** The shared anchoring with vulnerability confirms the structural connection between compassion and vulnerability identified at T1.7: the pitiable condition (eleeinos) is also a condition of vulnerability. Compassion and vulnerability share the same anchor because they are constitutively related — compassion is most directly addressed to the condition of vulnerability.
- `T6.6.1` · session_b · OBSERVATION/pending — Yes. The shared anchor table (Section 6 of the data package) identifies six anchor verses also anchored in other registries:  \| Anchor Verse \| Also anchored in \| \|---\|---\| \| Isa 47:9 \| strength/R187; sorcery/R150 \| \| Isa 53:5 \| sorrow/R151; contrition/R030 \| \| Jer 13:23 \| gladness/R186; power/R196; perverseness/R120 \| \| Hos 4:17 \| distress/R051 \| \| Phili 3:10 \| likeness/R209; deadness/R210 \| \| 2Pe 1:4 \| yielding/R180 \|
- `T6.6.2` · session_b · OBSERVATION/pending — **Isa 53:5 (fellowship via wound) shared with sorrow/R151 and contrition/R030:** This shared anchor reveals that the redemptive wound-fellowship operates in the same verse space as sorrow and contrition. The verse names multiple inner-being realities simultaneously: the servant's bearing of transgressions, the peace produced, the healing from wounds. Fellowship-through-wounding is co-present with sorrow (the emotional register of the wound) and contrition (the inner-being response to guilt the wound addresses). SP-062-002 captures this.  **Phili 3:10 (fellowship with Christ's sufferings) shared with likeness/R209 and deadness/R210:** This is analytically significant. Likeness and deadness are the two characteristics with which Phili 3:10's fellowship-with-sufferings is anchored. The verse names *becoming like him in his death* — the fellowship vocabulary (sharing) and the likeness vocabulary (becoming like) operate together. This shared anchor reveals that fellowship-through-suffering is structurally linked to the likeness/image-conformity register and the death/deadness register. SP-062-008 partially captures this.  **2Pe 1:4 (divine nature fellowship) shared with yielding/R180:** Divine nature participation (the highest fellowship register) co-anchors with yielding. This may reveal that the inner disposition of yielding/surrender is the inner-being posture that correlates with receiving divine nature fellowship. SP-062-008 notes the connection to transformation cluster.  **New finding:** The shared anchor pattern reveals that fellowship's most theologically dense verses (Isa 53:5, Phili 3:10, 2Pe 1:4) each anchor multiple other characteristics simultaneously. These verses are not merely fellowship-evidence but multi-characteristic junctions — places where fellowship, suffering, transformation, likeness, and hope converge. This makes them Session D priority sites for synthesis.
- `T6.6.1` · session_b · THEOLOGICAL_NOTE/resolved_qa — Yes — 48 shared anchor verses identified (§6, OBS-103-050). Most significantly: Exo 34:6 (5 registries: anger, patience, faith, compassion, calling), Gal 5:22 (5 registries: patience, joy, goodness, peace, faith), 1Cor 13:4 (3 registries: patience, pride, kindness), Mic 6:8 (6 registries: goodness, kindness, will, humility, compassion, condemnation), Jer 31:34 (3 registries: guilt, thought, forgiveness). The 48-anchor shared map is the richest in the programme — love's evidential base is the most cross-registered in the corpus.  [anchors: Exo 34:6, Gal 5:22, 1Cor 13:4, Mic 6:8]
- `T6.6.2` · session_b · THEOLOGICAL_NOTE/resolved_qa — The shared anchor map reveals that love is the semantic core around which the programme's most structurally significant verses cluster. Exo 34:6 (shared with 5 registries) is the OT's most cross-registry verse — it names love/chesed as the centre of a five-attribute divine portrait. Gal 5:22 (shared with 5 registries) places love as the head of the Spirit-fruit list and all subsequent fruits as co-anchors. These are not random co-occurrences — they are structurally chosen: the programme anchored the most important characteristic-verses at love because love is the conceptual centre from which the adjacent characteristics derive their meaning. The shared anchor pattern confirms love's generative position (Q&A-105): love is what the surrounding characteristics are expressions of, not merely what appears alongside them.  [anchors: Exo 34:6, Gal 5:22]
- `T6.6.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTRA_TIER/routed_cluster — Love's structural position in the programme is that of the central node in the inner-being vocabulary's relational architecture. Co-occurrence data: 196 with compassion, 105 with strength, 68 with peace, 62 with faith — confirming love's connections to the full range of C17 characteristics and extending to C20 (strength/authority). Shared anchor data: 48 verses shared across multiple registries, with Exo 34:6 (5 registries) and Gal 5:22 (5 registries) as the highest-density shared anchors — confirming love as the vocabulary cluster around which the programme's most significant cross-registry verses gather. Sequential position: love stands downstream of divine action (received) and upstream of the ethical-moral life (generative of commandment-keeping, Spirit-fruits, community identity). Causal productivity: love generates joy, rest, grief, obedience, praise — the programme's most causally productive characteristic. Vocabulary sharing: term sharing ratio 0.701, the highest in C17 — love shares vocabulary with compassion, mercy, kindness, goodness, desire, and peace. The primary gap: distinctions from adjacent characteristics (compassion, mercy, kindness) remain partially drawn; Session D is the resolution context.

- `T6.6.2` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER/routed_cluster — Love's definition as the programme's most semantically comprehensive inner-being characteristic is confirmed and deepened by the structural relationship analysis. The term sharing ratio (0.701 — highest in C17) and the co-occurrence breadth (196 with compassion through to 29 with mercy) reflect the definition's conclusion: love is not a bounded standalone characteristic but the semantic centre of a cluster. The sequential finding (love as mediating-and-generative node — Q&A-105) maps onto the definition's tripartite structure: love is downstream (condition-received from God), central (disposition that organises), and upstream (act that generates all subsequent ethical life). The definition's vocabulary arc (from divine being to love of darkness — Q&A-131) is confirmed by the structural relationship data: the vocabulary extends into every adjacent registry (through XREF sharing, co-occurrence, and shared anchors), touching every corner of the programme. The structural analysis thus confirms the definitional claim: love is not merely a characteristic among others but the organising principle of the inner-being vocabulary.
- `T6.6.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER/routed_cluster — The three relational axes in T4 generate the structural relationship pattern in T6. The GOD→HUMAN axis explains love's highest co-occurrences with the programme's theological vocabulary: calling (R019 — 51 shared, identity-as-beloved and identity-as-called), faith (R059 — 62 shared, the inner condition for receiving divine love), and compassion (R023 — 196 shared, the compassionate-responsive mode of divine love). The HUMAN→HUMAN axis explains the co-occurrence with peace (R117 — 68 shared, peacemaking as love's horizontal expression) and community-related vocabulary. The sequential structure (divine love prior → human love responsive → ethical life) explains love's mediating position (Q&A-105): it stands between the GOD→HUMAN axis and the HUMAN→HUMAN axis as the transmission point. Love received from God becomes love given to neighbour — the sequential position is the relational interface described structurally. The 48 shared anchor verses are the evidential sites where these relational intersections are most visible: Exo 34:6 (GOD→HUMAN axis at maximum density), Gal 5:22 (HUMAN→HUMAN axis generated by Spirit), Joh 13:34 (HUMAN→HUMAN axis constituted by GOD→HUMAN love as model).
- `T6.6.2` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER/routed_cluster — The three relational axes in T4 generate the structural relationship pattern in T6. - `T6.6.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER/routed_cluster — The structural relationship data and the evidential foundation are the most mutually confirming pair in the synthesis matrix. The co-occurrence breadth (T6.1) is directly explained by the vocabulary breadth (T7.1.10 — the widest semantic arc in the programme): a vocabulary that spans from divine being to love of darkness will co-occur with every other inner-being characteristic because it occupies every register of the inner-being vocabulary. The shared anchor density (48 verses — T6.6) is explained by the primary anchor's function (Lam 3:22 — T7.2.5/T7.2.6): the programme anchors the most significant cross-registry verses at love because love is the definitional centre from which adjacent characteristics derive their meaning. The vocabulary sharing ratio (0.701 — T6.4) is explained by the root-level connections (T7.1.2): the racham root connects love, compassion, and mercy; the ahav root connects love and desire; the tov root connects love and goodness. The structural relationship data is, at its core, the observable programme-level effect of the lexical architecture that the evidential foundation analysis reveals. Structural breadth and lexical breadth are the same reality at different levels of analysis.
- `T6.6.2` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER/routed_cluster — The structural relationship data and the evidential foundation are the most mutually confirming pair in the synthesis matrix.
