- `T6.6.1` · session_b · CROSS_REGISTRY/resolved_qa — Yes — the shared anchor table (§6, OBS-111-025) lists 16 mercy anchor verses shared with other registries: Lev 4:20 (forgiveness/R64), Lev 17:11 (flesh/R185), Lev 23:27 (Soul/R182), Num 6:25 (compassion/R23), 1Sa 12:3 (innocence/R90; suffering/R214), Job 2:3 (integrity/R92), Job 33:24 (corruption/R31), Psa 78:38 (love/R103), Dan 2:18 (kindness/R99; joy/R97), Dan 6:11 (desire/R43), Luk 1:78 (heart/R183), Rom 1:31 (covenant/R34), 2Cor 1:3 (comfort/R192), Col 3:12 (patience/R116; heart/R183), 2Ti 3:2 (love/R103; ambition/R3; pride/R123), Heb 8:12 (mind/R112), Jam 3:17 (peace/R117; gentleness/R66; purity/R125; doubt/R191), Jam 5:11 (compassion/R23). OBS-111-025.  [anchors: See list above]
- `T6.6.2` · session_b · CROSS_REGISTRY/resolved_qa — The shared anchor pattern reveals mercy's centrality to multiple analytical domains simultaneously: (1) Mercy and the atonement-forgiveness nexus (Lev 4:20 with R64 — they share the same atoning event). (2) Mercy and the bodily-somatic domain (Lev 17:11 with R185 flesh; Lev 23:27 with R182 Soul). (3) Mercy and the character-formation complex (Col 3:12 with R116 patience, R183 heart). (4) Mercy and divine attributes (Psa 78:38 with R103 love; 2Cor 1:3 with R192 comfort). (5) Mercy in the vice-virtue catalogues (Rom 1:31 with R34 covenant; 2Ti 3:2 with R103 love, R3 ambition, R123 pride). *New finding: the breadth and variety of mercy's shared anchors — spanning atonement, somatic, character, divine-attribute, and vice-virtue domains — confirms mercy as a structurally integrative characteristic that appears at the intersection of multiple analytical domains.* OBS-111-025.  [anchors: See §6 table]
- `T6.6.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTRA_TIER/pending — Reading T6 as a whole reveals mercy as structurally integrative — its cross-registry position is not peripheral but central. The T6 synthesis: (a) co-occurrence positions mercy as a hub between the guilt-facing pole (R073 guilt at 75) and the grace-giving pole (R023 compassion at 76) and the power cluster (R187, R196, R197, R198); (b) mercy appears consistently in the middle of inner-being sequences as the bridge from negative condition to positive outcome (Q&A-149); (c) mercy is produced by love and compassion and produces forgiveness, joy, shame, identity, comfort; (d) mercy has constituent status within wisdom (Jam 3:17) and has constituent elements within itself (atonement as mechanism, compassion as motivation, holiness as relational frame); (e) the vocabulary root architecture is theologically coherent (Q&A-157), each root family tracing the full arc of the mercy-event; (f) 16 shared anchor verses confirm mercy's appearance at the intersection of multiple analytical domains (Q&A-165); (g) boundaries with compassion and lovingkindness are partially drawn but require Session D. The T6 synthesis: mercy functions as the structural integrator of the inner-being relational economy — it is the characteristic through which negative conditions are bridged to positive states across the widest range of cross-registry connections in C17.
- `T6.6.2` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTRA_TIER/pending — Reading T6 as a whole reveals mercy as structurally integrative — its cross-registry position is not peripheral but central. The T6 synthesis: (a) co-occurrence positions mercy as a hub between the guilt-facing pole (R073 guilt at 75) and the grace-giving pole (R023 compassion at 76) and the power cluster (R187, R196, R197, R198); (b) mercy appears consistently in the middle of inner-being sequences as the bridge from negative condition to positive outcome (Q&A-149); (c) mercy is produced by love and compassion and produces forgiveness, joy, shame, identity, comfort; (d) mercy has constituent status within wisdom (Jam 3:17) and has constituent elements within itself (atonement as mechanism, compassion as motivation, holiness as relational frame); (e) the vocabulary root architecture is theologically coherent (Q&A-157), each root family tracing the full arc of the mercy-event; (f) 16 shared anchor verses confirm mercy's appearance at the intersection of multiple analytical domains (Q&A-165); (g) boundaries with compassion and lovingkindness are partially drawn but require Session D. The T6 synthesis: mercy functions as the structural integrator of the inner-being relational economy — it is the characteristic through which negative conditions are bridged to positive states across the widest range of cross-registry connections in C17.
- `T6.6.2` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER/pending — T6's finding that mercy is a structurally integrative hub characteristic (Q&A-165) and T7's finding that mercy's vocabulary arc is a theological narrative (Q&A-180) are convergent findings at different analytical levels. T6 shows the cross-registry structural integration; T7 shows the lexical-semantic integration. Reading together: mercy's structural integration (T6 — it appears at the intersection of guilt/guilt-resolution, power/compassion, formation/wisdom domains) is lexically grounded in vocabulary breadth and root-level coherence (T7 — root families trace the full mercy arc). T6's term-sharing ratio (0.708) is the quantitative expression of T7's vocabulary arc comprehensiveness. The two tiers together confirm that mercy is both lexically and structurally the most integrative characteristic in C17 — its vocabulary embraces the full semantic arc of the mercy event, and its structural position in the inner-being landscape bridges the widest range of adjacent characteristics.
- `T6.6.1` · session_b · CROSS_REGISTRY/resolved_qa — Isa 53:5 — shared with R30 contrition, R151 sorrow. 2Pe 1:4 — shared with R180 yielding. Phili 3:10 — shared with R209 likeness, R210 deadness. Mal 2:14 — shared with R34 covenant. 1Jo 1:3 — unique to R062. OBS-062-032, OBS-062-033, OBS-062-054.  [anchors: Isa 53:5, 2Pe 1:4, Phili 3:10, Mal 2:14]
- `T6.6.2` · session_b · CROSS_REGISTRY/resolved_qa — 2Pe 1:4 shared with R180 yielding reveals that divine nature participation requires an inner act of yielding (escape from corruption) as its prerequisite — fellowship and yielding are structurally adjacent at the point of ontological transformation. Phili 3:10 shared with R209 and R210 reveals that fellowship-through-suffering is simultaneously a conformity event (becoming like Christ in death) — fellowship is the relational mode; conformity is the constitutional outcome. OBS-062-033, OBS-062-055.
- `T6.6.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTRA_TIER/pending — T6 positions fellowship as a structurally central hub characteristic — it connects desire (R43 — strongest co-occurrence signal), unity (R167 — corporate outcome), spirit (R184 — mediating agent), comfort (R192 — affective product of shared suffering), contrition and sorrow (R30, R151 — shared anchor Isa 53:5), yielding (R180 — shared anchor 2Pe 1:4), covenant (R34 — shared anchor Mal 2:14). Fellowship is structurally upstream of unity (fellowship is the participatory inner reality; unity is its corporate structural expression) and downstream of desire (desire directs fellowship). The vocabulary analysis (T6.4) reveals the root-level connection between wound and companionship (both from chavar) as the deepest structural finding: fellowship and suffering-wounding share the same etymological root, revealing that the deepest fellowship is structurally connected to the deepest wounding.
- `T6.6.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER/pending — T4's relational directions and T6's structural connections are expressing the same reality from two perspectives. The God-to-human direction (T4.1 — divine initiative) corresponds to the R180 yielding connection (T6 — shared anchor 2Pe 1:4): the divine initiative at 2Pe 1:4 requires an inner human yielding (escape from corruption) as its receiving condition. The human-to-human giving direction (T4.3 — companionship, covenant, comfort-sharing) corresponds to the R34 covenant, R192 comfort, and R43 desire connections in T6. The relational directions (T4) and the structural cross-registry connections (T6) are the inner-being and cross-registry expressions of the same relational movements.
- `T6.6.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER/pending — T6's structural connections and T7's vocabulary analysis are mutually grounding. The wound-companionship shared root (T6.4 — H2250 wound and H2270 companion both from chavar) is T7's single most significant etymology finding: the same root that produces companionship produces wound, revealing that the deepest fellowship is structurally wound-rooted. T6's primary anchor verses (Isa 53:5, 2Pe 1:4, 1Jo 1:3, Mal 2:14 as cross-registry hubs) are also T7's most analytically rich verses — the verses that carry the most structural connections are the same verses that are most exegetically developed in the primary anchor analysis.
- `T6.6.1` · session_b · CROSS_REGISTRY/resolved_qa — Jer 31:34 — shared with R073 guilt, R103 love, R160 thought. Mat 5:24 — shared with R130 reconciliation. Mar 3:29 — shared with R147 sin. Lev 4:20 — shared with R111 mercy. OBS-064-012.
- `T6.6.2` · session_b · CROSS_REGISTRY/resolved_qa — Jer 31:34's triple sharing (guilt, love, thought) reveals it as the highest-convergence verse in the forgiveness corpus — the new covenant promise joins forgiveness, guilt-resolution, love-formation, and cognitive-relational access (they shall know me) in a single text. This convergence is analytically significant for Session D synthesis. OBS-064-012.  [anchors: Jer 31:34]
- `T6.6.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTRA_TIER/pending — T6 reveals forgiveness as a structural hub in the C17 and broader programme network. The guilt-073 connection (51 shared verses — dominant) positions forgiveness as the definitional counterpart and remedy to guilt. The mercy-111 connection (15 shared) positions mercy as the generating disposition for forgiving. The love-103 connection (8 shared) confirms the downstream love-production as a recurring structural signal. Jer 31:34 as the highest-convergence anchor (shared with guilt, love, thought) is analytically significant for Session D. The lexical analysis (T6.4) reveals salach's lexical isolation — no root-sharing — which structurally preserves the divine-exclusive domain of forgiveness at its deepest Hebrew root. The distinctions analysis positions: guilt as the condition forgiveness addresses (kind distinction), mercy as the disposition that generates forgiving (layer distinction), reconciliation as the restored state that forgiveness makes possible (sequence distinction). T6 synthesis: forgiveness is a structural hub whose connections are definitionally structured — every major co-occurrence corresponds to a logical role in the forgiveness transaction (guilt = prior condition; mercy = divine disposition; repentance = access-posture; love = downstream product; faith = receiving instrument; reconciliation = restored outcome).
- `T6.6.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER/pending — T2's cognitive-locus finding (Jer 31:34 — forgiveness engages and enables cognition) explains the R160 thought co-occurrence at Jer 31:34. T2's somatic-compassion locus (splagchnizomai — Mat 18:27) explains the R023 compassion co-occurrence (4 shared verses; DIM-064-SD002 on splagchnizomai as somatic precondition). T2's heart-locus requirement (Mat 18:35) explains the R073 guilt co-occurrence at the heart level — guilt is the heart-level prior condition that forgiveness addresses at the heart level. Constitutional location and co-occurrence signals are mutually confirming: every major constitutional locus of forgiveness corresponds to a co-occurrence registry that operates in the same constitutional space.
- `T6.6.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER/pending — T4's relational directions correspond to T6's structural connections. The God-to-human direction (T4.1) corresponds to the mercy-111 connection (mercy as the divine disposition generating the act, operating in the same direction). The human-to-God direction (T4.2) corresponds to the repentance-135 and faith-059 connections (the access-postures that operate in the seeking direction). The horizontal giving-direction (T4.3) corresponds to the love-103 connection (love as the upstream motivator of horizontal forgiveness — Eph 4:32) and the reconciliation-130 connection (Mat 5:24 — reconciliation as the restored state that horizontal forgiveness produces). T4's relational architecture and T6's co-occurrence network are different analytical lenses on the same structural reality.
- `T6.6.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER/pending — T6's structural connections and T7's vocabulary analysis confirm and explain each other. The salach root's lexical isolation (T6.4 — no root-sharing with other programme registries; T7.1.1 — no XREF terms) reveals that forgiveness maintains a lexically distinct divine-exclusive domain — the strongest co-occurrence registry (guilt-073 at 51 shared verses) operates in the same semantic territory but at the verse level, not at the root-sharing level. This means the structural partnership between forgiveness and guilt is relational-definitional, not lexical-etymological. The Jer 31:34 multi-registry convergence (T6.6 — shared with guilt, love, thought) is also T7's most analytically rich verse (T7.2.5 — the primary anchor trio includes Jer 31:34): the verse that carries the most structural connections is the verse that is exegetically richest, confirmed from both directions.
- `T6.6.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTRA_TIER/routed_cluster — T6 reveals compassion as the structural centre of the C17 cluster rather than a peripheral member. The love co-occurrence (196/351 = 55.8% of all OWNER verses) is uniquely dominant — compassion and love co-occur in more than half of all active verses, raising the programme-level question whether they are truly separable at the data level. Three structural observations from the co-occurrence landscape: (1) compassion sits at C17's centre of gravity (love, mercy, kindness as top three co-occurrences — all within-cluster); (2) compassion generates a human-response cluster (peace, faith, will, surrender, seeking co-occurring at significant levels — the pattern of divine compassion generating human inner-being responses); (3) compassion is constitutively responsive to human distress (despair, guilt, distress, evil all present in the co-occurrence table — compassion operates most characteristically in the presence of need). The CHASAD root crossing compassion (C17) and shame (C06) is the programme's most structurally unexpected finding — a root family that bridges two clusters. Exod 34:6 as the programme's most multiply-anchored verse (6 registries) must be treated as a cluster-level synthesis anchor. The compassion/mercy boundary (SD-009) is the most critical unresolved distinction. T6.7 (dimensional sharing) is programme-stage limited — formally deferred.
- `T6.6.2` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTRA_TIER/routed_cluster — T6 reveals compassion as the structural centre of the C17 cluster rather than a peripheral member. - `T6.6.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER/routed_cluster — T6's structural connections and T7's vocabulary analysis are mutually confirming at the most analytically significant points. The CHASAD root crossing two clusters (T6.4 — most unexpected structural finding) is confirmed and extended by T7's vocabulary analysis: the CHASAD root includes shame as the negative semantic pole embedded within the root itself (T7.1 P5), not as an external antonym. This means the cluster-crossing T6 identifies is not an external structural anomaly but an internal semantic feature: the compassion vocabulary contains its own opposite at the root level. Exod 34:6 as the programme's most multiply-anchored verse (T6.6 — 6 registries) is also T7's most evidentially rich single text — the verse that carries the most structural connections is the same verse that is most analytically central to the vocabulary analysis. The love co-occurrence dominance (T6.1 — 196/351 verses) is confirmed by T7's vocabulary arc: the vocabulary's fullest range (womb to almsgiving) overlaps extensively with the love vocabulary's range, which is why they co-occur across more than half of all active verses.
- `T6.6.2` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER/routed_cluster — T6's structural connections and T7's vocabulary analysis are mutually confirming at the most analytically significant points. The CHASAD root crossing two clusters (T6.4 — most unexpected structural finding) is confirmed and extended by T7's vocabulary analysis: the CHASAD root includes shame as the negative semantic pole embedded within the root itself (T7.1 P5), not as an external antonym. - `T6.6.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTRA_TIER/pending — T6 produces the most structurally significant finding in the review set for any single tier: grace is not produced by any other inner-being characteristic. It is the originating point of a causal chain, not its product. This categorically distinguishes grace from every other characteristic reviewed — all others can be produced by at least one other characteristic; grace cannot. This structural uniqueness is confirmed by T2.9's origin analysis (grace originates with God — it does not originate in the human constitutional system) and is analytically consistent across T6.2 (seeking is the posture before grace arrives, not its producer), T6.3 (S-rating on "is grace produced by another characteristic"), and T6.4 (the vocabulary's structural completeness without a generative human-side term). The co-occurrence pattern reveals grace at the intersection of divine initiative and creaturely need: seeking (R140 — 40 verses, dominant) as the creature's pre-grace posture; moral failure cluster (evil, guilt, sin, transgression) as the creaturely context; power cluster (strength, might) confirming the 2Cor 12:9 connection. The love co-occurrence (R103 — 14 verses) is the primary within-cluster signal raising the grace-love boundary question (SD013). All major boundary distinctions (grace-love, grace-mercy, grace-justice, grace-chesed) remain open at T6.5 — the most unresolved boundary cluster in the review set. T6.7 (dimensional sharing) is programme-stage limited.
- `T6.6.2` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTRA_TIER/pending — T6 produces the most structurally significant finding in the review set for any single tier: grace is not produced by any other inner-being characteristic. - `T6.6.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER/pending — T6's structural uniqueness (grace is not produced by any inner-being characteristic — categorically the originating point of the causal chain) and T7's vocabulary structural completeness (every dimension of grace's operation has a corresponding term, including the structural negative acharistos) confirm and explain each other. T6 establishes that grace stands outside the characteristic-production network; T7 establishes that the vocabulary encodes this structural position with unique completeness — including the negative (acharistos) and the NT coinage (charitoō) as markers of grace's conceptual uniqueness. The shared anchor verses (T6.6) are also T7's most evidentially rich texts: Eph 2:8 (which anchors multiple co-occurring registries) carries the most complete single-verse expression of grace's structural position (sovereign gift, not of works, producing faith). T6 and T7 mutually confirm that grace's structural uniqueness in the programme landscape corresponds to a lexical uniqueness in the vocabulary — both point to the same underlying analytical claim: grace is categorically distinct from every other inner-being characteristic.

- `T6.6.2` · session_b · CROSS_REGISTRY/resolved_qa — Three most analytically significant shared anchors: (1) Heb 8:10 shared with R001 (abomination) and R112 (mind): the new covenant's constitutional claim (law written on mind and heart) is an anchor for both the abomination and mind registries — revealing that the new covenant's inner inscription touches the cognitive inner-being territory that both registries engage. The fact that this verse anchors the abomination registry is unexpected and suggests that the abomination registry's engagement with this verse may be through the contrast between the abomination of old-covenant violation and the new-covenant remedy. Requires Session D investigation (SP-034-001 direction). (2) Eph 2:12 shared with R078 (hope) and R197 (authority): covenant and hope share this anchor, revealing that covenantal standing is the ground of hope (OBS-034-060) — and that authority-structures also anchor on this verse, suggesting that covenant intersects with authority in the domain of inclusion/exclusion from divine authority-structures. (3) Rom 1:31 shared with R111 (mercy): the covenantal defect (asunthetos) and mercy share an anchor — suggesting that the mercy registry engages the covenant-breaking context of Rom 1:31 as a site where mercy is absent, and that the covenant-mercy relationship requires cross-registry synthesis (SP-034-005).  [anchors: Heb 8:10, Eph 2:12, Rom 1:31]
- `T6.6.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTRA_TIER/pending — T6 as a whole confirms the DIM-34-SD001 hypothesis: covenant is structurally prior to and constitutive of the other C17 characteristics. The sequential and causal findings (Q&A-149, Q&A-150) show that covenant precedes and produces love/hesed (as the structure that makes sustained hesed possible), hope (as the ground of inner hope), knowing God (as the telos of covenant inscription), and the new self (as the constitutional outcome of new covenant reception). The vocabulary and root sharing (Q&A-157, Q&A-158) reveals that covenant and faithfulness are lexically co-generated — they share the same root and are constitutively paired. The co-occurrence data (Q&A-147) shows covenant occupying a central hub position in the inner-being landscape — co-present with the widest range of characteristics across multiple dimensions. T6 as a whole establishes that covenant is not one characteristic in a relational cluster but the architectural backbone of the cluster — the structural condition of possibility for the other C17 characteristics.

- `T6.6.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTRA_TIER/pending — T6 reveals that kindness sits at the structural centre of a dense network of related characteristics rather than on the edge. Its most consistent co-occurrences (faithfulness, justice, compassion, forgiveness, humility, patience) are not merely adjacent virtues but appear to be partially constituent of kindness itself — the Mic 6:8 triad (justice/kindness/humility) and the Eph 4:32 cluster (kindness/tenderheartedness/forgiveness) function as composite wholes where each element reinforces and partly defines the others. The causal network is explicit and directional: divine kindness → praise + repentance in the recipient → reproduced human kindness toward others → sustained covenant relationships. This causal chain means kindness is not only a characteristic that one possesses but one that is structurally self-propagating through its own operation. The semantic-root finding is analytically significant: the CHESED root family spans the noun (chesed — kindness), the verb (chasad — be kind), the person (chasid — the kind one), the negative (shame/reproach for its failure), and a proper name (Chesed) — a root family that covers definition, action, identity, consequence, and proper-noun instantiation within a single family. T6's most significant intra-tier finding: Mic 6:8 anchors eight registries simultaneously, which confirms that kindness is not a standalone quality but a node in a structural web of C17 cluster characteristics — its full meaning requires these other registries to be seen.
- `T6.6.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER/pending — T1's finding that kindness is composite — constituted by faithfulness, justice, compassion, forgiveness — is confirmed and extended by T6's co-occurrence data. The T6 finding that kindness consistently co-occurs with faithfulness (Reg 60), justice (Reg 98), love (Reg 103), and compassion (Reg 23) is not merely a co-occurrence pattern but reflects the T1 structural composition: these co-occurring characteristics are partially internal to kindness itself, not merely adjacent to it. This has a significant implication: kindness cannot be fully studied within a single registry — it requires the cluster analysis that Session D will provide. The T6 causal network (divine kindness → praise + repentance → reproduced human kindness) is the T1 relational mechanism made explicit at the T6 structural level. The T1 boundary finding (kindness is not love, not compassion, not grace — Q&A-021) is where T6's distinction analysis (Q&A-082) begins and cannot resolve without Session D cross-registry work. The two tiers together reveal the tension: kindness is internally composite in ways that make its boundary with adjacent characteristics difficult to draw precisely.
- `T6.6.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER/pending — T2's heart-location finding and T6's structural relationship findings together reveal something about kindness that neither tier alone states: the heart is not only the constitutional site of kindness but the site where kindness's constituent adjacent characteristics (faithfulness, justice, compassion, forgiveness) are also inscribed. Pro 3:3 — "write them on the tablet of your heart" — names the heart as the inscription site for chesed and emet (faithfulness/truth) together, suggesting that the T6 co-occurrence pattern (kindness + faithfulness as the most persistent pair) has a constitutional basis: they are co-inscribed. This means T6's network of adjacent characteristics is not merely relational or contextual co-occurrence but a constitutional clustering: they share the same constitutional site. The T2 constitutional movement outward (heart → acts) and T6's causal network (kindness → reproduced kindness → sustained covenant relationships) trace the same movement from two angles — T2 names the direction, T6 names the mechanism of propagation.
- `T6.6.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER/pending — T5's formation mechanisms and T6's structural relationships interact at the level of what each characteristic in the co-occurrence cluster contributes to kindness formation. The T6 finding that kindness consistently co-occurs with faithfulness, patience, humility, and compassion maps onto T5's chasid formation arc: each of these co-occurring characteristics is also part of the formation process — faithfulness is what makes the chasid reliable over time (formation dimension); patience is what makes kindness sustainable under provocation (T5 suffering connection); humility is what prevents the chasid's identity from becoming pride (T5 formation outcome that avoids the structural opposite). The T5 formation mechanisms (Spirit-formation, inscription, encounter) all produce not just kindness in isolation but kindness in its relational cluster — the Spirit produces the full fruit (Gal 5:22: love, joy, peace, patience, kindness, goodness, faithfulness) not one element at a time. T5's eschatological trajectory (Eph 2:7 — immeasurable future display of kindness in Christ) is the T6 causal chain extended to its ultimate end: the causal propagation of kindness through the covenant community reaches its telos in the eschatological display of divine kindness in Christ.
- `T6.6.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER/pending — T6's structural relationship findings and T7's lexical and literary findings are mutually confirming at the level of the semantic root. The T6 finding that the CHESED root family covers noun (chesed), verb (chasad), person (chasid), shame-negative (H2617B), and proper noun (Chesed) is a T7 lexical observation that has structural significance: a root family that spans all these categories is not merely a lexical curiosity but evidence that kindness (as the CHESED root names it) is a comprehensive inner-being reality — it has a noun form (what it is), a verb form (what it does), a person form (who embodies it), a consequence form (what happens when it fails), and a proper-noun instantiation (when it names a person). The T6 co-occurrence finding that kindness clusters with faithfulness, justice, and compassion maps directly onto the T7 semantic range finding that chesed itself encompasses goodness/kindness/faithfulness — the co-occurrence cluster is partly a reflection of the internal semantic complexity of chesed: different registries are studying different dimensions of what is, in the original Hebrew, a single concept. T7's primary anchor verse (Lam 3:22) is T6's most significant causal statement: the inexhaustibility of chesed (T7) is the precondition for the causal chain's stability (T6) — if chesed could run out, the chain would break.

- `T6.6.2` · session_b · THEOLOGICAL_NOTE/resolved_qa — The highest-priority shared anchors for Session D, selected by analytical significance: (1) Isa 30:15 shared with R76 (holiness), R163 (trust), R187 (strength) — the most structurally dense shared anchor: it names peace-as-quietness as the instrument producing strength, within a salvation context initiated by "the Holy One of Israel." Three registries share this anchor, suggesting Session D will find a major structural cluster here (SP-117-012); (2) Gal 5:22 shared with 5 registries — the Spirit-fruit cluster; the question is constitutive interdependence (SP-117-015); (3) Isa 53:5 shared with R30 (contrition), R62 (fellowship), R151 (sorrow) — the Servant's peace-purchasing suffering connects peace with contrition and sorrow as the human experience surrounding the Servant's act; (4) Phili 4:7 shared with R183 (heart) — peace as the guardian of the heart; (5) Deu 29:19 shared with R153 (stubbornness) and R194 (blessing) — false-shalom connected to stubbornness (Session D structural finding).  [anchors: Isa 30:15, Gal 5:22, Isa 53:5, Phili 4:7]
- `T6.6.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER/pending — T1 establishes peace's central T1 finding (covenant → gift → condition → embodiment in Person); T6 confirms this structure through the causal and constitutive relationships found in the corpus. T6's trust → peace causal sequence (Q&A-148/149) maps onto T1's "covenant → gift → inner condition" — trust is the human act that enables reception of the divine gift. T6's reconciliation-constitutes-peace finding (Q&A-153) maps onto T1's eschatological dimension — the cross-accomplished peace (T1 dimension 4) is what reconciliation produces. The shared anchor network (59 relationships across 43 registries) confirms that T1's four dimensions are each structurally connected to multiple other registries — peace is not self-contained but embedded in the programme's full inner-being ecosystem.
- `T6.6.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER/pending — T2's body evidence (Psa 38:3 — no sha.lom in bones because of sin) connects directly to T6's finding that reconciliation constitutes relational peace (Col 1:20, Eph 2:14) — the same sha.lom that is absent from bones when sin is present is the sha.lom that reconciliation restores. The SHA.L root (T6.4.2) names completeness — and Psa 38:3's use of sha.lom for somatic soundness is directly rooted in this: sha.lom IS somatic completeness, sha.lom IS relational completeness, sha.lom IS covenantal completeness — one root, all dimensions. The constitutional and structural analyses confirm the same root-level truth from different angles.
- `T6.6.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER/pending — T4's God-to-human direction and T6's causal sequences describe the same reality at different levels of abstraction. T4.1 (God gives peace through covenant/Christ/Spirit) is the relational description of what T6.3.2 catalogues as causal inputs (trust, righteousness, discipline, Spirit, confession, love of law all produce peace). The covenant-of-peace (T4.1 — Isa 54:10) is the relational framework for what T6.4.2 names as the SHA.L root shared between peace and covenant at the structural level. The two analyses are not parallel but nested: T4 names the relational direction; T6 names the structural mechanism through which that direction operates.
- `T6.6.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTER_TIER/pending — T6's structural relationships and T7's lexical foundation are the most directly complementary tiers in the analysis. T6's root-level finding (SHA.L shared with R34 covenant, T6.4.2) is validated and grounded by T7's etymology (T7.1.1 — SHA.L root meaning completeness-wholeness-settlement; sha.lam = to pay in full). The root-level semantic bond between peace and covenant (T6) is not lexical coincidence but semantic identity: they share the same root because they name the same underlying reality from different angles — the covenant is the relational structure of completeness; shalom is the experiential condition of completeness. T6's shared anchor network (59 relationships, T6.6) is confirmed as structurally grounded by T7's lexical richness (10 vocabulary dimensions, person-type terms, supplication terms, antonym vocabulary) — the wide vocabulary arc (T7) generates the wide structural network (T6).  ---

**T6.7 · Dimensional Sharing**  (34)

- `T6.7.1` · session_b · OBSERVATION/pending — The dim_review_status is Complete (WA-DimensionReview-Instruction-v1.9-2026-04-09). The primary dimension is Relational/Social (Relational Disposition in the programme vocabulary).  The data notes dimensional overlap in several SD pointers without quantifying the total:  - DIM-064-SD017 (grace) — "dimension overlap (3 shared dimensions)" is explicitly stated - DIM-064-SD016 (mercy) — "dimension overlap (3 shared dimensions)" is explicitly stated - DIM-064-SD020 (faith) — no dimension count stated  The secondary dimensions identified in T1.8 (Volition, Transformation, Dependence/Creatureliness) are the likely shared dimensions. Grace and mercy both carry Relational Disposition + Transformation + Dependence/Creatureliness as plausible shared dimensions — consistent with the "3 shared" notation in the SD pointers.  *Gap noted:* A full dimensional sharing map — listing all characteristics that share each of forgiveness's dimensions and the total count of sharing — is not assembled in the data. The SD pointer data provides partial evidence but not a complete picture.
- `T6.7.2` · session_b · OBSERVATION/pending — From the partial evidence:  **Grace and mercy share 3 dimensions each with forgiveness:** This confirms the structural proximity already identified in T6.5 — these three characteristics (forgiveness, grace, mercy) form a cluster that shares the majority of their analytical dimensions. The dimensional overlap is the strongest evidence for their close structural relationship. The distinction between them (T6.5 — kind/specificity) is a fine-grained one within a broadly shared dimensional profile.  **The dimensional cluster grace-mercy-forgiveness:** If all three share Relational Disposition + Transformation + Dependence/Creatureliness as primary and secondary dimensions, the three characteristics occupy overlapping constitutional territory. Their distinctions (grace = dispositional source; mercy = broad relational generosity; forgiveness = specific moral-debt resolution) are differences within a shared dimensional space — they describe related phenomena at different levels of specificity.  **Implications for Session D:** DIM-064-SD017 identifies this as a priority Session D question — whether grace and forgiveness occupy structurally different positions in an inner-being hierarchy (grace = dispositional; forgiveness = specific relational act). The dimensional sharing data supports the hypothesis that they are hierarchically related (grace contains or generates forgiveness) rather than parallel and independent.

Adds T6 framing.*

- `T6.7.2` · session_b · OBSERVATION/resolved_qa — The Relational Disposition primary dimension shared with love and mercy positions fellowship as one of the relational triad (love, mercy, fellowship) — love is the disposition-toward; mercy is the disposition-toward the needy; fellowship is the participatory-joining that sustains the relationship. All three operate in the same relational space but at different aspects. OBS-062-001.
- `T6.7.1` · session_b · OBSERVATION/resolved_qa — Primary dimension (Relational Disposition — 10/14 groups) shared with love, mercy, fellowship, and other relational-disposition registries. Secondary dimensions: Volition (shared with agency/power registries), Moral Character (shared with virtue registries), Dependence/Creatureliness (shared with trust/prayer registries), Divine-Human Correspondence (unique to the unforgivable sin outer limit). OBS-064-007.
- `T6.7.2` · session_b · OBSERVATION/resolved_qa — The Relational Disposition primary dimension shared with mercy (R111) and love (R103) positions forgiveness within the relational triad operating in C17: mercy is the disposition that generates forgiving; forgiveness is the act; love is the response. The dimensional sharing reveals structural adjacency within the cluster. OBS-064-007.
- `T6.7.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTRA_TIER/routed_cluster — T6 reveals compassion as the structural centre of the C17 cluster rather than a peripheral member. The love co-occurrence (196/351 = 55.8% of all OWNER verses) is uniquely dominant — compassion and love co-occur in more than half of all active verses, raising the programme-level question whether they are truly separable at the data level. Three structural observations from the co-occurrence landscape: (1) compassion sits at C17's centre of gravity (love, mercy, kindness as top three co-occurrences — all within-cluster); (2) compassion generates a human-response cluster (peace, faith, will, surrender, seeking co-occurring at significant levels — the pattern of divine compassion generating human inner-being responses); (3) compassion is constitutively responsive to human distress (despair, guilt, distress, evil all present in the co-occurrence table — compassion operates most characteristically in the presence of need). The CHASAD root crossing compassion (C17) and shame (C06) is the programme's most structurally unexpected finding — a root family that bridges two clusters. Exod 34:6 as the programme's most multiply-anchored verse (6 registries) must be treated as a cluster-level synthesis anchor. The compassion/mercy boundary (SD-009) is the most critical unresolved distinction. T6.7 (dimensional sharing) is programme-stage limited — formally deferred.
- `T6.7.2` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTRA_TIER/routed_cluster — T6 reveals compassion as the structural centre of the C17 cluster rather than a peripheral member. - `T6.7.1` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTRA_TIER/pending — T6 produces the most structurally significant finding in the review set for any single tier: grace is not produced by any other inner-being characteristic. It is the originating point of a causal chain, not its product. This categorically distinguishes grace from every other characteristic reviewed — all others can be produced by at least one other characteristic; grace cannot. This structural uniqueness is confirmed by T2.9's origin analysis (grace originates with God — it does not originate in the human constitutional system) and is analytically consistent across T6.2 (seeking is the posture before grace arrives, not its producer), T6.3 (S-rating on "is grace produced by another characteristic"), and T6.4 (the vocabulary's structural completeness without a generative human-side term). The co-occurrence pattern reveals grace at the intersection of divine initiative and creaturely need: seeking (R140 — 40 verses, dominant) as the creature's pre-grace posture; moral failure cluster (evil, guilt, sin, transgression) as the creaturely context; power cluster (strength, might) confirming the 2Cor 12:9 connection. The love co-occurrence (R103 — 14 verses) is the primary within-cluster signal raising the grace-love boundary question (SD013). All major boundary distinctions (grace-love, grace-mercy, grace-justice, grace-chesed) remain open at T6.5 — the most unresolved boundary cluster in the review set. T6.7 (dimensional sharing) is programme-stage limited.
- `T6.7.2` · session_b · SYNTHESIS_INTRA_TIER/pending — T6 produces the most structurally significant finding in the review set for any single tier: grace is not produced by any other inner-being characteristic. T6.7 (dimensional sharing) is programme-stage limited.

- `T6.7.1` · session_b · CROSS_REGISTRY/resolved_qa — Registry 034 (covenant) spans eight of the eleven programme dimensions across its 32 groups: RD (06), Volition (04), Moral Character (05), Transformation (08), Emotion-Negative (02), Vitality/Existence (07), Dependence/Creatureliness (10), Emotion-Positive (01). By definition, all programme registries assigned to the C17 cluster (relational-grace) share at minimum the RD dimension. Registries in other clusters that also engage Transformation (08) or Moral Character (05) dimensions would also share dimensions with R034. Without access to the dimension assignments of other registries (available to CC from the database), a precise count of dimension-sharing registries cannot be stated from the current data package. Gap: the precise dimensional sharing map requires CC database query. Recording as P — the directional finding is that covenant, spanning eight dimensions, is likely one of the widest-dimensional registries in the programme, and therefore shares dimensions with the largest number of other registries.  [anchors: Jer 31:33, Deu 7:9]
- `T6.7.2` · session_b · CROSS_REGISTRY/resolved_qa — From what can be inferred from the data: covenant's multi-dimensional span (8 of 11 programme dimensions) means it shares dimensions with a very wide range of registries across the programme. This is consistent with the DIM-34-SD001 hypothesis (covenant as C17 structural backbone) — the backbone characteristic would be expected to span the most dimensions and share dimensions with the most other registries. The pattern also reveals that covenant does not belong to a single inner-being domain but cuts across the relational, volitional, moral, transformative, emotional, vital, and creatureliness domains. This is not dimension-confusion (each group is correctly assigned to one primary dimension); it is dimensional comprehensiveness. Covenant engages the inner person across the widest constitutional range of any C17 characteristic. Gap: the specific sharing partners per dimension require CC data (SP-034-007).  [anchors: OBS-034-024]
- `T6.7.3` · session_b · CROSS_REGISTRY/resolved_qa — Precise dimensional sharing counts require CC database query. The inferred pattern (covenant shares dimensions with the widest range of registries due to its 8-dimension span) is stated explicitly as an inference, not a confirmed finding. SP-034-007 raised for CC/Session D to supply the dimensional sharing map.
- `T6.7.1` · session_b · OBSERVATION/resolved_qa — Three explicitly evidenced: (a) Pride/self-glorification: Dan 4:30 — pride claims; kindness gives [OBS-099-054]. (b) Cruelty/severity (apotomia): Rom 11:22 — contrasting face of divine kindness for those who have turned away [XREG: G5544, OBS-099-091]. (c) Deceptive self-service (chrēstologia): not merely absence but active imposture — looks like kindness from outside while serving self from inside [OBS-099-074, Q&A-024]. Structural contrast: genuine kindness is outward-oriented, costly, disposition-driven; its contrasts are inward-oriented, self-serving, or imposture-based.  [anchors: Dan 4:30 · Rom 11:22 · Rom 16:18]
- `T6.7.2` · session_b · OBSERVATION/resolved_qa — Three explicitly evidenced: (a) Pride/self-glorification: Dan 4:30 — pride claims; kindness gives [OBS-099-054]. (b) Cruelty/severity (apotomia): Rom 11:22 — contrasting face of divine kindness for those who have turned away [XREG: G5544, OBS-099-091]. (c) Deceptive self-service (chrēstologia): not merely absence but active imposture — looks like kindness from outside while serving self from inside [OBS-099-074, Q&A-024]. Structural contrast: genuine kindness is outward-oriented, costly, disposition-driven; its contrasts are inward-oriented, self-serving, or imposture-based.  [anchors: Dan 4:30 · Rom 11:22 · Rom 16:18]
- `T6.7.3` · session_b · OBSERVATION/resolved_qa — Three explicitly evidenced: (a) Pride/self-glorification: Dan 4:30 — pride claims; kindness gives [OBS-099-054]. (b) Cruelty/severity (apotomia): Rom 11:22 — contrasting face of divine kindness for those who have turned away [XREG: G5544, OBS-099-091]. [anchors: Dan 4:30 · Rom 11:22 · Rom 16:18]
- `T6.7.1` · session_b · THEOLOGICAL_NOTE/resolved_qa — R117 peace spans 10 dimensions (01 EP, 02 EN, 03 Cog, 04 Vol, 05 MC, 06 RD, 07 VE, 08 Trans, 10 DC, 11 DHC). Within cluster C17: R103 love similarly spans multiple dimensions (EP, EN, Cog, Vol, MC, RD — 6+ confirmed). R111 mercy spans MC, RD, DC, DHC (4). R99 kindness spans EN, Cog, Vol, MC, RD, DC (6). R34 covenant spans EP, EN, Vol, MC, RD, VE (6+). The dimensional overlap is wide — C17 registries predominantly share RD (Relational Disposition) and MC (Moral Character). Peace's distinctive dimensions within C17 are DHC (11 — shared with mercy and compassion but not all cluster members) and VE (07 — distinctive for peace in this cluster) and EP (01 — widely shared). The unique combination of 10 dimensions makes R117 the widest-ranging registry in C17.  [anchors: Gal 5:22, Eph 2:14]
- `T6.7.2` · session_b · THEOLOGICAL_NOTE/resolved_qa — The dimensional sharing pattern reveals two things: (1) Peace is the most integrative characteristic in C17 — it shares dimensions with every other cluster member while also having distinctive dimensions (VE, DHC-in-specific-form) that are less prominent in other C17 registries. This integrative position is consistent with peace as the structural outcome of all the relational-grace characteristics: love, mercy, compassion, forgiveness, grace, kindness, covenant, fellowship all contribute to the conditions under which shalom arises; (2) The DHC dimension (11) shared with mercy and compassion but expressed uniquely in peace through the "X IS peace" ontological structure (YHWH-Shalom, messianic peace, Eph 2:14) — this DHC expression is distinctive in peace and not shared in the same form by the other cluster members. The dimensional sharing reveals peace as both the cluster's most integrative member and its most theologically distinctive (through DHC).  [anchors: Eph 2:14, Judg 6:24]


### M01  (50 findings)

**T1.2.3 · Best working description**  (8)

- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — M01 as a whole: Fear is a multi-register inner characteristic that spans stable disposition (A, G), acute condition (B, D), imposed force (C), somatic expression (E), and chronic burden (F). Its primary constitutional homes are the heart (dominant across B, D, F), the will (primary operative faculty across all sub-groups), and the body (E). Its cognitive dimension is strongest in A and F; its somatic dimension is strongest in E; its moral-evaluative dimension is strongest in A and G. The cluster's unity is the common thread of the inner person's registering of power — whether divine, threatening, or overwhelming — and the volitional impact that registration produces.
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — M01-A: Reverential fear of God is a dispositional inner orientation, primarily located in the heart and will, that aligns the person's cognitive, ethical, and relational life toward God as supreme reality. It engages the cognitive faculty (orienting the mind toward genuine knowledge, Pro 1:7), the volitional faculty (governing conduct and allegiance), the moral evaluation faculty (providing inner ethical restraint in situations without external accountability, Lev 19:14), and the relational capacity (positioning the person for covenant intimacy, Psa 25:14). Its impact on the person is comprehensive: it produces wisdom, ethical restraint, relational alignment with God, and community identity among those who share it.
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — M01-B: Acute fear is an episodic inner condition, primarily operating through the perceptive faculty (triggered by sight of threat or supernatural encounter), that immediately engages the volitional faculty (governing the will's response through fight, flight, paralysis, or submission). Constitutional location is primarily in the heart (Psa 27:3: "heart will not fear"), the inner person generally, and the body as the fear's expressive vehicle. Its impact on the person is immediate and directive: it governs what the person does at the moment of encounter, and (in the "do not fear" corpus) it is the thing the divine word addresses, releases, and redirects.
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — M01-C: Terror as overwhelming force is a condition imposed on the inner person from an external source (divine, military, or natural), primarily engaging the volitional faculty by paralysing, melting, or redirecting the will. It is not primarily located in a constitutional level but operates as an overwhelming force whose impact on the person is dis-agentive: it removes the person's effective will to resist.
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — M01-D: Dismay is an acute inner collapse, primarily located in the soul (Psa 6:3 anchor: "the psalmist's soul itself is greatly dismayed") and the heart (Dan 5:10: "dismay originates in the inner life of thought and then visibly destabilizes the body"), engaging the cognitive faculty (shutting down perception and speech) and the volitional faculty (paralyzing action). Its impact is dissolution of inner coherence — the person cannot function normally in thought, speech, or action.
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — M01-E: Trembling as fear made somatic is a condition located primarily in the body as the expression of an inner state, operating as the body's involuntary register of fear or reverence. The E-VCG-02 subset (trembling at God's word) engages the spirit-level (Isa 66:2: "the trembling is located in the spirit") and orients toward God's communication as its object. The broader trembling vocabulary engages the body's fear-response systems directly. Impact: the inner fear becomes visibly manifest, either as a warning signal of genuine threat or as a marker of genuine reverence.
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — M01-F: Anticipatory dread and anxiety is a sustained inner condition, primarily located in the heart (Deu 28:67: "dread is explicitly located in the heart"; Pro 12:25: "anxiety weighs down a man's heart") and the cognitive faculty (de.a.gah: worry as ruminating future-oriented thought). It engages the volitional faculty by generating avoidance or flight behaviors (Deu 28:67: "constantly wishes the present moment away") and the affective faculty through pervasive emotional weight. Its impact is temporal distortion and the colonisation of ordinary activities by dread.
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — M01-G: Timidity is a dispositional inner failure, located at the spirit level (2Ti 1:7 anchor: "spirit-level disposition that God has not implanted"), primarily engaging the volitional faculty by suppressing the capacity to act faithfully under pressure. Its impact is paralysis, passive self-preservation, and ultimately apostasy under sustained threat.

**T1.8 · Dimension Classification**  (17)

- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — The cluster's primary dimension is **affective** across most sub-groups, with **volitional** as the most consistent secondary dimension across all sub-groups. M01-E is unique in its **somatic** primary; M01-G unique in its **volitional-constitutional** primary. The cluster confirms that fear is primarily a felt inner state (affective) with immediate volitional consequences.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — M01-A's primary dimension is **affective-relational** — the characteristic is the inner feeling-posture that orients the person in relationship toward God. However, it is unusual in that it is equally **volitional** (governing conduct through constraint and redirection) and **cognitive** (being the foundation of genuine knowledge). This multi-dimensionality is a finding rather than a classification problem: M01-A genuinely inhabits the intersection of affect, volition, and cognition.  **[Folded from wa_session_b_findings.id=38; finding_id=DIM-11-001; registry=awe]** Awe spans both Affective/Emotional and Spiritual/God-ward dimensions. God-ward groups (1682-001, 1682-003, 703-002, 703-003) show that the deepest fear/awe content is Spiritual/God-ward rather than merely affective. Session B should examine whether awe functions as a distinct inner-being category integrating affective intensity and God-ward orientation — the two are inseparable in the data.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — M01-B's primary dimension is **affective** — it is an acute emotional experience triggered by an external stimulus. The secondary dimension is **volitional** — the acute fear immediately governs the will's response.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — M01-C's primary dimension is **force** or **dynamic** — terror as a quality/power that acts upon the inner person from outside. If the dimension vocabulary does not include a "force" category, the nearest is **affective** (the terror produces a felt state in the recipient) with **volitional** as secondary (paralyzing the will).
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — M01-D's primary dimension is **affective** (the inner experience of dismay) with **cognitive** as a significant secondary (the dismay shuts down cognition — speechlessness, inability to see, inability to think). The bodily collapse dimension (D-VCG-03) adds a **somatic** secondary.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — M01-E's primary dimension is **somatic** — the characteristic is definitionally the body's expression of inner fear. The E-VCG-02 trembling-at-word subset adds a **spirit-level** and **volitional-reverential** secondary dimension.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — M01-F's primary dimension is **affective-temporal** — the characteristic is the inner experience of sustained anticipatory dread. The **cognitive** dimension is significant (de.a.gah as ruminating, forward-oriented thought). The **volitional** dimension is secondary (anxiety driving avoidance or flight).
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — M01-G's primary dimension is **volitional-constitutional** — the characteristic is a failure of the will's constitutive courage, operating at the spirit level (2Ti 1:7).
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — The affective classification is supported by the overwhelming dominance of experiential-state language across the cluster's 941 verses: fear is consistently described as something that fills the person, grips them, falls upon them, overwhelms them — the language of involuntary affective experience.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — M01-A's affective-relational classification is supported by the consistent nominal forms (*yir.ah* as a possessed quality, "Fear of Isaac" as a name, Mal 2:5 as a covenant defined by fear-as-inner-posture) and by the relational framing (Psa 25:14: "positioned for intimate covenant relationship"). The volitional dimension is supported by the consistent "governing conduct" pattern across A-VCG-01 (ethical restraint), A-VCG-02 (orienting the cognitive life), A-VCG-03 (aligning the relational life). The cognitive dimension is supported by Pro 1:7; Psa 111:10; Job 28:28.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — M01-B's affective primary is supported by the consistent involuntary character of the acute fear (Gen 3:10; 1Sa 17:24; Luk 2:9). The volitional secondary is supported by the systematic descriptions of fear governing what the person does immediately after it strikes.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — M01-E's somatic primary is supported by the entire vocabulary corpus: cha.rad, cha.ra.dah, ra.gaz, ra.ad, pal.la.tsut, sa.ar, rog.zah, re.tet, tromos, entromos — all name bodily states. The sub-group's 98 verses consistently describe physical manifestation as the characteristic's primary mode.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — M01-G's volitional-constitutional primary is supported by 2Ti 1:7 ("spirit-level disposition") and by the consistent description of timidity as will-suppression (Mat 25:25: "paralyzed his will and produced passive self-preservation").
- `T1.8.3` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — The secondary dimensions do not compete with but specify the primary across most sub-groups. The one genuine tension is M01-E-VCG-02, where the somatic primary and spirit-level secondary describe genuinely different originating levels for what is formally the same characteristic (trembling).
- `T1.8.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — M01-A's secondary dimensions — **volitional** (governing conduct) and **cognitive** (grounding knowledge) — do not compete with the affective-relational primary but integrate into it. The fear is a felt posture (affective) that simultaneously governs conduct (volitional) and orients knowing (cognitive). These dimensions are aspects of a single complex disposition rather than competing classifications.  **[Folded from wa_session_b_findings.id=38; finding_id=DIM-11-001; registry=awe]** Awe spans both Affective/Emotional and Spiritual/God-ward dimensions. God-ward groups (1682-001, 1682-003, 703-002, 703-003) show that the deepest fear/awe content is Spiritual/God-ward rather than merely affective. Session B should examine whether awe functions as a distinct inner-being category integrating affective intensity and God-ward orientation — the two are inseparable in the data.
- `T1.8.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — M01-E-VCG-02's **spirit-level reverential** secondary dimension is genuinely distinct from the **somatic** primary. The trembling-at-word tradition (Isa 66:2 anchor) is not primarily somatic in origin — it arises from a spirit-posture that then expresses somatically. This is the one sub-group where the somatic primary and the spirit-level secondary are in genuine tension as a classification question. The programme should note this at Phase 12.
- `T1.8.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — M01-G's **spirit-level constitutional** dimension does not compete with the volitional primary but specifies its constitutional depth: the failure of volition in timidity is a spirit-level failure (2Ti 1:7), not merely a situational weakness.

**T2.8 · Body-Deposit (DNA / generational)**  (10)

- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /silent — The verse evidence across M01 is silent on constitutional deposit in the body or generational biological consequence. The generational effects evidenced are formational (fear learned and transmitted, Deu 31:12-13) and covenantal (God's steadfast love running to those who fear him across generations, Psa 103:17) rather than constitutional-biological.
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /silent — M01-A's reverential fear does not produce explicit constitutional deposit in the body. The generational transmission evidenced (Deu 31:12-13; Psa 103:17) is through formation and covenant, not through biological or constitutional deposit.
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /silent — M01-B (acute fear), M01-C (terror), M01-D (dismay), M01-E (trembling), and M01-G (timidity) are silent on constitutional deposit in the body.
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /silent — M01-B (acute fear), M01-C (terror), M01-D (dismay), M01-E (trembling), and M01-G (timidity) are silent on constitutional deposit in the body.
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /silent — M01-B (acute fear), M01-C (terror), M01-D (dismay), M01-E (trembling), and M01-G (timidity) are silent on constitutional deposit in the body.
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /silent — M01-B (acute fear), M01-C (terror), M01-D (dismay), M01-E (trembling), and M01-G (timidity) are silent on constitutional deposit in the body.
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — M01-F-VCG-01 and VCG-03 have the nearest evidence to constitutional deposit: Heb 2:15 ("fear of death held people in **lifelong** inner slavery") suggests that sustained anxiety/dread produces a settled constitutional condition that is not easily reversed — it is not merely a repeated experience but a settled bondage. This is not explicitly bodily deposit but constitutional entrenchment.
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /silent — M01-B (acute fear), M01-C (terror), M01-D (dismay), M01-E (trembling), and M01-G (timidity) are silent on constitutional deposit in the body.
- `T2.8.2` · cluster_finding · /silent — No verse in M01 explicitly addresses constitutional deposit in the body. The Heb 2:15 "lifelong slavery" language could be read as suggesting entrenchment rather than deposit, but this is inferential. No supporting or contradicting verse is identified.
- `T2.8.3` · cluster_finding · /silent — The evidence across all M01 sub-groups is silent on constitutional deposit in the body or generational biological consequence. This finding is noted and fed forward to T5.7 as required.

**T5.7 · Deposit Consequence**  (4)

- `T5.7.1` · cluster_finding · /silent — T2.8 found the verse evidence across all M01 sub-groups silent on constitutional deposit in the body. The nearest evidence was M01-F's "lifelong inner slavery" (Heb 2:15) and the covenantal-curse dread (Deu 28:66-67) — but these describe a settled condition of bondage rather than a constitutional bodily deposit. T5.7 closes without a developmental-consequence finding because T2.8 found no deposit to trace forward. This is an expected finding given M01's nature as a primarily inner-being characteristic whose bodily dimension is expressive rather than deposit-forming.
- `T5.7.2` · cluster_finding · /silent — The verse evidence does not evidence generational biological/constitutional deposit from M01. The generational effects are covenantal (Psa 103:17) and pedagogical (Deu 31:12-13), not physical inheritance.
- `T5.7.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — M01-A shows generational consequence of a formational rather than constitutional kind. Psa 103:17 (God's steadfast love runs "to those who fear him across generations" — the covenantal benefit of the fear-posture extends generationally); Deu 31:13 (children being formed in fear of the Lord — the characteristic transmitted pedagogically, not biologically). The generational consequence is formational transmission, not constitutional deposit.
- `T5.7.3` · cluster_finding · /silent — T2.8 found no constitutional deposit in the body. T5.7 is closed. The generational dimension of M01 is formational (fear transmitted through instruction and community) and covenantal (God's faithfulness extending across generations to those who fear him) rather than constitutional-biological.

**T6.6 · Shared Verse Anchors**  (6)

- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — Multiple M01 anchor verses will function as shared anchors across the programme. The most significant cross-cluster anchor candidates are: 1Jo 4:18 (M01/Love), Pro 1:7 (M01/Wisdom), Heb 12:28 (M01/Worship), 2Ti 1:7 (M01/Spirit-Constitution), Psa 36:1 / Rom 3:18 (M01/Moral-nature).
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — The M01 evidence base includes verses whose vocabulary will appear in other programme clusters. Most notably: (1) **1Jo 4:18** (M01-A-VCG-06 / M01-F-VCG-04): "perfect love expels fear" — this verse will also be anchored in the Love/Agape cluster, where it establishes love's perfection as fear-displacing. (2) **Pro 1:7** (anchor M01-A-VCG-02): this verse also anchors wisdom clusters. (3) **Psa 36:1 / Rom 3:18** (M01-A-VCG-01): these verses establish the absence of fear as moral blindness and will appear in guilt/sin-nature analysis. (4) **Heb 12:28** (anchor M01-A-VCG-06): this verse also grounds Reverence/Worship clusters. (5) **2Ti 1:7** (anchor M01-G-VCG-01): this verse will appear in Spirit/Constitution analysis for the programme.
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — The M01 evidence base includes verses whose vocabulary will appear in other programme clusters. Most notably: (1) **1Jo 4:18** (M01-A-VCG-06 / M01-F-VCG-04): "perfect love expels fear" — this verse will also be anchored in the Love/Agape cluster, where it establishes love's perfection as fear-displacing. (2) **Pro 1:7** (anchor M01-A-VCG-02): this verse also anchors wisdom clusters. (4) **Heb 12:28** (anchor M01-A-VCG-06): this verse also grounds Reverence/Worship clusters. (5) **2Ti 1:7** (anchor M01-G-VCG-01): this verse will appear in Spirit/Constitution analysis for the programme.
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — [E] Shared anchors reveal that M01 is structurally central in the programme's inner-being landscape: multiple characteristics have foundational or structural relationships with fear, confirming that fear-of-God is not peripheral but central to the inner-being framework Scripture presents.  ---  [E] **M01 ↔ Love pair:** 1Jo 4:18's shared anchor between M01 and a Love cluster reveals that fear and love are constitutively related in the inner-being landscape — they are not parallel and independent characteristics but occupy the same interior space and are mutually exclusive in their extreme forms (perfect love → no fear of punishment; fear of punishment → not yet perfected in love). The relationship is one of **mutual exclusivity in completion** and **mutual implication in incompletion**.  ---  [E] **M01 ↔ Wisdom pair:** Pro 1:7's shared anchor between M01 and Wisdom reveals that these characteristics are constitutively linked: wisdom is not achievable without fear-of-God as its root. The relationship is **foundational/fruit**: fear is the root, wisdom is the fruit.
- `T6.6.3` · cluster_finding · /silent — M01-D's anchor verses (Psa 6:3; Isa 28:19; Jer 49:37; Dan 5:6; Deu 28:28) and most M01-E anchor verses are primarily local to the fear cluster and are not anticipated to serve as primary anchors in other programme clusters. No cross-cluster shared anchors identified for these sub-groups.
- `T6.6.3` · cluster_finding · /silent — M01-D's anchor verses (Psa 6:3; Isa 28:19; Jer 49:37; Dan 5:6; Deu 28:28) and most M01-E anchor verses are primarily local to the fear cluster and are not anticipated to serve as primary anchors in other programme clusters. No cross-cluster shared anchors identified for these sub-groups.

**T6.7 · Dimensional Sharing**  (5)

- `T6.7.1` · cluster_finding · /silent — Dimensional sharing data across the full programme is not yet available (other clusters are still in earlier phases). The cluster can confirm its own dimensions (per T1.8) but cannot quantify sharing until adjacent clusters complete Session B. This is noted as an anticipated finding for Phase 10/12 reconciliation.
- `T6.7.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — M01-A's confirmed dimensions (affective-relational primary; volitional and cognitive as secondaries) are likely shared with multiple programme characteristics: the affective dimension is shared with every programme cluster that involves a felt inner state; the volitional dimension is shared with all action-governing characteristics; the cognitive dimension is shared with wisdom-adjacent clusters. The number of shared dimensions across the programme will be large, given M01-A's foundational position.

This will be confirmed at Phase 12.
- `T6.7.3` · cluster_finding · /silent — Dimensional sharing data across the programme is not yet available. M01 is an early cluster in the programme's analytical sequence; adjacent clusters (M02, M03, M06 are complete) will contribute comparison data. Phase 12 closure will incorporate available dimensional sharing from completed clusters.


### M02  (26 findings)

**T1.2.3 · Best working description**  (1)

- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — Drawing from the verse evidence: Anger, Wrath and Indignation is the cluster of inner-being states that constitute the human person's morally charged reactive passion — a thermally intense, directionally structured, action-driving condition that arises when a moral claim (personal, relational, or covenantal) is perceived as violated. It operates at multiple intensities (from brooding displeasure at M02-C-VCG-03 through scorching indignation at M02-B-VCG-04 to rage that fills and possesses at M02-B-VCG-03), across multiple sub-registers (wrath as judicial settled verdict, burning as thermal eruptive force, indignation as principled moral stance, jealousy as possessive-protective passion, provocation as the arousal-dynamic, strife as the relational outworking), and in response to both personal and moral offense. The constitutional location is primarily the inner person as a whole, with somatic expression through heat, face, and bodily comportment.

**T1.8 · Dimension Classification**  (6)

- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — The primary dimension is the affective-emotional dimension, evidenced by the cluster's dominant vocabulary: burning, heat, kindling, eruption, filling, pouring — all describe an inner emotional state of high arousal. The programme's dimension vocabulary includes the emotional dimension as the primary classification for states like anger, and the verse evidence consistently places this characteristic in the register of felt inner experience.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — Dan 3:19 (M02-B-VCG-03) names the inner state explicitly — "filled with fury" — locating it in the inner person as an experienced condition. Eze 3:14 (M02-B-VCG-03) shows Ezekiel experiencing rage as a felt inner heat in his spirit, a bitterness and burning intensity in his own inner being. Est 1:12 (M02-B-VCG-03) locates the inner fire "within him." These are primary affective-emotional location evidences.
- `T1.8.3` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — Secondary dimensions confirmed: volitional (wrath drives purposeful decision); relational (provocation is inherently dyadic); moral-evaluative (anger consistently involves a moral assessment of wrongdoing); and in M02-E-VCG-01, constitutional-identity.
- `T1.8.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — Strong secondary volitional dimension: wrath in M02-A consistently operates through will — it produces oaths (Heb 3:11), drives decisions (Judg 2:14 — "the Lord decided to give them over"), and is described as purposeful and calibrated (Eze 7:8 — "calibrated to the specific abominations committed"). The volitional dimension is secondary but pervasive.
- `T1.8.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — Strong secondary relational dimension: the provocation sub-group (M02-D) is entirely about anger as a relational dynamic — it requires an agent, an action, and a subject. The relational dimension is definitional to M02-D.
- `T1.8.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — Secondary attachment/identity dimension in M02-E-VCG-01: God's jealousy as his constitutional name (Exo 34:14) carries a dimension beyond the affective — it is an identity-level constitutional attribute, placing jealousy at the level of what God is rather than what God feels in episodes.

**T2.8 · Body-Deposit (DNA / generational)**  (5)

- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — Deu 5:9 (M02-E-VCG-01) provides evidence of a generational bodily consequence: divine jealousy punishes children for their fathers' unfaithfulness across generations — this is a trans-generational deposit, carried forward beyond the individual who originally bore the characteristic.
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — Pro 14:30 (M02-E-VCG-04) provides the most direct evidence: "envy rots the bones" — sustained jealousy leaves a physical deposit that deteriorates the body's structural interior. Psa 38:3 (M02-C-VCG-01) provides corroborating evidence: divine indignation "removes soundness from flesh and health from bones" — the indignation's sustained pressure on the recipient produces a bodily deposit of unsoundness.
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — Pro 14:30 (M02-E-VCG-04) provides the most direct evidence: "envy rots the bones" — sustained jealousy leaves a physical deposit that deteriorates the body's structural interior. Psa 38:3 (M02-C-VCG-01) provides corroborating evidence: divine indignation "removes soundness from flesh and health from bones" — the indignation's sustained pressure on the recipient produces a bodily deposit of unsoundness.
- `T2.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — Gen 27:44 (M02-B-VCG-03) evidences that rage's immediate deposit subsides with time and distance — the volatile inner state resolves rather than leaving a permanent deposit. This could be read as contradicting a permanent constitutional deposit for the burning-rage register. However, Pro 19:19 (M02-B-VCG-05) shows that the deeply ingrained person of great rage perpetuates the cycle — suggesting that repeated rage-episodes do deposit a dispositional groove that is difficult to escape.
- `T2.8.3` · cluster_finding · /silent — The eschatological-wrath corpus (M02-A-VCG-02) is silent on constitutional deposit — it describes wrath as an external future force, not as something that leaves a deposit in the person who bears it. The deposit-question belongs to the inner-experience and sustained-character registers of the cluster, not the eschatological-judicial register.

**T5.7 · Deposit Consequence**  (6)

- `T5.7.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — Pro 19:19 (M02-B-VCG-05): "a man of great rage bears consequences he cannot escape, and rescuing him only perpetuates the cycle" — the deposit of sustained rage is an ingrained dispositional groove that generates self-reinforcing consequences. The developmental consequence is a character groove that becomes its own trap.
- `T5.7.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — T2.8 identified two deposit evidences: (1) envy rotting bones (Pro 14:30 / M02-E-VCG-04) and (2) divine indignation removing soundness from flesh and bones (Psa 38:3 / M02-C-VCG-01). The developmental consequence of the envy-deposit is progressive physical deterioration — the sustained inner state of covetous jealousy depletes the body's structural vitality. Pro 14:30 contrasts this with "a tranquil heart gives life to the flesh" — the developmental trajectory of sustained envy is bodily wasting, opposed to the life-giving trajectory of peace.
- `T5.7.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — T2.8 identified two deposit evidences: (1) envy rotting bones (Pro 14:30 / M02-E-VCG-04) and (2) divine indignation removing soundness from flesh and bones (Psa 38:3 / M02-C-VCG-01). The developmental consequence of the envy-deposit is progressive physical deterioration — the sustained inner state of covetous jealousy depletes the body's structural vitality. Pro 14:30 contrasts this with "a tranquil heart gives life to the flesh" — the developmental trajectory of sustained envy is bodily wasting, opposed to the life-giving trajectory of peace.
- `T5.7.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — Neh 13:18 (M02-B-VCG-01): "Nehemiah warns that Sabbath-breaking is bringing more burning anger on Israel, just as the fathers' sins did; the fierce anger is a recurring divine response to covenant violation in every generation." The generational pattern of provocation → anger → discipline is itself generationally transmitted — the fathers' pattern repeats in the children. The deposit is a pattern of relationship, not only a physical inheritance.
- `T5.7.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — Yes. Deu 5:9 (M02-E-VCG-01): "God declares himself a jealous God whose jealousy punishes children for their fathers' unfaithfulness across generations." This is the clearest trans-generational evidence in the cluster — the fathers' violation of divine jealousy deposits a consequence carried generationally. The deposit moves beyond the individual.
- `T5.7.3` · cluster_finding · /silent — T2.8 found deposit evidence. This prompt is not applicable.

**T6.6 · Shared Verse Anchors**  (5)

- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — Mar 3:5 (in M02-A-VCG-04) — Jesus's anger paired with grief — is likely relevant to M03 Grief and possibly to M21 (devotion to God's word) or other clusters studying righteous moral response. The cross-routing flag from Phase 7 identified this verse's dual-register.
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — Num 25:3 (anchor of M02-B-VCG-01) — the Phinehas incident — is likely an anchor in M02-E as well (Num 25:11 appears in M02-E-VCG-03). These are different vc records for different terms at the same pericope, but the shared passage reveals that jealousy and anger are simultaneously operative and that Phinehas's jealous act addresses both. The pericope is a shared evidence base for both sub-groups.
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — 2Cor 7:11 (anchor of M02-C-VCG-02) is likely also an anchor in M03 Grief (it mentions "godly grief" as what produced the indignation) and M11 Repentance (indignation is evidence of genuine repentance). This verse is a convergence point for multiple cluster studies.
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — **Pericope: Num 25 pericope:** The shared pericope at Num 25 (M02-B-VCG-01 anchor and M02-E-VCG-03 content) reveals that jealousy and anger are not simply sequential but co-constitutive in the specific instance of righteous zeal: Phinehas's jealousy is not prior to his anger and does not cause it separately — they are a unified inner state of righteous passion that is simultaneously jealous and angry. The two registers of M02-E and M02-B are not separate systems; they co-arise in the most intense manifestations of morally-grounded passionate response.  **[Folded from wa_session_research_flags.id=622; finding_id=SP-103-034; registry=love]** _(Phase 10 target was T6.6.2 [E-VCG-...]; folded into available T6.6.2 row via fallback)_ [v1.8 obslog SD pointer] Song 8:6 — (a) does the programme have a qinah registry? (b) does 'flame of Yah' support 1Jo 4:8 ('God is love') from the OT side?  Target: R103 (love) / jealousy-zeal registry (qinah) / divine nature Connecting term: H0160 ahavah / H7068 qinah Evidence basis: OBS-103-096
- `T6.6.3` · cluster_finding · /silent — Not applicable. Shared anchors are identified and discussed.

**T6.7 · Dimensional Sharing**  (3)

- `T6.7.1` · cluster_finding · /gap — The programme's full dimensional sharing data across clusters is not yet available (most clusters are not yet Analysis Completed). The known completed clusters (M01, M05, M06, M15, M20, M26, M39, M46) provide a partial reference set. Dimensional sharing analysis at cluster-to-cluster level is a Phase 10/12 activity. This gap is noted.

M02 and M03 (Grief) may share the affective dimension with divergence on the thermal/direction sub-dimension. M02 and M28 (Envy) share possessive-relational aspects through M02-E. These are inferential observations pending full dimensional cross-cluster data.
