**T6.6 · Shared Verse Anchors**  (24)

- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — **[CLUSTER]** E — The cluster has multiple high-significance shared anchor verses, the most analytically important being: (1) **Psa 118:23** — primary Wonder (Char 6) anchor and the NT's most explicitly christological wonder-text (Mat 21:42 — rejected stone becoming cornerstone described with the pa.la vocabulary); shared across Wonder, Exultation, and the christological arch of the cluster; (2) **Isa 53:10** — the theologically deepest Delight (Char 4) instance (God's cha.phets as the atonement mechanism); shared across Delight and every christological study in the programme; (3) **Heb 12:2** — the Joy (Char 2) anchor at the Joy/Suffering-Joy boundary ("for the joy that was set before him endured the cross"); shared across Joy and Suffering-Joy; (4) **Isa 9:6** ("Wonderful Counselor") — shared between Wonder (Char 6) and every study of Christ's identity; (5) **1Pe 1:8** — shared between Joy (Char 2, chara) and Suffering-Joy (Char 7, agalliao), both in the same verse; (6) **Mat 5:12** — shared between Joy (Char 2, chairo) and Suffering-Joy (Char 7, agalliao), both in the same verse. These shared anchors confirm three christological convergence points for the cluster: the Wonderful Counselor (Wonder), the cha.phets-atonement (Delight), and the "joy set before him" (Joy/Suffering-Joy).
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-1]** E — Yes: **Deu 30:9** (su.s) was assigned to M04-H-VCG-02 (God's relational delight toward persons — Volitional Delight characteristic) in Step 5. The same verse appears in the M04-A corpus here as an exultation instance. This shared verse reveals that Deu 30:9 carries evidence for both Exultation and Volitional Delight — the su.s term in that verse spans both characteristics. Similarly, **Psa 119:14 and 119:162** (su.s — rejoice in God's testimonies/word) were assigned to M04-A-VCG-01 but are directly adjacent to M04-G's delight-in-God's-word characteristic; these verses may function as anchors at the Exultation/Delight boundary.
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-2]** E — Yes, multiple shared anchors: (1) **Psa 35:9** — assigned to M04-A (Exultation, VCG-01) and also appears in M04-B-VCG-05 (Gil_verb) — shared between Exultation and Joy; (2) **Psa 16:9** — appears in M04-A corpus (Exultation) and in M04-B-VCG-05 (sa.mach) — shared; (3) **Isa 65:18** — uses both ma.s.vo.s (M04-B-VCG-05) and gi.lah (M04-E-VCG-01) — shared between sub-groups; (4) **1Pe 1:8** — appears in both M04-C-VCG-05 (chara) and M04-E-VCG-02 (agalliao) — shared across the Joy/Suffering-Joy boundary; (5) **Mat 5:12** — appears in M04-C-VCG-05 (chairo) and M04-E-VCG-02 (agalliao) — shared; (6) **Rev 19:7** — appears in M04-C-VCG-04 (chairo) and M04-E-VCG-02 (agalliao) — shared.
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-3]** E — Multiple shared anchors: (1) **Psa 63:3** ("your steadfast love is better than life") — M04-L-VCG-01 (Gladness) and Characteristic 1 (Exultation) — the same verse names God's steadfast love as the supreme good while also belonging to the exultation corpus; (2) **Isa 52:7** (M04-O-VCG-01) — shared with the Joy corpus; (3) **Gen 1:31** ("very good") — M04-L-VCG-01 anchor, but also the creation-foundation verse that grounds multiple other characteristics' understanding of created design; (4) **Mic 6:8** (M04-L-VCG-02) — the programme's OBS-027 identifies six other registries sharing this anchor; it is one of the most cross-characteristic anchors in the entire programme; (5) **Eze 36:31** (M04-L-VCG-02) — shared anchor with Registry 97 and multiple other registries per SP-006.
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-4]** E — Multiple shared anchors: (1) **Psa 51:6** ("you delight in truth in the inward being, and you teach me wisdom in the secret heart") — primary anchor for Delight (God's delight in interior truth as the ground of transformation); also a primary anchor for Characteristic 2's inner-transformation registers; (2) **Psa 40:8** ("I delight to do your will; your law is within my heart") — shared with Hebrews 10:7's christological application; (3) **1Sa 15:22** ("to obey is better than sacrifice") — appears in both M04-H-VCG-02 (Delight, God's pleasure in obedience) and M04-M-VCG-02 (obedience-orientation sub-group); (4) **Jer 9:24** — "I delight in steadfast love, justice, and righteousness" — shared with multiple programme registries; (5) **Isa 53:10** — "it was the will of the Lord to crush him" — primary christological anchor shared with multiple programme registries; (6) **Mat 3:17; 12:18; 17:5** — the eudokeo baptism/transfiguration verses are shared with every cluster discussing the Father-Son relationship.
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-5]** E — Multiple shared anchors: (1) **Psa 37:16** (better is the little of the righteous) — shared anchor between M04-K-VCG-03 (Pleasure) and M04-L-VCG-03 in Gladness (wisdom-comparative); (2) **Pro 15:15–17** (cheerful heart/better-than comparatives) — shared across Gladness (comparative evaluations) and Pleasure (experiential quality); (3) **1Sa 15:22** ("to obey is better than sacrifice") — shared between M04-M (Delight-obedience) and M04-K-VCG-03 adjacent (better-than framework); (4) **Psa 34:8** ("taste and see" — cross-assigned to M04-K in this corpus) — widely shared across multiple registries; (5) **Song 7:6** — shared between M04-J (Pleasure's relational register) and M04-N's parental Delight; (6) **Zec 8:19** — shared between M04-B (feast-joy) and M04-K (cheerful-feast Pleasure).
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-6]** E — Multiple shared anchors: (1) **Psa 118:23** ("this is the Lord's doing; it is marvellous in our eyes") — primary Wonder anchor; also the christological anchor of Mat 21:42 (rejected stone); shared across Wonder, Exultation, and the programme's Christology; (2) **Isa 9:6** ("Wonderful Counselor") — shared between Wonder and every study addressing the incarnate Christ's identity; (3) **2Sa 1:26** (pa.la for Jonathan's extraordinary love) — shared between Wonder (VCG-02) and Pleasure (M04-J-VCG-02 for the relational-pleasantness of Jonathan); (4) **Exo 15:11** (doing wonders, Exo 15 Song of the Sea) — shared between Wonder (VCG-01) and Exultation (Characteristic 1); (5) **Deu 28:59** (extraordinary afflictions — pa.la in judgment) — shared between Wonder (dark register) and Gladness's structural-opposite section.
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-7]** E — Multiple shared anchors: (1) **1Pe 1:8** — carries evidence for both Characteristic 2 (Joy — chara) and Characteristic 7 (Suffering-Joy — agalliao) simultaneously; (2) **Mat 5:12** — carries both M04-C-VCG-05 (chairo) and M04-E-VCG-02 (agalliao) — shared between Characteristics 2 and 7; (3) **Rev 19:7** — carries both M04-C-VCG-04 (chairo) and M04-E-VCG-02 (agalliao) — shared; (4) **Joh 8:56** — agalliao in a non-suffering context, making it sit in the boundary between Characteristics 2 and 7; (5) **Psa 45:7** (sa.s.von — M04-E-VCG-01) → applied christologically in Heb 1:9 — shared anchor between Characteristic 7's restoration register and Characteristic 1 (Exultation's royal-anointing dimension).
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — **[CLUSTER]** E — The shared anchors reveal the cluster's three christological convergence points: (1) **Isa 9:6 / Psa 118:23** — Wonder meets Christology at the point of incomprehensible divine acts that exceed human categories; Christ's identity is wonder-inducing (Isa 9:6's pe.le) and his resurrection/rejection is marvellous (Psa 118:23's pa.la niphal → Mat 21:42); (2) **Isa 53:10** — Delight meets the atonement; God's cha.phets directed toward the servant's suffering is the inner divine motivation of redemption; the most theologically charged Delight-verse in Scripture reveals God's pleasure as the operative principle of salvation; (3) **Heb 12:2** — Joy and Suffering-Joy meet the cross; Christ's endurance of the cross is enabled by the "joy set before him" — which is simultaneously Joy (the eschatological joy he anticipates) and Suffering-Joy (holding that joy through the suffering itself). Together these three christological convergences reveal that the positive inner-being cluster is not peripheral to soteriology but is deeply embedded in Scripture's account of how salvation works.
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-1]** E — The shared anchors confirm the porous boundary between Exultation and Volitional Delight (both using su.s in the God-to-human relational register) and between Exultation and Delight-in-God's-word (Psa 119:14; 119:162 — the exulting-in-testimonies verses that could equally evidence M04-G). These shared anchors are evidence of the inter-characteristic relationships that Phase 9 should flag for Session D synthesis.
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-2]** E — The most analytically significant shared anchors are those at the Joy/Suffering-Joy boundary (1Pe 1:8; Mat 5:12; Rev 19:7) — the same verses carry evidence for both Characteristic 2 and Characteristic 7, confirming that the split of M04-E across two characteristics is a genuine analytical judgment call and not an artificial division. The Joy/Exultation shared anchors (Psa 35:9; 16:9; Isa 65:18) confirm the porous boundary between those two characteristics at the high end of Joy's intensity.
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-3]** E — Mic 6:8's shared anchoring across six registries reveals that Gladness in its evaluative face (what is good = doing justice, loving kindness, walking humbly) is constitutively intersected with multiple other inner-being characteristics — each of the three named elements of the good (justice, kindness, humility) belongs to other registries. This confirms the observation from T0.3.1: Gladness's evaluative-cognitive face is the integrating faculty across multiple inner-being characteristics, not a single isolated state. Gen 1:31's shared anchoring confirms Gladness as the characteristic most directly tied to the ontological foundation of Scripture's goodness.
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-4]** E — Psa 40:8's shared anchoring (Delight and NT christological application in Heb 10:7) reveals Delight as a characteristic directly fulfilled in Christ — his law-within-the-heart Delight in doing the Father's will is the human image of divine-inner-being Delight in its perfect form. Isa 53:10's shared anchoring reveals Delight as the inner divine disposition that motivates the most costly redemptive act in Scripture — God's will/cha.phets to crush the servant is the theologically deepest instance of divine Delight in the corpus. Jer 9:24's shared anchoring confirms Delight as the characteristic most directly revealing God's moral preferences — what God delights in (steadfast love, justice, righteousness) is the core curriculum of moral character formation.
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-5]** E — Psa 37:16's shared anchoring between Pleasure and Gladness confirms the tov-root kinship: the same better-than evaluation serves both as Gladness (evaluative-cognitive recognition of what is genuinely good) and Pleasure (the wisdom-calibrated preference for what is genuinely more pleasant). Song 7:6's shared anchoring between Pleasure (relational pleasantness) and Delight (directed attachment toward the beloved) confirms the overlap zone between these two adjacent characteristics precisely in the Song of Songs, where beauty perceived as pleasant and beauty treasured as a specific beloved object converge in a single verse.
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-6]** E — Psa 118:23's shared anchoring (Wonder + Christology) is the most analytically significant: the verse's "marvellous in our eyes" locates the Christ-event as the supreme instance of Wonder — the rejected stone becoming the cornerstone is literally described with the Wonder vocabulary, making Wonder the inner state of those who rightly perceive the christological event. 2Sa 1:26's shared anchoring (Wonder + Pleasure) confirms the overlap zone between the two characteristics: the same pa.la term serves both the incomprehensibility register (Jonathan's love exceeding ordinary categories — Wonder) and the relational-pleasantness register (2Sa 1:26 in M04-J for Pleasure).
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-7]** E — The shared anchors at 1Pe 1:8; Mat 5:12; Rev 19:7 confirm what the carry-forward analysis established: the suffering-paradox register and the eschatological-Joy register are genuinely interpenetrating at the verse level — some verses carry evidence for both Characteristics 2 and 7 simultaneously. This is not a problem to be resolved but an analytically significant finding: the two characteristics are most closely related precisely at the point of intense, eschatologically-grounded rejoicing, and several verses evidence both.
- `T6.6.3` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — **[CLUSTER]** E — Multiple high-significance shared anchors confirmed across all seven characteristics.
- `T6.6.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-1]** E — Shared anchors identified at Deu 30:9 and Psa 119:14; 119:162.
- `T6.6.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-2]** E — Multiple shared anchors identified at the Joy/Exultation and Joy/Suffering-Joy boundaries.
- `T6.6.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-3]** E — Multiple shared anchors confirmed, Mic 6:8 being the most cross-characteristic.
- `T6.6.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-4]** E — Multiple high-significance shared anchors confirmed; not silent.
- `T6.6.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-5]** E — Multiple shared anchors confirmed; not silent.
- `T6.6.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-6]** E — Multiple shared anchors confirmed; not silent.
- `T6.6.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-7]** E — Multiple shared anchors confirmed at the Joy/Suffering-Joy boundary.

**T6.7 · Dimensional Sharing**  (24)

- `T6.7.1` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — **[CLUSTER]** G — Full dimensional sharing data across all 181+ registries requires Session D synthesis. From Phase 9's available evidence the cluster's dimensional architecture is: Gladness (Char 3) is the cluster's most cognitively-primary characteristic (unique cognitive-evaluative dimension); Suffering-Joy (Char 7) is the most Spirit-constitutional (unique spirit-level primacy); Pleasure (Char 5) is the most bodily-distributed (unique multi-level body-soul-sense range); Delight (Char 4) is the most dimensionally complex (Emotion-Positive, Relational Disposition, Volition, and Moral Character all evidenced as significant); Joy (Char 2) is the most dimensionally shared with other characteristics (affective primary shared with Exultation and Suffering-Joy; volitional shared with Delight; relational shared with Delight and Pleasure). Full dimensional mapping awaits Session D.
- `T6.7.1` · cluster_finding · /gap — **[CHAR-1]** G — Dimensional sharing data across all 7 M04 characteristics is not yet available — Phase 9 is running sequentially, and Characteristics 2–7 have not yet been analysed. A complete dimensional-sharing assessment requires all characteristics to be processed.
- `T6.7.1` · cluster_finding · /gap — **[CHAR-2]** G — Dimensional sharing data across all 7 M04 characteristics is not yet available — Phase 9 is running sequentially. From the evidence available: Joy shares the affective dimension with Exultation and Gladness; the volitional dimension with Delight (M04-H — volitional pleasure); the relational dimension with Shared Gladness (M04-D, which is partly within this characteristic). Full dimensional-sharing assessment requires Characteristics 3–7 to be processed.
- `T6.7.1` · cluster_finding · /gap — **[CHAR-3]** G — Dimensional sharing data across all 7 M04 characteristics is not yet available — Characteristics 4–7 have not been processed. From evidence available: the cognitive-evaluative dimension is shared with Wisdom (M15) and likely with other cognitively-constituted characteristics across the programme; the affective-warmth dimension is shared with Joy (Characteristic 2) and Delight (Characteristic 4); the moral-evaluation dimension is shared with multiple ethics-related characteristics. Full dimensional-sharing assessment requires remaining characteristics to be processed.
- `T6.7.1` · cluster_finding · /gap — **[CHAR-4]** G — Dimensional sharing data across all 7 M04 characteristics is not yet complete. From available evidence: Delight's affective-positive dimension is shared with Joy and Gladness; the volitional dimension is shared with characteristics that carry strong will-engagement; the relational-disposition dimension is shared with Love and Faithfulness registers in adjacent clusters; the moral-character dimension (M04-M) is shared with uprightness and obedience characteristics. Full assessment requires remaining characteristics to be processed.
- `T6.7.1` · cluster_finding · /gap — **[CHAR-5]** G — Full dimensional sharing data not yet available (Characteristics 4–7 partial at this stage). From available evidence: the Emotion-Positive dimension is shared with Joy, Gladness, and Delight; the Relational-Disposition dimension is shared with Delight and Love characteristics; the Vitality/Existence dimension (body-health) is shared with characteristics that carry somatic wellbeing evidence. What is relatively unique to Pleasure is its explicit body-level constitutional evidence (Pro 16:24; Deu 28:54, 56) and the ni.cho.ach worship-interface structure.
- `T6.7.1` · cluster_finding · /gap — **[CHAR-6]** G — Full dimensional sharing data not yet complete. From available evidence: Wonder's primary Cognition dimension is partially shared with characteristics that engage the mind (Gladness's cognitive-evaluative dimension; Delight's cognitive-wisdom register); Wonder's secondary Emotion-Positive dimension is shared with Joy, Gladness, Delight, and Pleasure; Wonder's Relational-Disposition dimension (communal proclamation) is shared with characteristics that have strong communal expression.
- `T6.7.1` · cluster_finding · /gap — **[CHAR-7]** G — Dimensional sharing data across all 7 M04 characteristics is not yet complete — Characteristics 4–6 have not been processed. From available evidence: the affective-Spirit dimension is shared with the Spirit-mediated registers of Joy (Characteristic 2); the volitional dimension is shared with Joy's commanded registers; the eschatological dimension is shared with Joy's eschatological registers. What is unique to Characteristic 7 is the constitutive role of suffering as the occasion of the joy.
- `T6.7.2` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — **[CLUSTER]** G — Awaits Session D; partial evidence confirms Joy as the most dimensionally-distributed characteristic and Wonder as the most dimensionally-specific (cognitively-perceptive-limit).
- `T6.7.2` · cluster_finding · /gap — **[CHAR-1]** G — Not yet determinable. Data needed: Phase 9 findings for Characteristics 2–7.
- `T6.7.2` · cluster_finding · /gap — **[CHAR-2]** G — Not yet fully determinable. The partial evidence suggests Joy shares more dimensions with more characteristics than any other characteristic in M04 — a finding consistent with Joy being the cluster's central characteristic and the one most semantically entangled with adjacent states.
- `T6.7.2` · cluster_finding · /gap — **[CHAR-3]** G — Not yet fully determinable. The partial evidence suggests Gladness shares its cognitive-evaluative dimension with the programme's wisdom-related characteristics, and its affective-warmth dimension with the positive inner-being cluster (Joy, Delight, Pleasure). Gladness may be the most dimensionally-distributed characteristic in M04 given its tov-root's breadth.
- `T6.7.2` · cluster_finding · /gap — **[CHAR-4]** G — Incomplete — partial evidence suggests Delight's dimensional profile is the widest in M04, spanning affective-positive, volitional, moral-character, relational-disposition, and transformation dimensions simultaneously. This breadth is consistent with its five-sub-group structure and 247-verse corpus.
- `T6.7.2` · cluster_finding · /gap — **[CHAR-5]** G — Incomplete; to be addressed in Session D.
- `T6.7.2` · cluster_finding · /gap — **[CHAR-6]** G — Incomplete; to be addressed in Session D.
- `T6.7.2` · cluster_finding · /gap — **[CHAR-7]** G — Not yet fully determinable. The partial evidence suggests Suffering-Joy's dimensional profile is largely shared with Joy but with suffering as the constitutive distinguishing dimension rather than a different set of dimensions. Suffering-Joy may not require a wholly separate dimension vocabulary but rather a contextual qualifier (suffering-context) applied to the Joy dimensions.
- `T6.7.3` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — **[CLUSTER]** G — Full dimensional sharing data is not yet available; Session D is required for the complete cross-characteristic dimensional mapping. This is the primary gap in the cluster's Phase 9 synthesis.  ---  ## T7 — Lexical, Literary, and Human Science Analysis

### M05  (128 findings)

**T1.2.3 · Best working description**  (8)

- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — [Sub-group not separately addressed in source — see cluster-level finding for this prompt]
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — Love is the inner orientation of directed attachment — stable, covenantally constituted, constitutively relational, and constitutively directional (defined by its object). Primary location: the heart. Structural opposite: hatred and disordered-directedness.
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — Compassion is the inner movement triggered by encounter with suffering — visceral-somatic in its primary register (womb/bowel vocabulary), generating action without deliberative gap. Primary location: the gut/bowel level of the inner being. Structural opposite: judicial withholding.
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — Mercy is the relational-judicial disposition of the greater toward the lesser — operating in the frame where guilt, condemnation, or helplessness is the operative context, and where the mercy of the authoritative party is what determines the outcome. Primary location: the moral-judicial register of the person. Structural opposite: mercilessness as character failure.
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — Kindness and goodness is the inner quality of gracious generous goodwill rooted in God's own character — constitutive moral excellence (goodness), relational disposition toward the other (kindness), ongoing practice under cost (doing good), and undivided giving orientation (haplotēs). Primary location: the heart. Structural opposite: evil/ponēros and severity/apotomia.
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — Gentleness is the settled inner disposition of the heart — strength under control — that yields where it could assert, endures where it could retaliate, and receives rather than resists. Primary location: the heart ("hidden person of the heart" — 1Pe 3:4 — Group 1261). Structural opposite: quarrelsomeness and coercive self-assertion.
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — Comfort and encouragement is the inner orientation directed specifically toward the distressed, grieving, or faltering person — bringing relief to inner grief, consolation as a sustained gift, and the calling-alongside that strengthens for continued living. Primary location: soul and heart level. Structural opposite: the absent comforter and the false comforter.
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — Fellowship and participation is the inner-relational bond of shared life — holding things in common, participating together in a shared reality, belonging to one another through a mutually constitutive bond. Vertical (with God) and horizontal (with one another) simultaneously constituted. Primary location: spirit (Trinitarian ground), soul (isopsuchos), mind (homofrōn). Structural opposite: darkness and estrangement.

**T1.8 · Dimension Classification**  (24)

- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — The primary dimensions across M05 are Relational (all seven) and Moral Character (A, D, E). The cluster is constitutively relational — every characteristic requires at least one other person for its operation. The secondary dimensions vary by sub-group and provide the distinguishing features between them.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — Primary: Relational Disposition (love as constitutively directional attachment). Secondary: Moral Character (chesed as heart-inscription — Pro 3:3 — Group 1584), Volition (commanded love), Affect (ahavah and phileō registers).
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — Primary: Relational-Affective inner movement (somatic-visceral trigger). Secondary: Volition (judicial-withholding mode), Agency (movement toward the sufferer).
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — Primary: Relational-Judicial (mercy in the authority-guilt frame). Secondary: Moral Character (eleēmōn as character quality), Covenantal-Dispositional.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — Primary: Moral Character/Virtue (goodness and kindness as constitutive inner qualities). Secondary: Relational-ethical (outward direction), Volitional (commanded practice), Generosity.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — Primary: Moral Character/Virtue (settled heart disposition — 1Pe 3:4 — Group 1261). Secondary: Relational-ethical (mode of engagement with others), Eschatological.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — Primary: Relational-Affective (constitutively relational and primarily affective in its operation). Secondary: Volitional (giving comfort is chosen), Communal, Eschatological.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — Primary: Relational-Communal (the irreducibly shared bond). Secondary: Moral (moral solidarity), Formative, Eschatological.

**T2.8 · Body-Deposit (DNA / generational)**  (24)

- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — Silent across all seven sub-groups. No verse in M05 evidences a bodily constitutional deposit from sustained operation of any characteristic. This is a significant cluster-level finding: the cluster's characteristics produce inner deposits (good conscience in M05-D, established character in M05-A) and relational deposits (the comforter-capacity in M05-F, the fellowship-bond in M05-G), but these are not somatic. M05-B's bowel/womb vocabulary is the strongest candidate for somatic constitutional grounding but even this is not evidenced as a lasting deposit — it describes the location of the compassion-stirring, not a permanent bodily change from sustained compassion.

**T6.7 · Dimensional Sharing**  (24)

- `T6.7.1` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — Dimensional overlap data is not yet available from the database (G-code T6.7.3 carried from M05-A through M05-G). Preliminary assessment from the sub-group passes: the primary dimensions of M05 (Relational Disposition, Moral Character, Relational-Affective movement, Relational-Communal) are shared extensively across sub-groups. The most widely shared dimensions are Relational (all seven sub-groups), Moral Character (M05-A, D, E), and Eschatological (M05-A, C, E, F, G). The least shared dimension is Somatic-Visceral (exclusively M05-B). Full quantitative assessment requires CC to run the dimensional overlap query.

### M06  (128 findings)

**T1.2.3 · Best working description**  (8)

- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — [Sub-group not separately addressed in source — see cluster-level finding (cluster_subgroup_id IS NULL) for this prompt]
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — Hatred (M06-A) is a settled inner disposition of the person oriented against another person, quality, or reality — constitutionally located in the heart and soul, engaging the relational and volitional faculties, producing either sustained enmity (when directed at persons) or moral alignment (when directed at evil in the pattern of God's own hatred). It is inherently relational and directional, always requiring an object, intensifying when not resolved, and concealing itself when expression is dangerous.
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — Contempt (M06-B) is an inner act and habit of the moral evaluation faculty in which the person assigns low or no worth to what is sacred, covenantally significant, or inherently worthy — constitutionally located in the heart, engaging the moral evaluation and cognitive faculties, producing a closed inner posture that excludes the despised object from its proper claim on the person's honour, compliance, or reverence.
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — Abhorrence (M06-C) is a visceral inner moral recoil from what is perceived as fundamentally repugnant to the person's moral constitution — constitutionally located at the conscience and moral-evaluation level, engaging perception and the body's resonance registers, producing maintained distance from the object of revulsion. It is shared between God and the aligned person, directionally determined entirely by context, and at its eschatological terminus becomes a permanent condition of the unrighteous.
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — Cruelty/Ruthlessness (M06-D) is a constitutive inner character quality of the person — not a disposition triggered by circumstance but a settled quality defining what the person is — characterised by the absence of mercy, restraint, and God-consciousness, and expressed in the unrestrained exercise of destructive power toward others. It has no righteous form; it is constitutively defined by the absence of what would make it ordered.
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — Reproach (M06-E) is the inner-social condition of being publicly designated as an object of shame and contempt — received from outside but felt and borne at the level of the heart and personal identity. It is constitutional in that it shapes the whole person's way of existing in the world, yet its source is in the disordered inner lives of those who hate, despise, and mistreat. Its eschatological trajectory is complete removal and the restoration of honour.
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — Hostility/Enmity (M06-F) is a relational structure of structured opposition — the adversary relationship — that generates characteristic inner-being responses in the person who faces it: appeal, urgency, vigilance, and relief. The inner-being content resides in the person facing the adversary (not in the adversary's own inner state), and the appropriate response is grounded in relationship with God as the one who contends for the person.
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — Malice (M06-G) is a compound inner disposition of the soul — the simultaneous operation of sustained enmity, contempt for the other's worth, and active pleasure in their destruction — explicitly located in the soul (nephesh), producing bodily expression (clapping, stamping), and characterised by self-perpetuating intensity (the pleasure renews itself in the continued destruction of the other).  ---  ### T1.3 — Boundary

**T1.8 · Dimension Classification**  (24)

- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — [Sub-group not separately addressed in source — see cluster-level finding (cluster_subgroup_id IS NULL) for this prompt]
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **Relational Disposition.** Hatred is constitutively relational — it always requires an object, always involves the positioning of the self against another. Every group in M06-A involves a relationship: person against person, spouse against spouse, person against evil (in alignment with God's relational stance), God's relational stance toward sin.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **Moral Character.** Contempt is an act of moral evaluation — the heart assigning low or no worth to what deserves high worth. It operates through the moral evaluation faculty rather than primarily through the relational capacity. The moral failure is in the evaluation (wrong verdict about worth), not in the relational orientation.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **Moral Character.** Abhorrence operates as the moral evaluation faculty's most visceral response to genuine evil — at the level of the conscience's deepest reactions. The distinction from contempt: contempt is the cool verdict; abhorrence is the visceral recoil. Both engage moral evaluation but at different temperatures and depths.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **Moral Character** — but with a distinctive character: cruelty is **moral character by absence** — the condition of being constituted without the character qualities that would restrain destructive impulses. It is not a wrong act of moral evaluation but the absence of positive moral qualities (mercy, fear of God, restraint).
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **Emotion — Negative.** Reproach is primarily an affective-experiential condition — the felt inner state of being publicly shamed. Psa 69:20's "broken my heart...in despair" names the affective dimension as the primary inner-being impact.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **Relational Disposition.** M06-F names a relational structure of structured opposition. Unlike the other sub-groups where the relational dimension is secondary, in M06-F the relational structure is the primary feature.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **Emotion — Negative** (compound). The primary affective orientation of the soul taking pleasure in another's destruction. Uniquely, M06-G's primary dimension is also constitutively relational and moral — the three dimensions are co-constituent rather than primary and secondary.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — [Sub-group not separately addressed in source — see cluster-level finding (cluster_subgroup_id IS NULL) for this prompt]
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — Every group in M06-A involves a relationship: person against person (1601), spouse against spouse (550-NEW-01), person against evil in alignment with God (550-NEW-02), God's relational stance toward sin (3595, 3596).
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — 1Sa 2:17: contempt of the Lord's offering named as "very great sin" — a moral failure. Gen 25:34: Esau's despising of the birthright is an act of wrong moral evaluation (valuing stew above covenant inheritance).
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — Every group in M06-C involves a moral evaluation of something as repugnant: God abhorring pride (248-001), the righteous abhorring falsehood (252-001), the corrupt abhorring justice (252-002), God abhorring covenant violators (252-003).
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — Pro 12:10: "the mercy of the wicked is cruel" — character-level operation pervasive through all acts. Psa 54:3's "do not set God before themselves" names the absent character quality.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — "Broken my heart" (Psa 69:20), "in despair," "I am a worm and not a man" (Psa 22:6), "great trouble and shame" (Neh 1:3) — consistently affective vocabulary throughout M06-E.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — Every M06-F term names a position or role in a relational structure, not an inner quality of the person occupying that position.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — Eze 25:6: "rejoiced with all the malice within your soul"; Eze 36:5: "wholehearted joy" — the affective content (joy, pleasure) is primary.
- `T1.8.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — [Sub-group not separately addressed in source — see cluster-level finding (cluster_subgroup_id IS NULL) for this prompt]
- `T1.8.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — Secondary: **Moral Character** (groups 550-NEW-02 and 3595 — hatred as moral quality and divine inner-being reality) and **Emotion — Negative** (the affective quality is present throughout, particularly in group 1200's sa.tam and 2Sa 13:15's "very great hatred").
- `T1.8.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — Secondary: **Relational Disposition** (contempt has relational consequences — estrangement, Isa 1:4) and **Cognition** (the cognitive grounding of contempt — the prior verdict that God will not call to account, Psa 10:13).
- `T1.8.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — Secondary: **Emotion — Negative** (the affective register of moral disgust) and **Dependence/Creatureliness** in the eschatological dimension (de.ra.on — the permanent condition of the unrighteous names the creaturely position of those who definitively rejected their Creator).
- `T1.8.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — Secondary: **Agency/Power** (cruelty operates through the exercise of power; ruthlessness is the disposition of those with power acting without divine constraint). **Emotion — Negative** (in the recipient register — group 1775-002 — fear, distress, longing for deliverance).
- `T1.8.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — Secondary: **Relational Disposition** (reproach fundamentally alters relational standing). **Dependence/Creatureliness** (the corporate mode — Israel's corporate reproach marks their dependence on God for vindication and restoration). The extensive appeal literature of M06-E (Psa 69:13, Jer 15:15, Joel 2:17, Lam 5:1) is the voice of creatureliness in its most vulnerable form.
- `T1.8.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — Secondary: **Agency/Power** (the adversary relationship is a power contest; the inner responses it generates — appeal, vigilance — are responses to a power differential).
- `T1.8.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — The three dimensions (Emotion — Negative, Relational Disposition, Moral Character) are co-constituent rather than primary and secondary. M06-G is the only sub-group where multiple dimensions are constitutive of the characteristic rather than one primary and others secondary.  ---  *End of T0 and T1 — continuing in next section*

**T2.8 · Body-Deposit (DNA / generational)**  (24)

- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — [Sub-group not separately addressed in source — see cluster-level finding (cluster_subgroup_id IS NULL) for this prompt]
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — Yes. Exo 20:5 and Deu 5:9 (group 3596, anchor Exo 20:5): "visiting the iniquity of the fathers on the children to the third and fourth generation of those who hate me." The sustained inner disposition of hating God produces a consequence that extends beyond the individual into the generations — not merely as social inheritance but as covenantal consequence. The iniquity is "visited," meaning God himself acts on the generational connection. The deposit is both sociological (children raised in a household of God-hatred) and covenantal-theological (the covenant holds the generations together under the consequence of the ancestors' inner orientation).
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — No explicit generational deposit evidenced. The social propagation of contempt (group 1281-001, biz.za.von — Est 1:18) shows that contempt spreads across a social system from one person's act, but this is lateral social contagion, not generational deposit. No verse names contempt as producing a constitutional bodily or generational consequence.
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — No explicit generational deposit evidenced. The eschatological permanence of de.ra.on (Dan 12:2, Isa 66:24) is the closest — but this is a final-state consequence, not a generational transmission. The permanent condition of being an abhorrence (Isa 66:24) is the eschatological terminus, not a deposit passed to subsequent generations.
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — No explicit generational deposit evidenced. The corporate/national consequences of cruelty are named (covenantal judgment falls on ruthless nations) but not as a constitutional bodily deposit in the way M06-A's hatred of God produces generational iniquity-visitation.
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — A personal constitutional deposit (not generational). Isa 54:4: "you will forget the shame of your youth, and the reproach of your widowhood you will remember no more" — the fact that the reproach must be *forgotten* implies it has been deposited at a level where it persists in memory and shapes ongoing experience. The reproach has left a constitutional deposit in the person's inner life requiring not merely cessation of the external condition but active new identity formation and forgetting.
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — No generational deposit evidenced.
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — No generational deposit evidenced. The "never-ending enmity" (Eze 25:15) is self-perpetuating within the person or nation that holds it, not transmitted to subsequent generations through a constitutional deposit.
- `T2.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — [Sub-group not separately addressed in source — see cluster-level finding (cluster_subgroup_id IS NULL) for this prompt]
- `T2.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — Exo 20:5 and Deu 5:9 are structural — the generational consequence is named as a constitutive element of the divine covenant, not an incidental observation. The pairing of "those who hate me" with the generational consequence appears in both the Exodus and Deuteronomy Decalogue texts, confirming its structural weight. Psa 105:25 provides a second dimension: "He turned their hearts to hate his people" — God redirected Egypt's inner orientation toward hatred of Israel as part of his purposes, and the subsequent Pharaoh was formed in a social and inner environment shaped by that generational hostility.
- `T2.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — [Sub-group not separately addressed in source — see cluster-level finding (cluster_subgroup_id IS NULL) for this prompt]
- `T2.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — [Sub-group not separately addressed in source — see cluster-level finding (cluster_subgroup_id IS NULL) for this prompt]
- `T2.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — [Sub-group not separately addressed in source — see cluster-level finding (cluster_subgroup_id IS NULL) for this prompt]
- `T2.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — Isa 54:4's requirement of active forgetting is the evidential basis for the personal deposit. The need to *forget* the reproach — not merely to cease experiencing it — implies it has been encoded in the memory at a level that persists independently of the external condition. Restoration is therefore not automatic but requires new formation.
- `T2.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — [Sub-group not separately addressed in source — see cluster-level finding (cluster_subgroup_id IS NULL) for this prompt]
- `T2.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — [Sub-group not separately addressed in source — see cluster-level finding (cluster_subgroup_id IS NULL) for this prompt]

The generational deposit finding in M06-A (hatred of God) is characteristic-specific, not a cluster-wide finding.  ---  ### T2.9 — Origin and Source

**T5.7 · Deposit Consequence**  (24)

- `T5.7.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — [Sub-group not separately addressed in source — see cluster-level finding (cluster_subgroup_id IS NULL) for this prompt]
- `T5.7.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — Generational deposit: Exo 20:5 — "visiting the iniquity of the fathers on the children to the third and fourth generation of those who hate me." The developmental consequence is that the inner disposition of one generation shapes the covenantal standing of subsequent generations. The children of those who hate God inherit an iniquity-laden condition — they are born into the consequence of the prior generation's inner-being orientation. Psa 105:25 shows the mechanism from the other side: God redirected Egypt's inner orientation toward hatred of Israel. The Egyptians' children and the subsequent Pharaoh were formed in a social and inner environment shaped by that generational hostility. The deposit from one generation's hatred becomes the formational environment of the next.
- `T5.7.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — [Sub-group not separately addressed in source — see cluster-level finding (cluster_subgroup_id IS NULL) for this prompt]
- `T5.7.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — [Sub-group not separately addressed in source — see cluster-level finding (cluster_subgroup_id IS NULL) for this prompt]
- `T5.7.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — [Sub-group not separately addressed in source — see cluster-level finding (cluster_subgroup_id IS NULL) for this prompt]
- `T5.7.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — Personal constitutional deposit: Isa 54:4 — "you will forget the shame of your youth, and the reproach of your widowhood you will remember no more." The developmental consequence of the personal deposit is that restoration from reproach requires not merely the cessation of the external condition but active new identity formation. The reproach has been encoded in memory at a level that persists independently. Restoration therefore requires active forgetting and the formation of a new identity — the prior identity organised around the reproach-condition must be replaced.
- `T5.7.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — [Sub-group not separately addressed in source — see cluster-level finding (cluster_subgroup_id IS NULL) for this prompt]
- `T5.7.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — [Sub-group not separately addressed in source — see cluster-level finding (cluster_subgroup_id IS NULL) for this prompt]
- `T5.7.2` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — Generational deposit is not evidenced for contempt (M06-B), abhorrence (M06-C), cruelty (M06-D), hostility/enmity (M06-F), or malice (M06-G). The generational deposit finding in M06-A (hatred of God) is characteristic-specific. M06-E has a personal constitutional deposit but not a generational one.
- `T5.7.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — Yes, explicitly. Exo 20:5 and Deu 5:9 are structural in the Decalogue: the generational consequence is named as a constitutive element of the divine covenant. The iniquity is "visited" — God himself acts on the generational connection. The deposit is both sociological (children raised in an environment of God-hatred) and covenantal-theological (the covenant holds the generations together under the consequence).

- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — Lev 19:17 — shared with the love-of-neighbour command (Lev 19:18); the two commands share a verse-boundary. Psa 45:7 — cited in Heb 1:9; shared with Christological studies and the love-of-righteousness characteristic. Pro 8:13 — shared with fear of the Lord, wisdom, and pride/arrogance studies (all named in the same verse). Exo 20:5 — shared with jealousy (God as jealous), idolatry, and covenant studies. Ecc 9:6 — shared with love studies and envy/jealousy studies.
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — Isa 53:3 — shared with abhorrence (M06-C: ba.zah and ta.av both used of the Servant's experience), suffering, Servant of the Lord studies, and Christological studies. Mal 1:6 — shared with honour, worship, and priestly formation studies. Gen 25:34 — shared with covenantal inheritance and love studies.
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — Dan 12:2 — shared with M06-E (cher.pah also appears in that verse), resurrection, eschatology, and judgment studies. - `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — Psa 54:3 — shared with fear of God, prayer under persecution, and the wicked person studies. Pro 11:17 — shared with kindness and mercy studies. - `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — Psa 69:20 — cited in NT contexts (Psa 69:9 → Rom 15:3; John 15:25 etc.); shared with Christological, suffering, and atonement studies. Isa 25:8 — cited in 1Cor 15:54; shared with resurrection and eschatology studies. Jos 5:9 — shared with circumcision and covenant studies. Dan 12:2 — shared with M06-C (de.ra.on also in that verse).
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — Exo 23:22 — shared with covenant, divine advocacy, and enemy studies. 1Pe 5:8 — shared with spiritual warfare, prayer, devil, and watchfulness studies.
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — Eze 25:6 — shared with Ammon judgment studies. Eze 36:5 — shared with restoration of Israel studies (Eze 36 is the restoration chapter — the judgment of malice in v.5 precedes the restoration promise).

**T6.7 · Dimensional Sharing**  (24)

- `T6.7.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **Resolved 2026-05-06 via DB query.** M06 groups carry 43 dimension assignments across 8 distinct dimensions in wa_dimension_index: 06 Relational Disposition (15), 05 Moral Character (12), 11 Divine-Human Correspondence (6), 02 Emotion–Negative (4), 04 Volition (1), 03 Cognition (1), 10 Dependence/Creatureliness (1), plus 3 NULL. Every dimension that M06 carries is broadly shared across the cluster system. Cross-cluster sharing counts: 06 Relational Disposition (46 clusters), 05 Moral Character (44), 11 Divine-Human Correspondence (43), 04 Volition (42), 03 Cognition (40), 02 Emotion–Negative (38), 10 Dependence/Creatureliness (31). M06 is therefore not dimensionally distinctive — its analytical specificity comes from WHICH dimensions dominate (Relational Disposition + Moral Character lead) rather than which dimensions are uniquely engaged.

- `T6.7.2` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — The Relational Disposition dimension (primary for M06-A and M06-F) is shared with love, covenant, and the fellowship characteristics in C17 — because love and hatred are the two poles of the relational faculty organised toward or against the other. The Moral Character dimension (primary for M06-B, C, D) is shared with righteousness, holiness, wisdom, and conscience characteristics. The Emotion — Negative dimension (primary for M06-E and M06-G) is shared with grief, shame, and fear characteristics. These provisional observations follow from the nature of the dimensions, but exact sharing counts require database verification.

### M07  (112 findings)

**T1.2.3 · Best working description**  (7)

- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — **[CLUSTER]** E — At the cluster level, the six working descriptions collectively define M07 as the cluster of shame in all its forms — the inner-being domain in which human dignity, worth, and moral standing are exposed, stripped, violated, assessed, and (in Char 6) protected. The cluster covers: what shame feels like from within (Char 1), how it is imposed from without (Char 2), how it is enacted through relational worth-denial (Char 3), how it is identified as a quality of conduct (Char 4), how it is produced by contempt and received by the person (Char 5), and what the inner moral state is that protects against it (Char 6). The six descriptions together constitute a complete inner-being account of the shame-field: its phenomenology, mechanism, relational form, evaluative dimension, contempt-production, and moral counter.
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-1]** E — Shame as experienced inner state is the felt collapse of a person's inner dignity, confidence, and standing — produced by exposure to what exceeds one's moral capacity, by the failure or destruction of what one trusted, or by the discovery of one's own moral failure before God and community. The experience is inner (the collapse happens within), relational (it always has a referent — God, enemies, community, self-assessment), and directional (it drives the person toward concealment, withdrawal, or stillness). It manifests across three registers in the evidence: as an unjust wound received from enemies or circumstances (M07-A), as a deserved moral consequence of sin and folly (M07-B), and as the appropriate inner response of the person before God's holiness (M07-C). The characteristic is primarily a condition rather than an act, though it has volitional dimensions. Its vocabulary centres on *bosh*, *ke.lim.mah*, *aischunē*, and their cognates.
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-2]** E — Humiliation as enforced abasement is the condition of a person, people, or pride-structure whose standing has been actively moved downward by an external agent. The mechanism is external enforcement: the person is brought low rather than choosing lowness. The experience is primarily social and bodily (disgrace that bars re-entry to public life, the retreat of the defeated, the stripped crown) but carries an inner dimension. Primary vocabulary: ka.lam, sha.phel, bosh (defeat/abasement register), atimia (mortal dishonor), qa.lon (pride reversed).
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-3]** E — Dishonour as relational worth-denial is the act or condition of treating a person as having less worth than they actually possess — either through the willful refusal to acknowledge worth (VCG-01) or through the objective assignment of diminished standing (VCG-02). It operates relationally, always with a dishonour-er and a dishonoured. Primary vocabulary: atimazō, atimia, entrepō (in its absence register), paradeigmatizō, kataischunō (in the head-covering symbolic-disrespect register).
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-4]** E — Shamefulness as moral-evaluative judgment is the conscience's recognition that certain conduct, objects, or inner orientations violate the moral propriety established by God's standard and mediated through created order, community norms, and scripture. It is the evaluative-qualitative dimension of shame (what is shameful) rather than the experiential dimension (what shame feels like). Primary vocabulary: aischros, aschēmosunē. Secondary: aischunē and atimia in their evaluative registers. Three registers: the shameful quality of conduct/objects, the shameful quality of disordered desires, and the inverted evaluative faculty that treats shameful as honourable.
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-5]** E — Shame produced by contempt and rejection is the condition of being treated as of diminished or no worth by another's active contempt — whether through dismissive inner attitude (VCG-01), violent mockery and rejection (VCG-02), or reputation-destroying speech (VCG-03). The mechanism is contempt (M06's characteristic); the characteristic within M07 is the shame produced in the recipient by that contempt. Cross-register note: M06 (contempt-projection) is the source-side; CHAR-5 is the received face of the same dynamic. Primary vocabulary: exoutheneō (the dominant term across VCG-01's 11 verses); atimazō (Mar 12:4; Luk 20:11 in VCG-02); loidoria (1Ti 5:14; 1Pe 3:9 in VCG-03).
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-6]** E — Innocence as structural counter to shame is the inner moral state of a blameless conscience and clean-handed will — the condition in which the person is free from the deliberate moral failure that would produce guilt-shame before God or community. CHAR-6 is the positive moral condition that protects against M07-C's guilt-shame register: when CHAR-6 is present, the person can approach God without the barrier of guilt-shame; when CHAR-6 is absent or its capacity is lost, the guilt-shame condition of M07-C is inevitable. Primary vocabulary: niq.qa.von (H5356A) and qe.ha.von (H5356B), shared across the 4 verse-records (8 verse_context rows because of the two-term structure). Cross-register note: M12 (purity/holiness) is the terms' primary register; they are retained in M07 because every verse evidences the innocence-shame polarity directly.

**T1.8 · Dimension Classification**  (21)

- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — **[CLUSTER]** E — The six primary dimensions do not converge on a single category, confirming that M07 is multi-dimensional: Char 1 (emotional/affective primary), Char 2 (social/relational primary), Char 3 (relational primary), Char 4 (moral-evaluative primary — the most directly moral-dimension characteristic in the cluster), Char 5 (relational primary), Char 6 (moral primary). Cluster-level pattern: the relational dimension appears as primary in three characteristics (Chars 2, 3, 5) and as significant in all six. M07 is therefore most fundamentally a relational cluster — its characteristics describe what happens to persons in relation to God, to other persons, and to moral community. The moral dimension is the second most prominent (Chars 1, 4, 6). The affective dimension, while phenomenologically prominent in Char 1, is not the primary dimension of the majority.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-1]** E — The primary dimension is **emotion/affect** — shame as experienced inner state is fundamentally a felt reality, the collapse of inner dignity and standing as an experienced event. The bosh corpus, the ke.lim.mah corpus, the aischunē corpus all centre on the phenomenology of the experience. However, the evidence also warrants classification of strong **moral dimension**: shame in the M07-B and M07-C registers is always morally weighted — it either signals accurate moral reality (deserved shame) or is produced in the absence of moral reality (unjust wound). And there is a **volitional dimension**: the capacity to refuse, accept, or deploy shame shows that the will is deeply engaged.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-2]** E — Primary dimension is social/relational — CHAR-2 operates at the level of a person's standing within their community, their political order, and before God. It is primarily a relational-status phenomenon. The bodily-visibility of CHAR-2 (shaved beards, stripped crowns, nakedness exposed) confirms the social-dimension primacy.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-3]** E — The primary dimension is relational — CHAR-3 is constituted by the relation between persons and specifically by the misassignment of worth within that relation. It is not primarily affective, cognitive, or volitional — though it engages all of these — but relational: it is what one person does to another in the space between them.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-4]** E — Primary dimension: moral-evaluative — CHAR-4 is specifically the conscience's evaluative operation, making it the most directly moral-dimension characteristic in M07. Secondary dimension: volitional (VCG-03 — the perverted will that loves dishonour). The moral-evaluative primary is distinctive to CHAR-4 within M07 and marks it as the characteristic most directly about the conscience's functioning.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-5]** E — The primary dimension is relational — CHAR-5 is constituted by the contempt-relation between persons, and its damage is in the relational field. The strong secondary dimension is affective: the shame produced by contempt is felt (it wounds the person's sense of worth). A volitional dimension is also present: the pull toward retaliation (1Pe 3:9) shows that the will is engaged when CHAR-5 is received.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-6]** E — The primary dimension is moral — CHAR-6 is specifically the inner moral condition of blameless conscience and clean will. It is the moral dimension characteristic most directly positive in M07: it names what the image-bearer should be (innocent, morally clean) rather than what goes wrong (shame, humiliation, dishonour). The moral dimension of CHAR-6 is primary because innocence is constituted by the moral choices and moral condition of the person.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — **[CLUSTER]** E — The relational primary across Chars 2, 3, and 5 is confirmed by the consistent social enactment of these characteristics: the shaved envoys cannot return to public life (Char 2 — 2Sa 10:5), the poor man's worth is violated in the community's relational field (Char 3 — Jam 2:6), the contemptuous dismissal operates through the relational attitude of one person toward another (Char 5 — Luk 18:9; Rom 14:3). All three characteristics are constitutively about what happens in the space between persons. Char 6's relational dimension (innocence enabling approach to God) confirms that even the positive counter-characteristic is primarily about a relational state — the person before God — rather than a private inner condition.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-1]** E — For the emotional primary: Psa 44:15 (vc=37310) — "shame has covered my face" — the experiential, covering quality. Job 10:15 (vc=37502) — "I am filled with disgrace" — the inner saturation. Jer 20:18 (vc=37316) — "spend my days in shame" — the chronic felt condition. For the moral dimension: Jer 6:15 (vc=65551) — the absence of shame following abomination identifies shame as the morally appropriate response that is missing. Eze 43:11 — shame as the precondition for knowledge of God's order — places shame within a moral-relational framework. For the volitional: Heb 12:2 (vc=34970) — Jesus "despising the shame" — an act of the will over against the felt shame-pressure. 1Pe 4:16 — "let him not be ashamed, but let him glorify God" — an imperative directed at the will.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-2]** E — 2Sa 10:5 (vc=38517): "cannot return to public life until their beards have grown" — shame constituted by social recognition of the bodily sign. Jer 46:12 (vc=37512): "nations have heard of your shame, and the earth is full of your cry" — humiliation is public, heard, announced. Eze 44:13 (vc=37496): shame restricts relational access to God's presence. All confirm the social-relational dimension as primary.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-3]** E — Joh 8:49 — "you dishonor me" — is relational: the dishonour is directed at Jesus personally in a relational exchange. Jam 2:6 — "you have dishonored the poor man" — is relational: the poor man's worth is being violated in the relational field of the community. The entrepō evidence (Luk 18:2/4) is relational: the judge's absent-regard fails the relational obligation toward God and persons.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-4]** E — 2Cor 4:2 — "commend ourselves to everyone's conscience in the sight of God" — names the conscience as both CHAR-4's location and its relational field of operation. Eph 5:12 — "it is shameful even to speak" — shows the conscience recognising a threshold of moral degradation that identifies certain conduct as unspeakable. These confirm the moral-evaluative dimension as primary.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-5]** E — The relational primary: exoutheneō (VCG-01) is entirely about the relational attitude toward others — the inner disposition of the contempt-er toward the contempt-ed expressed in how the person is treated. The shame produced (in VCG-01's case: exclusion from proper regard, devaluation of ministry) is relational in its operation.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-6]** E — Gen 20:5 — "integrity of my heart and the innocence of my hands" — names the moral primary directly: the heart's moral integrity and the hands' moral cleanness are both moral conditions, not relational or social states. Psa 26:6's approach to God's altar based on moral innocence confirms the moral dimension as what enables or blocks the relational outcome (approach to God).
- `T1.8.3` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — **[CLUSTER]** E — The secondary dimensions across the six are consistent: moral (in Chars 2, 3, 5), affective (in Chars 1, 3, 5), volitional (in Chars 1, 3, 4, 5, 6). The absence of a single primary dimension that runs through all six is itself a cluster-level finding: M07 does not have a single constitutional dimension but a relational-moral-affective complex in which different characteristics foreground different aspects of the same unified shame-field.  --- ## T2 — Constitutional Location
- `T1.8.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-1]** E — The secondary dimensions (moral and volitional) do not compete with the emotional primary but qualify it. The evidence shows that shame's emotional register is never purely physiological or psychological — it is always morally weighted (the shame is either deserved or unjust, appropriate or seared). The volitional dimension qualifies how the emotional register is received and expressed. The three dimensions work as a system: the emotional (felt collapse), the moral (appropriate or not), and the volitional (received, borne, resisted, deployed) together constitute CHAR-1's full inner anatomy.  ---  ## T2 — Constitutional Location
- `T1.8.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-2]** E — Secondary dimensions: (1) Affective — inner turmoil, dismay, confusion accompanying CHAR-2 (Jer 49:23 — "melt in fear, troubled like the sea"). (2) Moral — VCG-03 grounds CHAR-2 in moral cause (pride -> disgrace). These do not compete with the social-relational primary but accompany it.  ---  ## T2 — Constitutional Location
- `T1.8.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-3]** E — Secondary dimensions: (1) Moral-evaluative — the dishonoring of persons presupposes and corrupts a moral-evaluative faculty. (2) Volitional — the active-dishonour mode (VCG-01) is willful — it requires a volitional act of refused-acknowledgment. These secondary dimensions accompany the relational primary without competing with it.  ---  ## T2 — Constitutional Location
- `T1.8.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-4]** E — Secondary dimensions: volitional (VCG-03 — the rulers "dearly love shame," Hos 4:18 — the will has chosen dishonour as its object) and affective (VCG-03 — glorying in shame, Phili 3:19 — the affective response to shamefulness is perverted into joy). These secondary dimensions accompany the moral-evaluative primary without competing with it.  ---  ## T2 — Constitutional Location
- `T1.8.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-5]** E — Secondary dimensions: affective (the wound of being treated as nothing), volitional (the pull toward retaliation). These accompany the relational primary without competing.  ---  ## T2 — Constitutional Location
- `T1.8.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-6]** E — Secondary dimensions: (1) Relational — CHAR-6 enables the relational condition of approach to God (Psa 26:6); its absence blocks the relational approach (Hos 8:5). (2) Volitional — the maintained innocence of Psa 73:13 requires active moral choice; the incapacity of Hos 8:5 is the loss of volitional capacity for moral purity. These accompany the moral primary without competing with it.  ---  ## T2 — Constitutional Location

**T2.8 · Body-Deposit (DNA / generational)**  (21)

- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — **[CLUSTER]** E — All six evidence some form of constitutional deposit, ranging from the permanent to the reversible-by-divine-act. Permanent negative deposits: Char 2's VCG-04 (shame carried into death — Eze 32:24-30; the warriors' permanent identity-record); Char 4's inverted conscience with trajectory toward destruction (Phili 3:19 — "their end is destruction"). Reversible-by-divine-act negative deposits: Char 1's shame-load addressed by divine promise (Isa 54:4 — "you will forget the shame of your youth"); Char 5's beggar-identity overridden by Jesus's calling (Mar 10:50). Positive deposits: Char 4's habituated moral discernment; Char 6's moral character formed through sustained innocence-maintenance (Psa 73:13 — "I have kept my heart clean" as a formative sustained practice). Cluster-level finding: the most serious deposits are those that damage the conscience-faculty (seared, inverted, capacity-lost) — these are the cluster's most severe outcomes and the ones requiring divine intervention for reversal.  ---
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence suggests a functional deposit but not a biological/genetic one. Pro 12:4 (vc=65525) — shame as "rottenness in the bones" — uses the bones as the metaphorical locus of deep inner damage, suggesting that sustained shame-experience leaves a structural deposit in the person's core. Eze 32:24-30 (the Elam warriors carrying their shame into the pit) suggests a deposit that persists beyond death — the shame is constitutionally part of the person's identity-record. However, the evidence does not explicitly name genetic or DNA-level deposit. The deposit appears to be at the level of the person's inner record and identity rather than biological inheritance.
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-2]** E — Yes. VCG-04 evidence (Eze 32:24-30; 44:13) shows CHAR-2 leaving a permanent deposit: shame carried into death (identity-record), priestly access permanently restricted (structural consequence). Jer 23:40 — "everlasting reproach and perpetual shame, which shall not be forgotten" — names the deposit as permanent identity-marking. The deposit is social-relational.  ---
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-3]** E — In VCG-02, the potter's assignment of vessels for dishonorable use (Rom 9:21) and the household of God with vessels for both honourable and dishonorable use (2Ti 2:20) suggest that dishonour can be a structural/constitutional condition. The Pass A meaning of 2Ti 2:20 notes "the inner person's moral condition determines their standing" — suggesting the deposit is produced by moral condition rather than arbitrary assignment.  ---
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-4]** E — VCG-03's sustained inverted conscience establishes a deposit: Hos 4:18's "deep affection for dishonour" — the rulers have moved from occasional wrong evaluation to constitutionally-structured love of what disgraces them. Phili 3:19's "their end is destruction" implies the deposit of the inverted conscience is eschatologically irreversible in its current trajectory. The functional conscience (VCG-01) produces the deposit of habituated moral discernment.  ---
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-5]** E — Mar 10:50's Bartimaeus evidences the deposit: a life lived under sustained social contempt produces a settled beggar-identity — the contempt-shame has deposited itself as a structural element of the person's inner self-understanding. The willingness to throw off the cloak in response to Jesus's call suggests the deposit can be overcome by a re-worth-assignment from a higher authority (Jesus's call overriding the social contempt-verdict).  ---
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-6]** E — Hos 8:5's incapacity for innocence evidences the deposit of its loss: sustained idolatry has so deeply distorted the inner moral faculty that innocence-capacity is no longer accessible. This is a negative-deposit: the accumulation of idolatrous compromise has deposited into the inner person as an incapacity rather than a capacity. The deposit's consequence is the permanent blocking of the relational access to God that CHAR-6 would enable.  ---
- `T2.8.2` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — **[CLUSTER]** E — All six evidence some form of constitutional deposit, ranging from the permanent to the reversible-by-divine-act. **T5.7 · Deposit Consequence**  (21)

- `T5.7.1` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — **[CLUSTER]** E — The cluster's deposit map spans permanent-negative to transformable-positive. Permanent negative deposits: Char 2's VCG-04 (shame carried into death — Eze 32:24-30); Char 4's inverted conscience with trajectory toward destruction. Reversible-by-divine-act negative deposits: Char 1's shame-load (Isa 54:4 — "you will forget"); Char 5's beggar-identity (overridden by Jesus's calling — Mar 10:50). Positive deposits: Char 4's habituated moral discernment; Char 6's moral character formed through sustained innocence-maintenance. Cluster-level deposit finding: deposits in the shame-field accumulate at three constitutional levels — conscience (Chars 1, 4, 6), identity (Chars 1, 2, 5), and relational access (Chars 2, 6). The most serious deposits are those that damage the conscience-faculty: the seared conscience, the inverted conscience, and the incapacity for innocence are not self-reversible and are presented within the evidence as requiring divine intervention for restoration. These represent the cluster's most severe constitutional outcomes.  ---
- `T5.7.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-1]** E — T2.8 identified a functional deposit in the inner person — the "rottenness in the bones" (Pro 12:4) image of shame as structural inner damage, and the shame carried generationally (Ezr 9:7). The developmental consequence of this deposit is identity-formation shaped by shame: when shame is sustained without resolution, it becomes part of the person's inner self-understanding. Jeremiah's "spend my days in shame" (Jer 20:18) shows a life formed around the chronic shame-condition. Israel's identity is partially constituted by its shame-history — the post-exilic community in Ezr 9:7 defines itself partly through its inherited shame. This deposit drives the need for divine shame-removal: the eschatological promises (Isa 54:4; Zep 3:19) are not addressing a momentary feeling but a constitutively deposited inner condition that has shaped the person's and community's sense of self.
- `T5.7.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-2]** E — The deposit identified at T2.8 (permanent identity-marking) produces sustained consequence of restricted access and ongoing identity. The Levites permanently restricted from priestly service (Eze 44:13) carry the deposit as a vocational limitation. The warriors bearing shame into the pit (Eze 32) carry the deposit as a permanent identity-record. The deposit's developmental consequence is the narrowing of the person's relational scope — what they can access, where they can go, who they can be before God and community.  ---
- `T5.7.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-3]** E — For the dishonorer: the sustained practice of worth-denial toward God or persons establishes an entrenched inner disposition (the Luk 18:2/4 judge's recognized character trait) and produces the self-undermining inner contradiction (Rom 2:23). The deposit in the dishonorer's inner person is the hardened incapacity for genuine worth-recognition. For the dishonoured in the structural mode: the assigned standing of dishonour may become an identity-condition (2Ti 2:20).  ---
- `T5.7.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-4]** E — Functioning CHAR-4 deposits moral discernment — the habituated capacity to recognise and renounce the shameful. Inverted CHAR-4 deposits moral blindness — the established incapacity to see what is actually shameful, and the established love of what disgraces (Hos 4:18). These deposits shape the person's long-term moral trajectory toward their eschatological destination.  ---
- `T5.7.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-5]** E — The deposit of Bartimaeus's beggar-identity (Mar 10:50) is the most direct evidence: sustained contempt deposits a shame-shaped self-understanding into the person's inner identity. The developmental consequence is the reduction of the person's sense of possibility (their plans are shamed, Psa 14:6), their ministry capacity is undermined (1Cor 16:11), and their relational standing is compromised. The reversal of this deposit requires an authoritative counter-word from outside (Jesus calling Bartimaeus) that overrides the accumulated contempt-deposit.  ---
- `T5.7.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-6]** E — The deposit of sustained CHAR-6 maintenance is moral character formed through the habitual practice of keeping the heart clean and washing the hands in innocence. Psa 73:13's "I have kept my heart clean" is not a one-time act but a sustained pattern — and this sustained pattern deposits a moral-character quality into the person. The deposit of CHAR-6's loss (Hos 8:5) is the incapacity-condition: the accumulated moral choices of idolatry have so deeply distorted the inner moral faculty that innocence-capacity itself is lost. This deposit requires divine intervention for reversal.  ---
- `T5.7.2` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — **[CLUSTER]** E — The cluster's deposit map spans permanent-negative to transformable-positive. Permanent negative deposits: Char 2's VCG-04 (shame carried into death — Eze 32:24-30); Char 4's inverted conscience with trajectory toward destruction. Reversible-by-divine-act negative deposits: Char 1's shame-load (Isa 54:4 — "you will forget"); Char 5's beggar-identity (overridden by Jesus's calling — Mar 10:50). Positive deposits: Char 4's habituated moral discernment; Char 6's moral character formed through sustained innocence-maintenance. Cluster-level deposit finding: deposits in the shame-field accumulate at three constitutional levels — conscience (Chars 1, 4, 6), identity (Chars 1, 2, 5), and relational access (Chars 2, 6). The most serious deposits are those that damage the conscience-faculty: the seared conscience, the inverted conscience, and the incapacity for innocence are not self-reversible and are presented within the evidence as requiring divine intervention for restoration. These represent the cluster's most severe constitutional outcomes.  ---
- `T5.7.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-1]** E — Yes. Ezr 9:7 — "from the days of our fathers to this day" — explicitly names generational shame-accumulation. Dan 9:7-8 — "to our kings, to our princes, and to our fathers, because we have sinned against you" — names the corporate-generational character of the shame-condition. Israel's covenant shame is inherited across generations; the community that stands before God in confession (Dan 9; Ezr 9) carries the shame of its ancestors as part of its own inner-being condition. The eschatological promise that shame will be "forgotten" (Isa 54:4) operates at the same generational level — the whole identity-constituting memory of shame will be dissolved.
