### M10  (352 findings)

**T1.2.3 · Best working description**  (22)

- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-1]** E — Working description: *Wilful sinning is a volitional act of the inner person — primarily located in the will — in which the person knowingly commits a wrong directed against God, another person, or the self. It engages the will as the primary faculty, the conscience as the witness (whether active in restraint, as in VCG-03, or present as the moral register that recognises the act as wrong), and moral evaluation as the standard against which the act is measured. Its impact is rupture: it damages the vertical relationship with God (producing guilt, divine anger, and the need for atonement), horizontal relationships with persons (producing harm and requiring forgiveness), and the person's own moral integrity (self-damage, Psa 51:4; Pro 8:36; 1Cor 6:18). The act creates a debt — moral, relational, and (in the cultic corpus) ritual — that requires resolution.* Evidence: Gen 39:9 (will knowingly refuses); Psa 51:4 (vertical rupture named); Mat 18:15 (horizontal harm requiring confrontation); Lev 4:28/35 (cultic debt and remedy); 1Jo 2:1 (advocate for the sinning believer).
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-2]** E — Working description: *Unintentional sinning is a real moral act committed by a person in the absence of deliberate defiance of a known command — the will acts but not against a consciously registered moral prohibition. The act creates objective moral liability before God (Lev 4:2; Num 15:27/28) regardless of intent. When the sin surfaces to awareness — through realisation, disclosure, or confrontation — conscience engages (Lev 5:5: "when he realizes his guilt... and confesses"; Num 22:34: "I have sinned, for I did not know"). The prescribed remedy (sin offering, priestly atonement, forgiveness declared) addresses the objective moral liability and restores the person's standing. The cultic-purification register (VCG-02) uses the same vocabulary for the cleansing of sacred spaces from cultic defilement, which carries minimal personal inner-being content.* Primary evidence: Lev 4:2; Num 15:27/28; Lev 5:5; Num 22:34.
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-3]** E — Working description: *Confession is the conscience-to-speech event in which the inner person names a committed sin as sin — directed toward God, persons, or community, and expressing a genuine or partial inner recognition of moral wrong. It is constitutively a speech-act rooted in conscience: conscience identifies the wrong (2Sa 24:10 — "David's heart struck him"), the will agrees to name it (1Ch 21:17 — "It is I who have sinned"), and the mouth speaks it (Luk 15:21 — "I have sinned against heaven and before you"). Genuine confession is followed by a plea for forgiveness, a reorientation of the will, or both (Num 21:7 — confession + intercession request; 2Sa 24:10 — confession + "please take away the iniquity"). Hollow confession (VCG-03) produces the verbal form without this inner reorientation (Deu 1:41).*
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-4]** E — Working description: *Conscience suppression is the inner condition in which the person's moral-witness faculty (conscience) fails to register, actively denies, or refuses to acknowledge their own sinful state. The suppression operates in three modes: active verbal denial of sin (Jer 2:35 — "I have not sinned"), genuine epistemic self-blindness (Jer 16:10 — "what sin have we committed?"), and habituated absence of the fear of God producing persistent sinning without inner recognition (Ecc 8:12; 1Cor 15:34). The person may be objectively sinful (Jer 2:35 — God will bring them to judgment; Tit 3:11 — self-condemned) while subjectively claiming innocence or remaining unaware of their condition. The characteristic is the inverse of CHAR-3 (Confession): where confession voices the conscience's verdict, conscience suppression silences or falsifies it.*
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-5]** E — Working description: *Refusal to repent is the sustained volitional condition in which the will persistently declines to turn from a sinful course that the person knows they are called to forsake. The characteristic is constituted by a series of non-departures — each moment the will could turn and does not — accumulating into a settled inner disposition of remaining on the sinful path. It ranges from passive non-departure (the Kings who "did not depart from" the sins of Jeroboam — 2Ki 13:2/11/14:24) to active clinging (2Ki 3:3 — "clung to the sin") to bodily-metaphor hardening (Neh 9:29 — "stubborn shoulder... stiffened neck"). In the NT, it reaches its sharpest form as deliberate ongoing sinning after knowledge of truth (Heb 10:26). The primary faculty is the will; the primary location is the heart in its incomplete-commitment mode (2Ki 10:31 — "not with all his heart").*
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-6]** E — Working description: *Habitual defection is the settled condition of the will in which sinning has become the default directional pattern rather than the episodic exception. It is characterised by: the person-type terms of the sinner (*hachotet*) as a constituted moral identity (Ecc 7:26; 9:2/18; Pro 11:31/13:22); continuous-present sinning as the operational state (1Jo 3:6 — "keeps on sinning"); the self-reinforcing dynamic of institutionalised sinning (Hos 8:11 — altars for sinning producing more sinning); and the historical persistence of the pattern across generations (Hos 10:9 — from Gibeah; Heb 3:17 — forty years). The characteristic is distinct from the single act (CHAR-1) and from the specific volitional refusal to turn (CHAR-5): it is what those characteristics produce when sustained — a formed inner disposition that requires no new decision to sin, because sinning has become the will's operational default.*
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-7]** E — Working description: *Contagious sin is the inner moral dynamic in which a leader's personal sinful commitment is transmitted to the community under their authority through the exercise of that authority. The transmission mechanism is the Hiphil causative: the leader does not merely sin themselves (Qal) but causes others to sin (Hiphil). The source is always the leader's inner will committed to the sinful course (Jeroboam "walked in the way" — 1Ki 16:26; kings "walked in his sin" — 1Ki 15:26); the channel is the authority relationship ("I exalted you... leader over my people" — 1Ki 16:2); the result is the community's redirected inner orientation away from God (2Ki 17:21 — "drove them from following the Lord"). The characteristic operates through three stages: inner source (leader's sinful will) → authority transmission (Hiphil causative) → structural deposit (altars, institutions — 2Ki 23:15 — that embed and perpetuate the transmitted orientation).*
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-8]** E — Working description: *Political revolt (CHAR-8) is the decisive volitional act of breaking submission to an established governing authority, expressed through the pa.sha (H6586) political-revolt register. The act involves both negative departure from governing submission (rebelling from, revolting from — 2Ki 8:20/22; 2Ch 21:8/10) and positive assertion of independent authority (setting up a king of their own — 2Ki 8:20). The characteristic operates in the political domain — governing relationships between peoples and kingdoms — rather than the personal moral domain. Its most distinctive analytical feature is the causal connection to the inner spiritual condition: 2Ch 21:10 links Libnah's revolt directly to Jehoram's forsaking of God, revealing that the inner abandonment of God produces the outer fragmentation of political-covenantal order. The revolt carries the moral weight of wilful transgression (as the pa.sha root confirms) while being expressed in the political register.*
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-9]** E — Working description: *Sinful speech (CHAR-9) is sin committed through the mouth — the externalisation of inner moral corruption in harmful, defaming, or presumptuous speech directed at God or persons. It operates across three registers: (1) slander and abusive speech generated from the corrupted heart and directed at persons (VCG-01 — "out of the heart come... slander," Mat 15:19); (2) blasphemy-as-divine-authority-claim — presumptuous speech usurping what belongs to God alone, treated in the NT judicial context as the charge against Jesus (VCG-02 — "He is blaspheming! Who can forgive sins but God alone?" Mar 2:7); (3) blasphemy-as-constitutive-identity — the creature whose very being is defined by defiant anti-God speech, most fully realised in Revelation's beast imagery (VCG-03 — "full of blasphemous names," Rev 17:3). The primary inner-being locus is the heart (Mat 15:19); the speech is the heart's outer expression.*
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-10]** E — Working description: *Specialised sinful mechanisms (CHAR-10) names three distinct inner pathways through which sin operates: (1) Enticement through desire — the person's own desire-faculty (epithumia) is the hook through which temptation lures and captures (Jam 1:14 — "lured and enticed by his own desire"; 2Pe 2:14 — "hearts trained in greed" as the enticement apparatus; 2Pe 2:18 — enticement targeting those barely free from error); (2) Collective hypocrisy — social identity is falsified through joining in acted performance alongside others, with community-corrupting contagion effect (Gal 2:13 — "acted hypocritically along with him, so that even Barnabas was led astray"); (3) Apostasy — covenantal identity is abandoned through inner departure from the covenantal standing one has received (Act 21:21 — "forsake Moses"). Each mechanism is cross-registered with another cluster: M14 (desire/temptation), M08 (pride/hypocrisy), M31 (faith/unbelief).*
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-11]** E — Working description: *Sin as universal condition (CHAR-11) is the structural inner-being state in which every human person stands — not primarily an act (CHAR-1) or a habitual pattern (CHAR-6) but the ontological condition of being under sin's dominion, falling short of God's glory, carrying a standing moral debt before God, and subject to death as the condition's consequence. The corpus evidences three registers of this condition: (1) Wickedness as constituted moral character — the settled inner quality of evil as a defining identity (VCG-01 — rish.ah, Mal 1:4; al.vah, Hos 10:9); (2) Sin as moral debt requiring divine forgiveness and removal — the standing obligation that the redemptive acts address (VCG-02 — hamartia in the forgiveness register); (3) Sin as an enslaving, indwelling power that rules over the person and produces death — the master relationship that resists the person's own will to good (VCG-03 — hamartia in the power register, especially Romans 5-8). Rom 3:23 — "all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God" — is the definitional summary: universal scope + structural deficiency from the created end.*
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-12]** E — Working description: *Sin as enslaving power (CHAR-12) is the characteristic in which sin operates as an active governing force that masters the inner person's will — specifically through the mechanism of habitual practice producing constitutional slavery (Joh 8:34 — "everyone who practices sin is a slave to sin") and through the universal structural imprisonment that Scripture discloses (Gal 3:22 — "imprisoned everything under sin"). The enslaved person is constitutionally captive: their will is no longer free toward God. The deepest marker of the enslaved condition is allegiance-direction: 1Jo 3:8 — "whoever makes a practice of sinning is of the devil." The liberation from enslavement occurs through new birth (1Jo 3:9 — "God's seed abides in him... he cannot keep on sinning") and through Christ's protective intervention (1Jo 5:18 — "he who was born of God protects him, and the evil one does not touch him"). The characteristic is differentiated by eschatological consequence: 1Jo 5:16 names a form of sin that leads to death as the enslaving power's terminal outcome.*
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-13]** E — Working description: *Sin as divine record (CHAR-13) is the standing objective moral condition in which every human person's sin exists before God as a retained, accumulated account — recorded, named, not hidden from divine memory, and not erased by the person's ignorance or concealment from others. The corpus evidences five sub-registers: (1) Individual moral liability (chet — VCG-01): each person bears their own sin-record; (2) Broad chat.tat record (VCG-05 — 113 verses): sin as an act that creates a moral fact requiring priestly resolution; (3) Cha.ta.ah as objective moral category requiring covering (VCG-02): the record requires a covering act; (4) The Jeroboam-sin legacy (VCG-04 — 46 verses): a named institutional sin-pattern transmitted across royal generations as a defined recorded legacy; (5) The Job surveillance register (VCG-03): God actively searching and monitoring for sin as a divine investigator. The characteristic's definitive image is Jer 17:1 — "the sin of Judah is written with a pen of iron; with a point of diamond it is engraved on the tablet of their heart" — the record is both divinely maintained and internally inscribed.*
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-14]** E — Working description: *Forgiveness sought and received (CHAR-14) is the bi-directional characteristic in which the inner person moves volitionally toward God in appeal for the removal of the sin-record (the seeking dimension), and God responds by declaring, granting, or enacting the covering/removal of that record (the receiving dimension). The corpus evidences this through three registers: (1) The Levitical kip.pu.rim atonement system (VCG-01 — 12 verses): institutionalised annual and occasional seeking through the prescribed sacrifice and priestly mediation; (2) The Levitical sin-offering (VCG-03 — 10 verses): individual and communal seeking through the chat.tat offering system when specific sins become known; (3) Christological forgiveness declaration (VCG-02 — 12 verses): seeking through repentance and faith and receiving through Christ's atoning blood and authoritative declaration. The characteristic's primary anchor is Mat 26:28 — "my blood of the covenant, which is poured out for many for the forgiveness of sins" — naming the definitive once-for-all mechanism that replaces the annual kip.pu.rim cycle.*
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-15]** E — Working description: *Generational sin (CHAR-15) is the corporate moral condition that originates in an ancestral sinning act (Isa 43:27 — "your first father sinned") and is sustained across generations through the ongoing sinning of successive communities who inherit and continue the pattern. The inner person carries a moral liability not only for their own acts but for the accumulated sinning history of those before them. The corpus (8 verses, all cha.ta — VCG-01) evidences three dimensions: (1) The originating ancestral act (Isa 43:27); (2) The persistent corporate sinning pattern sustained across generations (Psa 78:17/32; Isa 64:5 — "a long time in our sins"); (3) The corporate confession that names the condition before God (1Ki 8:47 — "we have sinned"; Jer 8:14 — "we have sinned against the Lord"). God's promise to cleanse the accumulated backsliding (Eze 37:23) names the eschatological resolution.*
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-16]** E — Working description: *The sinner as moral character (CHAR-16) is the constitutive inner moral identity of the person defined by sin — not primarily the act (CHAR-1) or the structural condition (CHAR-11) but what kind of person they are at their moral core. The corpus evidences three registers: (1) The extreme moral type (VCG-03 — 14 verses): persons whose moral character is thoroughly defined by sin — Sodom's "great sinners against the Lord" (Gen 13:13), the Amalekites devoted to destruction (1Sa 15:18); (2) The social-moral category (VCG-01 — 30 verses): the sinner as a publicly recognised class whose status carries exclusion from community and stigma — "tax collectors and sinners" as social pairing (Mat 9:10/11); (3) The constituted/imputed identity (VCG-02 — 20 verses): the sinner-identity as formally established status — "many were made sinners" through Adam (Rom 5:19); "Christ Jesus came into the world to save sinners" (1Ti 1:15). The primary anchor is Rom 5:19 for the constituted dimension and Gen 13:13 for the extreme-type dimension.*
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-17]** E — Working description: *Guilt as inner-being state (CHAR-17) is the conscience's recognition of moral liability before God — the subjective inner experience of being guilty, of bearing a wrong that cannot be hidden. The corpus evidences five registers: (1) The conscience-recognition moment (VCG-01 — 18 verses): guilt-realization arising when a violation becomes known — "when he realizes his guilt" (Lev 4:13/22/27; 5:2/3/4/5) — the inner trigger for confession and restitution; (2) Guilt as accumulated weight (VCG-02 — 19 verses): the mounting, compounding condition — Ezr 9:6 "our guilt has mounted up to the heavens" — guilt accumulating beyond the head; (3) Guilt as transferable burden (VCG-03 — 16 verses): the guilt-weight that can be borne by another — Aaron on his forehead (Exo 28:38); Abigail's self-substitution (1Sa 25:24); (4) Guilt's punitive-consequence dimension (VCG-04 — 18 verses): the a.von/punishment register — Cain's "my punishment is greater than I can bear" (Gen 4:13); Ezekiel's enacted bearing (Eze 4:4/5/6); (5) Guilt exposed, indelible, and removed (VCG-05 — 26 verses): Jer 2:22 — indelible despite soap; Isa 6:7 — taken away by divine act. The characteristic is distinguished from CHAR-13 (the objective divine record) by being the subjective inner experience of what that record means to the conscience.*
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-18]** E — Working description: *Iniquity as accumulated moral crime (CHAR-18) is the inner dimension of accumulated moral wrongdoing — crime in its moral-spiritual sense, the store of inner wrongdoing before God as a measured, generational, heart-seated reality. The corpus evidences eight registers: (1) Priestly bearing (VCG-01 — 17V): Aaron confessing Israel's iniquities over the scapegoat (Lev 16:21/22); (2) Generational transmission (VCG-02 — 18V): iniquity of fathers visited on children to the third and fourth generation (Exo 20:5); (3) Divine memory (VCG-03 — 28V): God's omniscient awareness — "iniquity of Ephraim is bound up; his sin is kept in store" (Hos 13:12); (4) Personal ownership (VCG-04 — 29V): each soul dies for its own crime — Eze 18:20; (5) Overwhelming weight (VCG-05 — 30V): "gone over my head; like a heavy burden, they are too heavy for me" (Psa 38:4); (6) Pardon and removal (VCG-06 — 21V): servant bears/is crushed for iniquities of all (Isa 53:5/6/11); (7) Self-destructive force (VCG-07 — 15V): "ensnare him, held fast in cords of his sin" (Pro 5:22); (8) Heart-seated origin (VCG-08 — 4V): "taken their idols into their hearts... stumbling block of their iniquity" (Eze 14:3/4/7). Distinguished from CHAR-17 (the subjective experience of guilt) by being the objective moral-crime reality that subsists before God whether felt or not.*
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-19]** E — Working description: *Transgression as deliberate boundary-crossing (CHAR-19) is the wilful defiance of known moral or covenantal order — the will that knowingly crosses a boundary or breaches a covenant. The corpus evidences nine registers: (1) Against God in covenant (VCG-01 — 27V): "children have I reared... but they rebelled" (Isa 1:2) — relational betrayal; (2) Personal moral breach (VCG-09 — 34V): "transgression speaks to the wicked deep in his heart" (Psa 36:1); (3) Interpersonal breach (VCG-08 — 19V): "love covers all offenses" (Pro 10:12); (4) Revolt (VCG-02 — 7V): "whole head is sick and whole heart faint" (Isa 1:5) — sa.rah's systemic collapse; (5) Oppressive weight (VCG-07 — 14V): "bound into a yoke... set upon my neck" (Lam 1:14); (6) Pardoned and erased (VCG-06 — 13V): "as far as east from west" (Psa 103:12); (7) Threshold crossing (VCG-03 — 13V): "for three transgressions... and for four" (Amo 1:3); (8) Law-violation (VCG-04 — 15V): "where there is no law there is no transgression" (Rom 4:15); (9) Adam-paradigm (VCG-05 — 5V): "one man's trespass" (Rom 5:15) — the foundational boundary-crossing. Distinguished from CHAR-1 (wilful sinning as act) by the emphasis on the knowing crossing of a known boundary, and from CHAR-18 (iniquity as accumulated crime) by being specifically the act-of-defiance rather than the accumulated moral-crime weight.*
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-20]** E — Working description: *Faithlessness as covenant-breaking sin (CHAR-20) is the inner disposition of deliberate unfaithfulness — the will that breaks covenantal loyalty, betrays a trust, acts treacherously against God or persons. Three registers: (1) Unfaithfulness toward God (VCG-01 — 57V): the primary covenant-betrayal directed upward — Judah taken into exile "because of their breach of faith" (1Ch 9:1); Saul died "for his breach of faith" (1Ch 10:13); Ahaz became "yet more faithless" under distress (2Ch 28:22); (2) Relational treachery between persons (VCG-02 — 34V): the horizontal faithlessness within human covenant bonds — "guard yourselves in your spirit, and do not be faithless to the wife of your youth" (Mal 2:15); "like Adam they transgressed the covenant; there they dealt faithlessly with me" (Hos 6:7); marital ordeal (Num 5:12/27); (3) Constitutional inner character (VCG-03 — 10V): the settled identity of the betrayer — "the traitors have betrayed, with betrayal the traitors have betrayed" (Isa 24:16); "I knew you would surely deal treacherously" (Isa 48:8). Distinguished from CHAR-19 (transgression as boundary-crossing) by its relational-betrayal emphasis and from CHAR-18 (iniquity as accumulated crime) by its covenant-loyalty dimension. Cross-register flags with M13 (Truth/Faithfulness) and M31 (Faith/Unbelief) travel with all three VCGs.*
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-21]** E — Working description: *Perversion as inner inversion (CHAR-21) is the inner state or disposition of moral distortion — twisting what is right, corrupting what was good, inverting the moral order from within. Five registers: (1) Perversity of speech (VCG-01 — 10V): "perverted heart devises evil, continually sowing discord" (Pro 6:14); "perverted speech" as wisdom's named hatred (Pro 8:13); (2) Perversion of judgment (VCG-02 — 6V): "bribe blinds the clear-sighted and subverts the cause of those who are in the right" (Exo 23:8); (3) Perversion of mind, will, and way (VCG-03 — 19V): "we have sinned and have acted perversely and wickedly" (1Ki 8:47); "twisted mind is despised" (Pro 12:8); "you turn things upside down!" (Isa 29:16); (4) Sexual perversion and moral degeneracy (VCG-04 — 4V): bestiality and incest named "perversion" (Lev 18:23/20:12); "turned degenerate and become a wild vine" (Jer 2:21); (5) Moral corruption, decay, and lewdness (VCG-05 — 25V): "old self corrupt through deceitful desires" (Eph 4:22); "depraved in mind" (1Ti 6:5); "slaves of corruption" (2Pe 2:19). Distinguished from CHAR-19 (boundary-crossing) by operating as inner structural distortion rather than knowing-violation, and from CHAR-18 (accumulated crime) by being the twisting of the moral faculty rather than the accumulated record.*
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-22]** E — Working description: *Injustice as moral failure of right conduct (CHAR-22) is the inner state/disposition of doing wrong toward persons — unjust conduct, unjust character, the moral faculty bent toward wrongdoing against others and against right order. Three registers: (1) Judicial and commercial injustice (VCG-01 — 20V): "you shall do no injustice in court" (Lev 19:15); "till unrighteousness was found in you" (Eze 28:15); (2) Injustice as oppression and harm to persons (VCG-02 — 33V): "in your hearts you devise wrongs; your hands deal out violence on earth" (Psa 58:2); "the unjust knows no shame" (Zep 3:5); "drinks injustice like water" (Job 15:16); (3) Character of the unjust person (VCG-03 — 19V): "dishonest in a very little is also dishonest in much" (Luk 16:10); "the righteous for the unrighteous" (1Pe 3:18). Cross-register flag: M26 (Righteousness/Justice) on all terms — CHAR-22 is M10's entry-point into the justice/righteousness cluster. Distinguished from CHAR-21 (perversion as inner inversion) by its other-directed focus: injustice is always toward persons and against right-order.*

**T1.8 · Dimension Classification**  (66)

- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-1]** E — The primary inner-being dimension of wilful sinning is **volition / will** — the capacity to choose and to act. This is established by the definitional criterion ("the will committing a known wrong") and confirmed throughout the corpus: the acts of sinning in VCG-01 (Gen 39:9 refusal, Eze 18:4/20 personal accountability), VCG-02 (1Ki 8:31 sin against neighbor), and VCG-05 (1Jo 2:1 — "so that you may not sin") all presuppose a will that chooses. The term cha.ta as a verb further confirms this: the act of sinning is volitional, not merely circumstantial. The will is the seat of the characteristic; sinning is what the will does when it turns against the moral order it was designed to uphold.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-2]** E — The primary inner-being dimension of unintentional sinning is **moral accountability / conscience** — specifically conscience in its post-act engagement mode. The definitional distinction from CHAR-1 is that conscience does not engage at the moment of the act (because knowledge is absent) but engages upon realisation. Lev 5:5 ("when he realizes his guilt... and confesses") and Num 22:34 (Balaam's immediate confession upon disclosure) show conscience as the faculty that fires when the morally relevant information arrives. The conscience is thus the primary inner faculty that processes unintentional sinning — not the will (which did not knowingly choose the wrong) but the conscience (which responds to the moral reality once it becomes available).
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-3]** E — The primary inner-being dimension of confession is **conscience** — conscience in its confessional mode, as the brief's definition states. 2Sa 24:10 establishes this most clearly: "David's heart struck him" — the heart/conscience is the initiating faculty; the verbal confession follows. The conscience here functions not as a deterrent (as in CHAR-1's VCG-03) but as a *retrospective witness that drives speech*. It is the faculty that fires post-act, identifies the wrong, and refuses to remain silent — driving the will and mouth toward naming. Neh 1:6 ("confessing the sins... day and night") shows conscience in sustained confessional mode rather than as a single episode.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-4]** E — The primary inner-being dimension is **conscience** — specifically conscience in its *malfunctioning, absent, or refused mode*. The characteristic is constitutively a conscience-failure: the moral-witness faculty (conscience) that should register sin, produce guilt, and drive confession either does not register (Jer 16:10; Ecc 8:12; 1Cor 15:34) or is explicitly refused/denied (Jer 2:35; Tit 3:11 implicit). The characteristic is named for this faculty precisely because conscience is what is suppressed.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-5]** E — The primary inner-being dimension is **volition / will** — the characteristic is constitutively a will-act (refusal) sustained over time. The definitional language throughout is volitional: "did not depart," "clung to," "would not obey," "sinning deliberately" (Heb 10:26 — *hekousiōs* = willingly/voluntarily). The will is both the faculty that could turn and the faculty that is refusing the turn. The characteristic cannot exist without an operative will — there must be a will that is capable of turning for there to be a refusal to turn.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-6]** E — The primary inner-being dimension is **will/volition** — specifically the will in its *dispositional/formational* mode rather than its moment-of-decision mode. The will that has been habituated to sinning is the primary faculty: Exo 9:34 — "sinned yet again and hardened his heart" — the will expressed through the hardened heart drives the repetition. 1Jo 3:6's "keeps on sinning" names the will's continuous operational state. The will here is not making a fresh choice at each moment (that would be CHAR-1) but has been formed into a direction that produces sinning as its default output.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-7]** E — The primary inner-being dimension is **will/volition** — specifically the leader's will operating in its *causative* mode. The Hiphil causative of cha.ta — the grammatical marker that distinguishes CHAR-7 from all other sinning characteristics — is precisely the form that names the will acting to cause another's sinning, not merely one's own. 1Ki 16:2 — "you have walked in the way of Jeroboam and have made my people Israel to sin" — the will is the agent ("walked") and the causative force ("made... to sin"). The will is primary at both ends: the leader's will is committed to the sinning direction and exercises its authority to transmit it; the community's will is redirected by the transmitted influence.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-8]** E — The primary inner-being dimension is **will/volition** — the decisive act of breaking submission requires a will that chooses the break. The pa.sha root itself names a deliberate wilful transgression-breaking: 2Ki 8:20 — "Edom revolted... and set up a king of their own" — the constructive act of setting up their own king is a volitional assertion of independence. 2Ch 21:10's causal ground — "because he had forsaken the Lord" — shows that forsaking (a volitional act) is the inner precondition for the political revolt. The characteristic is constitutively volitional: revolt requires a will that decides to break submission.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-9]** E — The primary inner-being dimension is **conscience/moral evaluation** in the sense that sinful speech is the external externalisation of the inner moral condition — but more precisely, the primary faculty engaged is the **heart as the moral-generating centre**. Mat 15:19 — "out of the heart come evil thoughts... slander" — the heart is the origin; the speech is the product. The heart's moral-generating capacity is the primary inner-being dimension: sinful speech is what the corrupted heart produces when it speaks.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-10]** E — Given the composite nature of the characteristic, different mechanisms engage different primary dimensions. (1) *Enticement (deleazō)*: the primary dimension is **desire/appetite** — the desire-faculty (epithumia) is constitutively engaged as the mechanism's entry point (Jam 1:14 — "his own desire"). (2) *Collective hypocrisy (sunupokrinomai)*: the primary dimension is **will and social identity** — the volitional act of adopting a performed identity alongside others (Gal 2:13). (3) *Apostasy (apostasia)*: the primary dimension is **will and covenantal identity** — the volitional departure from the covenantal standing (Act 21:21 — "forsake Moses"). Across the characteristic, **will/volition** is the common faculty: each mechanism operates through the will's response to (or capture by) the specific mechanism.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-11]** E — The primary dimension is **the person as constituted moral subject under a power** — what the programme would call the will/volition level but more precisely the *person's inner constitutional standing* relative to sin as a power. The three VCG registers each engage a different inner dimension: (1) VCG-01 — *moral character/identity* dimension (rish.ah as settled inner quality); (2) VCG-02 — *conscience/guilt/relational-standing* dimension (debt before God requiring forgiveness); (3) VCG-03 — *will-under-power* dimension (enslaved will, indwelling sin-force). At characteristic scope, the primary dimension is the person's entire constitutional inner standing before God: Rom 3:23 — "all have sinned and fall short" — is a total-person statement engaging every inner-being dimension simultaneously.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-12]** E — The primary inner-being dimension is **will/volition in its captive mode** — the will whose freedom toward God has been taken over by sin as master. Joh 8:34 — "a slave to sin" — the slave's will is the will that cannot freely choose otherwise; the enslaving power specifically targets and captures the will's directional freedom. The definition confirms this: "governing the will so it cannot freely choose otherwise" — the will is explicitly named as the faculty the enslavement targets. The other inner-being dimensions (conscience, moral evaluation) are secondary consequences of the will's captivity.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-13]** E — The primary dimension is **moral-accountability standing before God** — not a specific faculty but the person's total relational-moral position. The characteristic is defined by its standing before God, not by its engagement of a specific inner faculty. Amo 5:12 — "for I know how many are your transgressions and how great are your sins" — God's knowledge of the record is the defining dimension; the record exists before divine omniscience. Hos 13:12 — "his sin is kept in store" — the storage is in the divine memory. The primary dimension is thus the person's standing in God's moral memory.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-14]** E — The primary dimension is **will/volition in its God-directed turning mode** — the will's movement toward God in seeking forgiveness. Act 2:38 — "repent" (metanoeō — change of mind + direction); Exo 32:30 — "I will go up to the Lord" — the volitional ascent; Act 10:43 — "everyone who believes in him receives" — faith as the will's trust-act directed toward Christ. The seeking dimension is constitutively volitional; the receiving dimension is relational-condition change that follows the volitional seeking. The will's God-directed movement is the primary inner-being faculty engaged.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-15]** E — The primary dimension is **corporate moral identity** — the person's constitutive belonging to a community whose accumulated sinning history shapes their inner moral condition. Isa 43:27 — "your first father sinned, and your mediators transgressed against me" — the present-tense "your" makes the ancestral sinning part of the current person's moral identity through their lineage. 1Ki 8:47 — "we have sinned" — the "we" is not merely a grammatical convenience but names the corporate moral subject as the primary dimension.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-16]** E — The primary dimension is **constituted moral identity** — the person's moral character as a whole, not a specific faculty. Rom 5:19 — "were made sinners" — the making is a total-person status; Luk 18:13 — "be merciful to me, a sinner" — the sinner-identity is a whole-person self-designation. The characteristic does not primarily engage a specific inner faculty (will, conscience, affect) but constitutes the person's *moral self as a whole* as a sinner.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-17]** E — The primary dimension is **conscience in its guilt-awareness mode** — the definition's own identification. The conscience is the faculty that registers guilt (Lev 4:13/22/27 — "realizes his guilt"), experiences its weight (Ezr 9:6 — "I am ashamed"), staggered by it (1Sa 25:31 — pu.qah — pangs of conscience), and initiates the remedial movement (Lev 6:4 — "realized his guilt and will restore"). Guilt-as-inner-state is constitutively a conscience-phenomenon; it cannot exist without conscience engagement.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-18]** E — The primary dimension is **the objective moral-crime account before God** — the person's standing in God's moral ledger as the one who has accumulated wrongdoing against him. This is the definition's "crime in its moral-spiritual sense." The characteristic is not primarily experiential (CHAR-17 is the experiential register) but constitutional: it subsists before God whether the person feels it or not (Hos 12:8 — Ephraim denies it; Hos 13:12 — God has it bound up and stored). The constitutional moral-crime account is the primary dimension; the felt-weight is secondary.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-19]** E — The primary dimension is **the will's volitional act of knowing defiance** — the definition's "wilful defiance of known moral or covenantal order." The will is the primary faculty: it knows the boundary (Rom 4:15 — law required), it chooses to cross (Isa 1:2 — "they have rebelled"), it sustains the direction (Eze 2:3 — "to this very day"). Every other dimension (cognitive distortion, affective collapse, conscience suppression) is secondary to the will's primary volitional choice.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-20]** E — The primary dimension is **the will's covenantal orientation — the inner disposition of loyalty or betrayal within a known relational bond**. Mal 2:15/16 — "guard yourselves in your spirit, and do not be faithless" — the spirit is named as the inner locus requiring guarding; Isa 48:8 — the foreknown constitutional bent of the will toward treachery — the will's settled inner orientation is the characteristic's primary faculty. The characteristic is constitutively volitional and dispositional rather than cognitive or affective at the primary level.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-21]** E — The primary dimension is **the moral faculty in its distorted/corrupted condition** — specifically the heart, mind, will, and desire as twisted from their designed moral orientation. Pro 6:14 — "perverted heart devises evil" — the heart as origin; 1Ti 6:5 — "depraved in mind" — the mind as primary corrupted faculty; Eph 4:22 — "corrupt through deceitful desires" — desire as corruption-agent. The characteristic operates at the level of the person's inner moral constitution rather than discrete acts or record-entries.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-22]** E — The primary dimension is **the moral-conduct faculty oriented toward wrong in dealings with others** — the will's deployment of the capacity for just conduct in the wrong direction. Psa 58:2 — "in your hearts you devise wrongs" — heart as the origin; Eze 18:8 — "withholds his hand from injustice" — the will restraining the hand confirms the will as the governing faculty; Luk 16:10 — the fixed-character dishonesty confirms the dispositional-volitional level as primary.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-1]** E — Multiple lines of evidence support the will-dimension classification. (1) Accountability: Eze 18:20 ("the soul who sins alone shall die; the son shall not suffer for the iniquity of the father") — personal accountability presupposes a personal will. (2) Resistance possible: VCG-03's entire evidence base (Gen 39:9, Exo 20:20, Job 1:22, Psa 4:4, Psa 39:1, 1Jo 2:1) shows that sinning can be resisted, which only makes sense if the will is the operative faculty. (3) Social pressure overriding it: 1Sa 15:24 ("I feared the people and obeyed their voice") shows the will being deflected by pressure — volitional language throughout. (4) The deterrence formula: Exo 20:20 ("that you may not sin") and Eze 3:21 ("if you warn the righteous person not to sin, and he does not sin") both treat not-sinning as a decision that warning and moral instruction can produce — confirming the will's centrality.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-2]** E — The conscience-as-primary-dimension is supported by: (1) Lev 5:5 — the transition from "realizes his guilt" to "confesses" is a conscience-activation event; (2) Num 22:34 — Balaam's immediate moral response ("I have sinned") upon seeing the angel demonstrates conscience engaging on new information; (3) The entire Lev 4/Num 15 framework presupposes that once the sin surfaces to awareness, the person is morally obligated to respond — this obligation is the conscience's claim. The will is involved in the remedy (bringing the offering, confessing) but not in the original sinning act. Conscience is the faculty that bridges the gap between the uninformed act and the informed response.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-3]** E — The conscience-primary classification is supported by: (1) The direct heart-conscience language in the paradigm case: 2Sa 24:10 — "David's heart struck him" — heart as the conscience-organ initiating confession; (2) Shimei's confession: 2Sa 19:20 — "your servant *knows* that I have sinned" — knowledge as the conscience-basis for the confession; (3) The hollow-confession contrast: Deu 1:41 — words spoken but conscience not fully engaged (will proceeds independently) — confirming that genuine conscience-engagement is the distinguishing feature; (4) The extended confession: Dan 9:5 — multiple named dimensions of failure show conscience operating with high articulation, naming specific aspects of what went wrong.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-4]** E — The conscience-primary classification is supported by: (1) Jer 2:35 — the explicit denial of sin is an anti-confessional conscience-act: the conscience that should say "I have sinned" is made to say "I have not sinned"; (2) Jer 16:10 — "what sin have we committed?" — the question reveals a conscience that is not firing its sin-recognition signal; (3) Ecc 8:12 — sinning "a hundred times" without the fear of God reveals conscience-absent or conscience-numbed habituation; (4) 1Cor 15:34 — the "drunken stupor" is explicitly a conscience-alertness failure; (5) Tit 3:11 — "self-condemned" — the person's own conscience implicitly witnesses against them (the self-condemnation is a conscience-verdict they do not consciously receive).
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-5]** E — (1) 2Ki 10:31 — "not careful to walk... with all his heart" — the heart as the integrating volitional centre is the faculty engaged; "all his heart" = complete volitional alignment; its absence = volitional partial-refusal. (2) Neh 9:29 — "would not obey" — the "would not" is a volitional statement. (3) Heb 10:26 — "sinning deliberately" (*hekousiōs*) — the adverb of willing/deliberateness names the will directly. (4) 2Ki 3:3 — "clung to" (*davaq*) — a volitional attachment. (5) Rom 6:15 — the hypothetical of sinning "because" of grace — the "because" implies a reasoned volitional choice about whether to sin.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-6]** E — (1) Exo 9:34 — "hardened his heart" — the heart (as the volitional centre) is the faculty that is hardened; (2) 1Sa 2:25 — "they would not listen" — the "would not" is volitional; (3) Hos 10:9 — "you have sinned... there they have continued" — the continuation is volitional persistence; (4) 1Jo 3:6's "abides in him" as the structural opposite — abiding is a volitional-dispositional state; (5) 2Ki 17:7 — "feared other gods" — the fear/allegiance is a dispositional volitional commitment. All evidence confirms the will/disposition as the operative faculty.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-7]** E — (1) 1Ki 16:19 — "walking in the way of Jeroboam, and for his sin which he committed, making Israel to sin" — the will expressed through walking (personal commitment) and the Hiphil causative (transmitted to others); (2) 2Ki 17:21 — "Jeroboam drove Israel from following the Lord" — drove (*niddah* — to push away, banish) names the causative volitional force; (3) 1Ki 16:2 — God holds the leader accountable for "making my people... to sin" — the accountability is volitional (you chose to walk, you chose to make); (4) Jer 32:35 — "they built the high places... to cause Judah to sin" — the building is an agentive volitional act with causative intent.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-8]** E — (1) Pa.sha root meaning — deliberate transgressive breaking-away (not accidental); (2) 2Ki 8:20 — "set up a king of their own" — positive volitional assertion of independence alongside the breaking; (3) 2Ch 21:10 — "because he had forsaken the Lord" — *forsook* (*azav*) is a volitional act of abandonment; the political revolt follows from the volitional inner spiritual forsaking; (4) 2Ki 1:1 / 3:5 — "after the death of Ahab, Moab rebelled" — the temporal marker implies a decision taken at a specific moment.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-9]** E — (1) Mat 15:19 — the heart named as the source; (2) 1Ti 6:4 — slander traced to the inner conditions of conceit and diseased craving (not to external triggers); (3) 2Ti 3:2 — the abusive/blasphemous disposition as a character trait (inner formation, not external act); (4) VCG-03 — the beast's blasphemy as constitutive identity rather than isolated act — the deepest level of the heart's orientation expressed in constitutive speech. All evidence supports: the inner moral-generative capacity of the heart is the primary faculty; speech is its expression.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-10]** E — (1) Jam 1:14 — "lured and enticed by his own desire" — the desire is the mechanism but the luring requires the will's yielding to the desire's pull; (2) Gal 2:13 — "acted hypocritically along with him" — the acting-alongside is a volitional adoption; (3) Act 21:21 — "forsake Moses" — forsaking (*apostasia*) is a volitional departure; (4) 2Pe 2:14 — "hearts trained in greed" — the training involves the volitional sustaining of the desire-appetite over time.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-11]** E — (1) Rom 3:9 — "under sin" — the preposition "under" names a positional relationship of the whole person relative to sin; (2) Eph 2:1 — "dead in trespasses and sins" — the deadness is total-person; (3) Rom 7:14 — "sold under sin" — sold as a whole person to a master; (4) Rom 6:6 — "the body of sin might be brought to nothing" — the "body" as the whole embodied person under sin's ownership; (5) Gen 4:7 — "its desire is for you" — the predatory desire is for the whole person as its target; the will is named only as the faculty that must resist ("you must rule over it") but sin desires the whole person.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-12]** E — (1) Joh 8:34 — "slave to sin" — slavery directly names the will's captivity; a slave's will is governed by the master; (2) Rom 6:1/2 — "continue in sin... live in it" — the continued living-in presupposes a will that cannot or does not freely choose to exit; (3) 1Jo 3:9 — "he cannot keep on sinning" — the "cannot" names a constitutional volitional impossibility that the new birth produces; (4) Gal 3:22 — "imprisoned everything" — the imprisonment targets the will's freedom to obtain the promise through human effort; the prison metaphor specifically names the restriction of free movement.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-13]** E — (1) Exo 32:33 — "whoever has sinned against me, I will blot out of my book" — the person's standing in the divine book is the operative moral reality; (2) Jer 31:34 — "I will remember their sin no more" — the erasure of divine memory is the defining liberation; (3) Job 10:6 — "seek out my iniquity and search for my sin" — divine scrutiny of the person's standing is the characteristic's primary mode; (4) Gen 18:20 — "their sin is very grave" — the gravity is measured by what has reached God as an objective reality, not by the person's own internal assessment.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-14]** E — (1) Act 2:38 — "repent" — repentance is a volitional act named first in the seeking-sequence; (2) Exo 32:30 — "I will go up to the Lord" — the first-person future volitional statement; (3) Act 10:43 — "everyone who believes in him receives" — belief as the volitional trust-act; (4) Lev 23:27 — "you shall afflict yourselves" — the deliberate inner self-affliction is a volitional posture of seeking; (5) 2Ch 29:23 — "they laid their hands on them" — the hand-laying is the volitional act of identification with the offering's purpose.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-15]** E — (1) Isa 43:27 — "your first father sinned" — the ancestral sin is addressed to the present community as their inherited condition; (2) 1Ki 8:47 — "we have sinned" — the corporate "we" is the locus of the characteristic; (3) Isa 64:5 — "in our sins we have been a long time" — the possessive "our" names the shared corporate condition; (4) Jer 8:14 — "we have sinned against the Lord" — the corporate acknowledgment. All uses are corporate rather than individually-bounded.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-16]** E — (1) Rom 5:19 — "made sinners" — the passive voice confirms the total-person constitution; (2) Gen 13:13 — "great sinners against the Lord" — the identity is the totality of the Sodomites' moral character; (3) Luk 5:8 — "I am a sinful man" — the whole-person self-declaration; (4) 1Ti 1:15 — "Christ Jesus came into the world to save sinners" — the salvific mission targets the whole person, not a specific faculty; (5) Heb 7:26 — "separated from sinners" — the separation is total-person, not faculty-specific.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-17]** E — (1) Lev 4:13/22/27 — "they realize their guilt" — the realisation is conscience's act; (2) Lev 5:5 — "when he realizes his guilt in any of these and confesses" — conscience-realisation produces confession; (3) Gen 42:21 — the brothers' conscience connects their past cruelty to their present distress; (4) 1Sa 25:31 — pu.qah (staggering of conscience) names the conscience-specific faculty directly; (5) Hos 5:15 — "until they acknowledge their guilt" — the acknowledgment is a conscience act.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-18]** E — (1) Gen 15:16 — "the iniquity of the Amorites is not yet complete" — the Amorites are not described as feeling their iniquity; it exists as a measured moral reality before God regardless of their inner awareness; (2) Hos 13:12 — "bound up; his sin is kept in store" — the bound-up condition is God's action, not Ephraim's subjective experience; (3) Hos 12:8 — Ephraim claims no iniquity while God knows it is real — the objective and subjective are explicitly divergent; (4) Eze 18:20 — "the wickedness of the wicked shall be upon himself" — the "upon himself" is an objective moral-legal attachment.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-19]** E — (1) Rom 4:15 — transgression requires knowledge of the law → will can only transgress what it knows; (2) Isa 1:2 — "children have I reared... but they have rebelled" — the rebellion is wilful choice against received care; (3) Eze 18:30 — "repent and turn from all your transgressions" — the remedial call is to the will to turn; (4) Isa 31:6 — "turn to him from whom people have deeply revolted" — the revolt and its reversal are both volitional movements; (5) Pro 28:13 — "he who confesses and forsakes them" — the forsaking is a volitional act confirming that the committing was volitional.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-20]** E — (1) Mal 2:15 — "guard yourselves in your spirit" — the spirit as the named locus of faithlessness-danger; (2) 2Ch 28:22 — Ahaz becoming "yet more faithless" under distress — the will's sustained dispositional hardening; (3) Isa 48:8 — "I knew you would surely deal treacherously" — the foreknown constitutional inner bent; (4) 1Ch 5:25 — "broke faith with the God of their fathers, and whored after the gods" — the will's active allegiance-transfer; (5) Pro 11:6 — "the treacherous are taken captive by their lust" — the will enslaved by its own faithless orientation.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-21]** E — (1) Pro 6:14 — heart as the perverted origin of continual evil-devising; (2) 1Ti 6:5 — mind as the named perverted faculty; (3) Eph 4:22 — desire as the active corruption-agent within the inner person; (4) Pro 12:8 — "twisted mind" as the characterising inner trait; (5) Isa 29:16 — the mind's cognitive-relational inversion as perversion; (6) 2Cor 11:3 — "your thoughts will be led astray" — the thoughts as the corruption-target.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-22]** E — (1) Psa 58:2 — heart-devising is the named origin; (2) Eze 18:8 — hand-withholding confirms volitional control; (3) Job 34:32 — "if I have done iniquity, I will do it no more" — the will's capacity to cease confirms it governs; (4) Luk 16:10 — fixed character confirmed by scaling from small to much; (5) Eze 3:20 — "a righteous person turns from his righteousness and commits injustice" — the turn is volitional.
- `T1.8.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-1]** E — Secondary dimensions are evidenced without competing with the primary will-classification. (1) *Conscience*: VCG-03's deterrence register engages conscience as the faculty that recognises the moral quality of the act (Gen 39:9 — "great wickedness"; Job 1:22 — restraint of charge against God). Conscience operates as the inner witness to the will's decision but is not itself the seat of the sinning. (2) *Moral evaluation*: the entire corpus presupposes a moral standard against which the act is measured as wrong — the law (Deu 19:15; 24:4), God's command (Eze 14:13), the relational claim of the neighbour (Mat 18:15). Moral evaluation is engaged but functions as the background standard, not the active faculty producing the act. (3) *Body*: speech-mode sinning (Psa 39:1, Job 2:10, Ecc 5:6) and bodily sinning (1Cor 6:18) implicate the body as the expressive organ. These secondary dimensions do not compete with the primary will-classification; they describe what the will works through and against.  ---  T1 self-evaluation:   1. (2) *Communal accountability*: Lev 4:3 (priest's sin implicating the community) and Lev 9:15 (community sin offering) show that unintentional sinning carries a communal dimension — individual inadvertent violation can produce corporate moral liability. **T2.8 · Body-Deposit (DNA / generational)**  (66)

- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-1]** E — The corpus offers limited but present evidence. Isa 1:4 ("sinful nation, a people laden with iniquity, offspring of evildoers, children who deal corruptly") describes a generational pattern in which sinning has become a constitutional characteristic of a people — "offspring of evildoers" suggests a hereditary dimension, though the text does not specify a biological mechanism. Neh 13:26 ("foreign women made even him to sin") implies that sustained exposure to sinning patterns erodes constitutional resistance. The strongest deposit-language in the corpus is Hos 4:7/13:2 — escalating sinning producing intensified idolatry — which suggests a formed disposition that progressively resists reversal. However, explicit DNA or bodily-deposit language is absent from the corpus.
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /silent — **[CHAR-2]** S — The 18-verse corpus provides no evidence of a constitutional deposit from sustained unintentional sinning. The Levitical framework addresses each occurrence episodically (Lev 4:2; Num 15:27/28) without describing a cumulative deposit in the person. The daily and annual cultic purifications (Exo 29:36; Eze 45:18) imply that unintentional defilement *accumulates on sacred spaces* (requiring periodic comprehensive cleansing), but the corpus does not evidence a corresponding inner-person deposit from repeated unintentional sinning.
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /silent — **[CHAR-3]** S — The corpus does not evidence a constitutional body-deposit from sustained confession. The sustained practice of confession (Neh 1:6 — "day and night"; Dan 9:5 — extended prayer) produces formative effects in the inner person (moral transparency, intercession capacity — T1.6.2) but the corpus does not name a bodily or generational deposit.
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-4]** E — Yes — Tit 3:11 provides the clearest deposit evidence: "warped and sinful" (*exestraptai kai hamartanei*) — the perfect participle "warped" (*exestraptai*) suggests a completed deformation that has become a settled constitutional state. Sustained conscience suppression has produced a person who is constitutionally distorted — not temporarily confused but structurally deformed. This is the deposit: a character whose moral faculty has been twisted out of its proper orientation through sustained refusal of the conscience's witness.
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-5]** E — Yes. Neh 9:29 — "stubborn shoulder and stiffened their neck" — the bodily-metaphor language of *stiffening* (*qashu* — to harden, make stiff) describes a completed deformation: the neck that has been stiffened is a neck that has been hardened into a fixed posture. The perfect-aspect language implies a formed constitutional state. The Kings corpus's trajectory from "did not depart" (early kings) to "clung to" (2Ki 3:3 — Jehoram) shows a progression from passive non-departure to active attachment — consistent with a deepening constitutional deposit of the refusal-orientation in the will.
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-6]** E — Yes. Exo 9:34 — "hardened his heart" — the hardening is a constitutional deposit: each sinning-and-hardening cycle leaves the heart in a harder state, which is the starting point for the next cycle. The hardening is cumulative: the heart becomes progressively harder with each iteration. Hos 8:11 — altars for sinning becoming established structures — shows the social-institutional deposit: habitual defection embeds itself in institutional structures that outlast any individual's disposition. Hos 10:9 — "from the days of Gibeah... there they have continued" — generational persistence as constitutional deposit.
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-7]** E — Yes — and the corpus evidences this more concretely than any other characteristic. 2Ki 23:15 — "the altar at Bethel, the high place erected by Jeroboam the son of Nebat, who made Israel to sin" — Jeroboam's contagious sin has left a physical-structural deposit (the altar) that persists for generations after his death. The deposit is institutional: the physical structure at Bethel functions as an ongoing transmission mechanism long after the original leader is gone. This is the most explicit deposit evidence in the M10 corpus: a named structure built through contagious sin leadership that endures for centuries.
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-8]** E — Yes — the "to this day" markers are the evidence. 1Ki 12:19 / 2Ch 10:19 — "Israel has been in rebellion against the house of David to this day" — the revolt has deposited a permanent political-relational fracture that persists to the narrator's present. 2Ki 8:22 / 2Ch 21:10 — "Edom revolted from the rule of Judah to this day" — similarly permanent. The deposit is a *political-relational* fracture: the governing relationship has been permanently broken and does not restore itself. This is a social-structural deposit rather than a bodily-constitutional one: the political community's relationship to the governing authority has been permanently altered by the revolt.
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-9]** E — Yes. Two deposit dimensions. (1) *Constitutional character formed around sinful speech*: 2Ti 3:2 — the blasphemous/abusive disposition is named as a settled character trait in end-times persons; 1Ti 6:4 — conceit feeds on the slander it generates, deepening the inner corruption. (2) *The unforgivable blasphemy as an irreversible inner state*: Mat 12:31 — "will not be forgiven" — this is the most severe deposit: a permanent condition of foreclosed forgiveness resulting from the specific act of blasphemy against the Spirit. This is not a bodily deposit but a spiritual-moral constitutional deposit of permanent consequence.
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-10]** E — Yes — most clearly for enticement. 2Pe 2:14 — "hearts trained in greed" — "trained" (*gegumnasmenos* — a perfect participle, indicating a completed formation with ongoing effect) names the constitutional deposit of sustained enticement: the heart has been systematically trained into greedy appetite through sustained engagement with the enticement mechanism. The deposit is the formed inner character: a heart constitutionally oriented toward greed.
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-11]** E — Yes — the most extensive constitutional deposit evidence in the M10 corpus. Four deposit forms. (1) *Death*: Rom 6:23 — "the wages of sin is death" — death is the permanent constitutional deposit of the condition; Rom 8:10 — "the body is dead because of sin" — the mortality of the human body is the physical deposit of the condition inherited from Adam (Rom 5:12 — "death spread to all men"). (2) *Hardening*: Heb 3:13 — "hardened by the deceitfulness of sin" — the progressive hardening of the heart against God's voice is a formed constitutional deposit of the condition's sustained operation. (3) *Sin's indwelling*: Rom 7:17/20 — "sin that dwells within me" — the indwelling is a constitutional reality, not a passing state; the Greek (*oikousa* — present participle) names it as an ongoing inhabitation. (4) *Accumulating measure*: 1Th 2:16 — "filling up the measure of their sins" — the condition produces an accumulating divine-ledger deposit that moves toward a full measure triggering judgment.
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-12]** E — Yes. Joh 8:34 — "everyone who practices sin is a slave to sin" — the slave-state is the constitutional deposit of sustained sinning practice: the accumulated practice has produced a constituted slave-identity. 1Jo 3:8 — "of the devil" — the allegiance-capture is the deepest constitutional deposit: the person's identity-belonging has been impressed toward the devil as its master. 1Jo 5:16 — "sin that leads to death" — the most severe deposit: the enslaving power in its most advanced form deposits the person in a condition where death is the trajectory.
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-13]** E — Yes — the most extensive deposit evidence in M10. Four deposit forms. (1) *Internal heart-engraving*: Jer 17:1 — "engraved on the tablet of their heart" — the sin-record is inscribed internally as a permanent deposit on the heart's constitutional structure; the iron pen and diamond point indicate irreversibility without divine intervention; (2) *Separation from God*: Isa 59:2 — accumulated record produces a structured relational barrier as its deposit; (3) *National-institutional legacy*: VCG-04 — the Jeroboam-sin deposit persists across royal generations (46 verses) as a named institutional inheritance; (4) *Stored divine record*: Hos 13:12 — "his sin is kept in store" — the divine side of the deposit is the sealed, stored record awaiting reckoning.
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-14]** E — Yes — but in the positive direction, uniquely among M10 characteristics. (1) *Love as deposit*: Luk 7:47 — "she loved much" — the forgiveness received deposits love as an ongoing inner orientation; the deposit is the love produced by the magnitude of forgiveness received; (2) *Life as deposit*: Col 2:13 — "made alive" — the life-constitutional change is a permanent deposit; the formerly-dead person is now constitutionally alive; (3) *Spirit-indwelling as deposit*: Act 2:38 — the Holy Spirit is received as a gift following forgiveness — the Spirit's indwelling is the characteristic's most profound constitutional deposit; (4) *Redemption-identity as deposit*: Col 1:14; Eph 1:7 — "we have redemption, the forgiveness of sins" — the forgiven person carries a permanent identity-deposit: they are redeemed, they have received the forgiveness.
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-15]** E — Yes — two deposit forms. (1) *Prolonged sin-conditionedness*: Isa 64:5 — "in our sins we have been a long time" — the extended duration of the sinning-condition is a constitutional deposit; the community has been formed by the sustained pattern into a people characterised by it; (2) *Defilement*: Eze 37:23 — "they shall not defile themselves anymore... I will save them from all the backslidings in which they have sinned, and will cleanse them" — the defilement produced by the accumulated backslidings is the constitutional deposit requiring divine cleansing; the community's inner constitution has been defiled by the sustained sinning-pattern.
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-16]** E — Yes — three deposit forms. (1) *Constituted identity*: Rom 5:19 — "made sinners" — the Adamic imputation is the constitutional deposit of the sinner-identity in all humanity; the deposit is the sinner-standing that precedes any individual's sinning acts; (2) *Accumulated sinning history*: Jam 5:20 — "cover a multitude of sins" — the sinner who wanders has accumulated a multitude of sins as the deposit of their sustained sinner-identity; (3) *Inner defilement*: Jam 4:8 — "purify your hearts" — the heart-defilement requiring purification is the constitutional deposit of the sustained sinner-identity on the inner person.
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-17]** E — Yes — three deposit forms. (1) *Accumulated weight*: Ezr 9:6 — "our guilt has mounted up to the heavens" — the accumulated guilt is a measurable constitutional deposit that has risen above the head; (2) *Indelible stain*: Jer 2:22 — "the stain of your guilt is still before me" — the guilt-stain is an indelible deposit on the inner person that no self-generated cleansing removes; (3) *Punitive legacy*: Jer 32:18 — "you repay the guilt of fathers to their children after them" — the guilt-deposit is transmitted intergenerationally; the deposit from the fathers' guilt becomes the children's inherited moral-consequential burden.
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-18]** E — Yes — three deposit forms. (1) *Divine-record deposit*: Hos 13:12 — "the iniquity of Ephraim is bound up; his sin is kept in store" — God maintains an accumulated sealed record; Gen 15:16 — "not yet complete" — the accumulation grows as a measured deposit; (2) *Body-bone deposit*: Eze 32:27 — "iniquities are upon their bones" — iniquity clings permanently to the person's physical remains; Psa 31:10 — bones wasting away — the physical deterioration is the bodily deposit of the crime; (3) *Generational deposit*: Exo 20:5 — "visiting the iniquity of the fathers on the children to the third and fourth generation" — the fathers' iniquity-deposit is transmitted as a generational moral inheritance.
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-19]** E — Yes — three deposit forms. (1) *Record held in divine count*: Amos 1:3 — "for three transgressions... and for four" — God maintains a counted record; Dan 8:13 — "the transgression that makes desolate" — a specific transgression enshrined in heavenly discourse; Dan 9:24 — "to finish the transgression" — the accumulated transgression must be completed/finished, implying it persists as a deposit until that moment; (2) *Identity-level labelling*: Isa 48:8 — "from before birth you were called a rebel" — the identity deposit of transgression precedes birth; Isa 57:4 — "children of transgression" — the deposit passes generationally; (3) *Oppressive weight-burden*: Lam 1:14 — the yoke-burden bound and set on the neck — a persistent bodily-moral weight.
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-20]** E — Yes — three deposit forms. (1) *Generational communal deposit*: 2Ch 30:7 — "your fathers and your brothers, who were faithless to the Lord God of their fathers, so that he made them a desolation" — the faithlessness of the fathers is what the children are warned not to repeat; the deposit is the pattern and consequence transmitted; 1Ch 9:1 — the exile as the deposit of accumulated faithlessness; (2) *Identity-level character deposit*: Isa 48:8 — "I knew that you would surely deal treacherously" — the constitutional bent foreknown from before birth is the deepest deposit; (3) *National shame deposit*: Dan 9:7 — "open shame... in all the lands to which you have driven them, because of the treachery that they have committed against you" — the shame persisting in every land of exile as the national deposit of faithlessness.
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-21]** E — Yes — three deposit forms. (1) *Mind-depravity deposit*: 1Ti 6:5 — "depraved in mind and deprived of the truth" — the mind's depravity is a constitutional deposit that persists as the settled condition of the corrupted person's cognitive faculty; (2) *Desire-capture deposit*: Eph 4:22 — "corrupt through deceitful desires" — the ongoing corruption through desire is an accumulating deposit; 2Pe 2:19 — "slaves of corruption" — the enslavement is a constitutionally established condition; (3) *Bodily perishability*: Rom 8:21 — "bondage to corruption" — the creation's bondage to corruption is a constitutional condition awaiting eschatological liberation.
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-22]** E — Yes — three deposit forms. (1) *Divine memory-record*: Rev 18:5 — "her sins are heaped high as heaven, and God has remembered her iniquities" — God's memory holds the accumulating record of injustice as a deposit; (2) *Fixed character deposit*: Luk 16:10 — "dishonest in very little is also dishonest in much" — the injustice is deposited as a stable character-trait that does not vary with scale; (3) *Shameless-conscience deposit*: Zep 3:5 — "the unjust knows no shame" — the sustained injustice deposits a conscience that is permanently insensible to its own wrongdoing.
- `T2.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-1]** E — Supporting evidence: Isa 1:4 (generational sin-characterisation), Neh 13:26 (constitutional vulnerability acquired through exposure), Hos 13:2 (escalating sinning suggesting formed capacity). Contradicting evidence: Eze 18:19–20 explicitly rejects generational punishment for sin: "the son shall not suffer for the iniquity of the father" — which addresses legal accountability rather than constitutional deposit but does warn against reading generational consequence as constitutional transmission. The cultic-atonement corpus (VCG-04: Lev 4:22 — unintentional sinning still real and requiring remedy) suggests that even non-deposit sinning is morally consequential, implying that the deposit question is not the primary evidence-frame.
- `T2.8.2` · cluster_finding · /silent — **[CHAR-2]** S — No evidence directly supports a constitutional body-deposit from unintentional sinning in this corpus. The closest evidence is the communal dimension of priestly sinning (Lev 4:3 — "bringing guilt on the people"), which implies a corporate moral condition accumulating from a leader's inadvertent acts. But this is communal guilt-accumulation rather than a constitutional deposit in individual persons. The evidence is insufficient to confirm or deny a bodily-deposit finding.
- `T2.8.2` · cluster_finding · /silent — **[CHAR-3]** S — No direct evidence for or against a bodily deposit from confession is present in the 34-verse corpus. Lam 5:7 ("our fathers sinned, and are no more; and we bear their iniquities") names a generational consequence of sin, but this is about inherited iniquity from the fathers' sinning, not a deposit from the fathers' confessing. The evidential gap is noted.
- `T2.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-4]** E — Supporting: Tit 3:11 ("warped" — completed deformation); Ecc 8:12 ("does evil a hundred times" — habituated repeated sinning implying a formed capacity for conscience-absent evil). The progressive character of the deformation is implied by the habituated-mode evidence: the person who has sinned a hundred times without the fear of God has built a habit-structure that constitutes a deposit. Contradicting or complicating: Job 35:6 — "if you have sinned, what do you accomplish against him?" — Elihu's point that sin harms the sinner but does not injure God suggests the deposit is in the creature, not in the divine-creature relationship per se.
- `T2.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-5]** E — Supporting: Neh 9:29 ("stiffened their neck" — formed posture); 2Ki 3:3 ("clung to" — active attachment beyond mere non-departure); 2Ki 15:18 ("all his days" — lifetime orientation confirming deep formation). Contradicting or complicating: the corpus does not evidence any case where refusal to repent is described as biologically inherited or bodily-organic; the "as his fathers had done" (2Ki 15:9) is social-formational transmission rather than genetic deposit. The deposit is formational (character formed through sustained refusal) rather than biological.
- `T2.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-6]** E — Supporting: Exo 9:34 (progressive hardening — cumulative individual deposit); Hos 8:11 (institutionalised altars — social-structural deposit); Hos 10:9 (from Gibeah — generational transmission). Contradicting or complicating: 1Jo 3:6's positive statement — "no one who abides in him keeps on sinning" — implies that the deposit is not irrevocable: abiding in Christ structurally prevents the pattern, implying that the pattern can be broken by the abiding relationship. The deposit is real but not permanent in the face of the abiding relationship.
- `T2.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-7]** E — Supporting: 2Ki 23:15 (altar at Bethel — permanent structural deposit); 1Ki 22:52 — three generations receiving and passing on the sinning direction (Ahaziah following father, mother, and Jeroboam); Jer 32:35 — "they built the high places of Baal" — institutionalised structures. Contradicting or complicating: 2Ki 23:15 — Josiah was able to demolish the altar, showing the deposit is not permanent; but the fact that the altar survived for ~300 years since Jeroboam's reign shows the deposit's remarkable persistence.
- `T2.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-8]** E — Supporting: four "to this day" instances (1Ki 12:19; 2Ki 8:22; 2Ch 10:19; 2Ch 21:10) — each naming a permanent political fracture as the enduring deposit. 2Ki 8:20 / 2Ch 21:8 — "set up a king of their own" — the positive assertion of independence becomes a permanent structural reality. Contradicting or complicating: no verse evidences restoration of the broken relationship; the deposits appear total and permanent within the corpus's time horizon.
- `T2.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-9]** E — Supporting: Mat 12:31 (permanent irreversible deposit — unforgiven condition); 2Ti 3:2 (blasphemous character as settled end-times type); VCG-03's beast (blasphemy as constitutive identity — the deepest deposit: entire being formed around sinful speech). Contradicting or complicating: 1Ti 1:13 — Paul's former blasphemy was reversed by mercy, suggesting that at least the VCG-01/02 forms of sinful speech are reversible; not all sinful speech produces an irreversible deposit. The deposit is irreversible only at the Spirit-blasphemy threshold (Mat 12:31).
- `T2.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-10]** E — Supporting: 2Pe 2:14 — "hearts trained in greed" — the perfect participle confirms completed formation with ongoing deposit; "insatiable for sin" — the insatiability is the deposited character of formed appetite. Contradicting or complicating: Jam 1:14 describes the enticement mechanism as episodic ("when he is lured") — not every instance of enticement produces a constitutional deposit; the deposit accumulates through sustained engagement. For hypocrisy and apostasy, the corpus does not explicitly name a deposit beyond the immediate consequence.
- `T2.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-11]** E — Supporting: all four deposit dimensions are multi-verse evidenced: death (Rom 5:12/6:23/8:10); hardening (Heb 3:13); indwelling sin (Rom 7:17/20); accumulating measure (1Th 2:16). Contradicting or complicating: Heb 4:15 — "without sin" — Christ's constitutional sinlessness and 2Cor 5:21 — "him who knew no sin" — demonstrate that the deposit is not uncreatable: a genuinely human person (Jesus) existed without it. The deposit is thus a fallen-condition deposit, not a creation-constitutional deposit. 1Cor 7:28/36 — "it is no sin" — the term's negative use confirms that not every human experience carries the deposit; the condition is universal but its specific acts are not.
- `T2.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-12]** E — Supporting: Joh 8:34 (slave — constituted state); 1Jo 3:8 ("of the devil" — identity deposit); 1Jo 5:16 (death-trajectory). Contradicting or complicating: 1Jo 3:9 — "no one born of God makes a practice of sinning" — the new birth deposit counters and prevents the sin-enslavement deposit; the born-of-God constitution produces a counter-deposit (God's seed abiding) that prevents the sin-enslavement deposit from forming. The deposit of sin-enslavement is real but not irresistible in the face of the new-birth deposit.
- `T2.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-13]** E — Supporting: Jer 17:1 (heart-engraving — permanent inscription); Isa 59:2 (separation — relational deposit); VCG-04 (Jeroboam-pattern — institutional deposit persisting 46 verses worth of kings); Hos 13:12 (kept in store — divine retention deposit). Contradicting or complicating: Isa 43:25 — "I will not remember your sins" — the deposit is not irremovable; Jer 31:34 — "I will remember their sin no more" — the new covenant promise erases the deposit from divine memory; Eze 33:16 — "none of the sins shall be remembered against him" — individual repentance can erase the deposit's record-against-me function. The deposit is real and deep but not permanently irremovable.
- `T2.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-14]** E — Supporting: Luk 7:47 (love — affective deposit); Col 2:13 (alive — vital deposit); Act 2:38 (Spirit — spirit-level deposit); Col 1:14/Eph 1:7 (redemption identity — relational deposit).
