The NT register (VCG-02) presents permanent deposits: "in him we have redemption" (Col 1:14 — present tense, permanent possession) vs. the OT "once a year" (Exo 30:10). The NT deposit is permanent; the OT deposit is provisional and renewable.
- `T2.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-15]** E — Supporting: Isa 64:5 ("a long time in our sins" — duration as deposit); Eze 37:23 (defilement requiring cleansing — constitutional deposit). Contradicting or complicating: 1Ki 8:47 — "if they turn their heart... and repent" — the possibility of turning implies the deposit is not constitutionally irreversible; the heart can turn even within the accumulated condition. The deposit is real (long-duration sinning, defilement) but is addressable through divine intervention (Eze 37:23 — "I will cleanse them") and human heart-turning (1Ki 8:47).
- `T2.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-16]** E — Supporting: Rom 5:19 (constituted standing — deposit from Adam); Jam 5:20 (multitude of sins — accumulated); Jam 4:8 (heart requiring purification — inner deposit). The deposit is real but reversible through divine action.
- `T2.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-17]** E — Supporting: Ezr 9:6 (accumulation deposit — risen above head); Jer 2:22 (indelible stain deposit); Jer 32:18 (intergenerational deposit). The deposit is constitutionally present and deepens without resolution, but yields to divine cleansing.
- `T2.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-18]** E — Supporting: Hos 13:12 (divine-record deposit — sealed); Eze 32:27 (bodily deposit — on bones); Exo 20:5 (generational deposit). - `T2.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-19]** E — Supporting: Amos 1:3 (divine count-record); Isa 48:8 (identity deposit); Lam 1:14 (yoke-burden deposit). - `T2.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-20]** E — Supporting: 2Ch 30:7 (generational deposit — desolation of fathers); Isa 48:8 (identity-level foreknown deposit); Dan 9:7 (national shame deposit). - `T2.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-21]** E — Supporting: 1Ti 6:5 (mind-depravity deposit — constitutionally settled); Eph 4:22 (ongoing desire-corruption deposit); Rom 8:21 (bondage-to-corruption). **T5.7 · Deposit Consequence**  (66)

- `T5.7.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-1]** E — T2.8 found limited evidence of constitutional deposit: Isa 1:4 (generational sinning as a national characteristic — "offspring of evildoers") and Hos 4:7/13:2 (escalating sinning producing disposition-formation). The developmental consequence of this deposit is evidenced as: (1) *Progressively constrained will*: escalating sinning (Hos 13:2 — "they sin more and more, and make for themselves metal images") shows the will progressively invested in and constrained by the sinning direction. (2) *Cultural/generational transmission*: Isa 1:4 ("offspring of evildoers, children who deal corruptly") suggests a generational consequence where the sinning pattern becomes a transmittable moral orientation. (3) *Diminished capacity for the opposite*: Neh 13:26 ("foreign women made even him to sin") implies that sustained exposure to sinning influences erodes constitutional resistance — the deposit of exposure narrows the will's effective range.
- `T5.7.1` · cluster_finding · /silent — **[CHAR-2]** S — T2.8 found no constitutional deposit evidence for CHAR-2. This prompt is closed accordingly. No developmental consequence from deposit can be evidenced.
- `T5.7.1` · cluster_finding · /silent — **[CHAR-3]** S — T2.8 found no constitutional deposit evidence for confession. This prompt is closed.
- `T5.7.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-4]** E — T2.8 found constitutional deposit evidence in Tit 3:11 ("warped" — completed deformation) and Ecc 8:12 (habituated formation through repeated sinning). The developmental consequence of the deposit is: (1) *Progressive moral distortion*: the character becomes increasingly "warped" — each iteration of conscience-suppressed sinning deepens the deformation. (2) *Increasing impermeability to correction*: the more deformed the character, the harder it is for the external corrective (prophetic word, apostolic rebuke, shame) to penetrate. 1Cor 15:34's "wake up" is addressed to those still in a recoverable state — but the trajectory of Tit 3:11's "warped" suggests a person for whom waking up has become progressively more difficult.
- `T5.7.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-5]** E — T2.8 found formational-constitutional deposit evidence: Neh 9:29 ("stiffened their neck" — formed posture of resistance), 2Ki 3:3 ("clung to" — active attachment), 2Ki 15:18 ("all his days" — lifetime orientation), 2Ki 15:9 ("as his fathers" — generational transmission). The developmental consequence: (1) *The will becomes progressively less responsive to the call to turn*: each iteration of refusal consolidates the will's non-departure direction, making the next refusal more natural and the possibility of departure less accessible; (2) *The communal moral environment is shaped toward the sinful course*: the leader's deposit of non-departure shapes the community's expectation of what is normal; (3) *The sacrifice becomes increasingly necessary and then foreclosed*: Heb 10:26's terminal condition is the developmental endpoint of the deposit.
- `T5.7.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-6]** E — T2.8 found deposit evidence at three levels: individual constitutional hardening (Exo 9:34), social-institutional embedding (Hos 8:11), and generational transmission (Hos 10:9). The developmental consequences: (1) *Individual*: progressively harder heart requiring increasingly strong corrective to reverse — Exo 9:34's hardening cycle shows each sinning act making the heart harder as the starting point for the next cycle; (2) *Institutional*: altars for sinning that outlast any individual's life and shape the community's sinning orientation for subsequent generations; (3) *Generational*: Hos 10:9's "from the days of Gibeah... there they have continued" — the deposit passes from generation to generation as the inherited sinning orientation that each generation receives without having to generate it from scratch.
- `T5.7.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-7]** E — T2.8 found the most concrete deposit evidence in the M10 corpus: the physical-structural deposit of Jeroboam's altar at Bethel (2Ki 23:15) plus the generational social deposit (1Ki 22:52 — three-generation pattern) and community moral-directional deposit (2Ki 21:11). The developmental consequences: (1) *Structural self-perpetuation*: the altar at Bethel transmits the contagious sinning orientation for ~300 years without requiring the original leader — the structural deposit is the most persistent transmission mechanism; (2) *Generational escalation*: 1Ki 14:22 — "more than all that their fathers had done" — the generational deposit does not merely maintain the transmitted level but escalates it; each generation adds to the inherited sinning orientation; (3) *Community identity formation*: 2Ki 21:11 — Manasseh's contagious sin produces a Judah whose moral identity is shaped around idolatrous sinning.
- `T5.7.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-8]** E — T2.8 found political-relational fracture as the deposit: the "to this day" permanence of the broken governing relationships (1Ki 12:19; 2Ki 8:22; 2Ch 10:19; 2Ch 21:10). The developmental consequences: (1) *Political community identity formed around the revolt*: Israel's identity as a kingdom separate from the Davidic house is formed and sustained by the ongoing revolt (1Ki 12:19); (2) *Permanent loss of governing relationship*: Edom and Libnah are no longer under Judah's authority — the development is the permanent establishment of independent governance; (3) *2Ch 21:10's spiritual deposit*: Jehoram's forsaking of the Lord has produced both the immediate revolts and an implied ongoing spiritual-political fragmentation in Judah.
- `T5.7.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-9]** E — T2.8 found three deposit forms. (1) *Character-formation deposit*: 2Ti 3:2 — the abusive/blasphemous disposition as a settled character trait; 1Ti 6:4 — conceit and craving deepened by sustained slander. The developmental consequence is a person increasingly constituted around sinful speech as their character. (2) *Irreversible Spirit-blasphemy deposit*: Mat 12:31 — "will not be forgiven" — the developmental consequence is permanent: no future act of repentance or faith can reverse the condition produced by this specific sinful speech. (3) *Constitutive-identity deposit* (VCG-03 — beast): the creature whose entire being is formed around its blasphemous nature — development has produced a creature for whom sinful speech is not an act but a being-condition.
- `T5.7.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-10]** E — T2.8 found deposit evidence for enticement: "hearts trained in greed" (2Pe 2:14 — *gegumnasmenos* — perfect participle indicating formed ongoing condition). The developmental consequences: (1) The formed heart becomes a self-generating enticement apparatus — "insatiable for sin" names the appetite that the formed heart continuously produces; the training has produced an insatiable craving rather than a satisfiable one. (2) The formed heart becomes an increasingly effective enticer of others — "they entice unsteady souls" (2Pe 2:14) — the deposit (trained greed) is what enables the effective extension of enticement to targets. The deposit is thus both self-perpetuating (insatiable craving) and other-extending (effective enticement apparatus).
- `T5.7.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-11]** E — T2.8 identified four deposit dimensions: death, hardening, indwelling sin, accumulating measure. The developmental consequences of each. (1) *Death deposit*: Rom 6:23 — "the wages of sin is death" — the ultimate developmental consequence is physical and spiritual death; Rom 8:10 — "the body is dead because of sin" — the body's mortality is the ongoing developmental consequence of the death-deposit. (2) *Hardening deposit*: Heb 3:13 — the deceitfulness-produced hardening progressively closes the heart against correction, making the condition increasingly entrenched with each cycle. (3) *Indwelling-sin deposit*: Rom 7:17/20 — "sin that dwells within me" — the ongoing developmental consequence is the divided-person experience where the indwelling force continuously overrides the will's intent. (4) *Accumulating-measure deposit*: 1Th 2:16 — the progressive accumulation toward the full measure means the developmental consequence is wrath-at-last; the deposit grows until it triggers the final judicial response.
- `T5.7.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-12]** E — T2.8 identified three deposit dimensions: slave-identity (Joh 8:34), devil-allegiance (1Jo 3:8), and death-trajectory (1Jo 5:16). The developmental consequences: (1) *Slave-identity deposit*: the constituted slave-identity produces a self-reinforcing enslavement — the slave-will can only choose within the sin-domain; each choice within the domain deepens the slave-identity deposit further. (2) *Devil-allegiance deposit*: 1Jo 3:8 — "of the devil, for the devil has been sinning from the beginning" — the allegiance deposit aligns the person with the devil's primordial sinning; the developmental consequence is increasing assimilation to the devil's sinning-identity rather than God's image. (3) *Death-trajectory deposit*: 1Jo 5:16 — the sin-unto-death trajectory is the terminal developmental consequence; the deposit moves the person toward a condition where the intercessory remedy is unavailing.
- `T5.7.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-13]** E — T2.8 identified four deposit dimensions: heart-engraving (Jer 17:1), relational separation (Isa 59:2), institutional legacy (VCG-04), stored divine record (Hos 13:12). Developmental consequences. (1) *Heart-engraving deposit*: Jer 17:1 — the iron-pen, diamond-point engraving develops into a progressively hardened inner character; the heart's inscription deepens with sustained sin-walking (VCG-04 — kings who walked in all the sins and did not depart); (2) *Relational separation deposit*: Isa 59:2 — the accumulated separation develops into a condition where prayer is no longer heard and God's face is permanently hidden; (3) *Institutional-legacy deposit*: VCG-04 — the 46-verse Jeroboam-sin legacy develops across royal generations into a national character defined by the founding leader's sin-record; (4) *Stored divine record*: Hos 13:12 — the sealed storage develops toward the eschatological day of visitation (Exo 32:34 — "in the day when I visit").
- `T5.7.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-14]** E — T2.8 identified four positive deposits: love (Luk 7:47), life (Col 2:13), Spirit (Act 2:38), redemption-identity (Col 1:14; Eph 1:7). Developmental consequences. (1) *Love deposit*: Luk 7:47 — "she loved much" — the love produced by forgiveness develops into sustained love-orientation; "he who is forgiven little, loves little" implies the developmental trajectory is directly proportional to the forgiveness received; (2) *Life deposit*: Col 2:13 — "made alive" — constitutional life enables all subsequent positive developmental capacity; without the life-deposit, positive formation cannot proceed; (3) *Spirit deposit*: Act 2:38 — the Spirit's indwelling is the formative agent for all subsequent sanctification; the Spirit's work depends on the forgiveness-reception that opened the Spirit's access; (4) *Redemption-identity deposit*: Col 1:14/Eph 1:7 — "we have redemption" — the permanent identity shapes the person's subsequent self-understanding and God-relationship.
- `T5.7.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-15]** E — T2.8 identified two deposit forms: prolonged sin-conditionedness (Isa 64:5) and defilement (Eze 37:23). Developmental consequences. (1) *Prolonged-condition deposit*: Isa 64:5 — "in our sins we have been a long time" — the developmental consequence is deepening entrenchment; the "long time" is not a static condition but a progressively deepening one; each generation adds its own sinning to the accumulated weight, and the salvific uncertainty ("shall we be saved?") increases as the duration extends. (2) *Defilement deposit*: Eze 37:23 — the accumulated backslidings produce a defilement that requires divine cleansing; the developmental consequence of sustained defilement is an increasingly deep need for the divine cleansing that only God can provide (Eze 37:23 — "I will cleanse them").
- `T5.7.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-16]** E — T2.8 identified three deposit forms: constituted Adamic standing (Rom 5:19), accumulated sins (Jam 5:20), and heart-defilement (Jam 4:8). Developmental consequences. (1) *Adamic-standing deposit*: Rom 5:19 — the constitutional sinner-identity precedes any individual sinning act; the developmental consequence is that every person begins the formation arc already bearing the sinner-identity before their own acts contribute to it; (2) *Accumulated-sins deposit*: Jam 5:20 — "a multitude of sins" — the developmental consequence is increasing moral weight and increasing directional drift toward death ("save his soul from death"); (3) *Heart-defilement deposit*: Jam 4:8 — "purify your hearts" — the developmental consequence is a defiled inner moral centre that requires divine cleansing; the heart's defilement deepens with sustained sinner-identity.
- `T5.7.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-17]** E — T2.8 identified three deposit forms: accumulated weight (Ezr 9:6), indelible stain (Jer 2:22), intergenerational transmission (Jer 32:18). Developmental consequences. (1) *Accumulated-weight deposit*: Ezr 9:6 — the guilt rising above the head and to the heavens produces the developmental consequence of inability to stand before God (Ezr 9:15); the accumulation progressively removes the capacity for upright God-relationship; (2) *Indelible-stain deposit*: Jer 2:22 — the stain that no soap removes produces the developmental consequence of permanent divine visibility; the person cannot achieve concealment from God regardless of self-cleansing effort; (3) *Intergenerational-transmission deposit*: Jer 32:18 — "guilt of fathers to their children" — the developmental consequence is that each subsequent generation begins with an inherited guilt-burden added to the previous generation's deposit.
- `T5.7.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-18]** E — T2.8 identified three deposit forms: divine-record deposit (Hos 13:12 — bound up), bodily deposit (Eze 32:27 — on bones), generational deposit (Exo 20:5 — visited on children). Developmental consequences. (1) *Divine-record deposit*: Hos 13:12's sealed iniquity develops toward the moment of divine recall for judgment (Hos 9:9 — "he will remember their iniquity; he will punish their sins") — the sealed deposit is developing toward its appointed reckoning; Gen 15:16 — toward completion; (2) *Bodily deposit*: Psa 31:10 — "my strength fails because of my iniquity, and my bones waste away" — the developmental consequence is progressive bodily deterioration; the bodily deposit deepens into physical ruin; (3) *Generational deposit*: Lam 5:7 — "our fathers sinned, and are no more; and we bear their iniquities" — the developmental consequence is that each successive generation receives and must address an inherited moral-crime burden alongside their own.
- `T5.7.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-19]** E — T2.8 identified three deposit forms: divine-count record (Amos 1:3), identity-level labelling (Isa 48:8), and yoke-burden (Lam 1:14). Developmental consequences. (1) *Divine-count deposit*: Amos 1:3's counted transgression develops toward the threshold (Dan 8:23 — "reached their limit") — the count grows until judgment; the developmental consequence is progressive approach to irrevocable judgment; (2) *Identity-level deposit*: Isa 48:8 — "called a rebel from before birth" develops into Isa 57:4 — "children of transgression" — the identity-label is transmitted generationally; the developmental consequence is the formation of transgression-identity as a hereditary character; (3) *Yoke-burden deposit*: Lam 1:14 — yoke on neck causing strength to fail — the developmental consequence is progressive physical-volitional incapacitation.
- `T5.7.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-20]** E — T2.8 identified three deposit forms: generational-communal deposit (2Ch 30:7), identity-level foreknown deposit (Isa 48:8), national shame deposit (Dan 9:7). Developmental consequences. (1) *Generational deposit*: 2Ch 30:7's desolation of the faithless fathers becomes the warning inherited by the children; the developmental consequence is both the warning (don't repeat) and the pattern (the children have inherited a proven-faithless moral heritage); Lev 26:40 — "iniquity of their fathers in their treachery" — the generational deposit must be named and confessed by the next generation; (2) *Identity-level deposit*: Isa 48:8's pre-birth constitutional bent develops into the compounded treachery of Isa 24:16 and Jer 3:8/11; (3) *National shame deposit*: Dan 9:7's open shame in all lands of exile is the sustained developmental consequence of accumulated faithlessness.
- `T5.7.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-21]** E — T2.8 identified three deposit forms: mind-depravity (1Ti 6:5), desire-corruption (Eph 4:22; 2Pe 2:19), bodily bondage (Rom 8:21). Developmental consequences. (1) *Mind-depravity deposit*: 1Ti 6:5 — "depraved in mind and deprived of the truth" — the developmental consequence is progressive truth-deprivation; the depraved mind cannot self-correct because the corrective faculty itself is corrupted; (2) *Desire-corruption deposit*: Eph 4:22 — ongoing "corrupt through deceitful desires" produces the developing enslavement of 2Pe 2:19 — "slaves of corruption"; the desire that initially corrupted becomes the master; (3) *Bodily bondage deposit*: Rom 8:21 — the creation's "bondage to corruption" awaits eschatological liberation — a sustained constitutional condition.
- `T5.7.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-22]** E — T2.8 identified three deposit forms: divine memory-record (Rev 18:5), fixed character (Luk 16:10), shameless conscience (Zep 3:5). Developmental consequences. (1) *Divine memory deposit*: Rev 18:5 — "her sins are heaped high as heaven" — the developmental consequence is the accumulating height of the record, moving toward the threshold of judgment; God's sustained memory of injustice develops toward the eschatological accounting; (2) *Fixed character deposit*: Luk 16:10 — dishonest across all scales — the character-deposit develops toward complete character-consolidation as the unjust type (VCG-03); the small-scale dishonesty develops into the large-scale character-consistency; (3) *Shameless conscience deposit*: Zep 3:5 — develops toward complete moral insensibility — the progression from shame-capable to shame-insensible.
- `T5.7.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-1]** E — Limited evidence. Isa 1:4 ("offspring of evildoers, children who deal corruptly") names a generational consequence in the form of a moral heritage — children shaped in the sinning pattern of their forebears. This is a social/formative transmission rather than a biological mechanism. The counter-evidence is also present: Eze 18:20 ("the son shall not suffer for the iniquity of the father") — though this addresses legal accountability rather than formative transmission, it warrants caution about over-reading generational consequence from Isa 1:4 alone.
- `T5.7.2` · cluster_finding · /silent — **[CHAR-2]** S — T2.8 found no deposit evidence. The communal guilt from priestly sinning (Lev 4:3) was noted as communal rather than generational. No generational-deposit evidence is present in the CHAR-2 corpus. This prompt is closed.
- `T5.7.2` · cluster_finding · /silent — **[CHAR-3]** S — T2.8 found no deposit. Lam 5:7 ("our fathers sinned, and are no more; and we bear their iniquities") evidences generational consequence of sin, not of confession. No generational consequence from confession-as-deposit is evidenced. This prompt is closed.
- `T5.7.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-4]** E — Limited generational evidence. Jer 16:10's context (Jer 16:11–13 — "your fathers forsook me... and you have done worse than your fathers") implies a generational transmission of conscience suppression: each generation's moral blindness is at least partially inherited from and deepened beyond the previous generation's. The community that asks "what sin have we committed?" has been formed over generations of accumulated unfaithfulness — their conscience suppression is not merely individual but generationally transmitted. The deposit of conscience suppression across generations is the progressive deformation of a community's moral self-awareness.
- `T5.7.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-5]** E — Yes, clearly evidenced. 2Ki 15:9 — "as his fathers had done. He did not depart from the sins of Jeroboam" — the generational consequence is explicit: the next king inherits the refusal-orientation and does not depart from it. The Jeroboam-sin formula repeated across 10+ kings over the full span of Israel's history in Kings demonstrates that refusal to repent deposits a generational moral orientation that each successor receives and sustains. The "as his fathers" formula is the evidential marker of the generational deposit: the deposit is a transmitted moral posture of non-departure that each generation receives and does not reject.
- `T5.7.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-6]** E — Yes, explicitly. Hos 10:9 — "from the days of Gibeah, you have sinned, O Israel; there they have *continued*" — the generational consequence is named: the sinning that began at Gibeah has been continued by subsequent generations to the present. Pro 13:22 — "the sinner's wealth is laid up for the righteous" — the generational consequence of habitual defection is lost inheritance: the sinner cannot transmit wealth to the next generation within his own family line; it is redirected to the righteous. The generational deposit is both the transmitted sinning orientation (Hos 10:9) and the foreclosed inheritance (Pro 13:22).
- `T5.7.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-7]** E — Yes, explicitly and extensively. The Jeroboam-formula's repetition across 12+ kings over the full span of Israel's history is the evidence: each king referenced as following "the sins of Jeroboam... who made Israel to sin" shows the deposit being transmitted and inherited generation by generation. 1Ki 22:52 — three generations receiving successively. 2Ki 23:15 — Josiah acting against a deposit ~300 years old. The generational consequence is the most prominent feature of the CHAR-7 deposit: contagious sin produces a multigenerational structural and moral deposit that shapes each successive generation's leadership and community moral orientation without requiring the original leader's personal presence.
- `T5.7.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-8]** E — Yes. 1Ki 12:19 / 2Ch 10:19 — "to this day" — the revolt of Israel from the Davidic house is a generational consequence: the fracture persists across generations from Rehoboam's time to the narrator's present. 2Ki 8:22 / 2Ch 21:10 — "to this day" — Edom's revolt similarly persists generationally. The generational consequence is the political identity of the revolting communities: they remain in revolt across generations without restoration, making the revolt a generationally transmitted political-identity condition.
- `T5.7.2` · cluster_finding · /silent — **[CHAR-9]** S — The corpus does not evidence generational transmission of sinful speech's consequences. The characteristic's consequences are primarily individual (the speaker's irreversible condition in Mat 12:31; the character formed in 2Ti 3:2) rather than generationally transmitted. The beast-blasphemy (VCG-03) is a cosmic-eschatological phenomenon not a generational transmission.
- `T5.7.2` · cluster_finding · /silent — **[CHAR-10]** S — The 5-verse corpus does not evidence generational transmission of these mechanisms' consequences beyond the immediate social context (Gal 2:13 — within a single community episode). The "hearts trained in greed" of 2Pe 2:14 is individual formation rather than generational transmission. Noted.
- `T5.7.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-11]** E — Yes — the most extensively evidenced generational consequence in the M10 corpus. Rom 5:12 — "sin came into the world through one man, and death through sin, and so death spread to all men" — the Adamic transmission is the paradigm of generational consequence: the condition entered through one man and spread to all subsequent generations of humanity. Joh 9:34 — "born in utter sin" — the birth-framing confirms that the condition is generationally inherited, received at birth from the prior generation's participation in the Adamic condition. The generational consequence is universal (all humanity, all generations) and foundational to the characteristic's definition.
- `T5.7.2` · cluster_finding · /silent — **[CHAR-12]** S — The 9-verse corpus does not evidence generational transmission of sin's enslaving power beyond the individual. The Adamic transmission of the universal sin-condition (addressed in CHAR-11) is not explicitly named here. The enslaving power in this corpus operates through individual practice (Joh 8:34) and universal declaration (Gal 3:22) rather than through explicit generational transmission.
- `T5.7.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-13]** E — Yes — the most extensive generational evidence in M10. (1) *VCG-04 (46 verses)*: the Jeroboam-sin legacy spans multiple royal generations — from Jeroboam's own act through dozens of successor kings who "walked in his sins and did not depart" — the sin-record is transmitted generationally as an institutional inheritance; (2) *Exo 34:7*: "visiting the iniquity of the fathers on the children and the children's children, to the third and the fourth generation" — the divine record's generational reach is explicitly named; (3) *Dan 9:16*: "for our sins, and for the iniquities of our fathers, Jerusalem and your people have become a byword" — the combined record of the present generation and their fathers is the basis for national judgment; (4) *Psa 51:5*: "in sin did my mother conceive me" — the inherited sin-record condition at birth names the generational constitutional transmission.
- `T5.7.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-14]** E — Yes — in both registers. (1) *OT generational rhythm*: Exo 30:10 — "once in the year throughout your generations" — the kip.pu.rim cycle is explicitly generational; each generation participates in the seeking-and-receiving through the annual rhythm; Lev 25:9 — "on the Day of Atonement you shall sound the trumpet throughout all your land" — the annual proclamation is a generational formation practice; (2) *NT proclamation scope*: Act 10:43 — "to him all the prophets bear witness that everyone who believes in him receives forgiveness" — the universal proclamation to all generations is implied in "everyone who believes"; Mat 26:28 — "for many" encompasses all generations who receive through faith.
- `T5.7.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-15]** E — Yes — by definition. The characteristic is constitutively generational: Isa 43:27 — "your first father sinned... and your mediators transgressed" — the deposit of the first father's sinning is carried forward through successive generations to the present community. The generational consequence is the most directly evidenced form of deposit-carrying-forward in M10: the condition is received rather than individually generated, which is the characteristic's defining feature. Isa 64:5 — "in our sins we have been a long time" — the "long time" spanning generations confirms the deposit's transmission across generations as the characteristic's primary mechanism.
- `T5.7.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-16]** E — Yes. Num 32:14 — "a brood of sinful men, to increase still more the fierce anger of the Lord against Israel" — the new generation is characterised as a "brood" inheriting the sinner-type from their fathers; the generational consequence is that the sinner-identity is transmitted through lineage and community formation. The "brood" language implies the sinner-type is reproduced: the fathers' sinful character generates offspring formed in the same mould. This connects CHAR-16 to CHAR-15 (Generational sin) at the character-formation level.
- `T5.7.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-17]** E — Yes — explicitly. Jer 32:18 — "you repay the guilt of fathers to their children after them" — intergenerational guilt-transmission is named as a characteristic of how God's justice operates; the guilt-deposit from one generation becomes part of the next generation's inherited moral burden. Isa 14:21 — "prepare slaughter for his sons because of the guilt of their fathers" — the children bear consequence for ancestral guilt. Ezr 9:7 — "from the days of our fathers to this day we have been in great guilt" — the accumulated generational guilt-deposit extends from the founding ancestors to Ezra's present community.
- `T5.7.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-18]** E — Yes — most extensively of any M10 characteristic studied. Exo 20:5/34:7; Deu 5:9; Num 14:18 — the third-and-fourth-generation formula names the temporal extent of the generational consequence; Lev 26:39 — "because of their iniquity, and also because of the iniquities of their fathers they shall rot away like them" — the rotting is compounded across generations; Lam 5:7 — present generation bears fathers' iniquities; Jer 32:18 — "you repay the guilt of fathers to their children after them"; Isa 65:7 — "both your iniquities and your fathers' iniquities together." The generational consequence of iniquity is the most densely evidenced inter-generational moral dynamic in M10.
- `T5.7.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-19]** E — Yes. Isa 57:4 — "children of transgression, offspring of deceit" — the transgression-identity is transmitted to the next generation; Isa 48:8 — "from before birth called a rebel" — the rebel-label precedes personal choice within a transgression-legacy family or community. The Amos oracle-series (VCG-03 — Amos 1:3–2:6) shows that national transgressions accumulate across generations before reaching the threshold; the children inherit both the accumulated transgression-count and its judgment-trajectory.
- `T5.7.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-20]** E — Yes — most explicitly through Lev 26:40 which requires the confession to cover the fathers' treachery alongside one's own: "confess their iniquity and the iniquity of their fathers in their treachery" — the generational deposit is real enough that its confession is required. 2Ch 30:7 — "do not be like your fathers and your brothers, who were faithless... so that he made them a desolation" — the fathers' faithlessness produced a desolation that the children could visibly observe as warning. The generational consequence of faithlessness is thus both spiritual (pattern transmitted) and material (observable desolation).
- `T5.7.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-21]** E — Yes. Deu 32:20 — "a perverse generation, children in whom is no faithfulness" — the perversity characterises an entire generation inherited; the generational deposit is the settled perverse character of the people as a whole. 1Cor 15:33 — "bad company ruins good morals" — the social-contagion mechanism produces generational consequence as each corrupted generation corrupts the next through company-influence. The generational consequence of perversion is the formation of successively more corrupt generations whose "former manner of life" (Eph 4:22) is progressively more perverted.
- `T5.7.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-22]** E — Yes — through two passages. (1) Psa 125:3 — "the scepter of wickedness shall not rest on the land allotted to the righteous, lest the righteous stretch out their hands to do wrong" — sustained injustice's rule over the land creates generational pressure that could corrupt even the righteous across time; (2) Deu 32:4 — God's "without iniquity" character established in the context of the Song of Moses addressing a perverse generation (Deu 32:20 — CHAR-21 adjacent) — the generational injustice context implies that the community's accumulated injustice creates the condition in which the next generation is formed.

Proceeding to Tier T6.   ---  ## T6 — Inter-characteristic Relationships (24 prompts)

**T6.6 · Shared Verse Anchors**  (66)

- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-1]** E — Several verses are likely to function as anchors in adjacent characteristics. Psa 51:4 ("against you, you only, have I sinned") is the paradigmatic confession verse — it is the anchor for CHAR-3 (Confession) as well as for CHAR-1. Eze 18:4/20 ("the soul who sins shall die") anchor the personal-accountability dimension that is relevant to CHAR-17 (Guilt as inner-being state) and CHAR-13 (Sin as divine record). Lev 4:35 ("the priest shall make atonement for him for the sin which he has committed, and he shall be forgiven") is the Levitical formula that anchors CHAR-14 (Forgiveness sought and received) as well as CHAR-1. 2Sa 12:13 (sin confessed → forgiveness declared) spans CHAR-1, CHAR-3, and CHAR-14.
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-2]** E — Lev 5:5 ("when he realizes his guilt... and confesses the sin he has committed") is likely to function as an anchor in CHAR-3 (Confession) — the realisation-and-confession sequence is the definitive CHAR-3 trigger event. Num 15:28 ("the priest shall make atonement... and he shall be forgiven") is a candidate anchor for CHAR-14 (Forgiveness sought and received) — the forgiveness formula for inadvertent sinning may anchor that characteristic's study. Lev 4:3 ("the anointed priest who sins, thus bringing guilt on the people") is a candidate anchor for CHAR-7 (Contagious sin / leadership transmission), showing how one person's sinning implicates others.
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-3]** E — Several verses serve as anchors in adjacent characteristics. 2Sa 24:10 — the paradigm case of conscience-initiated confession — is also a primary anchor for CHAR-17 (Guilt as inner-being state) because "David's heart struck him" is the conscience's retrospective guilt-engagement. Dan 9:5 — the extended multi-dimensional confession — is likely a primary anchor for both CHAR-3 and for any intercessory-prayer characteristic. Eze 33:16 — "None of the sins shall be remembered against him" — is a candidate anchor for CHAR-14 (Forgiveness sought and received), evidencing God's response to turning and implied confession. Luk 15:21 — the prodigal's enacted confession — is likely the NT anchor for both CHAR-3 and CHAR-14.
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-4]** E — Jer 2:35 — the active denial "I have not sinned" — is a strong candidate for anchoring within CHAR-3 (Confession) as the anti-type: the verse that most precisely defines what genuine confession is not. Ecc 8:12 — the sinner who does evil a hundred times — is a candidate anchor for CHAR-6 (Habitual defection) — the portrait of habitual sinning without consequence-recognition. 1Cor 15:34 — the command to "wake up and do not go on sinning" — may function as an anchor for CHAR-1 (ongoing wilful sinning addressed) as well as CHAR-4. Tit 3:11 — "warped and sinful, self-condemned" — is a candidate anchor for CHAR-16 (Sinner as moral character), evidencing the constituted character-type produced by sustained sinning.
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-5]** E — 2Ki 3:3 — "he clung to the sin of Jeroboam the son of Nebat, which he made Israel to sin; he did not depart from it" — is a candidate anchor for both CHAR-5 and CHAR-7 (Contagious sin — "which he made Israel to sin"). Heb 10:26 — "if we go on sinning deliberately after receiving the knowledge of the truth, there no longer remains a sacrifice for sins" — is a strong candidate anchor for CHAR-1 (Wilful sinning — the deliberate ongoing sinning) as well as CHAR-5. Neh 9:29 — "stubborn shoulder and stiffened their neck and would not obey" — may anchor studies of community-level conscience suppression (CHAR-4) or broader rebellion characteristics.
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-6]** E — Exo 9:34 — "sinned yet again and hardened his heart" — is a candidate anchor for both CHAR-5 (the sinning-yet-again as volitional refusal) and CHAR-6 (the hardening as habitual-defection mechanism). 1Ki 8:46 — "there is no one who does not sin" — is a candidate anchor for CHAR-1 or CHAR-16 (Sinner as moral character) given its universal sinning statement. 1Jo 3:6 — "no one who keeps on sinning has either seen him or known him" — is a strong candidate anchor for CHAR-16 (Sinner as constituted moral character in contrast to the abider). Hos 8:11 — altars for sinning producing more sinning — is likely to anchor discussions of institutional sin-transmission (CHAR-7 — Contagious sin).
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-7]** E — Several verses serve multiple characteristic anchors. 1Ki 14:16 — "the sins of Jeroboam, which he sinned and made Israel to sin" — is the clearest shared anchor between CHAR-7 (contagious sin — "made Israel to sin" / Hiphil) and CHAR-5 (refusal to repent — the Jeroboam-sin reference as the benchmark for non-departure). 2Ki 23:15 — Josiah's demolition of Jeroboam's altar — anchors CHAR-7 (the structural deposit of contagious sin being dismantled) and may anchor CHAR-5 (Josiah as the counter-example to the Kings' non-departure pattern). Exo 23:33 — "lest they make you sin" — is a candidate anchor for any study of temptation/enticement (potentially CHAR-10 — Specialised sinful mechanisms/deleazō enticement).
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-8]** E — 2Ch 21:10 — "because he had forsaken the Lord, the God of his fathers" — is a candidate anchor for CHAR-20 (Faithlessness as covenant-breaking sin) given its forsaking language (azav — abandonment of covenantal relationship). 1Ki 12:19 / 2Ch 10:19 — "Israel has been in rebellion against the house of David to this day" — are candidates for any study of the Davidic covenant fracture in M10's broader scope. The pa.sha verses across the corpus are candidates for CHAR-19's anchor-study given the shared root.
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-9]** E — Mat 15:19 — "out of the heart come evil thoughts, murder, adultery, sexual immorality, theft, false witness, slander" — is a candidate anchor for CHAR-16 (Sinner as moral character) and CHAR-1 (Wilful sinning), as the verse names the full range of evil outputs of the corrupted heart. Mat 12:31 — "the blasphemy against the Spirit will not be forgiven" — is a candidate anchor for any study of the unforgivable sin and Spirit-level moral consequence, connecting to CHAR-17 (Guilt as inner-being state) territory. Jude 9 / 2Pe 2:11 — the angelic restraint of blasphemous speech — may anchor studies of creaturely limits and the human-divine boundary in M10 and related clusters.
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-10]** E — Jam 1:14 — "each person is tempted when he is lured and enticed by his own desire" — is a primary anchor candidate for M14 (desire/temptation cluster), where desire-exploitation is the central subject. 2Pe 2:14 — "hearts trained in greed, entice unsteady souls" — is a candidate anchor for M14 (enticement as desire-mechanism) and CHAR-6 (the trained-greed heart as formed sinning disposition). Gal 2:13 — "acted hypocritically along with him, so that even Barnabas was led astray" — is a candidate anchor for M08 (hypocrisy/pride cluster).
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-11]** E — Several verses function as primary anchors across multiple characteristics. Rom 3:23 — "all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God" — is the definitive anchor for CHAR-11 (universal scope + glory-gap definition) and would anchor any M10 discussion of universality. Gen 4:7 — "sin is crouching at the door. Its desire is for you, and you must rule over it" — anchors both CHAR-11 (sin as power crouching) and CHAR-12 (enslaving power specifically). Rom 5:12 — "sin came into the world through one man" — anchors both CHAR-11 (the universal condition's entry) and CHAR-15 (Generational sin — the transmission across humanity). Mat 9:2 (parallel in Mar 2:5; Luk 5:20) — "your sins are forgiven" — anchors both CHAR-11 (the condition's debt addressed) and CHAR-14 (Forgiveness sought and received).
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-12]** E — Two primary shared anchors. (1) Joh 8:34 — "everyone who practices sin is a slave to sin" — is a primary anchor for CHAR-12 (the practice-to-slavery mechanism) and would also anchor CHAR-6 (habitual practice as the mechanism) and CHAR-11 (slavery as one expression of the universal sin-condition). (2) Gal 3:22 — "imprisoned everything under sin" — is shared with CHAR-11 (the universal imprisonment as the structural condition) and with the broader M14/M31 cross-cluster registers (faith-promise and its receipt). (3) 1Jo 3:9 — "no one born of God makes a practice of sinning" — anchors both CHAR-12 (the liberation from enslavement) and CHAR-5/6 (the non-habitual-sinning pattern as the born-of-God characteristic's opposite).
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-13]** E — Several verses function as shared anchors. (1) Psa 51:3 — "my sin is ever before me" — shared anchor with CHAR-17 (guilt as inner-being state); (2) Psa 51:5 — "in sin did my mother conceive me" — shared anchor with CHAR-11 (universal condition — birth-origin framing); (3) Jer 17:1 — "engraved on the tablet of their heart" — primary anchor for CHAR-13; also an anchor for the heart-dimension analyses of CHAR-4 and CHAR-17; (4) Lev 16:21 — Day of Atonement — shared anchor with CHAR-3 (confession — the high priest's confession over the goat) and the broader priestly atonement system; (5) Num 32:23 — "your sin will find you out" — shared with CHAR-1 (the act's inescapable consequence).
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-14]** E — Yes. (1) Exo 32:30/31 — shared with CHAR-13 (sin as divine record — Moses's naming of the great sin before God); (2) Mat 26:28 — "blood of the covenant... for the forgiveness of sins" — also anchors M11 characteristics (forgiveness declared, new covenant); (3) Act 2:38 — "repent and be baptized... for the forgiveness of your sins" — anchors M11 (repentance) and is structurally related to CHAR-3 (confession) and M11 (restoration through Spirit); (4) Col 2:13 — "dead in your trespasses... made alive" — shared with CHAR-11 (universal condition — death in trespasses) and CHAR-12 (enslaving power — the trespass-death condition).
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-15]** E — Yes. (1) Isa 43:27 — "your first father sinned" — shared with CHAR-13 (sin as divine record — the ancestral sin as the origin of a generational record); (2) Psa 78:17/32 — "they sinned still more... they still sinned" — shared with CHAR-1 (wilful sinning — the persistent escalating acts) and CHAR-5 (refusal to repent — the "still sinned despite wonders"); (3) Eze 37:23 — "save them from all the backslidings... cleanse them... my people" — shared with CHAR-14 (forgiveness sought and received — the divine cleansing) and M11 (restoration).
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-16]** E — Several. (1) Rom 5:19 — "made sinners... made righteous" — shared anchor with CHAR-11 (universal condition through Adam); (2) Luk 5:8 — "I am a sinful man, O Lord" — shared with CHAR-3 (confession); (3) Luk 18:13 — "be merciful to me, a sinner" — shared with CHAR-14 (forgiveness sought) and CHAR-3 (confession); (4) Jam 4:8 — "cleanse your hands, sinners; purify your hearts" — shared with CHAR-4 (conscience) and CHAR-14 (seeking cleansing); (5) 1Ti 1:15 — "Christ Jesus came to save sinners, of whom I am the foremost" — shared with CHAR-14 (Christ's saving mission).
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-17]** E — Several. (1) Lev 5:5 — "when he realizes his guilt... and confesses" — shared with CHAR-3 (confession) and CHAR-14 (forgiveness sought); (2) Isa 6:7 — "your guilt is taken away" — shared with CHAR-14 (forgiveness received through priestly act) and CHAR-13 (divine record cleared); (3) Gen 4:13 — "my punishment is greater than I can bear" — shared with CHAR-13's punitive-dimension and potentially CHAR-15 (generational sin — the sin of Cain as the founding ancestral act); (4) Jer 2:22 — "stain of your guilt still before me" — shared with CHAR-13 (indelibility of divine record); (5) Exo 28:38 — Aaron bearing Israel's guilt — shared with CHAR-14 (priestly atonement system).
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-18]** E — Several. (1) Lev 16:21/22 — Aaron confessing Israel's iniquities over the scapegoat — shared with CHAR-1 (the sinning acts being confessed), CHAR-3 (the confession act itself), CHAR-14 (the atonement mechanism); (2) Isa 53:5/6/11 — servant bearing our iniquities — shared with CHAR-14 (the forgiveness received through the servant) and CHAR-13 (the divine record cleared through vicarious bearing); (3) Exo 20:5 — "visiting the iniquity of the fathers" — shared with CHAR-15 (generational sin transmission mechanism); (4) Psa 51:5 — "brought forth in iniquity" — shared with CHAR-11 (sin as universal condition); (5) Jer 31:34 — "I will forgive their iniquity" — shared with CHAR-14 (new covenant forgiveness).
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-19]** E — Several. (1) Lev 16:16/21 — all iniquities and transgressions confessed over scapegoat — shared with CHAR-18 (iniquity) and CHAR-3 (confession); (2) Exo 34:7 — three-term formula — shared with CHAR-18 (iniquity) and CHAR-1 (sin); (3) Psa 51:1/3 — "blot out my transgressions; I know my transgressions" — shared with CHAR-17 (guilt-awareness) and CHAR-14 (forgiveness sought); (4) Isa 53:5/8 — "pierced for our transgressions; stricken for the transgression of my people" — shared with CHAR-14 (vicarious bearing as forgiveness) and CHAR-18 (iniquity crushing him); (5) Rom 5:15/16/17/18 (paraptōma) — shared with CHAR-11 (sin as universal condition) through the one-man's-trespass condemnation of all.
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-20]** E — Several. (1) Lev 26:40 — "confess their iniquity and... treachery" — shared with CHAR-18 (iniquity) and CHAR-3 (confession); (2) Hos 6:7 — "transgressed the covenant; dealt faithlessly with me" — shared with CHAR-19 (covenant-transgression); (3) Eze 18:24 — "treachery of which he is guilty and the sin he has committed, for them he shall die" — shared with CHAR-19 (the sinning) and potentially CHAR-17 (guilt); (4) 2Ch 29:6 — "our fathers have been unfaithful... they have forsaken him and have turned away their faces" — shared with CHAR-5 (refusal to repent) and CHAR-6 (habitual defection); (5) 1Ch 10:13 — "Saul died for his breach of faith... also consulted a medium" — shared with CHAR-1 (wilful sinning — the consulting a medium) and CHAR-19 (transgression of command).
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-21]** E — Several. (1) Dan 9:5 — "we have sinned and done wrong (*a.vah*) and acted wickedly and rebelled (*pa.sha*)" — shared with CHAR-1 (sinning), CHAR-18 (acted wickedly), CHAR-19 (rebelled); (2) 1Ki 8:47 — "we have sinned and acted perversely and wickedly" — shared with CHAR-1 and CHAR-18; (3) Eph 4:22 — "old self corrupt through deceitful desires" — shared with CHAR-1 (the sins arising from the old self) and potentially CHAR-16 (the sinner as moral character — the old self as the sinner-identity); (4) Pro 6:14 — "perverted heart devises evil, continually sowing discord" — CHAR-21's primary anchor; functions within CHAR-16's person-type characterisation as well.
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-22]** E — Several. (1) Psa 58:2 — "in your hearts you devise wrongs; your hands deal out violence" — shared with CHAR-21 (heart-devising as perversity) and CHAR-1 (the wilful sinning acts); (2) Eze 18:24 — "a righteous person turns away from his righteousness and does injustice" — shared with CHAR-20 (faithlessness — turning from righteousness) and CHAR-19 (transgression — the turning as crossing); (3) Psa 53:1 — "the fool says in his heart, 'There is no God'... doing abominable iniquity" — shared with CHAR-21 (perversion as inner inversion of the moral foundation); (4) 1Pe 3:18 — "the righteous for the unrighteous" — shared with the M26 (Righteousness) cluster's Christological usage.
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-1]** E — Shared anchors (Psa 51:4, Eze 18:4/20, 2Sa 12:13, Lev 4:35) reveal that wilful sinning, confession, guilt, and forgiveness form an integrated sequence in Scripture — the same verse evidences multiple characteristics because the characteristics are not separate events but *facets of a single moral episode*. Psa 51:4's anchor function across CHAR-1 and CHAR-3 reveals that in the primary biblical evidence, sinning and confession are not separable into entirely distinct inner events but are the two faces of a single relational encounter with God: the act of sinning and the act of naming it before God.
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-2]** E — Lev 5:5 as shared anchor between CHAR-2 and CHAR-3 reveals that the realisation-of-guilt event is simultaneously the conclusion of the inadvertent sinning episode (CHAR-2) and the opening of the confession episode (CHAR-3) — the two characteristics are contiguous and overlap at the moment of realisation. The same verse is the hinge-event that closes CHAR-2 and opens CHAR-3. This confirms that CHAR-2 and CHAR-3 are not independent events in the inner person but consecutive phases of a single moral episode that the programme analyses from different characteristic lenses.
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-3]** E — 2Sa 24:10 as shared anchor between CHAR-3 and CHAR-17 reveals what was noted in T6.5.2: the two characteristics are contiguous events within a single moral episode, and their shared anchor confirms that they cannot be fully understood separately. The verse holds both simultaneously — inner (guilt) and outer (confession) — because in Scripture's portrayal of genuine moral response these two are nearly inseparable. The shared anchor is not a classification problem but a theological reality: genuine confession is guilt's completion.
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-4]** E — Jer 2:35 as a shared anchor between CHAR-4 and CHAR-3 reveals that confession and conscience suppression are not merely adjacent characteristics but *structurally defined by each other's negation*: the verse is an anchor in both studies precisely because "I have not sinned" is the most explicit inversion of "I have sinned." The shared anchor confirms that CHAR-4 cannot be fully understood without CHAR-3 and vice versa — they are the defining contrast pair in the inner-being landscape of sin-and-its-acknowledgment.
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-5]** E — 2Ki 3:3 as shared anchor between CHAR-5 and CHAR-7 reveals that leadership's refusal to repent and the communal contagion of sin are *constitutively linked* in the Kings pattern: the leader's inner volitional non-departure is simultaneously the mechanism of communal sin-transmission. The two characteristics are not separable in the leadership context — the leader's CHAR-5 is the CHAR-7 mechanism. Heb 10:26 as shared anchor between CHAR-5 and CHAR-1 reveals that at the extreme end of refusal to repent, wilful sinning and refusal to stop sinning are inseparable: the deliberate ongoing sinning is simultaneously the act (CHAR-1) and the refusal to stop (CHAR-5).
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-6]** E — Exo 9:34 as shared anchor between CHAR-5 and CHAR-6 confirms what the developmental analysis already reveals: the two characteristics are contiguous in time and developmentally linked — the CHAR-5 volitional refusal (sinning yet again) and the CHAR-6 constitutional deformation (hardening) occur in the same verse because they are the same event described from two analytical perspectives. 1Jo 3:6 as shared anchor between CHAR-6 and CHAR-16 confirms that habitual defection and the constituted sinner-identity are the process and its product.
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-7]** E — 1Ki 14:16 as shared anchor between CHAR-7 and CHAR-5 confirms what T6.5 established: the two characteristics are co-present in the same narrative events but analytically distinguishable — the Jeroboam-sin formula simultaneously names the leader's personal non-departure (CHAR-5) and the community transmission (CHAR-7). The shared verse is not a classification problem but a theological reality: the leader's personal sinning and its communal transmission are a single moral event that the programme analyses from two analytical perspectives. 2Ki 23:15's shared anchoring confirms that the structural deposit (CHAR-7's most distinctive feature) and the volitional reversal (counter to CHAR-5) are addressed by the same counter-formation act (Josiah's demolition).
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-8]** E — 2Ch 21:10 as shared anchor between CHAR-8 and CHAR-20 reveals that political revolt (CHAR-8) and faithlessness (CHAR-20) are causally linked: the faithlessness precedes and enables the political revolt. The shared anchor confirms what the corpus evidences: the inner spiritual condition (CHAR-20 — forsaking the Lord) generates the outer political consequence (CHAR-8 — political revolt). The anchor verse holds both the cause (CHAR-20) and the consequence (CHAR-8) in a single verse, confirming their causal proximity.
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-9]** E — Mat 15:19 as shared anchor between CHAR-9 and CHAR-1/16 reveals that slander is explicitly placed by Jesus within the full inventory of heart-generated evil acts — sinful speech is not a marginal or secondary evil but part of the core list of what the corrupted heart produces alongside murder, adultery, and theft. The shared anchor confirms CHAR-9's full moral weight: sinful speech belongs in the same category as the gravest moral acts because it shares the same origin (the corrupted heart) and the same moral register (wilful transgression).
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-10]** E — Jam 1:14 as shared anchor between CHAR-10 (M10 sinful-mechanism register) and M14 (desire/temptation register) reveals the cross-cluster nature of the enticement mechanism: it is simultaneously a sinful-failure pathway (M10 domain — the moral failure of yielding to enticement) and a desire/temptation dynamic (M14 domain — the operation of temptation through desire). The verse belongs to both clusters because it names both the mechanism (temptation through desire) and the moral dimension (the person yields to a lure — a sinful-failure outcome).
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-11]** E — The shared anchors (Gen 4:7 with CHAR-12; Rom 5:12 with CHAR-15; Mat 9:2 with CHAR-14) reveal that CHAR-11 is the *hub characteristic* of M10: its verses are the primary sources from which multiple other characteristics draw their anchoring evidence. This confirms what T6.1.2 established: CHAR-11 is the foundational condition; other characteristics are specific registers or consequences of that condition. The shared anchors are the points where the universal ground-condition intersects with specific aspects of that condition (its power-form, its transmission, its remedy).
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-12]** E — Joh 8:34 shared with CHAR-6 and CHAR-11 reveals that the practice-to-slavery mechanism is the analytical nexus where CHAR-6 (practice), CHAR-12 (slavery), and CHAR-11 (condition) converge: the same verse simultaneously names the mechanism (CHAR-6's practice register), the outcome (CHAR-12's enslaving-power register), and the condition (CHAR-11's structural state). The verse is analytically multi-characteristic precisely because it names the full causal chain: practice → slavery → condition.
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-13]** E — Psa 51:3 shared with CHAR-17 confirms that the objective record (CHAR-13) and the subjective guilt experience (CHAR-17) are the two faces of the same moral reality — they co-occur in the same verse because they are inseparable experientially even if analytically distinct. Jer 17:1's heart-engraving (shared with CHAR-4/17) reveals that the divine record has a heart-internal inscription dimension that connects it to conscience-suppression (CHAR-4) and guilt-experience (CHAR-17) at the constitutional level.
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-14]** E — Exo 32:30/31 shared with CHAR-13 confirms that the great-sin naming and the forgiveness-seeking are inseparable in Moses's intercession: the record (CHAR-13) and the seeking of its removal (CHAR-14) co-occur in the same passage because they are the two faces of the same relational moment. Col 2:13 shared with CHAR-11/12 confirms that CHAR-14's "made alive" is the direct reversal of CHAR-11's "dead in trespasses" — the universal condition (CHAR-11) is what CHAR-14 addresses at the life-death level.
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-15]** E — Isa 43:27 shared with CHAR-13 confirms that the ancestral sinning that begins CHAR-15's generational chain is simultaneously the starting entry in CHAR-13's divine record: the first father's sinning (CHAR-15 origin) is the first record-entry (CHAR-13 content). Psa 78:32 shared with CHAR-5 confirms that the sustained "still sinned despite wonders" is both CHAR-15's generational perpetuation mechanism and CHAR-5's refusal-to-repent territory: the two characteristics are the inside and outside faces of the same sustained sinning pattern.
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-16]** E — Luk 5:8 shared with CHAR-3 (confession) and Luk 18:13 shared with CHAR-14 (forgiveness sought) confirm that the self-aware sinner's honest identity-recognition (CHAR-16) is simultaneously a confessional act (CHAR-3) and a forgiveness-seeking act (CHAR-14). The three characteristics are operationally fused in the moment of authentic sinner-identity recognition: naming oneself "a sinner" (CHAR-16) is confessing (CHAR-3) and initiating the plea for mercy (CHAR-14). Rom 5:19 shared with CHAR-11 confirms that the sinner-identity (CHAR-16) is the characterological expression of the universal condition (CHAR-11).
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-17]** E — Lev 5:5 shared with CHAR-3 and CHAR-14 confirms that guilt-recognition (CHAR-17), confession (CHAR-3), and forgiveness-seeking (CHAR-14) are operationally inseparable: the same verse evidences all three because they are sequential stages of a single conscience-to-remedy pipeline. Isa 6:7 shared with CHAR-14 and CHAR-13 confirms that guilt-removal (the resolution of CHAR-17) is simultaneously forgiveness-received (CHAR-14) and divine-record-cleared (CHAR-13): the three characteristics resolve together in the same divine act.
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-18]** E — Lev 16:21/22 shared with CHAR-3, CHAR-14 confirms that the priestly confession-and-transfer mechanism addresses CHAR-18's accumulated crime, CHAR-3's confession, and CHAR-14's forgiveness simultaneously — they are the same atonement-event seen from three perspectives. Isa 53:5/6/11 shared with CHAR-14 confirms that the servant's iniquity-bearing is the ultimate forgiveness-event that CHAR-14 names as received: the iniquity (CHAR-18) is what the servant bears, which constitutes the forgiveness (CHAR-14). Psa 51:5 shared with CHAR-11 confirms that CHAR-18's accumulated iniquity-condition is the characterological expression of CHAR-11's universal sin-condition.
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-19]** E — Lev 16:16/21 shared with CHAR-18 and CHAR-3 confirms that the atonement-event addresses CHAR-19 (transgression), CHAR-18 (iniquity), and CHAR-3 (confession) simultaneously — the priestly ritual encompasses all three dimensions of the same moral-failure reality. Isa 53:5/8 shared with CHAR-14 and CHAR-18 confirms that the servant's bearing addresses both CHAR-19's specific transgressions and CHAR-18's accumulated iniquities simultaneously — one redemptive act resolves both characteristics. Rom 5:15/16/17/18 shared with CHAR-11 confirms that CHAR-19's paradigmatic first transgression (Adam's trespass) is the event that created CHAR-11's universal sin-condition.
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-20]** E — Lev 26:40 shared with CHAR-18 and CHAR-3 confirms that the priestly-confessional framework addresses iniquity (CHAR-18), treachery (CHAR-20), and confession (CHAR-3) as a single moral-repair event. Hos 6:7 shared with CHAR-19 confirms that covenant-transgression is simultaneously boundary-crossing (CHAR-19) and relational-betrayal (CHAR-20) — the same theological event generates both characteristics and requires both to describe it fully. 1Ch 10:13 shared with CHAR-1 confirms that the specific sinning acts (consulting the medium) constitute the faithlessness of CHAR-20; CHAR-1 names the acts while CHAR-20 names their covenantal character.
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-21]** E — Dan 9:5 shared with CHAR-1, CHAR-18, CHAR-19 confirms that the comprehensive confession formula encompasses all M10's primary sin-categories simultaneously — the confession is not sin-category-specific but covers the full inner-being moral reality in one act. This suggests CHAR-21's perversity (*a.vah* — "done wrong") is the inner-structural companion to every other M10 characteristic's specific expression: you cannot have sinning (CHAR-1), accumulated crime (CHAR-18), and transgression (CHAR-19) without the inner moral distortion (CHAR-21) that pervades them all.
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-22]** E — Psa 58:2 shared with CHAR-21 confirms the heart-to-hands chain as the boundary between CHAR-21 (heart-devising) and CHAR-22 (hand-enacted violence); both characteristics are present in the same verse at different levels. Eze 18:24 shared with CHAR-20 confirms that the turning from righteousness to injustice is simultaneously CHAR-20's faithlessness (covenant-betrayal) and CHAR-22's injustice (unjust conduct) — the same volitional turn generates both characteristics. 1Pe 3:18 shared with M26 confirms CHAR-22 as M10's structural point of contact with M26's righteousness-cluster.
