**T6.6 · Shared Verse Anchors**  (21)

- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — **[CLUSTER]** E — The cluster has several significant cross-cluster anchor candidates. Psa 36:1 (vc=42567 — Char 1 anchor): candidate for any cluster addressing God-fear, prayer, or the relationship between divine-presence awareness and moral orientation (M26, M34/fear). Eze 33:11 (vc=42701 — Char 1 VCG-07): candidate for repentance clusters (M11 or equivalent) as the primary prophetic statement of divine desire for the wicked to turn. Mat 7:11 (vc=14438 — Char 2 anchor) and Mat 12:34-35 (vc=14419-14420 — Char 2 VCG-03): candidates for any heart-as-generative-source study across the programme. Hos 9:10 (vc=1632 — Char 4 anchor candidate): candidate for any study on the formative power of devotion or love (implications for positive characteristics — what one loves one becomes toward). Heb 10:22 (vc=14426 — Char 2 VCG-06): candidate for conscience clusters — the evil conscience / sprinkled conscience is a direct conscience-state evidence point with broad programme relevance. Joh 18:30 (vc=14378 — Char 6 VCG-03): christological anchor for false accusation against the innocent. Psa 66:18 (vc=38182 — Char 5 anchor): candidate for prayer clusters — cherished iniquity closing the divine ear is a foundational prayer-condition evidence point.
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-1]** E — The most analytically significant shared anchor is Psa 36:1 (vc=42567 — M10b-A-VCG-02 anchor candidate), which is also a potential M26 (Righteousness) anchor given its structural importance for understanding the relationship between God-fear and wickedness. Eze 33:11 (vc=42701 — M10b-A-VCG-07 anchor) is highly likely to function as a key anchor in M11 (repentance or moral turning) given its concentrated statement of the divine desire for the wicked to turn. Gen 18:23-25 (vc=42643/42644 — M10b-A-VCG-04) likely serves as an anchor for M26 (Righteousness) given the structural importance of the righteous/wicked distinction for the righteousness characteristic. Job 34:10 (vc=15525 — M10b-A-VCG-11) is a structural anchor for any study of divine holiness or moral perfection.
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-2]** E — Several significant shared anchors are identifiable. Mat 12:34-35 (vc=14419-14420 — VCG-03) is highly likely to serve as a primary anchor for any cluster addressing the heart as a generative source (the treasury-of-heart principle has implications across multiple inner-being clusters). Mar 7:22-23 (vc=14409/14473/14422 — VCGs 03/05) is a primary anchor candidate for any cluster addressing the vice-catalogue from the heart — the heart as the origin of all moral defilement (Mar 7:23) is broadly significant. Heb 10:22 (vc=14426 — VCG-06) is significant for any conscience-focused cluster — the evil conscience as barrier to God and the cleansed conscience as enabling approach are central to any study of conscience (potentially M22 or equivalent). Rom 1:29 (vc=14336-14338 — VCG-05) is a primary anchor for multiple vice-saturation studies.
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-3]** E — Probable shared anchors: Pro 3:32 (vc=1686 — VCG-02) is a strong candidate as a primary anchor for any study addressing the relational intimacy of divine confidence — the contrast between "abomination to the Lord" (exclusion) and "upright are in his confidence" (inclusion) is foundational for understanding divine-human relational intimacy. Pro 15:8 (vc=1678 — VCG-03) is a strong anchor candidate for any study of worship integrity — the wicked person's sacrifice as abomination captures the worship-inner-character interface precisely. Eze 9:4 (vc=1698 — VCG-04) is a candidate for any conscience-study — the mark given to those who grieve over abominations is a central conscience-function evidence point. Jer 6:15 and 8:12 (vc=1680; 1696 — VCG-04) are candidates for any conscience-silencing study.
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-4]** E — Probable shared anchors: Eze 11:21 (vc=1631 — VCG-05) — "whose heart goes after their detestable things and their abominations" — is a strong candidate as an anchor for any study addressing the heart's directional faculty. Hos 9:10 (vc=1632 — VCG-04) — "they became detestable like the thing they loved" — is likely a significant anchor for any study on the formative power of devotion (the person is shaped by what they love), which has implications across many positive characteristics as well. Dan 9:27 / Mat 24:15 (vc=65914; 65876 — VCG-06) are anchors for eschatological studies within M27. Deu 7:25 (vc=1682 — VCG-02) is a candidate for any study on covetousness or the ensnaring power of attractive objects.
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-5]** E — Probable shared anchors: Psa 66:18 (vc=38182 — VCG-04): "if I had cherished iniquity in my heart, the Lord would not have listened" — is a strong anchor for any study of prayer, divine hearing, or the conditions of approach to God. Psa 7:14 (vc=38245 — VCG-02): "conceives evil… pregnant with mischief… gives birth to lies" — is a candidate for any study on the inner generative process of evil or on the relationship between inner moral condition and deceptive speech. Mic 2:1 (vc=38217 — VCG-02): "devise wickedness on their beds; when the morning dawns, they perform it" — is a candidate for any study on the relationship between cognitive planning and bodily execution. Isa 59:7 (vc=38203 — VCG-02): "thoughts are thoughts of iniquity; feet run to evil" — is a candidate for any study on the cognitive-volitional link to violent action.
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-6]** E — Probable shared anchors: Jude 8 (vc=14354 — VCG-01) is likely an anchor for M06 (contempt/hostile speech/defiant rejection of authority) — the authority-rejection and blasphemy of the glorious ones serves both characteristics. Rev 13:6 (vc=14356 — VCG-01) is the most concentrated beast-blasphemy evidence, likely serving any eschatological/apocalyptic study. Joh 18:30 (vc=14378 — VCG-03) is a high-significance anchor for the christological narrative — the false charge against Jesus is foundational for understanding false accusation in the NT. 1Pe 2:12 (vc=14377 — VCG-03) is likely an anchor for any study of conduct, testimony, and the relationship between behaviour and God's honour.
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — **[CLUSTER]** E — The shared anchors reveal that M10b's inner-evil characteristics are not self-contained — they are connected to the inner-being landscape's positive characteristics through the evidential network. Eze 33:11 (Char 1) shared with repentance clusters confirms that wickedness and repentance are definitionally co-present — the Ezekiel corpus's call to the wicked to turn is simultaneously a definition of wickedness as a reversible volitional direction and a description of repentance as the movement away from it. Hos 9:10 (Char 4) shared with devotion clusters confirms that idolatrous attachment and genuine devotion are the same faculty of the person (the heart's going-after) operating in wrong vs. right direction — M10b and the positive devotion characteristics share the same constitutional faculty. Heb 10:22 (Char 2) shared with conscience clusters confirms that the evil conscience and the cleansed conscience are opposite states of the same inner faculty — M10b and conscience-cluster studies share the same evidential ground.
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-1]** E — The Eze 33:11 shared anchor (potential M11 repentance anchor) reveals that the wicked person's possible turning is constitutively connected to the characteristic of repentance: the Ezekiel corpus's call to the wicked to turn is simultaneously a definition of wickedness (as a reversible volitional direction) and a description of repentance (as the characteristic movement away from wickedness toward life). The Gen 18:23-25 shared anchor (potential M26 righteousness anchor) reveals that righteousness and wickedness are definitionally co-present — you cannot define one without the other; they are structurally interdependent characteristics. The Psa 36:1 shared anchor reveals the connection between wickedness-as-settled-identity and the absence of God-fear as a characteristic.
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-2]** E — The Mar 7:22-23 shared anchor reveals a structural relationship between CHAR-2 (evil as constitutional inner nature) and any heart-generative characteristic: the heart that produces evil (CHAR-2) is the same faculty whose generative function, when rightly ordered, produces other characteristics. The shared-heart-generative-function reveals CHAR-2 as the corruption of a faculty that is, when uncorrupted, the generative source of positive inner-being characteristics. Heb 10:22's shared conscience anchor reveals the structural relationship between CHAR-2 and the conscience characteristic: the evil conscience (CHAR-2's impact on the conscience) is the specific inner state addressed by the new covenant's cleansing, connecting CHAR-2 to any cluster studying the conscience's restoration.
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-3]** E — Pro 3:32 as a shared anchor (if it serves CHAR-3 and any divine-intimacy characteristic) reveals that abomination is the antithesis of divine-relational intimacy — the two characteristics (abomination as exclusion and divine-confidence intimacy) are structural polar opposites constituted by the same verse. Pro 15:8 as a shared anchor reveals the relationship between CHAR-3 and any worship-integrity characteristic: the corrupt inner person's approach to God is a specific instance of the abomination category, showing that worship becomes a site of abomination when the worshipping person's inner character is abominable. Eze 9:4 as a shared conscience anchor shows that the grieving conscience is a marker of both abomination-awareness (CHAR-3) and conscience-function (whichever characteristic conscience falls under in the programme).
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-4]** E — Hos 9:10 as a potential shared anchor reveals the relationship between CHAR-4 (idolatrous abomination) and any characteristic addressing character-formation: the principle "became like the thing they loved" applies inversely to positive characteristics — those who love God are formed toward God's likeness. The shared anchor reveals that the formation mechanism (devotion shapes character) operates for both positive and negative outcomes; CHAR-4 documents its negative operation while positive characteristics will document its positive operation. Eze 11:21 as a shared anchor reveals the heart's directional faculty as a site of both covenant fidelity (heart going after God) and idolatrous defection (heart going after idols) — a faculty that appears across multiple characteristics.
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-5]** E — Psa 66:18 as a shared anchor (if it also serves prayer-relationship studies) reveals that the prayer/divine-hearing relationship is directly damaged by inner cherished iniquity — the two characteristics are in a negative causal relationship: iniquity-scheming blocks prayer; prayer-relationship requires released iniquity. Psa 7:14 as a shared anchor (if serving speech/deception studies) reveals that active inner scheming and deceptive speech are not merely co-occurring but causally linked — the scheming generates the deceptive speech as its born output. Mic 2:1 as shared anchor (cognitive-to-bodily studies) reveals the tight coupling between cognitive planning and immediate bodily execution in the characteristic's inner-outer movement.
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-6]** E — Jude 8 as a shared anchor with M06 reveals that authority-rejection and blasphemous speech are two faces of the same inner disposition: the contemptuous defiance of M06 finds its speech-expression in CHAR-6's blasphemy. They are not merely co-occurring but causally linked in the same verse: the authority-rejection generates the blasphemous expression. Joh 18:30 as a shared anchor with christological studies reveals that CHAR-6's false-accusation mode is embedded in the pivotal moment of Christ's trial — the characteristic of false accusation is christologically significant as the verbal instrument through which the innocent was condemned. 1Pe 2:12 as a shared anchor with conduct/testimony studies reveals that the social-testimonial dimension of conduct is the antidote to false accusation: honourable conduct addresses the characteristic's social impact.
- `T6.6.3` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — **[CLUSTER]** E — Shared verse anchors are identified (T6.6.1–T6.6.2). This prompt is satisfied.

**T6.7 · Dimensional Sharing**  (21)

- `T6.7.1` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — **[CLUSTER]** G — Full dimensional-sharing data across the programme is not yet available. Partial cluster-scope picture: the volitional primary dimension is confirmed for four of six M10b characteristics (Chars 1, 4, 5, 6) — these four will share at least their volitional primary with other programme characteristics. Char 2's constitutional-structural primary appears distinctive within M10b. Char 3's relational-moral primary also appears distinctive. The secondary dimensions (dispositional-character, cognitive, affective, accumulated-condition) cross multiple M10b characteristics and likely cross M26 (Righteousness) and adjacent clusters. Full synthesis deferred to Session D.
- `T6.7.1` · cluster_finding · /gap — **[CHAR-1]** G — Full dimensional-sharing data requires completed Phase 9 analysis across all M10b characteristics (and comparison with other clusters), which is not available at this stage. What can be noted from the evidence: the volitional dimension (primary classification — T1.8.1) is likely shared with M26 (Righteousness — also volitional in its primary dimension), M10b-E (Iniquity — volitional scheming), and M11 (repentance/turning — the volitional reversal). The affective dimension (T1.8.3 secondary) is likely shared with M03 (Grief), M01 (Fear), and M04 (Joy) given the characteristic's engagement with the affective faculty. The full dimensional-sharing picture is a G — gap requiring cross-cluster synthesis data.
- `T6.7.1` · cluster_finding · /gap — **[CHAR-2]** G — Full dimensional-sharing data requires completed Phase 9 analysis across all M10b characteristics and cross-cluster comparison, which is not yet available. Partial picture from the evidence: the constitutional-structural dimension is likely unique to CHAR-2 within M10b (CHAR-1 is volitional-directional; CHARs 3-6 are specific-domain characteristics). The affective secondary dimension (envy, pride — Mat 20:15; Jam 4:16 — VCG-06) is likely shared with M01 (Fear), M02 (Anger), M03 (Grief), M04 (Joy) as all engaging the affective faculty. The cognitive secondary dimension (Col 1:21; 1Ti 6:4 — VCG-06) is likely shared with M15 (Wisdom) as its structural opposite. Full synthesis deferred to Session D.
- `T6.7.1` · cluster_finding · /gap — **[CHAR-3]** G — Full dimensional-sharing data requires completed Phase 9 analysis across all M10b characteristics and cross-cluster comparison, which is not yet available. Partial picture: the relational-moral primary dimension (divine revulsion as the constitutive response) is likely unique to CHAR-3 within the programme — no other characteristic identified so far has the divine-relational verdict as its primary dimension. The secondary dispositional dimension (crooked heart, arrogant heart) is likely shared with CHAR-1 (volitional-dispositional) and CHAR-2 (constitutional-structural). The conscience secondary dimension is likely shared with whichever programme cluster specifically addresses conscience. Full synthesis deferred to Session D.
- `T6.7.1` · cluster_finding · /gap — **[CHAR-4]** G — Full dimensional-sharing data requires completed Phase 9 analysis across all M10b characteristics and cross-cluster comparison, not yet available. Partial picture: the volitional-devotional primary dimension appears unique to CHAR-4 within M10b — no other characteristic has the volitional-devotional misdirection as its primary dimension. The affective secondary dimension (soul-delight in abominations — Isa 66:3) is likely shared with M04 (Joy) as its structural opposite — the soul that delights in abominations rather than in God. The constitutional-formative secondary dimension (Hos 9:10 — becoming like the idol) is likely shared with any characteristic addressing character-formation by sustained devoted attention. Full synthesis deferred to Session D.
- `T6.7.1` · cluster_finding · /gap — **[CHAR-5]** G — Full dimensional-sharing data requires completed Phase 9 analysis across all M10b characteristics and cross-cluster comparison, not yet available. Partial picture: the cognitive-volitional-generative primary dimension appears distinctive to CHAR-5 within M10b — no other completed M10b characteristic has the active cognitive-scheming-and-generating process as its primary dimension. The dispositional-character secondary dimension is likely shared with CHAR-1. The accumulated-condition secondary dimension may be shared with M10c or M10 characteristics. Full synthesis deferred to Session D.
- `T6.7.1` · cluster_finding · /gap — **[CHAR-6]** G — Full dimensional-sharing data requires completed Phase 9 analysis across all M10b characteristics and cross-cluster comparison, not yet available. Partial picture: the volitional-expressive primary dimension appears unique to CHAR-6 within M10b — no other completed M10b characteristic has the will's expression through speech as its primary dimension. The will-hardening that emerges in CHAR-6's VCG-01 shares the volitional hardening dimension with CHAR-1. Full synthesis deferred to Session D.
- `T6.7.2` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — **[CLUSTER]** G — Cannot be fully answered without cross-cluster data. Partial cluster-scope picture: the cluster's volitional-dominant primary dimension pattern (four of six characteristics) suggests that M10b will share its primary dimension with other will-centred clusters in the programme, while Chars 2 (constitutional-structural) and 3 (relational-moral) will have less dimensional overlap with most other clusters. Full synthesis deferred to Session D.
- `T6.7.2` · cluster_finding · /gap — **[CHAR-1]** G — This cannot be fully answered without the dimensional-sharing data from other completed characteristics (see T6.7.1). The partial picture: the characteristic shares the volitional dimension with several others, suggesting it is located in the same dimensional space as the will-oriented characteristics — it is not a peripheral or isolated characteristic but one that intersects the programme's central volitional axis. Full synthesis deferred to Session D.
- `T6.7.2` · cluster_finding · /gap — **[CHAR-2]** G — Cannot be fully answered without dimensional-sharing data from other completed characteristics. The partial picture: CHAR-2's primary constitutional-structural dimension may make it a relatively distinctive characteristic at the dimensional level — other characteristics primarily engage specific faculties (will, affect, cognition) as their primary dimension; CHAR-2's primary dimension is the constitutional condition that underlies all faculties rather than a specific faculty's engagement. This may make CHAR-2 structurally foundational in dimensional terms. Full synthesis deferred to Session D.
- `T6.7.2` · cluster_finding · /gap — **[CHAR-3]** G — Cannot be fully answered without cross-cluster dimensional data. Partial picture: if CHAR-3's primary relational-moral dimension is unique, it would occupy a distinctive position in the programme's dimensional architecture — not sharing its primary dimension with other characteristics but sharing secondary dimensions widely (conscience, volition, moral evaluation are broadly shared dimensions). This would make CHAR-3 a dimensionally peripheral characteristic in one sense (unique primary dimension) but a dimensionally connected one in another (multiple shared secondary dimensions across many characteristics). Full synthesis deferred to Session D.
- `T6.7.2` · cluster_finding · /gap — **[CHAR-4]** G — Cannot be fully answered without cross-cluster dimensional data. Partial picture: CHAR-4's unique volitional-devotional primary dimension may make it the only characteristic in the programme whose primary dimension is specifically misdirected devotion — a dimension that appears as the negative of the programme's positive characteristics addressing devotion, worship, and love. Full synthesis deferred to Session D.
- `T6.7.2` · cluster_finding · /gap — **[CHAR-5]** G — Cannot be fully answered without cross-cluster data. Partial picture: if CHAR-5's cognitive-volitional-generative primary is unique, it occupies a distinctive position in the programme's dimensional architecture as the only characteristic whose primary dimension names the active inner production of evil (rather than the settled identity, the verdict on it, or the devotion misdirection). Full synthesis deferred to Session D.
- `T6.7.2` · cluster_finding · /gap — **[CHAR-6]** G — Cannot be fully answered without cross-cluster data. Partial picture: CHAR-6's unique volitional-expressive primary dimension positions it as the speech-expression characteristic of the M10b cluster — the only characteristic whose primary dimension names the speech act as the constitutive mode of inner wickedness. Its relationship to M06 (contempt/hostile speech) will be the most important dimensional comparison. Full synthesis deferred to Session D.

### M10c  (80 findings)

**T1.2.3 · Best working description**  (5)

- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — **[CLUSTER]** E — The four working descriptions collectively reveal the M10c cluster's core conceptual structure: one condition of defilement/uncleanness operating across four domains with distinct mechanisms, loci, and resolutions. Characteristic 1: a legally-sacrally defined bodily-status condition, acquired through mechanism or verdict, bearing on sacred access and conscience. Characteristic 2: a morally-inner condition of conscience, will, and heart corrupted by desire and habitual sin, penetrating to body and spirit. Characteristic 3: a condition of contamination extending from the community's inner-volitional disposition outward to land, sanctuary, and divine name through corporate moral defection. Characteristic 4: a condition of spiritual domination imposed by an external adversarial agent, overriding will, suppressing faculties, and severing communal belonging. The four descriptions share: (a) stative core — all name a condition; (b) sacred-spatial consequence — all produce consequences for access to divine presence or communal belonging; (c) inner-being engagement — all engage the inner person's faculties (will, conscience, discernment, relational capacity), though at different points and through different mechanisms; (d) structural opposite M12 — all position purity/holiness as the resolution state. The cluster's definitional unity is the condition of defilement-as-incompatibility-with-divine-holiness operating at the bodily-sacral, moral-interior, corporate-covenantal, and externally-imposed-spiritual levels simultaneously.
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-1]** E — Ritual defilement-state: a legally and sacrally defined condition of the whole person — acquired through bodily contact with a defiling agent (corpse, discharge, carcass, fluid) or established through authoritative priestly examination and verdict — that restricts the person's access to sacred participation and communal life for a defined duration. Constitutionally, the condition bears on the person as embodied being in sacral community: the body is the locus of acquisition; the conscience, will, and social-relational capacity are primarily engaged in its management and resolution. Immediate impact: exclusion from worship and communal participation; duty of self-declaration; social distancing. The primary faculties engaged are: conscience (awakening to contamination incurred — Lev 5:2-3 VCG-01; grief at exclusion — Num 9:7 VCG-07), will (active watchfulness over bodily actions; pursuit or refusal of cleansing — Num 19:20 VCG-07), discernment (the faculty required for clean/unclean distinction — Lev 10:10 VCG-01 M10c-B), and social conscience (the duty not to transmit one's state — Lev 15:11 VCG-06). Structural opposite: M12 (purity/holiness).
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-2]** E — Moral-inner defilement-state: the condition of the inner person — conscience, will, and heart — in a state of corruption arising from desire, habitual sin, and allegiance disorder. The defilement is located within the person (not through external contact), penetrates the full inner-outer constitution (2Cor 7:1: "defilement of body and spirit"), and functions as a prior master that directs the will and bodily members toward escalating moral disorder (Rom 6:19, VCG-02). It encompasses the constitutional human vulnerability to moral uncleanness (Job 14:4, VCG-01), the habitual flesh-impulse register (Gal 5:19, VCG-02), the conscience as locus of defilement (1Cor 8:7, VCG-03), and the heart's displacement of God with corrupt desire (Eph 5:5, VCG-04). Its structural opposite is M12 (purity/holiness of the inner person). Its primary cross-register relationships are M10 (sin-act producing the state), M11 (cleansing as the active response), M29 (desire as vehicle), M08 (pride as companion), M09 (consecration as positive orientation).
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-3]** E — Corporate/covenantal defilement: the condition of contamination that extends from the community's inner volitional disposition (unfaithfulness, idolatrous desire, deliberate desecration) outward to the sacred relational order it inhabits — land defiled (Num 35:34, Jer 2:7, Eze 36:17/18, VCG-04), sanctuary desecrated (Eze 5:11, 2Ch 36:14, VCG-03), and divine name degraded (Eze 43:7/8, VCG-03). It includes both covenantal-relational defilement (sexual betrayal of covenant bonds — VCG-01, ordeal law, Nazirite defilement) and the corporate prophetic register (idolatry defiling sacred spaces — VCG-02, VCG-03). Its unique inner-being contribution is Eze 20:43 (VCG-02): memory of defilement functioning as the inner mechanism of corporate repentance. Structural opposite: M12 (purity/holiness of sacred space, divine name, covenant relationship).
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-4]** E — Defilement by external spiritual agency: the inner-being condition of a person whose will, faculties, and bodily agency are invaded and dominated by an unclean spirit — an external adversarial force that enters the person, overrides their self-possession (Luk 8:29 — "driven by the demon"), suppresses their communicative faculties (Mar 9:25 — "mute and deaf spirit"), severs their communal access (Mar 5:2), and subjects their bodily agency to the spirit's purposes. The spirits recognise and submit to superior divine authority (Mar 3:11, Luk 4:36, VCG-03) and resist expulsion (Act 8:7). Restoration reverses the condition, returning the person to wholeness (Luk 6:18 — "were cured") and communal belonging (Luk 9:42 — "gave him back to his father"). Structural opposite: M12 (purity/wholeness).

**T1.8 · Dimension Classification**  (15)

- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — **[CLUSTER]** E — All four characteristics share the primary classification of condition — a state the person or community is in. This unanimity across the cluster is the most analytically stable finding of T1.8: the defilement domain as a whole is constitutionally a condition domain, not an act or disposition domain. The stative vocabulary (ta.me adj "unclean," akatharsia "impurity" as a noun of state, echōn pneuma "having a spirit" as a stative participle) is consistent across all four. This shared primary classification is what makes M10c a unified cluster despite the four mechanisms and loci. The defilement-state — whatever its source, whatever its domain — is always a condition the person is in, not something they do.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-1]** E — The primary dimension of Characteristic 1 (Ritual defilement-state) is the **condition** dimension — specifically, a sacrally and legally defined condition of the person. The characteristic is a state, not an act or disposition. Its primary vocabulary (H2931 ta.me adjective: "unclean" as a descriptor of the person's condition; Lev 13:46: "he is unclean") is stative. The condition is the inner-being reality that the entire characteristic's system of acquisition, management, and resolution is ordered around.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-2]** E — The primary dimension is condition — a morally corrupted state of the whole inner person. Like Characteristic 1, the vocabulary is predominantly stative: akatharsia (impurity — noun/state), molusmos (defilement — noun/state), H2931 ta.me adj (unclean — adjective/state). The condition pervades the inner person: it is not an act performed but a state that the person is in, which then generates acts (Gal 5:19 — "works of the flesh"; 2Cor 12:21 — impurity "practiced").
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-3]** E — The primary dimension is condition — a state of corporate contamination of the sacred relational order that the community inhabits. The vocabulary is predominantly stative and result-focused: the land is "defiled" (Jer 2:7, Eze 36:17/18), the sanctuary is "defiled" (Eze 5:11), the name is "defiled" (Eze 43:7/8), the community is "defiled" (Hos 5:3: "Israel is defiled"; Hos 6:10: "Israel is defiled"). The condition is the endpoint of acts of unfaithfulness; the characteristic names the resulting state rather than the generating acts.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-4]** E — The primary dimension is condition — a state of spiritual domination and defilement imposed on the person from without. The language throughout VCG-01 is stative: "a man with an unclean spirit" (Mar 1:23 — present condition); "a man who had an unclean spirit" (Mar 5:2 — echōn — having, as a present condition); "those afflicted with unclean spirits" (Act 5:16 — ochloumenoi — being afflicted, present participle of sustained condition). The condition is what the person is in; it was not produced by their action and is not a faculty they exercise.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — **[CLUSTER]** E — The stative evidence patterns differ by characteristic but converge on the same conclusion. Characteristic 1: Num 19:13 ("his uncleanness is still on him"), Lev 13:46 ("he shall remain unclean as long as he has the disease"), Lev 5:2-3 ("he has become unclean"). Characteristic 2: Rom 6:19 ("slaves to impurity" — stative slavery), Eph 4:19 ("given themselves up" — the entry event, with impurity as the condition entered), 2Cor 7:1 ("defilement of body and spirit" — inhabiting as a condition). Characteristic 3: Psa 106:39 ("thus they became unclean by their acts" — condition as result), Eze 22:4 ("defiled by the idols that you have made" — arrived-at condition), Hos 5:3 ("Israel is defiled" — present stative summary). Characteristic 4: Mar 1:23 ("a man with an unclean spirit"), Act 5:16 ("those afflicted with unclean spirits" — present participle of ongoing condition). The evidential convergence is complete: across 263 verses, four characteristics, and three languages (Hebrew, Aramaic-adjacent, Greek), the stative character of defilement is the constant.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-1]** E — The stative character is pervasive across all 133 verses. The most direct evidence: Num 19:13 (M10c-A-VCG-07) — "his uncleanness is still on him" — names the condition as a persisting, clinging state. Lev 13:46 (M10c-B-VCG-03) — "he shall remain unclean as long as he has the disease" — frames uncleanness as a duration-defined condition. Lev 5:2-3 (M10c-A-VCG-01) — "he has become unclean" — marks the transition into the condition. The resolution formulas ("shall be unclean until the evening / then it shall be clean") confirm the condition as bounded in time. The priestly verdict system (M10c-B-VCG-02) confirms the condition as established by authoritative declaration. Across all sub-registers, the characteristic is consistently a condition the person is in.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-2]** E — Supporting evidence for the condition classification: Rom 6:19 (VCG-02) — "you once presented your members as slaves to impurity" — the slavery metaphor names the condition as a state the person was in, not an act they performed; Eph 4:19 (VCG-02) — "given themselves up to... every kind of impurity" — the giving-over is the event; the impurity is the condition entered; Job 14:4 (VCG-01) — "who can bring a clean thing out of an unclean?" — the unclean is a state of being, not an activity; 2Cor 7:1 (VCG-03) — "defilement of body and spirit" — the defilement is described as inhabiting the body and spirit, confirming stative nature.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-3]** E — The stative character is confirmed by the consistent use of H2930A and H2931 as result-descriptors rather than process-descriptors. Psa 106:39 (VCG-02): "thus they became unclean by their acts" — the becoming-unclean is the result-state produced by prior acts. Eze 22:4 (VCG-02): "you have become guilty... and defiled by the idols that you have made" — defilement is the arrived-at condition following the acts. Hos 5:3 (VCG-02): "Israel is defiled" — the present stative condition is the summary statement. Lam 1:17 (VCG-05): "Jerusalem has become a filthy thing among them" — the city's current condition is the result of the corporate defilement process.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-4]** E — Stative character is consistently evidenced: Mar 1:23 — "a man with an unclean spirit"; Mar 5:2 — "a man with an unclean spirit"; Mar 5:8 — the command "come out of the man" presupposes the spirit is currently within (stative presence); Luk 8:29 — "many a time it had seized him" — the seizures produce a persisting condition of domination. Act 5:16 — "those afflicted with unclean spirits" — the affliction is an ongoing state. The characteristic's resolution requires authoritative external intervention (divine command), not the person's own volitional response — confirming the condition classification.
- `T1.8.3` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — **[CLUSTER]** E — All four carry secondary dimensions, and the secondary dimensions reveal the cluster's internal differentiations. Characteristic 1's secondary dimensions: volitional (will engaged at the perimeter — avoidance, remedy-seeking, refusal) and social-relational (defilement as a condition with built-in social structure — exclusion, self-declaration, duty of non-transmission). Characteristic 2's secondary dimensions: dispositional (habitual appetite for more defilement — Eph 4:19 — once conscience is suppressed), volitional (the molunō verb — the act by which the condition is incurred), and constitutional (Job 14:4 — inherited constitutional uncleanness). Characteristic 3's secondary dimensions: volitional (the condition generated by volitional acts — Eze 23:7) and social-covenantal (the condition inherently affecting the covenant community and its relational environment). Characteristic 4's secondary dimensions: affliction/suffering (Act 5:16 — the condition paired with sickness as a form of affliction) and adversarial spiritual engagement (the spirits resist expulsion — Act 8:7, Mat 12:43). Cluster pattern: the volitional secondary dimension is shared by Characteristics 1, 2, and 3 — in all three, the will is engaged at some point in the condition's acquisition, maintenance, or resolution. Characteristic 4 lacks a volitional secondary dimension because the condition arrives without volitional cooperation. The social-relational secondary dimension is shared by Characteristics 1 and 3 (both have community-structural consequences built into the condition). Characteristic 4's unique secondary dimension is the adversarial-spiritual engagement — no other characteristic has this dimension because no other characteristic involves an external personal spiritual agent.  ---  T1 cluster self-evaluation:   1. Prompt count:        24/24 ✓   2. Scope marker:        24× [CLUSTER] ✓   3. Citation discipline: All E findings cite specific characteristics and their per-char evidence across the cluster ✓   4. No cross-tier refs:  ✓   5. Distinct openings:   ✓   6. Across-char comparison: ✓ (no single-char restating; all 24 prompts integrate findings across Chars 1-4)   → PASS. Proceeding to Tier T2.  ---  ## T2 — Constitutional Location
- `T1.8.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-1]** E — Yes, two secondary dimensions are evidenced. First, a **volitional** secondary dimension: within the condition, the will is engaged at the perimeter — one may deliberately avoid defiling contact (Lev 11:43-44, M10c-A-VCG-04), seek remedy or refuse it (Num 19:20), guard the clean/unclean boundary through active discernment (Lev 10:10, 20:25, M10c-B-VCG-01). The will does not constitute the condition but actively shapes the person's relationship to it. Second, a **social-relational** secondary dimension: the characteristic is inherently communal — exclusion from camp and worship (Num 5:2, Lev 13:46), public self-declaration (Lev 13:45), the duty not to transmit (Lev 15:11), institutional gatekeeping (2Ch 23:19, M10c-B-VCG-03) all show the characteristic as a condition with built-in social structure. Neither secondary dimension competes with the primary condition classification — they describe what the condition activates, not what the condition is.  ---  T1 self-evaluation:   1. - `T1.8.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-2]** E — Yes, three secondary dimensions. (1) Dispositional: Eph 4:19 (VCG-02) — "greedy to practice every kind of impurity" — the characteristic includes a habitual appetite, a disposition of the will oriented toward corruption. (2) Volitional: the molunō verb (1Cor 8:7, VCG-03 — "conscience is defiled"; Rev 3:4, VCG-05 — "not soiled their garments") names the act by which the condition is incurred or avoided — there is a volitional event at the characteristic's acquisition. (3) Constitutional: Job 14:4 (VCG-01) — the unclean as the constitutional origin point — this is not a condition entered through specific choices but an inherited constitutional reality. None of these secondary dimensions competes with the primary condition classification — they describe how the condition is acquired (volitional), how it operates once entrenched (dispositional), and how deep it runs (constitutional).  ---  T1 self-evaluation:   1. - `T1.8.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-3]** E — Yes, two secondary dimensions. (1) Volitional: the defilement-state is generated by volitional acts — Eze 20:7 (VCG-02): "do not defile yourselves with the idols of Egypt" — the imperative presupposes a volitional capacity to choose or refuse; Eze 23:7 (VCG-02): "she defiled herself with all the idols of everyone after whom she lusted" — the act of defiling is volitional self-direction. (2) Social-covenantal: the defilement-state inherently affects and involves the covenant community and its relational environment — the land, sanctuary, name, and God himself are all implicated in the characteristic's operation. Neither secondary dimension competes with the primary condition classification; they describe how the condition is generated (volitional) and in what relational domain it operates (social-covenantal).  ---  T1 self-evaluation:   1. Prompt count:        24/24 ✓   2. Scope marker:        24× [CHAR-3] ✓   3. Proceeding to Tier T2.  ---  ## T2 — Constitutional Location (31 prompts)
- `T1.8.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-4]** E — Yes, two secondary dimensions. (1) Affliction/suffering: Act 5:16 names those with unclean spirits alongside the "sick"; Luk 6:18 — "cured" — the healing terminology frames the condition as an affliction to be addressed. (2) Adversarial spiritual engagement: the spirits resist expulsion (Act 8:7 — crying out; Mat 12:43 — seeking re-entry), and authority is required to overcome them (Mat 10:1). The adversarial dimension is secondary to the condition dimension but essential for understanding how the condition is both produced and resolved.  ---  T1 self-evaluation:   1. **T2.8 · Body-Deposit (DNA / generational)**  (15)

- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — **[CLUSTER]** E — The cluster's deposit-profile is 2 E : 2 S — Characteristics 2 and 3 evidence sustained deposits; Characteristics 1 and 4 do not. Characteristic 2's deposit is a constitutional hardening of conscience (Eph 4:19 — "become callous") at the individual soul level. Characteristic 3's deposit is a sustained corporate-generational pattern of defection (Eze 20:30 — "after the manner of your fathers") at the community level. Characteristics 1 and 4 show no lasting deposit: Characteristic 1's condition is designed as temporary and resolvable (prescribed remedies clear it without residue); Characteristic 4's restoration is consistently described as complete (Luk 6:18 — "cured"; Act 5:16 — "all healed"). The cluster pattern: sustained deposits arise when the defilement mechanism engages the soul's own faculties (Chars 2, 3 — inner moral habituation and corporate moral identity are the sites of hardening); no sustained deposit arises when the mechanism is external-material (Char 1 — the condition is temporary by design) or external-spiritual (Char 4 — expulsion restores without residue).
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /silent — **[CHAR-1]** S — The 133-verse corpus does not evidence a constitutional deposit in the body from sustained ritual defilement. The characteristic is structured as a temporary and resolvable condition: "shall be unclean until the evening; then it shall be clean" (Lev 11:24, M10c-A-VCG-02). Even the most severe form (corpse-contact, seven-day defilement) is bounded and resolved through the prescribed remedy (Num 19:19-20, M10c-A-VCG-07). The earthenware vessel that must be broken (Lev 11:33, M10c-A-VCG-03) is an object, not a person; and the condemned house (Lev 14:44-45, M10c-B-VCG-04) is a structure. The evidence does not suggest a generational constitutional deposit within the person from the ritual defilement-state per se.
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-2]** E — The evidence suggests a constitutional hardening rather than a physical deposit. Eph 4:19 (VCG-02): "become callous" — the sustained moral-inner defilement produces a progressive hardening of the conscience and of the inner moral faculties; this is a constitutional change in the inner person's capacity for moral response. The process described in Rom 1:24 (VCG-02) — God's judicial abandonment leading to further deepening of the impurity — suggests a self-reinforcing deterioration of the inner constitution over time. The "callousness" of Eph 4:19 is a constitutional deposit in the soul: the faculty that should respond to moral reality (conscience) has been hardened. Whether this constitutes a deposit in the body in a biological sense is not evidenced in the 26-verse corpus.
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-3]** E — The evidence suggests a sustained corporate moral deterioration rather than a specific constitutional deposit in the individual body. Eze 22:3-4 (VCG-02): the city that "makes idols to defile herself" and has "become guilty" by sustained idolatrous practice — over time the community's identity becomes constitutively defiled, no longer incidental. Psa 106:39 (VCG-02): "thus they became unclean by their acts, and played the whore in their deeds" — the became-unclean is a condition that has taken hold. The "deposit" is at the corporate-communal level rather than in the individual body: the community's collective moral-spiritual identity is the site of the sustained deposit.
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /silent — **[CHAR-4]** S — The 21-verse corpus does not evidence a lasting constitutional deposit from sustained spirit-possession. The restoration evidenced is complete: Luk 6:18 (VCG-02) — "were cured"; Luk 9:42 (VCG-01) — "healed the boy, and gave him back to his father"; Act 5:16 (VCG-02) — "they were all healed." The language of healing and cure implies full restoration without named residual deposit. Mat 12:43/Luk 11:24 — the spirit seeking re-entry after expulsion suggests remaining vulnerability to re-invasion, but this is a habitation-dynamic rather than a constitutional deposit.
- `T2.8.2` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — **[CLUSTER]** E — Characteristic 1: the prescribed remedial provisions (the standing red-heifer ash — Num 19:9 — "for the congregation as a perpetual statute") show that defilement's recurrence is accounted for in the system without evidence of lasting bodily deposit. Characteristic 2: Eph 4:19 (callousness) supports deposit; 2Cor 7:1 (cleansing possible) shows the deposit is within scope of reversal. Characteristic 3: Eze 20:30/31 (generational pattern of defection) supports deposit; Eze 37:23 (permanent non-defilement promised) shows reversal through divine action. Characteristic 4: Luk 6:18, Act 5:16 (complete cures) and Luk 9:42 (boy restored to father) support no lasting deposit; Mat 12:43/Luk 11:24 (spirit seeking re-entry) shows remaining vulnerability — but vulnerability is not a deposit. Cluster pattern: Characteristics 2 and 3's deposits are real but reversible through divine action; Characteristics 1 and 4 leave no deposit by design.
- `T2.8.2` · cluster_finding · /silent — **[CHAR-1]** S — Supporting the absence of lasting deposit: the consistent pattern of remedy and restoration throughout the corpus (washing, waiting, priestly clearance, water of impurity) implies the condition leaves no permanent trace. Contradicting it weakly: the pattern of Num 19 where the defilement is substantial enough to require an elaborate community-wide remedy (the standing red-heifer ash provision — "for the congregation of the people of Israel," Num 19:9) suggests that defilement is a persistent communal reality requiring perpetual institutional provision. But this does not indicate a deposit within the individual body — it indicates the recurrent nature of the condition in creaturely life.
- `T2.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-2]** E — Supporting a constitutional deposit (in the soul): Eph 4:19 (VCG-02) — callousness as a lasting inner condition produced by sustained impurity; 2Cor 12:21 (VCG-02) — unrepented impurity as a hardened state resistant to change; Rom 1:24 (VCG-02) — the judicial abandonment leading to further defilement suggesting a downward trajectory that accumulates. Contradicting a permanent/irreversible deposit: 2Cor 7:1 (VCG-03) — "let us cleanse ourselves from every defilement of body and spirit" — the call to cleansing implies the deposit is not permanent; the condition can be resolved through cleansing. The evidence shows a serious constitutional hardening but one that remains within the scope of possible cleansing.
- `T2.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-3]** E — Supporting a sustained corporate deposit: Eze 20:30/31 (VCG-02) — the pattern is generational ("will you defile yourselves after the manner of your fathers?"), suggesting the corporate defilement-state accumulates across generations as an inherited pattern of moral defection. Eze 20:43 (VCG-02) — the future community will "loathe yourselves for all the evils that you have committed" — the deeds themselves are something the community carries into the future as a burden of memory. Contradicting permanent irreversibility: Eze 37:23 (VCG-05) — "they shall not defile themselves anymore" — the promise of permanent non-defilement implies the deposit can be addressed through divine cleansing. Zec 13:1 (VCG-05) — the cleansing fountain removes the defilement.
- `T2.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-4]** E — Supporting full restoration: Luk 6:18 — "cured"; Act 5:16 — "all healed"; Luk 9:42 — "healed... gave him back to his father" — no residual condition named. Potentially complicating: Mat 12:43/Luk 11:24 (VCG-02) — the "house" metaphor suggests the person remains a potential habitation after expulsion, implying vulnerability. But this is a relational-habitation dynamic rather than a biological/constitutional deposit.
- `T2.8.3` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — **[CLUSTER]** E — Characteristics 1 and 4 are silent on constitutional deposit (their T5.7 findings are closed). Characteristics 2 and 3 evidence deposits that feed into their T5.7 developmental consequence findings. The cluster's T5.7 findings will therefore be differentiated: consequences addressed for Chars 2 and 3; T5.7 closed for Chars 1 and 4.
- `T2.8.3` · cluster_finding · /silent — **[CHAR-1]** S — The evidence is silent on constitutional deposit in the body from the ritual defilement-state. T5.7 will note this finding accordingly.
- `T2.8.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-2]** E — The evidence is not silent. A constitutional hardening (callousness of conscience — Eph 4:19) is evidenced as a sustained deposit of the condition. T5.7 will address the developmental consequence.
- `T2.8.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-3]** E — The evidence is not silent. A sustained corporate-generational deposit is evidenced (Eze 20:30 generational pattern; Psa 106:39 becoming-unclean through acts). T5.7 will address the developmental consequence.
- `T2.8.3` · cluster_finding · /silent — **[CHAR-4]** S — The evidence is silent on lasting constitutional deposit. Restoration is consistently framed as complete. T5.7 will note this finding accordingly.

**T5.7 · Deposit Consequence**  (15)

- `T5.7.1` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — **[CLUSTER]** E — Characteristics 2 and 3 identified deposits; Characteristics 1 and 4 did not. The two deposit-bearing characteristics' developmental consequences reveal different forms of inner-being deterioration. Characteristic 2's deposit (constitutional hardening — callousness of conscience, Eph 4:19 VCG-02) produces: progressive moral blindness (the defiled mind can no longer correctly assess purity — Tit 1:15 reference VCG-03), increased susceptibility to further defilement (each act weakens the conscience for the next), distorted moral cognition (mind corrupted alongside conscience), and entrenched unrepentance (2Cor 12:21 — "have not repented"). The deposit is a soul-level constitutional change. Characteristic 3's deposit (sustained corporate-generational accumulation — Eze 20:30/31 VCG-02) produces: generational entrenching of idolatrous practice (each generation inherits the temptation to repeat — Eze 20:18/30), corporate moral identity becoming publicly defiled (Eze 22:5 — "your name is defiled"), and institutional collapse of the priestly knowledge system (Eze 22:26 — priestly failure to transmit the clean/unclean distinction). The deposit is at the corporate-communal level. Characteristics 1 and 4 are closed at T5.7. The cluster's deposit pattern: the two characteristics whose defilement is self-generated by the person's or community's own moral defection (Chars 2, 3) produce lasting constitutional deposits; the two characteristics whose defilement arrives externally (Chars 1 — bodily contact; Char 4 — spiritual invasion) leave no lasting deposit. Self-generated defilement hardens; externally-received defilement resolves without residue.
- `T5.7.1` · cluster_finding · /silent — **[CHAR-1]** S — T2.8 found no constitutional deposit in the body from the ritual defilement-state. The characteristic is structured as a temporary, resolvable condition, not one that leaves a lasting constitutional trace. T5.7 is closed on this basis.
- `T5.7.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-2]** E — T2.8 identified a constitutional hardening (callousness of conscience — Eph 4:19, VCG-02) as the deposit of sustained moral-inner defilement. The developmental consequence: progressive moral blindness — the ability to perceive one's own condition correctly is progressively lost (Tit 1:15 reference in VCG-03 — "to the defiled and unbelieving, nothing is pure; both their minds and their consciences are defiled"). The deposit produces: (1) increased susceptibility to further defilement (each act of defilement weakens the conscience further, making the next act easier); (2) distorted moral cognition (the mind cannot correctly assess purity); (3) entrenched unrepentance (2Cor 12:21, VCG-02 — those who "have not repented").
- `T5.7.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-3]** E — T2.8 identified a sustained corporate-generational deposit: the community's collective moral identity becomes constitutively defiled over time, and the generational pattern of idolatrous defection (Eze 20:30 — "after the manner of your fathers") is a transmitted pattern. The developmental consequence: (1) generational entrenching of idolatrous practice — each generation inherits the pattern of the previous (Eze 20:18 — children warned not to defile as their fathers did; 20:30 — warning that the pattern is repeating). (2) The corporate moral identity as publicly defiled — Eze 22:5 (VCG-05): "your name is defiled" — the community's name becomes a marker of defilement known to all nations. (3) Institutional collapse — Eze 22:26 (VCG-05): the priestly institution meant to interrupt the defilement-cycle fails; the institutional deposit of priestly knowledge of clean/unclean is not transmitted.
- `T5.7.1` · cluster_finding · /silent — **[CHAR-4]** S — T2.8 found no constitutional deposit from sustained possession in the 21-verse corpus. The restoration is consistently named as complete. T5.7 is closed on this basis.
- `T5.7.2` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — **[CLUSTER]** E — Characteristic 3 evidences generational consequence directly (Eze 20:18/30 — each generation repeating the fathers' defilement-pattern). Characteristic 2 evidences a generational dimension through Job 14:4 ("who can bring a clean thing out of an unclean?") — the inherited constitutional uncleanness of the human condition implies generational transmission. Characteristics 1 and 4 do not evidence generational consequences (Char 1 — defilement incurred by individuals in their own lifetime; Char 4 — a child is possessed by the spirit's own invasion, not through parental deposit). The cluster's generational pattern: the characteristics with sustained volitional deposits produce generational transmission (Chars 2, 3); the externally-received characteristics do not.
- `T5.7.2` · cluster_finding · /silent — **[CHAR-1]** S — No generational constitutional deposit is evidenced in the 133-verse corpus for Characteristic 1. The perpetual statutes (e.g., Num 19:10 — "this shall be a perpetual statute for the people of Israel, and for the stranger who sojourns among them") are institutional provisions, not evidence of a generational biological or constitutional deposit. The defilement-state is incurred by individuals in their own lifetime through their own bodily events.
- `T5.7.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-2]** E — The 26-verse corpus does not directly evidence a generational biological deposit of the moral-inner defilement-state. Job 14:4 (VCG-01) — "who can bring a clean thing out of an unclean? There is not one" — can be read as pointing to a generational constitutional condition (the human being as constitutionally unclean, from which no clean offspring can emerge), which implies a generational transmission of the constitutional uncleanness. However, the verse does not specifically name a biological mechanism; it names an existential condition. The evidence supports a generational transmission of the constitutional moral uncleanness (Job 14:4) but does not evidence a specific deposit mechanism beyond the constitutional human condition inherited from the fall.
- `T5.7.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-3]** E — Yes directly. Eze 20:18/30 (VCG-02) documents the generational transmission of the defilement pattern: the children are warned not to defile as their fathers did ("do not walk in the statutes of your fathers, nor keep their rules, nor defile yourselves with their idols" — 20:18); but the rhetorical challenge of 20:30 ("will you defile yourselves after the manner of your fathers?") reveals that the warning goes unheeded and the generational pattern is repeated. The generational consequence is: each generation inherits the temptation to repeat the defilement pattern of the previous generation, and without prophetic interruption and covenantal formation, the pattern is reproduced.
- `T5.7.2` · cluster_finding · /silent — **[CHAR-4]** S — No generational consequence is evidenced in the 21-verse corpus. A child is afflicted (Mar 7:25, Luk 9:42) but this is the spirit's own invasion of the child, not a generationally transmitted condition from the parents.
- `T5.7.3` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — **[CLUSTER]** E — Characteristics 1 and 4 are closed at T5.7 (no deposit identified at T2.8). Characteristics 2 and 3 are open and addressed above. The cluster's differentiated T5.7 closure is itself analytically significant: the cluster contains two characteristics that leave lasting deposits (Chars 2, 3 — both self-generated) and two that do not (Chars 1, 4 — both externally-received in different modes). The division maps exactly onto the cluster's origin taxonomy from T2.9.  ---  T5 cluster self-evaluation:   1. Prompt count:        21/21 ✓   2. Scope marker:        21× [CLUSTER] ✓   3. **T6.6 · Shared Verse Anchors**  (15)

- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — **[CLUSTER]** E — The cluster's shared-anchor analysis confirms deep cross-cluster integration at the verse level. Lev 11:44 (Char 1 VCG-04) functions as anchor for both Characteristic 1's volitional-avoidance register and M12's positive holiness statement ("I am holy" — the verse co-defines defilement and holiness). 2Cor 7:1 (Char 2 VCG-03) functions as anchor for Characteristic 2's defilement-state and for M12's holiness-completion register. Eze 20:43 (Char 3 VCG-02) functions as anchor for Characteristic 3's memory-repentance mechanism and potentially for M11's corporate-repentance domain. Zec 13:1 (Char 3 VCG-05) functions as anchor for Characteristic 3's cleansing-promise and for M11's comprehensive-cleansing domain. Mar 3:11 and Mat 10:1 (Char 4 VCG-03) function as anchors for both Characteristic 4's authority-recognition/commissioning register and M27's character-of-adversarial-spirits domain. The cluster's shared anchors are most numerous at the M10c/M12 boundary (defilement and holiness defined together), the M10c/M11 boundary (defilement and cleansing defined together), and the Char 4/M27 boundary (defilement-state and adversarial-agent defined together).
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-1]** E — Possible shared anchor material within the M10c domain: Lev 11:44 (M10c-A-VCG-04, vc_id 21418) anchors the volitional self-defilement VCG in Characteristic 1 and also functions as a key expression of the holiness/defilement boundary that M12 (Purity) owns as its structural positive — the verse serves both as Characteristic 1's evidence (the will's refusal of defilement) and as M12's framing (divine holiness as the positive standard). Act 10:28 (M10c-B-VCG-05, vc_id 21281) is the anchor for the NT categorical conscience VCG in Characteristic 1; it also functions as a pivotal verse for covenantal-status questions within the broader programme. Within the M10c cluster, the Num 9:7 (vc_id 21546) verse anchors the grief-at-exclusion finding in VCG-07 of Characteristic 1.
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-2]** E — 2Cor 7:1 (vc_id 9329, G3436 molusmos, VCG-03) functions as the primary anchor for Characteristic 2's body-and-spirit purification call (VCG-03). This verse is also cited in the broader programme's purity/holiness discussion as a locus classicus of M12 (purity completing holiness). Isa 6:5 (vc_id 21547, H2931, VCG-01) — the prophet's conscience-recognition of moral-inner defilement under divine holiness — could function as an anchor for the broader guilt/awareness characteristic studies. Rev 14:4 (vc_id 9328, G3435 molunō, VCG-05) — the undefiled following the Lamb as firstfruits — is relevant as an anchor for M09 (consecration) and M12 (purity) studies given its explicit consecration language.
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-3]** E — Eze 20:43 (vc_id 21420, H2930A, VCG-02) is the primary anchor for Characteristic 3's memory-of-defilement-as-repentance mechanism — this verse functions within both Characteristic 3 and potentially within clusters addressing corporate repentance (M11) and corporate memory. Num 5:27 (vc_id 21472, H2930A, VCG-01) anchors the ordeal-of-jealousy in Characteristic 3's covenantal-relational register; this verse is also relevant to the M07 (shame) register and to the justice/ordeal register in other programme studies. Zec 13:1 (vc_id 21706, H5079, VCG-05) functions as anchor for Characteristic 3's cleansing-promise register and would also function as an M11 (Cleansing) anchor given its explicit cleansing-fountain language.
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-4]** E — Mar 3:11 (vc_id 21286, G0169, VCG-03) — "whenever the unclean spirits saw him, they fell down before him and cried out, 'You are the Son of God'" — functions as a primary anchor for Characteristic 4's authority-recognition register and would also function as a primary anchor in M27 (cosmic-evil agents) for the spirits' self-aware recognition of divine superiority. Mat 10:1 (vc_id 21282, G0169, VCG-03) — the commissioning of authority over unclean spirits — functions as an anchor for both Characteristic 4's authority-commissioning register and M27.
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — **[CLUSTER]** E — The shared anchors reveal the cluster's most important structural insight: M10c is constitutively defined by adjacent clusters at its boundaries. M10c/M12 co-definition (Lev 11:44, 2Cor 7:1): defilement and holiness are not independently defined — the same verses that most fully express the defilement-state also most fully express the holiness that the defilement opposes. The two clusters are not merely structurally opposed; they are lexically and textually co-defined. M10c/M11 co-presence (Eze 20:43, Zec 13:1, 2Cor 7:1): the defilement-state and the cleansing-response are present in the same anchor verses — defilement and cleansing are textually inseparable; the same texts express the condition and its remedy. M10c Char 4/M27 co-presence (Mar 3:11, Mat 10:1): the defilement-condition and the adversarial agent's nature are present in the same anchor verses — Characteristic 4 and M27 are analytically inseparable; the same events must be read through both lenses simultaneously. The cluster's co-definitional insight: M10c does not stand alone but is constituted by its relationships to M12 (holiness), M11 (cleansing), and M27 (cosmic-evil agents) at its key anchors.
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-1]** E — Lev 11:44's shared role (Characteristic 1 and M12) reveals that the defilement-state and purity are constitutively defined by each other — the verse that most clearly defines what it means not to defile (Characteristic 1's VCG-04 anchor) is simultaneously the verse that most clearly defines what holiness means (M12's positive statement). The two characteristics are not merely structurally opposite — they are co-defined by the same verse. Act 10:28's shared role reveals that the categorical revision of clean/unclean (Characteristic 1) is simultaneously a revelation about covenantal inclusion (which belongs to broader programme clusters).
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-2]** E — 2Cor 7:1's shared role across Characteristic 2 (defilement of body and spirit) and M12 (bringing holiness to completion) reveals that the defilement-state and purity are co-defined by this verse — it simultaneously describes the problem (defilement of body and spirit) and the goal (holiness brought to completion). The verse is a boundary-point between the two characteristics: it belongs to Characteristic 2 because of the defilement named, and to M12 because of the holiness called for. Rev 14:4's shared role across Characteristic 2 (not defiled) and M09 (consecration — following the Lamb) reveals the positive-inverse of the defilement-state as a consecration state, confirming M09 as the positive structural companion of Characteristic 2's resolution.
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-3]** E — Eze 20:43's shared role (Characteristic 3 and M11 repentance/corporate memory) reveals that the memory of corporate defilement and the beginning of repentance are co-constituted in this verse — the characteristic and its remedy are inseparable at the point of initiation. Zec 13:1's shared role (Characteristic 3 and M11) reveals that the comprehensive cleansing of corporate defilement and the M11 cleansing cluster are addressed by the same eschatological provision. The shared anchors confirm that Characteristic 3 is structurally embedded in a larger soteriological arc (defilement → memory → repentance → cleansing) that spans multiple clusters.
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[CHAR-4]** E — The shared anchors (Mar 3:11, Mat 10:1) reveal the deep integration between Characteristic 4 and M27: the verses most fully expressing the authority structure governing Characteristic 4's resolution also most fully characterise the nature of the M27 agents. The two clusters are analytically inseparable at the anchor-verse level — the same events simultaneously evidence both the defilement-condition (Characteristic 4) and the agent's nature (M27). This confirms the INTEGRATION_NOTE: the two lenses are complementary, not duplicative.
- `T6.6.3` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — **[CLUSTER]** E — Shared verse anchors are identified and evidenced across multiple cluster boundaries. Not applicable.

**T6.7 · Dimensional Sharing**  (15)

- `T6.7.1` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — **[CLUSTER]** G — Full cross-programme dimensional sharing counts are not available within this session's scope. From the evidence within the cluster's 263-verse corpus and the known cross-register flags, the following dimensional sharing is evidenced. The condition dimension: shared across all four characteristics and confirmed as the cluster's universal primary classification (T1.8.1). The social-relational dimension: shared across Characteristics 1, 2, 3, and 4 (all produce relational isolation). The volitional dimension: shared across Characteristics 1, 2, and 3 (all engage the will, though differently); absent from Characteristic 4 (will bypassed). The spirit-level dimension: shared between Characteristics 2 and 4 (both operate at the spirit level, but from different directions). The corporate dimension: distinctive to Characteristic 3 (the cluster's only characteristic extending defilement to corporate sacred space). The external-agent dimension: distinctive to Characteristic 4 (the cluster's only characteristic with an adversarial spiritual agent as the producing cause). The memory-repentance dimension: distinctive to Characteristic 3 (Eze 20:43 — no parallel in Characteristics 1, 2, or 4).
- `T6.7.1` · cluster_finding · /gap — **[CHAR-1]** G — Full dimensional-sharing data across programme characteristics is not available within the current session scope — the broader programme's completed cluster analyses are not all accessible here. The dimensions identifiable within this characteristic (condition, volitional-perimeter, social-relational, discernment-formative) are likely shared with M12 (Purity — shared condition/discernment dimensions as structural mirror), M11 (Cleansing — shared condition/remedial dimensions as sequential counterpart), and M07 (Shame — shared social-relational/exclusion dimensions). A complete cross-programme dimensional sharing count requires access to the full programme's completed characteristic analyses.
- `T6.7.1` · cluster_finding · /gap — **[CHAR-2]** G — Full cross-programme dimensional sharing data is not available within this session's scope. Within this characteristic's 26-verse corpus, the dimensions identified (condition, dispositional, volitional, constitutional, spirit-level, heart-level, desire-vehicle) are likely shared with: M12 (purity — structural mirror, sharing condition/constitutional dimensions); M11 (cleansing — sharing condition/remedial dimensions as sequential counterpart); M29 (desire — sharing desire-vehicle dimension as constituent element); M10 (sin-act — sharing volitional/condition dimensions as causal predecessor); M09 (consecration — sharing constitutional/spirit-level dimensions as positive inverse).
- `T6.7.1` · cluster_finding · /gap — **[CHAR-3]** G — Full cross-programme dimensional sharing data is not available within this session's scope. Within this characteristic's 83-verse corpus, the dimensions identified (condition, volitional-generative, corporate-covenantal, memory-repentance, spatial-extension to sacred environment) include some shared with other characteristics and some distinctive. The memory-repentance dimension (Eze 20:43) is the most distinctive; it is not evidenced in Characteristics 1 or 2. The volitional-generative dimension is shared with Characteristics 2 and partially 1. The spatial-extension dimension (to land, sanctuary, name) is distinctive to Characteristic 3.
- `T6.7.1` · cluster_finding · /gap — **[CHAR-4]** G — Full cross-programme dimensional sharing data is not available within this session's scope. Within this characteristic's 21-verse corpus, the dimensions identified (condition, external-spiritual-agency, will-domination, faculty-suppression, communal-severance, spirit-level operation) include some shared and some distinctive. The spirit-level-externally-sourced dimension is shared with M27. The will-domination dimension is partially shared with Characteristic 2 (where the will is bound internally) and M27. The communal-severance dimension is partially shared with Characteristic 3. The condition dimension is shared with all M10c characteristics.
- `T6.7.2` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — **[CLUSTER]** G — Based on available evidence: the pattern reveals the cluster's structural architecture as a set of four characteristics sharing a core (condition, social-relational) and differentiating by mechanism-specific dimensions. The shared core confirms M10c as a coherent cluster. The mechanism-specific dimensions (Char 3's corporate-extending and memory-repentance; Char 4's external-agent; Char 2's spirit-penetrating; Char 1's bodily-mechanism) confirm each characteristic as making a distinct analytical contribution that the others do not duplicate. The cluster is neither merely four versions of the same thing (which the shared core might imply) nor four unrelated conditions (which the mechanism-specific dimensions might imply) — it is four differentiated registers of one condition (defilement) distinguished by mechanism, locus, volitional engagement, and constitutional depth.
- `T6.7.2` · cluster_finding · /gap — **[CHAR-1]** G — Based on the available evidence within this characteristic's corpus: the dimensional sharing with M12 (Purity) reveals the two characteristics as constitutively mirror-defined (each requires the other for definition); with M11 (Cleansing) as sequentially paired (defilement generates cleansing as its necessary response); with M07 (Shame) as contextually overlapping in the stigma/exclusion sub-register but distinct in primary mechanism. The pattern suggests a cluster of characteristics centred on the sacral condition of the person in relation to divine holiness, each occupying a distinct position in the purity economy.
- `T6.7.2` · cluster_finding · /gap — **[CHAR-2]** G — Based on available evidence: dimensional sharing with M12 reveals them as constitutively mirror-defined; with M11 as sequentially paired; with M29 as Characteristic 2 being a downstream consequence of M29's operation; with M10 as M10 being the upstream causal act whose consequence is Characteristic 2; with M09 as Characteristic 2's resolution pointing toward M09's consecrated state. The pattern suggests Characteristic 2 is at the intersection of a network of characteristics: downstream from sin-act and desire, requiring cleansing, opposed by consecration, mirroring purity.
- `T6.7.2` · cluster_finding · /gap — **[CHAR-3]** G — Based on available evidence: dimensional sharing with Characteristics 1 and 2 (volitional-generative, condition dimensions) reveals all three M10c defilement characteristics as sharing the same basic condition/volitional structure. Characteristic 3's distinctive dimensions (corporate, spatial-extension, memory-repentance) show its unique analytical contribution: it is the only M10c characteristic whose domain is the sacred spatial-relational environment (land, sanctuary, name) and the only one that names memory as the instrument of repentance.
- `T6.7.2` · cluster_finding · /gap — **[CHAR-4]** G — Based on available evidence: dimensional sharing with M27 reveals Characteristic 4 as the M10c characteristic most directly embedded in the M27 cosmic-adversarial domain. Dimensional sharing with Characteristic 2 (will-domination, through different mechanisms) reveals that both characteristics strike at the same faculty (will) through different mechanisms (external override vs internal bondage). The distinctive dimension — externally-sourced spirit-level invasion — is Characteristic 4's unique contribution to the inner-being landscape.
- `T6.7.3` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — **[CLUSTER]** G — Full cross-programme dimensional sharing data is not yet available. The cluster-level analysis above draws on the per-characteristic evidence within the M10c 263-verse corpus and the known cross-register flags.
