### M15  (147 findings)

**T1.2.3 · Best working description**  (9)

- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — [BOUNDARY — structural characterisation note only; full catalogue pass not applicable per §11 BOUNDARY treatment rule]
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — The word as inner-being engagement — the divine or apostolic word that addresses, constitutes, and evaluates the inner person; the human speech that expresses the inner quality outward; the comprehensive moral principle held as inner standard. The logos characteristic spans receiving (divine word entering) and expressing (human speech proceeding from inner quality). Col 3:16, 1Cor 14:19, Gal 5:14.
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — A divinely-endowed constituted quality of the whole person, grounded in covenantal orientation toward God, expressed in the governance of mind, speech, and action, and distinguished from self-attributed or world-oriented substitutes. Primary constitutional location: heart and mind. Pro 16:23, Jam 1:5, Rom 8:5.
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — The inner capacity to receive, distinguish, and grasp what is disclosed — divine revelation, situation, meaning. Constituted in the person by divine endowment, formed through practice, exercised in receiving disclosed content, and capable of blockage. Primary location: heart (1Ki 3:9) and mind (Luk 24:45). Isa 40:28, 1Ki 3:9, Luk 24:45.
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — The relational-covenantal inner state of knowing — spanning from God's comprehensive knowing of all things (VCG01) through the covenant-defining mutual knowing of God and people (VCG02/VCG04) to the content-based knowing of facts, persons, and skills (VCG10). Its defining mode is relational-experiential (ya.da), not merely cognitive storage. Its eschatological destiny is universal knowledge of the LORD (VCG12). Hos 6:3, Pro 1:7, Hab 2:14.
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — The applied inner perceptive act of making right evaluative distinctions in concrete situations — prudent assessment, moral discrimination, crediting, deliberative evaluation. Directionally neutral in some of its terms (a.rum serves wisdom or cunning; cha.shav devises for good or ill). Primary location: heart/inner reasoning. Isa 52:13, 1Cor 11:31, Gen 15:6.
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — The inner capacity for forward-oriented purposive willing — forming deliberate intent, devising plans, giving and receiving counsel, making irrevocable decisions. Spans from the human counselor's wisdom (2Sa 16:23) to God's irresistible sovereign purpose (Isa 14:24). Primary constitutional location: the heart (Pro 16:9 proximate: "the heart plans its way"). 2Sa 16:23, Jer 29:11, Isa 14:24.
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — The sustained inner activity of dwelling on received content — turning over God's precepts, pondering visions, reasoning within oneself about situations. Activity-oriented (distinct from static content). The person is actively engaged in an inner dialogue with something beyond themselves. Primary location: the heart (inner reasoning, Luk 5:22 VCG01 anchor) and the engaged mind (Psa 119:97 VCG03 anchor). Psa 119:97, Luk 5:22.
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — The formed thoughts held within the mind — inner content as objects that can be captured, blinded, or disclosed. Static (distinct from the activity of M15-F). Satan can blind noēmata (2Cor 4:4 VCG01 anchor); God can know the inner thoughts of the heart; the person holds thought-content directed toward God (Psa 139:17 VCG02 anchor). 2Cor 4:4, Psa 139:17.

**T1.8 · Dimension Classification**  (27)

- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — [BOUNDARY — structural characterisation note only; full catalogue pass not applicable per §11 BOUNDARY treatment rule]
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — Communicative-formative (the word in its inner-being-engaging mode). Secondary: moral (VCG03: comprehensive moral principle), cognitive (the word as content received). Evidence: Col 3:16 (word dwelling richly — constituting wisdom), 1Cor 14:19 (logos as intelligible speech).
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — Cognitive-constitutive (constituted quality of heart-and-mind). Secondary: volitional (direction), relational (covenantal), creative (VCG10). Evidence: Pro 16:23 (heart of wise → judicious speech), Rom 8:5 (froneō — mind-orientation).
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — Cognitive-perceptive (the faculty of receiving and grasping). Secondary: volitional (formed through practice), moral-evaluative (distinguishing good from evil). Evidence: Isa 40:28, 1Ki 3:9, Heb 5:14.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — Relational-covenantal (knowing as relational state). Secondary: cognitive (held content), moral (knowledge of good and evil, VCG07). Evidence: Hos 6:3 (pressing to know the LORD), Gen 4:1 (ya.da in intimate knowing), Amo 3:2 (elective knowing).
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — Cognitive-evaluative (making right distinctions through applied perception). Secondary: moral (distinguishing good from evil), volitional (crediting and imputing). Evidence: 1Cor 11:31 (self-judgment), Isa 52:13 (prudent effective action).
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — Volitional (purposive willing and planning). Secondary: relational (counsel relationship), cognitive (forming the deliberate plan). Evidence: 2Pe 3:9 (boulomai — God's purposive willing), Jer 29:11 (purposive devising in the heart).
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — Cognitive-reflective (active mental engagement with received content). Secondary: affective (love formed through meditation, Psa 119:97). Evidence: Psa 119:97 (si.chah — all-day meditation), Luk 5:22 (dialogizomai — interior reasoning).
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — Cognitive-static (formed thought-objects held in the mind). Secondary: relational (thought directed toward God or toward the person by God). Evidence: 2Cor 4:4 (noēmata — objects of adversarial blinding), Psa 139:17 (re.a — precious thoughts of God).
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — [BOUNDARY — structural characterisation note only; full catalogue pass not applicable per §11 BOUNDARY treatment rule]
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — Col 3:16: word dwelling and constituting. 1Cor 14:19: intelligible speech.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — Pro 16:23: heart of the wise → governing character. Rom 8:5: froneō as directed-mind act.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — Isa 40:28: God's understanding as model. Heb 5:14: training by practice. Luk 24:45: opened minds.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — Hos 6:3: relational pursuit. Amo 3:2: elective-covenantal knowing. Gen 4:1: intimate knowing.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — 1Cor 11:31: self-judgment. Gen 15:6: crediting act.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — 2Pe 3:9: God's purposive willing. Jer 29:11: deliberate purposive devising. Isa 14:24: irresistible counsel.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — Psa 119:97: all-day meditation. Luk 5:22: interior reasoning.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — 2Cor 4:4: blindable noēmata. Psa 139:17: precious thoughts of God directed at person.
- `T1.8.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — [BOUNDARY — structural characterisation note only; full catalogue pass not applicable per §11 BOUNDARY treatment rule]
- `T1.8.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — The moral and cognitive secondary dimensions are modes of the communicative-formative primary. No competition.
- `T1.8.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — Volitional, relational, creative — secondary dimensions do not compete with cognitive-constitutive primary; they extend it. Pro 16:23 (heart governing output) is unambiguously primary.
- `T1.8.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — Volitional (practice) and moral-evaluative (distinguishing) are secondary and serve the primary perceptive function. No competition.
- `T1.8.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — Cognitive (content) dimension is secondary to relational-covenantal primary. In VCG10 (practical knowing), the relational dimension recedes — these may be in minor tension within the sub-group. The relational-covenantal is definitionally primary (Hos 6:3, VCG04 anchor).
- `T1.8.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — Moral-evaluative and volitional secondary dimensions serve the primary cognitive-evaluative. No competition.
- `T1.8.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — The relational (counseling relationship) and cognitive (plan formation) are secondary to the volitional primary. No competition; they are modes of the volitional expression.
- `T1.8.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — The affective secondary dimension (love formed through meditation, Psa 119:97) is a result of, not in competition with, the cognitive-reflective primary.
- `T1.8.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — The relational secondary dimension (thought directed toward/from God) gives the cognitive-static content its significance. No competition.

**T6.6 · Shared Verse Anchors**  (27)

- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — [BOUNDARY — structural characterisation note only; full catalogue pass not applicable per §11 BOUNDARY treatment rule]
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — Anticipated shared anchors for M15-H: Col 3:16 (shared with community formation clusters and worship vocabulary — word, psalms, teaching, admonishing together); 1Cor 1:18 (shared with cross/salvation clusters and power vocabulary); Gal 5:14 (shared with love vocabulary and moral law clusters — love-your-neighbour as the law-fulfilling logos). M15-H is anticipated as the sub-group with the densest cross-cluster anchor sharing, due to logos vocabulary spanning the widest range of theological topics.
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — Anticipated shared anchors for M15-A: Pro 10:14 (shared with M15-C — "the wise lay up knowledge" — wisdom and knowledge co-anchored); Pro 9:10 (shared with M15-C and potentially fear/reverence clusters — "fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom"); 1Cor 1:24 (shared with M15-H and cross/salvation clusters — "Christ the wisdom of God and power of God"). The wisdom sub-group's anchors are most likely to be shared with knowledge (M15-C) and logos (M15-H) within the cluster, and with fear/reverence vocabulary outside the cluster.
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — Anticipated shared anchors for M15-B: 1Ki 3:9 (shared with M15-A — the same verse names understanding and wisdom together); Heb 5:14 (shared with M15-D — "aisthētērion trained to distinguish good from evil" — both understanding and discernment are engaged); Luk 24:45 (potentially shared with mind/heart clusters). Understanding's anchors are most likely shared with wisdom (M15-A) and discernment (M15-D) within the cluster.
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — Anticipated shared anchors for M15-C: Pro 10:14 (shared with M15-A — wisdom and knowledge together); Hos 6:3 (shared with M15-A and covenant vocabulary clusters); Hab 2:14 (shared with eschatological clusters — earth full of the knowledge of the LORD); Gen 2:9 (shared with moral vocabulary clusters — knowledge of good and evil); Psa 139:6 (shared with divine attribute clusters — omniscience, sovereignty). M15-C is the sub-group with the highest anticipated cross-cluster anchor sharing, due to the breadth of ya.da vocabulary.
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — Anticipated shared anchors for M15-D: Gen 15:6 (shared with faith/justification clusters — "credited as righteousness"; this is among the most widely shared verses in the programme); Heb 5:14 (shared with M15-B — aisthētērion as trained understanding and discernment); 2Cor 10:4-5 (shared with M15-G — the logismos/noēma strongholds appear in the same passage). Gen 15:6 is anticipated as the most cross-cluster-connected anchor in M15-D.
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — Anticipated shared anchors for M15-E: Isa 14:24 (shared with sovereignty/divine attributes clusters — God's irresistible counsel is a sovereignty statement); Pro 16:9 (shared with heart/intention clusters — "the heart of man plans his way"); 2Sa 16:23 (shared with governance and wisdom clusters — the counselor-king relationship). M15-E's anchors are most likely shared with heart/intention vocabulary and governance vocabulary outside the cluster.
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — Anticipated shared anchors for M15-F: Psa 119:97 (shared with love vocabulary — M15-F's anchor is simultaneously a love declaration; likely shared with C17 love cluster); Dan 7:28 (shared with vision/revelation clusters and potentially with anxiety/fear vocabulary). M15-F's primary anchor (Psa 119:97) is among the most likely to be shared with the love cluster due to its explicit love-declaration context.
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — Anticipated shared anchors for M15-G: 2Cor 4:4 (shared with spiritual warfare vocabulary and blindness/unbelief clusters — the adversarial blinding is a spiritual warfare statement); 2Cor 10:5 (shared with M15-D logismos — the same passage); 2Chr 1:10 (shared with governance clusters and M15-C knowledge). M15-G's anchors are most likely shared with spiritual warfare vocabulary clusters.
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — [BOUNDARY — structural characterisation note only; full catalogue pass not applicable per §11 BOUNDARY treatment rule]
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — Where logos (M15-H) shares anchor with community vocabulary — Col 3:16 — the shared anchor reveals that the logos characteristic and the community-building characteristic are inseparable: the word dwelling richly IS the mechanism of community formation.
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — Where wisdom (M15-A) shares anchor verses with knowledge (M15-C) — as in Pro 10:14 and the broader Wisdom literature — the shared anchor reveals that wisdom and knowledge are not merely related but are co-constitutive: the same texts hold them simultaneously without tension, suggesting they describe the same inner reality from different perspectives.
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — Where understanding (M15-B) shares anchors with wisdom (M15-A) — as in 1Ki 3:9 (asking for both discerning heart and the wisdom to govern) — the shared anchor reveals that understanding and wisdom are functionally inseparable in governance contexts. They appear together because they operate together.
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — Where knowledge (M15-C) shares anchor with eschatological clusters — Hab 2:14 — the shared anchor reveals that the knowing of God is not only a present covenantal reality but the defining content of eschatological hope. Knowledge at the eschatological level is the fullness of the covenant relationship universalised.
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — Where discernment (M15-D) shares anchor with justification vocabulary — Gen 15:6 — the shared anchor reveals that evaluative crediting and righteousness-standing are inseparable. M15-D's forensic dimension is the inner-being correlate of the justification complex.
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — Where purposive counsel (M15-E) shares anchor with sovereignty vocabulary — Isa 14:24 — the shared anchor reveals that the divine counsel characteristic and the divine sovereignty characteristic are expressions of the same divine reality. God's counsel IS his sovereignty in action.
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — Where meditation (M15-F) shares anchor with love vocabulary — Psa 119:97 — the shared anchor reveals that meditative practice and affective love are constitutively united: you meditate on what you love; meditation deepens love. M15-F and the love characteristic (C17) are functionally continuous at their richest.
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — Where thought-content (M15-G) shares anchor with spiritual warfare vocabulary — 2Cor 4:4 and 10:4-5 — the shared anchor reveals that the inner cognitive domain and the spiritual conflict domain are structurally linked. The most contested inner territory in M15 is the thought-content characteristic.

- `T6.7.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — Where logos (M15-H) shares dimensions with proclamation, community, and covenant vocabulary, the pattern reveals M15-H as the cluster's connective characteristic: the one that reaches into the most diverse other characteristics and clusters through the word's communicative and formative function.
- `T6.7.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — Where wisdom (M15-A) shares dimensions with knowledge (M15-C) and understanding (M15-B), the pattern reveals that the triad is not three separate characteristics but three aspects of a single inner complex. Dimensional sharing confirms: these characteristics are designed to operate together and can only be properly understood in relation to each other.
- `T6.7.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — Where understanding (M15-B) shares dimensions with discernment (M15-D) — both being perceptive-evaluative faculties — the pattern reveals that understanding and discernment are the cluster's perceptive pair: understanding grasps; discernment evaluates what has been grasped.
- `T6.7.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — Where knowledge (M15-C) shares dimensions with the whole cluster — as foundational content for wisdom, understanding, discernment, counsel, and logos — the pattern reveals knowledge as the structurally central characteristic: remove knowledge and the other characteristics lose their content-base.
- `T6.7.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — Where discernment (M15-D) shares dimensions with conscience vocabulary and justification vocabulary, the pattern reveals M15-D as the inner-being characteristic most directly connected to both the moral-evaluative faculty and the forensic-covenantal domain. Dimensional sharing confirms M15-D's unique double connection: inward (conscience) and outward (divine judgment).
- `T6.7.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — Where purposive counsel (M15-E) shares dimensions with governance and sovereignty vocabulary, the pattern reveals M15-E as the cluster's action-bridge: connecting the inner purposing of both human and divine persons to their historical and relational consequences.
- `T6.7.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — Where meditation (M15-F) shares dimensions with devotional and love vocabulary, the pattern reveals M15-F as the affective-cognitive bridge: the characteristic that sustains the relationship between love (affect) and wisdom (cognition) through sustained inner dwelling.
- `T6.7.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — Where thought-content (M15-G) shares dimensions with spiritual warfare vocabulary, the pattern reveals the thought-content characteristic as the inner-being correlate of the spiritual conflict domain. The dimension shared is adversarial contestation: this is the characteristic most directly under attack.

### M20  (37 findings)

**T1.2.3 · Best working description**  (4)

- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — A condition of the inner person in which the faculty of directed attentive concern is misdirected toward anticipated threat, material need, or uncertain outcomes — producing a state of preoccupied, scattered, or weighted inner orientation that competes with trust in God. The same faculty, rightly directed toward God and others, constitutes love and devotion.
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — The collapse of the inner person's forward-orientation — whether as overwhelming circumstance exceeding the person's endurance (exaporeō) or as the active surrendering of hope, the giving up of pursuit, or the verbal declaration that return is impossible (ya.ash). The inner person can no longer face what is ahead.
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — The inner person's courage and resolve diminishing under sustained external pressure — opposition, suffering, provocation, or temporal frustration — producing a state of depleted inner energy that requires external encouragement, theological grounding, or inner renewal to recover.
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — The inner person failing to maintain a unified, committed orientation — either in a specific encounter moment when faith wavers between seeing and trusting (distazō) or as a settled dispositional state in which the person does not commit and is therefore unstable in all ways (dipsuchos).  ---  ### T1.3 — Boundary

**T1.8 · Dimension Classification**  (12)

- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — Affective-attentional — the characteristic is primarily located in the inner person's capacity for directed feeling and attention. The cognitive dimension (thought about threat) is present but secondary.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — Volitional-affective — ya.ash involves the will (giving up, declaring); exaporeō is affective (collapse of inner endurance). The two terms carry different primary dimensions, but the volitional register of ya.ash is the most analytically distinctive finding.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — Affective-agency — the inner energy that sustains action (prayer, doing good, perseverance) fails; the primary dimension is the loss of the affective-energy foundation for sustained agency.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — Cognitive-volitional — distazō is primarily cognitive (epistemic wavering); dipsuchos is primarily volitional (dispositional non-commitment). Both involve a failure to settle, which is a volitional act.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — The choking and weighting images (350-001, Luk 21:34) locate anxiety in the affective-constitutional domain; the attentional-scattering image (Luk 10:41 — "anxious about many things") locates it in the attentional faculty.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — Ecc 2:20 ("gave my heart up to despair") locates ya.ash's primary dimension in the will; exaporeō in 2Cor 1:8 ("despaired of life itself") locates it in the affective-endurance faculty.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — 2Cor 4:16 ("our inner self is being renewed") locates the inner-person renewal that counteracts ekkakeō — implying the primary dimension is inner-personal energy/resolve.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — Jam 1:8 ("unstable in all his ways") locates the consequence in behavioural-volitional instability; Jam 4:8 ("purify your hearts") locates the remedy in the heart's volitional-affective centre.
- `T1.8.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — Cognitive — merimnaō involves sustained thought about anticipated outcomes (the ruminative quality identified in the science extract as Borkovec's verbal-cognitive worry). Relational — da.ag shows anxiety as relational (care for an absent loved one).
- `T1.8.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — Cognitive — ya.ash's weaponised form (Jer 18:12) involves deliberate cognitive framing (deploying futility-language). Verbal/speech — the declaration mode of ya.ash is a secondary speech-dimension.
- `T1.8.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — Relational — athumeō (Col 3:21) is produced relationally; ka.ah/ka.eh show discouragement within social vulnerability.
- `T1.8.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — Relational — dipsuchos's divided allegiance has a relational dimension (divided between God and world). Affective — distazō involves emotional fear (Mat 14:30 — Peter saw the wind and was afraid before he doubted).

**T6.6 · Shared Verse Anchors**  (7)

- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — Mat 6:25-34 (merimnaō group) — these verses anchor the teaching on trust/faith and on provision; they are likely shared with M19 (Trust, Expectation, Waiting — now containing related vocabulary). Phili 4:6 — likely shared with Prayer vocabulary and with M19. 1Cor 12:25 (merimnaō mutual care) — likely shared with Love/care clusters.
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — 2Cor 1:8-9 — likely shared with M19 (trust in God who raises the dead) and with Suffering/affliction clusters.
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — 2Cor 4:16 — "inner self renewed day by day" — likely shared with Renewal/sanctification vocabulary and with Hope clusters. Gal 6:9 — likely shared with Perseverance vocabulary.
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — Mat 28:17 — the resurrection appearance doubting verse — likely shared with Faith vocabulary.
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — Mat 6:25-34 shared with trust/faith vocabulary reveals that anxiety and trust are direct structural opponents operating on the same constitutional ground — the same verse is the primary evidence for both characteristics.
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — 2Cor 4:16 shared with renewal vocabulary reveals that the mechanism that counters discouragement (inner renewal) is itself a significant characteristic requiring its own study.
- `T6.6.3` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — Shared verse anchors are likely across multiple sub-groups — however, confirmation of what functions as a primary anchor in other clusters requires cross-cluster data not available in the M20 verse corpus alone. Recording as G — gap: shared-anchor status cannot be confirmed without cross-cluster data.  ---  ### T6.7 — Dimensional Sharing

**T6.7 · Dimensional Sharing**  (5)

- `T6.7.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — The affective-attentional dimension is shared with: M01 (Fear — affective), M19 (Trust — attentional, directed). The cognitive dimension is shared with M06 (Hate, Contempt — cognitive-evaluative) and with doubt (M20-D).
- `T6.7.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — The agency-depletion dimension is shared with characteristics of weakness and suffering — the inner energy that sustains agency is common to multiple characteristics.
- `T6.7.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — The cognitive-volitional division dimension is shared with M20-A (merimnaō as divided attention, 1Cor 7:33-34) and with conscience characteristics that describe inner moral division.
- `T6.7.2` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — M20's dimensional territory overlaps significantly with M01 (Fear), M19 (Trust), and conscience/moral-character clusters. This confirms M20's structural position in the inner-being landscape: it is the cluster of negative inner states that stand in structural opposition to faith, hope, courage, and single-minded commitment. Its dimensions are the negative forms of the dimensions that constitute M19 and related clusters.
- `T6.7.3` · cluster_finding · /gap — dimensional sharing data across the full programme is not yet available. The above is based on evidence within M20's verse corpus and prior cluster work. Full mapping requires Session D cross-cluster synthesis.  ---  ## T7 — Evidential and Methodological Foundation  ### T7.1 — Lexical and Semantic Analysis


### M26  (34 findings)

**T1.2.3 · Best working description**  (9)

- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — **[CLUSTER]** The best working description of M26 as a whole: Righteousness and Justice names the moral domain centred on the quality of conformity to God's standard of right — expressed as God's constitutive attribute (A1), realised in the human person as a governing state (A2), expressed dispositionally as inner straightness (B), conferred through justification (C), enforced through avenging justice (D), and marked by its negative verdict (E). The reckoning faculty (F) is the inner-person mechanism that makes the reception and exercise of righteousness possible. The structural opposites (G) define the domain's boundary. The cluster is unified by one moral domain but internally differentiated across multiple kinds of inner-being phenomenon.
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[A1 — God Righteousness]** God's righteousness is the constitutive quality of his nature by which all his ways and works conform perfectly to what is right — not as a standard external to him but as what he essentially is. It expresses itself through his judgments (A1-002), his covenant faithfulness (A1-003), his saving acts (A1-004), his proclamation (A1-005), his standard of exposure (A1-006), and supremely through the Messiah who embodies it (A1-007). Constitutional location: not applicable in a human sense — it is the architectonic quality of divine being.
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[A2 — Human Righteousness]** Human righteousness is a governing state of the person — the condition of being rightly oriented toward God, expressing itself in measurable life consequences (A2-001), received by faith in Christ (950-001), practiced in daily living (950-002), and oriented outward toward others (A2-012) and God (A2-014). It engages the whole inner person — the heart (A2-005: law written on the heart), the will (A2-006: hungering), the speech and discernment (A2-011). Its impact is comprehensive: the righteous person is rooted (A2-013), upheld by God (3246-002), walking a recognisable path (A2-017), and serving as a preservative presence for their community. Constitutional location: the heart, the whole inner being. In the NT, constituted "in Christ" (2Cor 5:21).  **[A2 — qualification: conduct/state bidirectionality]** The relationship between the state and its conduct expression is bidirectional, not one-directional. The dominant pattern is state → conduct as evidence: the governing state of righteousness produces observable evidence in behaviour, speech, and orientation (Mat 25:37 is the clearest case — the righteous serve without calculation, the action is the evidence, the actor is not performing for an audience). But Ezekiel's framework presents a genuine reverse direction: conduct can constitute the state, not merely evidence it. Eze 18:5–9 — "if a man is righteous and does what is just and right... he is righteous" — lists specific behaviours and concludes from them that the person *is* righteous. The doing defines the being rather than merely evidencing it. Deu 6:25 reinforces this: "it will be righteousness for us, if we are careful to do all this commandment" — obedience produces righteousness as a result, the conduct comes first and the state follows. This bidirectionality is a genuine feature of the verse evidence, not a tension to resolve: the state must express itself in conduct to remain genuine, and sustained righteous conduct constitutes and deepens the state. Neither direction cancels the other.
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[B — Moral Uprightness]** Moral uprightness is an inner disposition of the person toward what is straight and true — an orientation of the inner being that aligns speech, conduct, and inner life with the directional quality of God's own truthfulness. It engages the inner will, speech (Isa 33:15), and the heart (1211-001). Its impact: the person with uprightness can be trusted, walks in alignment with God's ways, and is capable of recognising what is right (Pro 2:9). Constitutional location: the heart; the inner orientation of the will.
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[C — Justification]** Justification is a judicial act directed at a person's inner standing before God, declaring or making them righteous through the work of Christ received by faith. It engages the faith-faculty of the person (reception), the judicial assessment of God (declaration), and the inner standing that results. Its impact: the justified person passes from guilty-standing to righteous-standing before God, becoming an heir of eternal life (Tit 3:7). Constitutional location: the new status inheres in the person's standing before God — declared and received condition.
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[D — Avenging Justice]** Avenging justice is a responsive moral act — the impulse and execution of retribution for wrong done, expressed either as the human cry for God to act (944-001) or as the delegated human commission to execute God's wrath (3250-001). It engages the will (commitment to leave vengeance to God, 6841-002), the moral faculty (recognising wrong as wrong), and the relationship with divine authority. Its impact: the one who entrusts vengeance to God is freed from the destructive drive of self-executed revenge.
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[E — Condemnation]** Condemnation is a judicial sentence directed against a person on the basis of assessed guilt — producing the inner experience of being under verdict, or of being freed from it. It engages the judicial faculty, the conscience (autokatakritos), and the faith-faculty (reception of freedom from condemnation, 4846-002). Its impact: the condemned person bears the weight of divine judgment; the self-condemned person bears their own conscience turned against them; the freed person lives without the condemning voice (Joh 8:11).
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[F — Moral Reckoning]** Moral reckoning is the evaluative faculty of the inner person — the capacity to weigh moral worth, credit standing, assign accountability, and live from the conclusions of inner moral deliberation. It engages the cognitive-rational dimension of the inner person (logizomai: to reason, reckon), the moral assessment capacity (axioō: to deem worthy), and the evaluative-judicial faculty (a.rakh: to compare, set in order). Its impact: the person whose reckoning faculty is properly engaged counts themselves dead to sin and alive to God (Rom 6:11), weighs present suffering against future glory (Rom 8:18). Constitutional location: the mind, the inner cognitive-moral capacity.
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[G — Structural Opposites]** Wickedness and injustice are the governing conditions of a person whose inner being is oriented away from God's moral order — not merely episodic failures but constitutional states of the person who has turned from righteousness. Their impact: the wicked person lives under the judgment of God (Psa 1:5–6), is cut off from the community of the righteous, and will not stand in the final judgment.

**T1.8 · Dimension Classification**  (9)

- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — **[CLUSTER — T1.8.1 through T1.8.3]** G — CC should run the dimension vocabulary query against the current database (`wa_obs_question_catalogue` or equivalent dimension table) before this tier is completed. All three T1.8 prompts are deferred to Gap resolution in Phase 10.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /gap — **[A1 — God Righteousness]** dimension vocabulary retrieval required. Content suggests moral-judicial dimension as primary.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /gap — **[A2 — Human Righteousness]** dimension vocabulary retrieval required. Content suggests moral-conditional dimension (governing state) with relational-judicial components.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /gap — **[B — Moral Uprightness]** dimension vocabulary retrieval required. Content suggests dispositional-moral dimension.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /gap — **[C — Justification]** dimension vocabulary retrieval required. Content suggests judicial-relational dimension.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /gap — **[D — Avenging Justice]** dimension vocabulary retrieval required. Content suggests judicial-retributive dimension.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /gap — **[E — Condemnation]** dimension vocabulary retrieval required. Content suggests judicial-conscience dimension.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /gap — **[F — Moral Reckoning]** dimension vocabulary retrieval required. Content suggests cognitive-moral dimension.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /gap — **[G — Structural Opposites]** dimension vocabulary retrieval required. Content suggests moral-conditional (negative pole) dimension.

**T2.8 · Body-Deposit (DNA / generational)**  (10)

- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — **[CLUSTER — T2.8]** S. No body-constitutional deposit is evidenced across the entire M26 cluster. The generational dimensions present in A2 and A1 are covenantal-relational, not physical-genetic. This finding closes T5.7 (see Part 3 — T5.7 will note: T2.8 found no deposit).
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /silent — **[A1 — God Righteousness]** No body-deposit evidenced for God's righteousness. The extension of God's righteousness across generations (Psa 103:17, VSG M26-A1-003) is covenantal-relational, not physical-genetic.
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[A2 — Human Righteousness]** There is generational language in A2: God extends righteousness "across generations to those who fear him" (Psa 103:17, VSG M26-A1-003, in A1 but relevant to A2's generational dimension). The children of the righteous are mentioned in the stability material — "the righteous are established forever" (Pro 10:25; 10:30, VSG M26-A2-013). However, this is covenantal-promissory language about God's generational commitment to the righteous, not a claim that righteousness itself is deposited in the body or transmitted genetically. No body-constitutional deposit evidenced; the generational consequence is spiritual-covenantal.
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /silent — **[B — Moral Uprightness]** No body-deposit evidenced for uprightness.
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /silent — **[C — Justification]** No body-deposit evidenced for justification. Justification as an act produces a declared status, not a physical deposit.
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /silent — **[D — Avenging Justice]** No body-deposit evidenced.
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /silent — **[E — Condemnation]** No body-deposit evidenced. The sustained weight of condemnation (accumulated guilt) is a spiritual-judicial state, not a physical deposit. The "storing up of wrath" (Rom 2:5, VSG 3207-001) is judicial accumulation, not physical.
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /silent — **[F — Moral Reckoning]** No body-deposit evidenced for the reckoning faculty.
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /silent — **[G — Structural Opposites]** The hardening of the heart (Rom 2:5) is described as a spiritual-judicial condition, not a physical deposit. No body-deposit evidenced.
- `T2.8.2` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — **[CLUSTER]** The verse evidence contains generational language but consistently frames it in covenantal-relational rather than physical-constitutional terms. The God who extends righteousness across generations (Psa 103:17) does so because of the covenant, not because righteousness is physically transmitted. This absence of body-deposit evidence is consistent with the cluster's consistent inner-to-outer direction (T2.7.2): the body is the expression-organ, not the deposit-organ.

**T5.7 · Deposit Consequence**  (3)

- `T5.7.1` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — **[CLUSTER]** T2.8 found no body-constitutional deposit across the entire M26 cluster. This closes T5.7 — no deposit was identified, so no deposit consequence can be assessed.
- `T5.7.2` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — **[CLUSTER]** The generational dimension is covenantal, not constitutional. The righteous person's children receive the benefit of God's covenant faithfulness — but this is God's faithfulness to his covenant, not a deposit of righteousness in the children's nature. Righteousness must be received by each person through faith (C: each person is justified individually); it is not genetically inherited.
- `T5.7.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **[A2 — Human Righteousness]** Covenantal-relational generational consequence (not physical deposit). "The offspring of the righteous will be delivered" (Pro 11:21, VSG M26-A2-001); God extends righteousness "across generations to those who fear him" (Psa 103:17, VSG M26-A1-003). The generational consequence is God's covenantal commitment to the righteous and their children — a relational-promissory consequence, not a physical inheritance of righteousness.

**T6.6 · Shared Verse Anchors**  (3)

- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — **[CLUSTER]** E. Several high-probability shared anchor verses: - **Eze 18:20** (VSG M26-A2-002) — "The soul who sins shall die... the righteousness of the righteous shall be upon himself." This verse likely appears as a primary anchor in any cluster studying the soul (ne.phesh), personal moral accountability, sin, or death. It is a highly cited verse across multiple domains. - **Rom 8:1–4** (VSG 4846-002) — "There is now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus... he condemned sin in the flesh." This verse is foundational across justification, condemnation, flesh/spirit, and likely multiple other clusters in the NT material. - **Rom 4:3–9** (VSG 3410-001) — "Abraham believed God, and it was counted to him as righteousness." This verse is the anchor for faith (pistis/emunah), justification, and the Abrahamic covenant theme. - **Psa 85:10** — "Steadfast love and faithfulness meet; righteousness and peace kiss each other." This verse likely appears in love, faithfulness, peace, and righteousness clusters. - **Gen 15:6** — Abraham's credited righteousness — a primary anchor for faith and justification clusters.
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — **[CLUSTER]** E. The shared verse anchors reveal that M26 is structurally interconnected with the faith cluster, the soul/ne.phesh cluster, the love/steadfast-love cluster, the peace cluster, and the condemnation/sin cluster. The shared anchors confirm the vocabulary-sharing findings of T6.4: M26 is a hub cluster whose evidence base overlaps with multiple adjacent domains.
- `T6.6.3` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — **[CLUSTER]** G. Dimension vocabulary is not yet available in this session (T1.8 was coded G for all sub-groups pending CC dimension vocabulary query). Dimensional sharing cannot be assessed until T1.8 is resolved in Phase 10. Noting explicitly: T6.7 is deferred to Phase 10, dependent on the same CC query as T1.8.


### M39  (31 findings)

**T1.2.3 · Best working description**  (2)

- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — Constitutively relational. No verse in M39-A evidences grace or blessing operating in isolation — every occurrence has a relational frame. Ba.rakh (divine blessing) always has a recipient; chen/charis (favour/grace) always has a giver and receiver; cha.nan (gracious) always describes a relationship between superior and suppliant; ra.tsah (acceptance) always describes a relational evaluation between offerer and receiver. The science extract (Section 1 — gift theory, Mauss) confirms: the gift is inherently a social act creating relational bonds. The grace-characteristic is constitutively two-directional.
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — More individual in primary expression, but not without communal dimension. Pro 15:13 and 17:22 (glad/good heart — good medicine) operate within the individual constitutional structure. Gen 4:7 (moral goodness and sin's crouching) is addressed to one person's inner orientation. However, 1Sa 2:26 (Samuel growing in favour), the communal approval texts (Jos 22:33), and Eze 36:11 (God doing good to Israel as a people) show that goodness has communal expression. The characteristic begins individually but does not stay there.  ---  ### T1.3 — Constitutional Location

**T1.8 · Dimension Classification**  (6)

- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — Primary anchors from the verse evidence: (1) **Exo 34:6** (2330-001) — the divine self-declaration as gracious; the foundational constitutional statement. (2) **Eph 2:8** (888-001) — the definitive NT grace-mode-of-salvation statement. (3) **Gen 12:2** (1299-001) — the paradigm covenantal sovereign blessing. Together these three anchors cover the three primary registers of M39-A: divine character (Exo 34:6), NT soteriology (Eph 2:8), OT covenantal blessing (Gen 12:2).
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — Primary anchor: **1Ki 8:18** (542-001) — "you did well that it was in your heart" — the most direct statement of goodness as a heart-prior quality evaluated by God. Secondary: **Pro 17:22** (632-001) — the somatic consequence statement, evidencing the dimensional depth of the characteristic.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **Rom 11:6** — "But if it is by grace, it is no longer on the basis of works; otherwise grace would no longer be grace." This verse draws the sharpest analytical boundary of any in the cluster: it names the logical exclusivity of grace, the impossibility of hybrid grace-works, and the self-defeating nature of grace earned. No other verse so precisely captures what makes grace structurally distinctive as an inner-being characteristic. Second: **Phili 2:13** — "God who works in you, both to will and to work for his good pleasure" — grace operating inside the human volitional faculty is the deepest inner-being claim in the M39-A corpus.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — **Gen 4:7** — "If you do well, will you not be accepted? And if you do not do well, sin is crouching at the door." This verse is analytically richest because it: (a) connects goodness (M39-B) to acceptance (M39-A's ra.tsah), establishing the inter-sub-group link; (b) names the consequence of goodness's absence (sin's entrance) with structural precision; (c) frames goodness as a maintained moral orientation with immediate inner consequences when it lapses.
- `T1.8.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **Gen 33:10** (795-003) — "I have seen your face, which is like seeing the face of God, and you have accepted me." Jacob equates Esau's accepting face with the face of God. The analytical question: is this mere hyperbole or does the verse encode a structural claim — that genuine human acceptance of another is an imaging of divine ra.tsah? If the latter, the grace-characteristic at the human-horizontal level is not merely analogical but participatory in divine acceptance. This has implications for how M39-A's relational-favour register (889-002; 888-003) is understood.
- `T1.8.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — **Num 24:1** (542-001) — "when Balaam saw that it pleased the Lord to bless Israel." Tov here names divine pleasure-at-blessing — operating in the M39-A register (divine pleasure = eudokia/ra.tsah) while being carried by the M39-B term (tov). The unresolved question: does tov's semantic breadth mean that the goodness and grace characteristics are ultimately facets of a single divine attribute (God is both good and gracious as one unified character), or are they genuinely distinct inner-being characteristics that tov happens to bridge?

**T2.8 · Body-Deposit (DNA / generational)**  (6)

- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — Yes — generational deposit is explicitly named in the blessing corpus. Ba.rakh group 1299-001 (Gen 12:2–3; 22:17–18): "I will bless you and make your name great"; "in your offspring all the nations of the earth shall be blessed" — the Abrahamic blessing passes generationally through the offspring. This is a covenant-biological deposit: the blessing-characteristic constitutes a generational inheritance. Ba.rakh (Gen 9:1; 9:26) — Noah's blessing of Shem — similarly passes generationally.
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /silent — No verse in M39-B explicitly names a generational or bodily constitutional deposit of the goodness characteristic. Pro 17:22 names a functional physical effect (bones healed) but not a permanent deposit. This finding is formally closed: S.
- `T2.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — Supporting: the Abrahamic blessing-to-offspring pattern (1299-001) is the most extensive generational-deposit evidence in the cluster. Gen 28:14 ("your offspring shall be like the dust of the earth") and Gen 26:4 ("I will give to your offspring all these lands") name the blessing as constitutionally transmitted through biological lineage. The charisma group (1301-001 — Rom 11:29): "the gifts and the calling of God are irrevocable" — once given, the charisma-grace-deposit is not revoked; it has a permanent character.
- `T2.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — Contradicting: no generational or permanent bodily deposit is named. The somatic effects (Pro 17:22) are functional and reversible — they describe the ongoing consequence of the inner state, not a permanent constitutional change.
- `T2.8.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — Not silent — generational deposit evidenced (Abrahamic blessing corpus, Rom 11:29). This feeds into T5.7.
- `T2.8.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — Silent on constitutional body deposit. T5.7 will record this as S.  ---  ### T2.9 — Origin and Source

**T5.7 · Deposit Consequence**  (4)

- `T5.7.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — T2.8 identified the Abrahamic generational blessing-deposit and the irrevocable charismata. Developmental consequences: (1) The Abrahamic blessing-deposit (Gen 12:3; 22:18; 1299-001) produces a lineage of blessing-bearers — each generation inherits the blessing-mandate and extends it. Gen 26:4 ("I will give to your offspring all these lands... and in your offspring all the nations of the earth shall be blessed") — the deposit multiplies through generations toward universal scope. (2) The irrevocable charismata (Rom 11:29 — 1301-001) produce ongoing community capacity across time — the charisma-endowment is not diminished by use or time. The developmental consequence is sustained and expanding community-building capacity.
- `T5.7.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — Generational consequence is explicitly evidenced in M39-A. The Abrahamic blessing-deposit (ba.rakh group 1299-001 — Gen 12:3; 22:18; 26:4; 28:14) passes generationally through the offspring, constituting subsequent generations' identity and vocation: "in your offspring all the families of the earth shall be blessed" (Gen 22:18). The charisma-deposit (1301-001 — Rom 11:29: "the gifts and the calling of God are irrevocable") names the charismata as permanently carried forward beyond the individual — not diminished by use or time. The generational deposit is forward-scoped by design: what God gives in grace to one generation constitutes the inherited standing of those who follow.
- `T5.7.2` · cluster_finding · /silent — S — T2.8 found no constitutional body deposit for the goodness characteristic in M39-B's verse set. T5.7.2 is accordingly closed. No generational deposit evidenced for goodness in M39-B.
- `T5.7.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — T2.8 found no constitutional body deposit for M39-B. T5.7 formally closed for M39-B: S.  ---  ## T6 — Structural Relationships with Other Characteristics  ### T6.1 — Co-occurrence

**T6.6 · Shared Verse Anchors**  (6)

- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — Yes — three high-confidence shared anchors: (1) Exo 34:6 (2330-001 anchor): this verse is shared across multiple registries — it is the primary anchor for compassion (R023), the mercy registry (R111), and appears in the love registry (R103). The divine character formula is the most cross-registry verse in the programme. (2) Eph 4:32 (5470-002 anchor): also a primary anchor for kindness (R099) — "be kind to one another, tenderhearted, forgiving one another" — the forgiveness and kindness characteristics share this anchor. (3) Psa 103:8 (2330-001): also appears in the anger/patience and love registries.
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — Partial: Gen 4:7 (632-002 anchor) appears in the sin/conscience domain (the sin-crouching imagery). Pro 17:22 may appear in health/wellbeing adjacent registries. The shared anchors for M39-B are less extensively confirmed than M39-A's.
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — Exo 34:6's appearance across grace, compassion, mercy, love, and anger/patience registries reveals that these characteristics are not separate divine attributes but aspects of one unified divine character, displayed in one moment of divine self-revelation. The verse is the programme's most cross-registered precisely because the divine character is indivisible — grace and compassion and mercy and steadfast love and patience all emerge simultaneously from one self-declaration.
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — Gen 4:7's sharing with the sin/conscience domain reveals the structural link between the goodness characteristic and the sin/conscience cluster: goodness is what sin displaces; the conscience registers goodness's absence. The two characteristics are structurally interdependent.
- `T6.6.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — Not silent — shared anchors are identified for both sub-groups.  ---  ### T6.7 — Dimensional Sharing
- `T6.6.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — Not silent — shared anchors are identified for both sub-groups.  ---  ### T6.7 — Dimensional Sharing

**T6.7 · Dimensional Sharing**  (7)

- `T6.7.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — M39-A's 23 active VCGs carry 5 of the programme's 11 analytical dimensions: 06 — Relational Disposition (14 VCGs — dominant), 05 — Moral Character (4), 11 — Divine-Human Correspondence (3), 04 — Volition (1), 01 — Emotion — Positive (1). The sub-group's dimensional signature is relational disposition: more than half of M39-A's VCGs sit in that register, consistent with grace as constitutively a disposition of one party toward another. The full 5-dimension set is shared with 14+ other M-clusters (M02, M03, M04, M05, M08, M09, M10, M18, M19, M22, M23, M26, M27, M28); M39-A occupies the core inner-being dimensional space rather than a rare corner.
- `T6.7.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — M39-B's 4 active VCGs carry 3 dimensions: 01 — Emotion — Positive (2 VCGs — dominant), 05 — Moral Character (1), 03 — Cognition (1). The sub-group's dimensional signature is positive emotion, consistent with the affective-gladness pole of tov/ya.tav. All 3 dimensions are shared with 15+ other M-clusters; M39-B sits in the affective register that M04 (Joy), M22 (Praise), and M33 (Peace) also occupy.
- `T6.7.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — The Relational Disposition dimension is shared across the widest cluster of adjacent characteristics (grace, compassion, mercy, kindness, love). This confirms M39-A's structural position as a hub characteristic: its primary dimension is shared with all the characteristics in the relational-love family, confirming that grace is the foundational relational disposition from which others are differentiated expressions.
- `T6.7.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — M39-B's sharing of the Moral Character dimension with wisdom and the goodness registry confirms its place within the moral-character family of characteristics.
- `T6.7.3` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — M39 carries 6 of the programme's 11 dimensions across its sub-groups; shares ≥1 dimension with 45 other M-clusters. 19 clusters share all 6 of M39's dimensions, placing M39 firmly in the core inner-being dimensional register, not at a rare-dimension corner. The discriminating signal is at sub-group level rather than cluster level: M39-A on 06 — Relational Disposition, M39-B on 01 — Emotion — Positive. The lowest-sharing M-clusters with M39 are M15 (wisdom — 1 shared dimension: 03 — Cognition) and M20 (doubt/despair/anxiety — 1 shared dimension: 05 — Moral Character); these two complete clusters sit furthest from grace and goodness on the dimensional axis.
- `T6.7.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — Noted: precise dimensional sharing counts are a gap (G). The analytical direction based on verse evidence is recorded above; CC database query will provide the counts for Phase 9 verification.  ---  ## T7 — Evidential and Methodological Foundation  ### T7.1 — Lexical and Semantic Analysis
- `T6.7.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — Noted: precise dimensional sharing counts are a gap (G). The analytical direction based on verse evidence is recorded above; CC database query will provide the counts for Phase 9 verification.  ---  ## T7 — Evidential and Methodological Foundation  ### T7.1 — Lexical and Semantic Analysis


### M46  (32 findings)

**T1.2.3 · Best working description**  (4)

- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — Inner closure is the progressive condition of the inner person who has received material abundance without the inner tethering of trust in God and gratitude: the heart grows fat and unresponsive (sha.men, Isa 6:10; Deu 32:15), trust is redirected to material security (Pro 11:28; Psa 52:7), and self-assessment becomes anchored in material condition (Rev 3:17). The primary faculty engaged is trust (ba.tach) in its misdirected form. The impact: the person cannot perceive, cannot receive from God, and cannot evaluate their own inner condition accurately.
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — Hardness and insatiability are two related inner-life failures produced by abundance. Hardness: the person whose capacity for pity (cha.mal) has been eroded by wealth becomes unable to see or respond to the suffering of others (Luk 16:19-21; 2Sa 12:4; Jam 2:2-3). Insatiability: the person whose inner capacity for satisfaction has been damaged cannot receive what they have as sufficient (Ecc 4:8; 6:2; Pro 30:15). Both represent the inner person's contraction — unable to give outward, unable to receive inward.
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — The ordering of character and wealth: the wisdom tradition's consistent observation that inner character (diligence, trust, humility, fear of God) determines the quality and trajectory of material life. Pro 13:4 is the clearest formulation: the soul (ne.phesh) of the diligent is richly supplied — inner character produces inner richness that includes and exceeds material wealth. Isa 33:6 names the telos: the fear of the Lord is Zion's true treasure. Material wealth is the surface; character is the depth.
- `T1.2.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — The inner life beyond material circumstance: the cluster of inner postures and acts through which the person is no longer governed by the wealth domain. Poverty of spirit (Mat 5:3) — the constitutive openness and dependence before God. Voluntary release (2Cor 8:9; Gen 14:23; Luk 19:8) — the deliberate act of giving or refusing wealth in response to a higher valuation. Learned contentment (autarkēs, Phil 4:11) — the achieved inner stability that material circumstances cannot move. These three do not constitute a single characteristic but a family of inner postures oriented in the same direction: away from material circumstance as the governing force of the inner life.

**T1.8 · Dimension Classification**  (9)

- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — The primary dimension of M46-A (inner closure) is Orientation/Trust — the faculty of trust (ba.tach) is the primary faculty engaged in the misdirection pattern. The trust faculty is redirected from God to material security. Secondary dimension: Perception — the closure produces loss of perceptual capacity (Isa 6:10 — cannot see, hear, understand).
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — The primary dimension of M46-B (hardness and insatiability) is Relational Capacity (outward hardness face) and Affective Faculty (insatiability face). The hardness erodes the relational-compassion capacity; the insatiability is an affective condition (the inability to be satisfied). These are distinct dimensions within the same sub-group.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — The primary dimension of M46-C (ordering of character and wealth) is Moral Character / Virtue — the wisdom tradition frames the material domain as the domain in which character (diligence, trust, humility, fear) produces its fruits. The secondary dimension is Agency — the wise person's diligence (Pro 13:4) is an exercise of sustained, character-shaped agency.
- `T1.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — The primary dimension of M46-D (inner life beyond material circumstance) is Orientation/Trust toward God — poverty of spirit is the condition of dependence and openness before God; learned contentment is the inner independence from circumstance that reflects a trust-grounded settledness. Secondary dimension: Volition — voluntary release (Gen 14:23; Luk 19:8; 2Cor 8:9) is explicitly a volitional act.
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — Trust-dimension evidence: Psa 49:6 (trust in wealth and boast of riches), Psa 52:7 (trusted in abundance of riches rather than God), Pro 11:28 (trusts in riches will fall), 1Ti 6:17 (charge rich not to set hopes on uncertain riches). Perception-dimension evidence: Isa 6:10 (cannot see, hear, understand), Rev 3:17 (not realising their actual condition).
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — Relational-capacity evidence: 2Sa 12:4 (cha.mal absent — unwilling to spare from own flock), Luk 16:19-21 (ignoring Lazarus at gate), Jam 2:2-3 (differential treatment of poor and rich). Affective evidence: Ecc 4:8 (eyes never satisfied), Ecc 6:2 (wealth present but power to enjoy absent), Pro 30:15 (never says enough).
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — Moral-character evidence: Pro 13:4 (ne.phesh of diligent richly supplied — character produces soul-richness), Pro 10:4 (diligent hand makes rich), Pro 22:4 (reward of humility and fear is riches). Agency evidence: Pro 13:11 (gathering little by little — patient sustained activity produces wealth).
- `T1.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — Trust/Orientation evidence: Phil 4:11-13 (learned contentment explicitly grounded in "I can do all things through him who strengthens me" — the contentment is trust-grounded); Mat 5:3 (poor in spirit before God — orientation of dependence). Volition evidence: Gen 14:23 (Abram's deliberate refusal), Luk 19:8 (Zacchaeus's spoken resolution), 2Cor 8:9 (Christ's voluntary act of becoming poor).
- `T1.8.3` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — The secondary dimensions do not compete with the primary classifications; they enrich them. M46-A's trust-misdirection produces the perceptive loss as a consequence — trust (primary) leads to perception (secondary) in a causal sequence. M46-B's relational failure (hardness) and affective condition (insatiability) are distinct but both produced by the same source condition (wealth without right orientation). M46-C's moral character (primary) generates agency (secondary) — the character expressed through sustained action. M46-D's trust-orientation (primary) enables and motivates the volitional acts (secondary) — contentment grounded in God (trust) produces the willingness to release (volition). The dimensions are related in causal sequences, not competitive.

**T2.8 · Body-Deposit (DNA / generational)**  (8)

- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — The sha.men / fat-heart pattern has a potential generational dimension. Deu 32:15 is addressed to Israel as a people across generations: "Jeshurun grew fat and kicked" — the national pattern of prosperity-apostasy is a generational observation. Neh 9 recounts it across multiple generations of Israel's history. The prosperity-apostasy pattern is documented as a generational-transmission phenomenon: one generation's prosperity producing the next generation's inheritance of a culture of apostasy. Whether this is a biological deposit or a cultural-covenantal transmission is not specified, but the generational pattern is evidenced.
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /silent — No explicit body-deposit or generational-transmission language for M46-B, M46-C, or M46-D in the cluster's verse evidence.
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /silent — No explicit body-deposit or generational-transmission language for M46-B, M46-C, or M46-D in the cluster's verse evidence.
- `T2.8.1` · cluster_finding · /silent — No explicit body-deposit or generational-transmission language for M46-B, M46-C, or M46-D in the cluster's verse evidence.
- `T2.8.2` · cluster_finding · /finding — Hos 10:1 ("the more his fruit increased, the more altars he built") names the proportional relationship between material prosperity and idolatry — suggesting a cumulative amplifying pattern across Israel's history, consistent with a generational deposit. The pattern appears across Hosea, Deuteronomy, and Nehemiah 9, all evidencing the same cross-generational cycle.
- `T2.8.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — Body-deposit evidence is silent for these sub-groups. This silence feeds into T5.7 — deposit consequences are limited to M46-A's generational pattern.
- `T2.8.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — Body-deposit evidence is silent for these sub-groups. This silence feeds into T5.7 — deposit consequences are limited to M46-A's generational pattern.
- `T2.8.3` · cluster_finding · /finding — Body-deposit evidence is silent for these sub-groups. This silence feeds into T5.7 — deposit consequences are limited to M46-A's generational pattern.

**T5.7 · Deposit Consequence**  (4)

- `T5.7.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — T2.8 identified the generational-transmission pattern in M46-A: the prosperity-apostasy cycle evidenced across multiple generations of Israel's history (Deu 32:15 → Neh 9:25-26 → Hos 10:1 → the repeated pattern across the OT). The developmental consequence of the deposit: successive generations inheriting both the material prosperity and the cultural-spiritual pattern of apostasy that accompanies it. Each generation's prosperity produces the conditions for the next generation's inner closure. The deposit is cultural-covenantal rather than biological, but it is generational in scope.
- `T5.7.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — T2.8 found no explicit body-deposit evidence. T5.7 cannot be extended for these sub-groups.
- `T5.7.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — T2.8 found no explicit body-deposit evidence. T5.7 cannot be extended for these sub-groups.
- `T5.7.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — T2.8 found no explicit body-deposit evidence. T5.7 cannot be extended for these sub-groups.

**T6.6 · Shared Verse Anchors**  (4)

- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — Rev 3:17 (the cluster's primary M46-A anchor) is almost certainly a shared anchor: it is the definitive inner-being statement about the Laodicean church and is likely anchoring multiple characteristics (pride, false-wisdom, self-sufficiency, and the inner-closure pattern of M46-A). Isa 6:10 (M46-A secondary anchor) is a major anchor in any study of divine judicial hardening, perception, hearing, and turning.
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — Pro 13:4 (M46-C primary anchor — "the soul of the diligent is richly supplied") is also a major wisdom-tradition anchor for diligence, soul-content, and the character-outcome connection. It will appear in M15 (Wisdom) and any cluster dealing with diligence or ne.phesh-level inner being.
- `T6.6.1` · cluster_finding · /finding — 2Cor 8:9 (M46-D primary anchor) is one of the NT's major Christological wealth verses and is a shared anchor with any cluster dealing with Christ's incarnation, grace, kenosis, or self-giving. Phil 4:11 (M46-D secondary anchor) will appear in M33 (Peace/Contentment adjacent), and any cluster dealing with contentment, circumstantial stability, or Pauline inner life.
- `T6.6.2` · cluster_finding · /cluster_synthesis — The shared-anchor pattern confirms that M46's four sub-groups are not isolated but are deeply embedded in the broader inner-being vocabulary of Scripture. The wealth domain's primary anchors are also primary anchors for trust, wisdom, Christology, peace, and perception — confirming that the wealth domain is a crossroads where many characteristics converge and are simultaneously engaged.

**T6.7 · Dimensional Sharing**  (3)

- `T6.7.1` · cluster_finding · /gap — Dimensional sharing data is not yet available from the programme's cross-cluster dimensional analysis for M46's newly-constituted term set. G noted: the data would need to be generated from the dimension registry cross-cluster query once the M46 findings are registered (Phase 9 onward). What can be stated from the current evidence: the primary dimensions identified in T1.8 (Trust-orientation, Relational Capacity, Moral Character, Volition) are dimensions that appear across multiple clusters in the programme — they are not unique to M46.
